Share your idea

The state employees engagement has closed. Visit Engaged California for more information.

Use the comment box below to submit one idea to make California’s government more effective and efficient.

After you comment:

  • Explore, ‘like’, and comment on other posts to help refine and strengthen ideas
  • Share additional ideas you may have on the next pages of this forum
expand_less expand_more Share your idea - Primary problem and ideas to solve the problem 1150

Add your thoughts

  • Guest-22926

    Consistent training across multiple offices within the same department. Greater collaboration to reduce redundancy

    October 24, 2025
  • Guest-337785

    Two of the biggest problems I've come across in State Employment in three different departments are closely related. These problems are the the lack of standardized training, and that the allocated number of job positions do not scale based on each office's workload. The number of staff available to give effective training is impacted by the face that the number of staff in each office within the same department, branch, and division is not scaled for the workload within their respective jurisdictions. For example, every office has the same number of AGPAs and PT IIs while 35% of the offices had at least double the workload within their respective jurisdictions than the rest of the offices. Unbalanced workloads between two locations will lead to the loss in retention of staff. This eventually will require training for new staff that replace those employees that leave a busier office. When there isn't standardized training each employee, and office, will come up with processes that may be incorrect. Eventually an audit may be completed and the staff in the office find out after the audit that they were incorrectly trained and things have been processed incorrectly. Some of the shortcuts in processes occur because there is a lack of staff, which ties back to the issue of job positions not scaling based on workload.

    October 24, 2025
  • Guest-208784

    1. There is far too much red tape. Is it possible to review some of the legislated activities to see if they are obsolete and have them removed. 2. Recruitments in particular take a ridiculously long time, which often results in losing the best candidates. Maybe a Lean project from CalHR down would help to reduce recruitment time. 3. Finding information is staggeringly difficult. (Maybe AI will improve that.)

    October 24, 2025
  • Guest-597498

    An issue I've noticed is that rather than improving on already existing legislation/regulations/rules, we create new ones. I think it would be more effective and clear to all involved if we edited our previous work rather than creating new places for people to find information.

    October 24, 2025
  • Guest-959167

    I work in a department that trains new staff to do their role within our agency and we have been working to improve onboarding processes within the agency for all staff. When I was first hired the agency in work for only focused on onboarding for one group of staff and not the staff that support our agency mission, today it is better with more training focusing on onboarding and training for all classifications within the agency. Looking at current practices for onboarding and training is the first step to improving how an agency moves forward to training and retain employees. Training departments also need to understand the importance of surveys/evaluations in their training to get real feedback on how they are doing with their training. However, surveys are no good if a department/agency does not have someone to look at the data and bring forth the results of the survey. The second step is to have good documentation of current and past processes used within the agency or department. I have found the lack of documentation waste time and does not allow improvement to easily happen. Understand that people leave and how they do there job is important for the next person who will continue that role within the department. If the role is new to the department, have a clear plan of what is expected for the new person and the resources available to them. The third step is to have someone assigned to new staff as a mentor. Make sure the mentor understands their role.

    October 24, 2025
  • Guest-27579

    Upgrade financial systems to me more efficient and transparent.

    October 24, 2025
  • Guest-24445

    The state would save SO much money if it would revisit the mandatory new supervisor 80 hour training, 40 hour mandatory CEA training, and 40 hour mandatory manager training. These used to be in-person classes where departments would pay CalHR approximately $1500 and staff would have an opportunity to collaborate with colleagues from other departments, get training, have an opportunity to ask questions and share ideas, etc. During COVID these classes went virtual and of the approximately 30 staff who have taken these courses since who I have personally discussed this with, they receive no value from the virtual trainings. They are go-at-your-own pace, there is no engagement with others, and staff just have them running in the background to "knock them out" while they get their work done; yet departments still pay CalHR $1500 per employee. Is there really a $1500 cost to CalHR when it's a virtual course with no instructor, no meeting room rental, no CalHR staff time, etc? I understand this funds CalHR, but for industry-funded departments and programs who have to pay this cost, are they really getting any value for their dollar? If CalHR would like to keep these courses automated and with no CalHR involvement the cost should be drastically reduced. Or, to get more bang for their buck, it would be a much better investment if these courses went back to in-person. Or at least allow departments that option.

    October 24, 2025
  • Guest-557866

    I have worked as a State employee for approximately 7 years now and I feel the lack of accountability in departments and individual employees contributes to the burn out of highly productive employees. I also manage employees, and the current performance management system makes supervisors and managers feel unsupported to the point where it is easier to let an employee do what they want rather than hold them accountable. Prior to working in the public sector, I worked 12 years in the private sector and find it difficult to understand why it is so much more difficult to hold employees in the public sector accountable than in the private sector.

    October 24, 2025
  • Guest-334228

    Hiring practices often appear inconsistent and inequitable. On one hand, there is a strong emphasis on rewarding current civil service experience, while on the other, external candidates are expected to meet disproportionately high educational and experiential thresholds. For instance, some classifications equate one year of state service to a master’s degree plus three years of professional experience. This raises a critical question: how can a single year of internal service be considered equivalent to such extensive academic and professional preparation? This practice not only undermines the value of formal education and diverse professional backgrounds but also creates systemic barriers for qualified individuals outside the state system—effectively making the process exclusionary. To improve both efficiency and equity in hiring, California should implement a transparent, competency-based hiring framework. This would involve clearly defining the skills, knowledge, and outcomes expected for each classification and allowing candidates—whether internal or external—to demonstrate their qualifications through a combination of experience, education, and performance-based assessments. Such a model would reduce arbitrary equivalencies, promote fairness, and ensure that the most capable individuals are selected to serve the public, regardless of their entry point into government.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-776933

    I would encourage us to look at increasing a sense of hope and trust that our government is doing the right things to protect and improve the quality of life for all Californians. For state staff, the return to office mandates do not seem to be based in good evidence. During the public health emergency, state staff adapted and delivered the same or better quality work and improved the quality of their own lives.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-923901

    There are too many review steps in almost every government process. This usually happens because of errors in the process which lead to management wanting additional review to ensure there are less errors. When errors continue to happen, the review level continues to escalate. This results in leadership thinking their staff are unable to complete the task correctly and frustrating staff on the "moving target" or unclear expectations caused by various style preferences from different reviewers. This leads to requirements for staff to create additional documentation such as summaries, cover memos, and routing packets to navigate the higher management level reviewing the work. It is extra, non-value added work to explain what the work is why they are approving it. I recommend lowering the approval level to the lowest supervisory level who knows the program. This will reduce turn-around time for requests, improve customer satisfaction (since they get whatever service we provide sooner), increased confidence in employees, and better morale for teams. Additional levels of review do not eliminate errors, but instead increase them due to different standards between different levels of reviews. Instead, find out why the errors are happening and fix them at the source. This can be done easily between supervisors and front-line staff.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-813071

    The primary issue among state employees is the wasteful spending of having to use a small business. Small businesses are marking items up so much that it adds up so quickly and eats away at our lab consumable/supply budget. The small businesses also add a $49 shipping fee (why?) when items do not come from the small business but rather come from various vendors to us. They are the middleman placing orders with the vendors. $49 x # orders equal a lot of money. Small businesses are taking advantage of the state it seems to line their pockets. Also, the time it takes to process orders through purchasing takes a month to two months. We are not set up to conduct research in the lab as a result. When I joined the state many years ago, we had a limited $ blanket and we called the vendor directly and placed our order. We received our orders fairly quick. We need to have the ordering better streamlined in terms of processing time and how to save taxpayer $ and get rid of utilization of small business and be able to go back to ordering from vendors directly.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-580257

    Housing. Continued support and expansion of housing first.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-518566

    First of all, I want to thank all the staff responsible for bringing this Forum into existence and the staff that are running and monitoring this forum. Here's my contribution after working for the State of California on and off for 15+ years. State salaries Certain State positions are pigeonhole into arbitrary (or based on outdated data) salary ranges without taking account location and parity with the private sector. For example, the HFEN (Health Facility Evaluator Nurse) position. A comparable position in the private sector, in the San Francisco area, (according to Google) is around $155K (for a nurse consultant). My compensation for 2023 according to Transparent California =$98,271.44 (not counting benefits as the average salary quoted by Google does not include benefits) I've been with the State long enough to know that trying to ask for fair compensation is like trying to get blood from stone. I get it. We are painted with the same brush. We are stigmatized as being lazy, inefficient, bureaucratic etc. What tax payers would reward State workers with a pay raise? (slim to none) So here is my proposal. Don't reward my efficiency with more work and flowery praise. Compensate me financially. There's an Over Time budget within each department. Give me access to the OT budget, I don't mind writing a proposal on how I can increase your/my Department's efficiency or backlogs to solve your problems. I'm ready to kick start this in my Department ...Are any Managers ready?

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-279081

    Excessive programming, projects, and the associated encumbered funds. Over the years I've seen much waste. Some in inescapable since ideal systems don't exist. But I do see: -funds encumbered for staff or programs that go unstaffed for several years. -Projects and programs and their associated spending that are "good ideas" but do not support greater objectives or strategies. -Overfunded projects and programs that don't seem to bring value to Californians when compared to other underfunded programs that do bring value. -Frivolous last minute spending to justify retention of "Use it or Lose it" funding resulting in acquisition and storage of unnecessary and useless materials that ultimately get thrown out years later. If a program has no need of the money, it should be let go for other programs that do have need.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-909962

    Not enough staffing, having to work so much overtime to cover the command center and manning 911. Low pay that doesn't match the work that we do, the trauma we endure and the numerous hours and days we are away from our families

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-381147

    Telework Favoritism Problem: Managers allow their favorite employees to telework without supervision and provide all these reasons why their favorite people are allowed to telework. The employees they do not like, they do not allow them to telework, and make up all these excuses as to why they are not allowed to telework. Solution: Telework should not be on a case-by-case basis, because that allows managers to create their own criteria for which employees can telework and which cannot. Telework should be based on qualifications. Can that position be done remotely, or can it only be done in the office.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-381147

    Job Positions on Hold Problem: Some positions have been on hold for months and years, and managers give them to their favorite people; they do not advertise the position —they give it to a transfer employee. People who can apply for that position are having their opportunity taken away because the position is not advertised and given to transfer employees. These transfer positions are not the exception; they are the rule and are being abused. Solution: Transfer employees and current employees should be able to apply for the available position. No favoritism should be allowed in job positions, and they should be advertised.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-894422

    I have noticed that innovation and technology are being implemented inconsistently and mostly for the benefit of the state employee, not for the public or registered users accessing the system or requiring assistance. Electronic payment options, integration with access systems similar to IDme, and more direct engagement with the public (which has declined over the last few years) would all be beneficial.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-564265

    The levels of approval that are required for many processes and procedures are often way too long and laborious. We need to find ways to improve efficiency not only for internal processes, but for services that directly impact the public. I get discouraged that the general public thinks state workers are "lazy" or "incompetent" when often, our hands are tied and we cannot move any faster.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-526340

    Adults with no insurance may not have access to all vaccines they are eligible to receive. Some ideas to bridge the gap include a proposal to subsidize vaccines and make them accessible to all, at no cost, regardless of insurance status so long as the person has lived in California for at least 12 months.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-515133

    The purchasing system at my organization is very difficult and slows down our work a lot. All purchases have to go through small businesses, which makes the process of purchasing quite slow. Sometimes it takes up to six months to get lab supplies that we need. Additionally, the small businesses are much more expensive. I like the ethos behind the idea of buying from small businesses, but these businesses are basically just someone in an office ordering from large companies then marking up the prices and reselling to us when we place our orders with them. It's really inefficient in terms of time and money.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-617436

    Clarity, specificity, and guidance. I am fairly new to the state. My role isn't the easiest to define and probably can't be trained as such. However, the vast amount of resources and information needed to do and understand my job is hard to find and not clear as to what is essential for my position. An all in place for gathered information for ALL state employees that covers new employee info, paychecks, benefits, sites I need to log into etc. And an other one stop shared site for specific positions or departments that hold information, important links, documents, frequently used sites, applications, user guides, training videos etc. that are necessary to do your job.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-180061

    There are many ways in which we can boost morale that need to be taken into consideration. Employees should not be paying to park to work. At my office, if you are lucky, you can find a spot at the lot that charges $8 per day. Some offices have a parking structure that charge $300 per month. We already pay with our time to commute, we pay for car insurance, we pay for gas, and to pay to park when we have to return to the office is a financial stressor for many employees. There are additional hidden fees of employment besides what is already taken from our checks. We had to pool our resources to replace the microwave, we pay for our water, the coffee, replacement of kitchen appliances, and mandatory holiday gatherings. We are told we have to attend team building events, but we are expected to pay or make the food and decorations. We are asked to give for food drives, for fundraising, for birthdays, and potlucks, and we give and give and give... I don't want to break even when I work after paying for food, gas, parking, and stocking my employer with appliances because we won't be able to heat or store our food otherwise. Morale isn't low because we lack a talent show. I don't want a party; I want free parking and free cold water.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-800299

    Poverty. I think there should be ongoing services that will eliminate poverty for all people.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-863160

    As a new employee in a large department, I'm already seeing some issues with siloing between branches and divisions that is resulting in duplication of effort, which leads to turf issues and confusion among staff and external partners. In some of these cases, we have consultants doing work in one branch that staff are doing in another branch, with no awareness between the two branches that the same work is being done. I think a few things would help with this: more communication both up and down the chain within branches, better communication between branches, and stronger leadership to communicate the expectation of collaboration and alignment between branches to break down silos, reduce duplication of effort, and clarify roles and responsibilities.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-800299

    Poverty. Establish fully equipped services centers in every community that provides food, shelter, jobs, child care, transportation, etc every day for needed families.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-283622

    As a Californian, I believe health facilities need to continue being monitored by government to ensure patients receive proper care. As a CA state employee, one thing that has brought about engagement is the teleworking option. I believe that giving state employees the opportunity to serve California's from their home gives us work life balance and makes us more productive and engaged in helping others.

    October 23, 2025
  • Guest-176854

    Simplify and streamline the hiring process by removing requirements on both the applicant and the hiring department. Polling applicants and departments could reveal the most arduous requirements, which could then be considered for revision or removal.

    October 22, 2025
  • Guest-24543

    I think as a society in general we should be working towards less cars on the road, and therefore there should be a push for EVERYONE that can work from home to do so, not just state employees. It is a total waste of time, money, space, resources to make millions of people clog up the freeways to commute to an office to do the same work they can do in their own home office. That is the way of the future, and I really wish California would step up and lead the way. Sadly, I feel like this might never happen because of our capitalist society.

    October 22, 2025
  • Guest-26496

    A primary problem that I would address is improving the state's budget and financial management, the high cost of living, housing and homelessness. My ideas to solve the state's budget problems would be to implement strategies to either increase revenue or decrease spending and improving budgeting processes. As for the high cost of living, housing, and homelessness, I recommend a combination of increasing housing supply (i.e., affordable housing), implementing tenant protections, expanding rental assistance programs, and lowering other essential costs such as public transportation and groceries.

    October 22, 2025
  • Guest-319683

    The State purchasing and spending rules have not been updated to modern and realisting thresholds. This creates an incredible burdensome and bureaucratic process for staff to accomplish our mission. Purchasing limits need to be updated and then an annual COLA applied to keep them modern. An example is still considering $1,000 a public works project. $1,000 does not go very far for facility repairs.

    October 22, 2025
  • Guest-319683

    Our inability build capital projects in a timely fashion as well as the incredible soft costs built in by the Department of General Services. We need to be more creative with use of Public Private partnerships and buying existing buildings. The same can be said for facility leases, it should not take a year to execute a lease. DGS as an ISF control agency has no incentive to advocate for Departments and keep costs at a reasonable level.

    October 22, 2025
  • Guest-25325

    Reduce the number of Departments that have the same goal.

    October 22, 2025
  • Guest-27715

    Improper leadership is the biggest hurdle. Often those who are selected to lead us are unqualified, hold old and rigid mentalities, are afraid of change and won't even entertain possibilities, are mediocre at best.

    October 22, 2025
  • Guest-26022

    Streamline hiring process for all entry level/seasonal positions. For agencies that work regularly with the California Conservation Corps (CCC) and provide side by side mentorship by working together to accomplish projects and maintenance work there should be an option to utilize direct hire authority to bring on Corps Members into pre-selected entry/seasonal level positions. The two agencies should set milestones, and if a Corps Member is successful and demonstrates the strengths and abilities required over a specified amount of time, direct hire authority should be allowed when both parties agree the Corps Member should be hired into the other agency.

    October 22, 2025
  • Guest-256231

    I would like to see continuous promotion list. Not create a new list every 2 years. If there are only 3 ranks, why do we need to take a promotional exam every 2 years? If a person is on the list, they should stay on the list until they promote or choose to leave the list. It takes a lot of personnel hours and money to do promotional exams. Just add to the list, not abolish it and start over.

    October 22, 2025
  • Guest-319806

    As a Californian with an adult daughter who is developmentally delayed it appears that the people who are making the decision of the needs for people like her are out of touch with reality. To expect to be heard would be to show up at a hearing or write a legislator which neither of those things she could do or neither could I on her behalf. Maybe send out a poll to the clients at the regional centers and ask what needs are needed.

    October 22, 2025
  • Guest-381147

    Problem: Allowing managers to interview candidates only creates favoritism in hiring, favoring those they want in a position and excluding other candidates. This has been going on for years and it needs to stop immediately. Solution: Have an independent panel interview candidates to exclude favoritism. Since the independent panel do not know the candidates and can focus on hiring qualify candidates only.

    October 21, 2025
  • Guest-27707

    An issue we have been dealing with is lack of support from DGS regarding auto inspector approvals for repairs to state vehicles. They are not providing training or communication for their policies, besides expecting employees to memorize the Fleet manual. When an employee makes a mistake with obtaining pre-approval for a repair, or if the shop completes additional repairs and authorization is not obtained, the auto inspectors are denying approvals and are not able to provide constructive next steps. Auto Inspectors should be advocating for the State's vehicles and equipment being maintained for safe operation and should be HELPING employees with the process. There is currently no process in place for when DGS will not approve a repair since SCO requires the DGS stamp of approval (for auto repairs over the established dollar amounts) in order to process voucher payments. DGS needs to work with state agencies instead of against us in this matter. Proactive communication and training should be available and provided to enforce and educate on changing expectations.

    October 21, 2025
  • Guest-537514

    The State lacks in succession planning and lacks in providing an environment / process for effective and efficient succession planning.

    October 21, 2025
  • Guest-25305

    I think there should be more collaboration within the CA Natural Resources Agency (CNRA). CNRA houses many large critical Departments such as CalFire, CDFW, State Parks, and Dept of Water Resources. Each of these large agencies hoard positions and funding, often for overlapping initiatives. Greater collaboration on multiple issues could help address critical funding issues in the preservation and protection of our natural resources for the benefit of all CA citizens.

    October 21, 2025
  • Guest-26912

    One major issue I would address is how difficult and confusing the state hiring process is, especially for people outside of state services. Even with over 25 years of experience in the field, it took me a full year and 12 applications before I finally got an interview and was hired. The process can feel overwhelming, unclear, and discouraging to qualified candidates. This creates a barrier to bringing in fresh perspectives and experienced professionals who could really contribute to public service.

    October 21, 2025
  • Guest-25298

    I thin the state needs more consistency between departments. I understand that each department has different directive and challenges, but let just start with having each department follow the same foundational naming convention. For example, HR should be titled the same in all departments. Budgets should be titled the same. Let's get some commonality so it starts to feel like we are all one basic community made up of different families.

    October 21, 2025
  • Guest-27612

    Several problems really. But, the first is the integration of newer technology into the workplace. Each State agency has individual IT departments without a centralized, standardized model for operations. Yes, CDT exists, but they have limited control over which systems each agency uses. This minimizes their ability to provide statewide solutions to tech-based problems. It also means that each agency is essentially trying to solve their tech problems independently, sometimes without the necessary expertise to do so. This same idea applies to HR departments. Each HR department is under the guidance of CalHR, but often do things their own way. That lack of standardization causes issues in the short and long term, as policies and procedures are often more at the whim of senior leadership, without a larger strategy, and can even, at times, be in conflict with CalHR expectations. In both the CDT and CalHR cases, having these agencies be the owner of all IT and HR positions that are effectively deployed to other State agencies could resolve the lack of standardization across both critical functions. It ensures that every position receives the same base training for their respective fields, and even centralizes the hiring process for thousands of positions, freeing up other State agency partners to focus on their individual mission areas.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27612

    There's a need for geospatial intelligence to support State operations. The Federal government has the NGA, but California has no State counterpart. CDT could adopt the role if their staff was increased. Or, a separate agency could be established to acquire, analyze, and provide geospatial intelligence products to State agencies that need them for their operations. Without such a centralized hub, each State agency is on its own to develop a GIS cadre, contract out to receive overhead (satellite) imagery and/or drones. The decentralization ultimately costs the state more money and provides less support, as those with limited budgets might not even request something they need but cannot afford. Establish a California Geospatial Intelligence entity.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27652

    Onboarding new employees is a painful process; especially gearing them up with the right tools, which are usually technology based. Since very few prospects start the day they receive the offer letter; imaging laptops/tablets/pads /cellular telephones/ computers so that they have the same imprint, days before an employee starts should be helpful; checklists to see if they have access to all of the right tools (HR/timekeeping, browsers, e-mails) should be part of the duty statement; there should be an automatic ticket created with IT, days before new employees start, for a check-in at the end of their 2nd full day; to put some teeth behind it. managers/supervisors should be evaluated on how fast their new hires get equipped (specific to the department & role) & as a further incentive, laggards (those in the bottom quartile) should have to attend mandatory training on proper onboarding processes.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27942

    Over my state career I have been employed by more than one agency, and across the board this problem exists. The lack of procedures, resources and training references on the varied applications utilized by differing state agencies to achieve assigned task. Specifically in the Human Resources capacity, there should be agency specific recognized and adhered to manuals and procedures for reoccurring task. The processes contain too much grey area, and it cause errors and penalties.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27853

    I would address the wasteful spending during procurement of goods services and contracts. It's so difficult to procure goods and services that the department doesn't factor in the cost of staff time to adhere to antiquated policies to make purchases utilizing SB/DVBE and low bid companies and contractors. Many times the low bid is the low bid for a reason and we get a subpar service and often times has to be redone or fixed in a short period. If a more qualified, higher cost contractor was awarded we would save money in the long run. This makes it difficult to be good stewards of the state dollar.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27794

    Our pay which has a direct effect on our retention . We spend hundreds of thousands of dollars training employees, they work for us for years promote through the ranks with valuable experience and we lose them to local government fire departments. They get paid more money as a probation firefighter then as a seasoned fire captain with us. We can’t keep good employees.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27975

    The primary problem I would address is ensuring that personnel have the proper tools, resources, and support to effectively perform their roles. As a station captain, I’ve completed all the necessary classes for purchasing, but I currently do not have a purchase card, which means I must rely on other captains for essential station needs like paint, upkeep, and crew subsidies, slowing down operations. Additionally, technology issues with systems like the MDT on fire engines frequently require manual resets, which creates more radio traffic and interrupts workflow. To address these challenges, I would focus on providing the proper tools and resources for each position, clarifying roles and responsibilities so staff understand their scope and accountability, and implementing a mentorship program where experienced personnel guide and train newer individuals. These steps would improve efficiency, support staff in their roles, and ensure the station operates effectively to serve the community.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27788

    Primary Problem: When state departments restructure teams or redistribute workloads without hiring additional staff, employees are often left managing the same amount, or more work, with fewer people. This creates burnout, inefficiency, and errors. Team members begin to feel like they’re constantly behind, unable to meet expectations, and working reactively instead of proactively. Over time, this erodes morale and productivity, leading to higher turnover and lost institutional knowledge. Before any team restructuring, require a Workload Impact Assessment from management to evaluate how the redistribution of tasks will affect timelines, quality of work, productivity, and employee well-being. This should include clear metrics such as workload ratios, task priorities, and realistic turnaround times and involve direct input from the staff who understand the day-to-day demands. Departments should also implement temporary workload support plans, and training managers how to support their team when they are taking on a doubled workload. By assessing impact before implementing a big change like that, California can prevent inefficiencies caused by chronic overload, promote employee well-being and reduce the likelihood of good employees leaving, and ensure high-quality service delivery even during tough transitions.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27690

    One problem I'd like to address is the amount of unnecessary traffic created by work that could be done digitally. Every day, thousands of unnecessary extra cars are on the road for tasks that don't actually need to happen in person, which adds to air pollution and street/freeway congestion. This not only harms the environment, but also makes commuting harder for workers whose jobs truly require them to be on-site, like public safety or field staff. By improving and leaning into digital systems and tools to promote remote work, we'd cut emissions, ease traffic, and make it easier for essential employees to get there where they need to be.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27945

    As a Californian and a state employee, one primary problem I would address is the gap in consistent internal training and the redundancy that often slows down our processes. Over time, new employees receive strong onboarding, but tenured staff may not always get the same opportunities for refreshers or updates on evolving policies, technology, and our mission. This can create uneven knowledge levels and inefficiencies in how we carry out our responsibilities. To address this, I would advocate for a structured internal training program that includes regular refresher sessions focused on our core duties, our mission, and the ‘why’ behind our work. This would help reinforce a shared sense of purpose and accountability. Another problem I would address is the overlap and redundancy that can occur between agencies or divisions within our department. While each unit has an important role, sometimes our responsibilities intersect in ways that create duplicated efforts, confusion over jurisdiction, or inefficiencies in communication and data sharing. Additionally, I would look at streamlining redundant paperwork and leveraging technology to make our processes more efficient — for example, consolidating overlapping forms into a single digital system or using data-sharing tools between departments. This would reduce administrative burden and allow us to focus more time on the impactful parts of our job, like serving the public and ensuring workplace safety.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27605

    inefficiencies due to outdated technology and resources. In my department so many processes are still paper and hardcopies that could be streamlined to computer-based technology.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27821

    There is too much bureaucracy when doing basic things, like buying a printer that works with our computers. It takes too long to approve contracts with vendors and service providers. How can we lessen or automate some of the hurdles put in place? We need to audit the process to determine whether all steps are really necessary, and to the extent they are not or can be streamlined, work to do that. We need to invest in software and other technological solutions to improve our systems.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27993

    From a hiring and recruitment standpoint, I believe the state should explore implementing relocation stipends and location-based incentives in areas where compensation does not align with local housing costs. For example, the Communications Operator classification in Siskiyou County currently offers the same pay as in San Diego County, despite the significant difference in cost of living — with housing costs in San Diego nearly triple those in Siskiyou. This imbalance contributes to low tenure and retention within the classification, especially in higher-cost regions. If the state were to establish automatic stipends or geographic pay adjustments, similar to those used by the federal government and based on GSA locality rates, it could make these positions more competitive and appealing to prospective candidates. This approach would support workforce stability, improve retention, and help ensure statewide operational readiness across all regions.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27253

    We need documented current processes and *updates* for our unit work so that people can aim for the same goal. This seems like it should be obvious and not difficult. My unit has changes to procedure that occasionally come from within the unit, but they also come from sources outside the unit (other units, law change, higher ups) - these changes have never, in the 7 years I have been here, every change has been word of mouth, sometimes in a meeting but without a written record. Nothing is used to adjust written procedures used to train new hires. This creates stratified production. Managers each train to a different standard from when they last learned the work.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-26037

    I would like to address the issue of homelessness as regards housing resources. Right now, we have various service providers doing various things, and each organization has their own way of going about things. We need a standardized system of working that makes sense across the board and is uniform. The Access Centers seem to me on the whole to not be as efficient and helpful to our clients as I would hope. As a result, I have had to approach 1 or 2 organizations that I know to be reliable. This should not be the case. Each organization should be able to provide us with the resources needed to assist our clients in a way that is clear, functional, and pragmatic. We need to fix this system. It is broken, and people in need are not being helped in the ways they need. We need transparency about housing availability at each organization and at the various housing sites that they have. So much money has poured down into a problem that is not getting fixed. It is excruciating to be working with those who desperately need help but can't get it.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-26348

    In CALFIRE with have 3 command and control platforms North Ops, South Ops and Sac ECC. With technology we should at look going to a super gacc where all command and control comes from one spot the effectiveness would be one stop shop for ordering equipment etc during an emergency

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27657

    As a funding agency, I would recommend the State Controller's Office move away from paper checks and start digital payments. Paper checks have caused many delays and lost checks increasing work for my agency as well as control agencies.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27760

    Sometimes, state agencies holds money that belongs to the public, in the form of refunds, or lost wages, as examples. The state agencies sometimes cannot locate those people who are owed money. We should create databases that the public can use to look up if they are owed money. This would increase the percentage of people who get their money back.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27512

    Let employees continue to telework like they did for four years when the state needed it. This saves money, improves morale, and gives state employees work/life balance that leads to better services for all of California.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-27512

    The cost of doing state business is too high. We pay contractors ridiculous amounts to do work state employees could do and the amount of money we pay for leases and office space that we proved was not necessary for 4 years from 2020-2024 is outrageous. Every department could hire more staff to do work for the people if they weren’t paying so much for unnecessary business leases. Additionally, forcing employees to return to office has reduced morale because employees have less money and time to spend with their families, leading to less positive customer service experiences for the public. If the people delivering the service are miserable, they aren’t going out of their way to make that experience positive for the public. Lastly, exempting employees more than 50 miles away but limiting their ability to promote is a guaranteed way to lose staff that hold critical institutional knowledge. When I leave my department as a result of this limitation, I will be taking with me the knowledge that only I possess. I support systems that save my department millions of dollars each year and there is one else in my department can support these critical systems. It would take at least a year and a LOT of money to hire and train someone such that they can fill the gap I’ll be leaving behind. I don’t want to leave state service but the governor’s return to office mandate leaves me no other choice because uprooting my family is not an option.

    October 20, 2025
  • Guest-26119

    As a funding opportunity, I believe state agencies should be eligible to qualify for grant funding secured by other agencies. Many state departments serve sensitive populations and often rely heavily on the state general fund to support expansive services. While some grant funding or federal revenues are specifically designated for these sensitive populations, strict requirements sometimes prevent state agencies from qualifying directly. To address this, it should be encouraged that state agencies partner through interagency agreements to collaboratively fund relevant work where applicable.

    October 18, 2025
  • Guest-23416

    I would also like to see updated administrative practices. In my department, we are using an Excel Sheet that we export to a PDF and give to our manager. Also, although there is too much bureaucracy in some situations, in other situations, I find I don't have the answers and there are inconsistent rules...

    October 17, 2025
  • Guest-26036

    Communication from management and upper management to rank and file employees. Example: There are two managers in one department; one manager will share information with rank and file employees, and the other manager will not share information. Half of the rank and file employees report to one manager and other half report to the other manager. When discussions amongst rank and file employees occur, there is confusion and/or concern for everyone not having the same knowledge. Idea to solve problem of communication from management: Departmental staff meetings once a week/month with all employees followed by an email to all employees with information, discussion, and outcome from the meeting. Idea to solve problem of communication from upper management: Upper management meetings with departmental managers at least once a month. Departmental managers should have knowledge of pertinent information at the State agency (especially 24-hour facilities). All-Hands staff meetings should be encouraged at every State agency. Emails are only one way to communicate, and the most effective way is face-to-face.

    October 17, 2025
  • Guest-27664

    Considering of the cost of living in California, State Employees are experiencing compensation issues

    October 17, 2025
  • Guest-27807

    I wish there was more basic structural information given to new employees. At the DOR, we have counselors (aka QRPs) who help consumers, aided by SCs and OT-Gs (different from OT-Ts). We have 13 districts, each which has a DOS unit, which assists the VR teams. This is all basic information, but I didn't know any of this for months. New employees watch a lot of intro videos, but very few of the structural basics of the organization. In my current position, I create and coordinate a lot of training material and would love to work on a basic introduction to the DOR process and think it would be very helpful.

    October 17, 2025
  • Guest-27644

    California Governor’s Office of Emergency Services Executive Memorandum To: Fire Chief Brian Marshal-CALOES-Fire Rescue From: Dave Norman, Assistant Chief-CALOES-Special Operations-US&R Date: October 12, 2025 Subject: Request for CAL-Card Issuance to Special Operations Assistant Chiefs Purpose: This memorandum requests the issuance of CAL-Cards to CAL-OES Special Operations Assistant Chiefs to support operational effectiveness during emergency deployments and pre-positioning of specialized response teams. Background: Special Operations Assistant Chiefs serve as Agency Representatives (A-REPs) during high-impact emergency operations and pre-positioning missions involving: • Hazardous Materials (HAZMAT) Teams • Swiftwater Rescue Teams • Regional Urban Search & Rescue (US&R) Task Forces • FEMA US&R State Teams deployed in-state or out-of-state via EMAC These deployments require rapid, on-the-ground decision-making and logistical coordination. Currently, the absence of direct purchasing authority via CAL-Cards limits the ability of Assistant Chiefs to procure essential resources, services, and supplies in real time—impacting mission agility and service delivery. The CAL-Card can be used for unexpected costs that arise, and that the assigned IMT might not be familiar with, such as the N95 mask needs for US&R Personnel during the Wide Area Search Operations at the January Brush Fires. Currently several agencies provide CAL-Cards to their supervisors, in some cases to Company Office

    October 17, 2025
  • Guest-27525

    I feel that so many positions are so closely tied to Sacramento that it negatively impacts other communities - especially small ones. I would like to see state departments focus on spreading their positions to smaller geographies. Since we've adapted to telework and remote work, we could really expand job offerings into communities that lack state employees. This would not only bring good, stable careers to smaller communities, it would also bring the perspective and influence of smaller communities to state level decisions and efforts. This could also include hiring those with limited means and/or disabilities that make work in Sacramento difficult for some.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27641

    State workers need to acknowledge the foundation we stand on. The state should work with our California State Universities or University of California's in collaboration with Historians to document how each Department was ran and operated in previous generations (as well as our current generation). This would provide oversight and a framework for future generations to build on and acknowledge the overall direction of each department. This would also allow public record for our tax paying/voting citizens to have informed knowledge on what these departments have provided over the years, as well as give insight to how we can improve. This in effect would lesson reactionary politics and allow further positive growth towards democratically guided agencies in a show of absolute transparency. Some agencies like the Department of Rehabilitation have had leaders which fundamentally shaped our state for the better. Having that history fully spelled out for the general public would also strengthen loyalty and ensure that our leadership today and tomorrow can embrace the foundational values of these departments exist in the first place.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27911

    Allowing 2.5 or 3 days of teleworking would be helpful for employees with quadriplegia and other physical disabilities. Bringing awareness around using a quieter location in an office to utilize dictation software when typing would be great as well.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27678

    As a Rehabilitation Counselor the problem that I wanted to address is carrying huge case loads that requires expertise and it's time consuming to take care of each case but the counselor's are getting lower pays which is not enough to survive in L.A because the current pay is not enough to support a family.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27666

    I believe we need supervisors at my location, currently in my work location multiple departments are being overseen by a small number of managers, the issue is that the managers have not performed the jobs they are overseeing and have no Supervisors working under them. This leads to groups of level 1 employees having to constantly convince management are their needs when a supervisor would have noticed these operational needs before they even become an issue. Serious things like having 10 year old chemical in the building, and I have to argue with management about why that's bad because none of my management has that experience to supervise this position

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27992

    Purchasing goods and services for participants is needlessly burdensome. A single library of vendors that work with CA, with appropriate documentation already in place and maintained, would have a major impact.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-26739

    Problem: As state agencies and departments, we spend so many resources just sharing information between each other (drafting contracts, MOUs, etc.) We are all instruments of one state government, yet there are many data sharing agreements that get created to share what we all believe to be private information. Possible Solution: Could we lower the restrictions for sharing information through legislative action or more global and general data-sharing agreements? Another option is to collectively share data to a central warehouse. If we leverage all of our data together, we can be more effective/efficient in our individual mandates.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27746

    One of the most difficult things about working for the State as a healthcare employee is having decisions made at headquarters in Sacramento that directly affect the lives of the people that I serve, often to their detriment, made by individuals who do not ever meet our clients or understand what they need. Decisions made by a top-down hierarchy often are made with administrators or managers in mind, and I think it should be the other way around. The primary role of a state worker is to serve the public, but often I feel that I am following regulations that are contradictory, don't make sense, or are needlessly burdensome at the expense of my clients for the sake of placating headquarters. I would want fewer top-down decisions; for leaders at headquarters to spend more time at the worksites they direct and oversee, to interact with the people who live there, and to pay closer attention to their needs and input; and more open communication from our leaders about what they are doing to address the concerns that we and our clients bring to them. Another piece that I think deserves a review is the State's policy of going with the lowest bidder for purchasing. Oftentimes, we get what we pay for, and products and services from the lowest bidders are also the lowest quality. There at least should be another metric we go by in addition to price, such as taking into consideration product reviews or how responsive a vendor is to customer needs.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27795

    Concern: Cal/OSHA practices Political Safety almost exclusively, when the Citizens of California deserve Professional Safety. Fix?: Professional Safety is all about evidence and fact. Political Safety is not that.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27583

    I feel like we have a serious personal problem. We do not pay our employees enough to compete in the employment opportunities that arise from other than State employees.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-21611

    There is zero training for QRP's. Not even a 10-minute video. We have huge case loads and come from different disciplines and yet no two offices do the same thing. Alignment and modernization is crucial for the success for DoR and aligned training is crucial.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27501

    To support the state's language access initiatives, as much as we have a bilingual certification process for state employees there should be a process to certify biliteracy too. In order to ensure the quality of state-issued written communications, the state needs a formal process to vet state employees for acceptable writing skills in target languages. Being bilingual is not the same as being biliterate, being bilingual is easier.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-21611

    DoR has solicited Master's level professionals with a statement on the JD that says service with DoR may possibly be able to get clinical hours yet they have been evasive about this opportunity. QRP's should have a ladder. QRP I: Master's degree non-associate. QRP II - Associate (ASW, AMFT, APCC). QRP III - IV Licensed professional (under 2 years (III), or more than 2 years (V). There is no reason to group differently qualified QRPs together and we will lose talent without the opportunity for clinical advancement. Additionally, we need licensed people at each site. The acuity is too high for just MSWs or CRCs. We need clinical expertise and support of that expertise in VR settings. Unfortunately, our executive leadership does not have the clinical lens or skill set to validate the importance of this need.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-26489

    I would like to suggest opening up the possibility for state employees of one department being allowed to use another departments empty/vacated locations for alternative onsite days. As an example, a telecommute employee working under a hybrid plan and living a distance away from their respective home office, could use a hotel cubicle in another state office closer to their home for an onsite day. Just a thought for using those unoccupied office locations. Consideration would have to be made for Internet access, but most all state offices have that capacity already.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27805

    The workplace culture needs improvement. Staff often compete with one another instead of collaborating. The mindset of “that’s not my job,” “someone else will do it,” or “I’m not volunteering” contributes to the negative stereotype of state workers being lazy. Unfortunately, that perception is often reinforced by reality. We need more people willing to step up, work together, and focus on shared goals. I appreciate the effort to provide jobs and recognize the importance of accountability and stewardship to taxpayers. But we must also streamline processes, empower managers, and foster a culture of teamwork if we want to improve outcomes and public trust.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27805

    One major issue is the lack of managers who actively manage their staff. This isn’t about micromanagement, it's about setting clear expectations, assigning tasks with due dates, tracking progress and holding staff accountable. Instead, agencies often hire expensive project managers, funded by taxpayer dollars, to direct the work. So what, then, is the role of the actual manager? Too often, it’s limited to signing off on timesheets, completing IDPs, attending conferences, and to often encourage staff to “get the work done when they can”, so 2-weeks later or never. In my experience, when managers don’t establish timelines or hold staff accountable, tasks take significantly longer to complete. Project managers, on the other hand, drive progress and lead teams to results more efficiently than many state managers. If managers owned their empowerment and held accountable for directing work, we could reduce reliance on costly PM contracts and save public funds.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27726

    I've worked in the private sector and I'm shocked by how antiquated and labor intensive our travel approvals, travel booking, travel expense reporting processes are. It takes hours to wade through all the layers and forms and rules. And extensive pre-approvals have us losing out on lower cost flights, early bird discounts, and hotel blocks at higher cost to the State of CA. We used concur in the private sector and things could be done in close to real time. If the time my small department spends on limited travel is magnified across all departments and agencies it's a huge loss of time and productivity. The CalAters site is so slow and even says on the front screen plan for several minutes to even open the page. I believe CA, with our technology and innovation can do better in a digital world.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-27737

    Fragmentation across state/local systems that serve the same people. Programs in health/housing/social services work separately with different rules but serve the same people. They have fragmented data and separate funding/rules. This creates confusion for the consumers, duplication of efforts, and gaps in care especially for the people that most need it. 1. State depts should work together for shared goals and coordinate service instead of running parallel programs that don't connect. And leadership should facilitate that collaboration. 2. Agencies should secure shared data to see full picture of a person's needs and measure outcomes of wellbeing. 3. Give counties/regions flexibility to test local solutions using funding and simplified access to services. 4. Train/rotate staff across departments/agencies so employees can understand how other systems work and work together better to solve problems.

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-26789

    One of my biggest daily hassles as a state worker is the dearth of parking in downtown Sacramento. Construction of new parking garages instead of more new buildings for those who cannot rely on public transportation would go a long way for many!

    October 16, 2025
  • Guest-26864

    More staff, boots on the ground, perhaps a new look to attract new staff for everyday duties. A possibility update why you would like to work at Parks.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-21058

    Allow state employees to work out of a state office of their choice. The return-to-office mandate has created challenges for many employees—particularly those hired into remote roles. While some employees value in-office collaboration (myself included), requiring relocation to a specific office location is impractical and disruptive for many. Many people were hired for remote positions and are now facing the option to move to the specific location of their state office or find a new job. However, in many cases, employees live near a state office, just not the one affiliated with their agency. Forcing them to move or resign creates unnecessary turnover and hardship. Further, many offices that are designated for a specific agency/department are sitting mostly vacant because they have hired staff all over the state for remote work that do not live near their specific office. Therefore, state employees should be allowed to choose an office to work from, rather than office space being designated only for one agency/department. Providing more flexibility in where state employees can work solves problems for the employees who would like to work near where they live and solves problems for the state by filling currently vacant office space. Side benefits would occur from this change by introducing state employees that would otherwise not meet, breaking down silos, and creating community based on location.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-27732

    Besides the challenges related to RTO, one of the biggest issues I’ve experienced as a state employee is that some salaried managers expect hourly staff to work on-call, weekends, or beyond regular hours without additional compensation. When I’ve tried to set boundaries or decline unpaid work, it has at times resulted in retaliation or a sense of “quid pro quo,” where cooperation is exchanged for fair treatment. A potential solution could be to ensure that duty statements clearly define expectations around on-call work and overtime. Additionally, management should receive better training on employee classifications, what constitutes on-call/duty officer status, and how compensation for these duties should be handled.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-27571

    One of the primary problems I see within my department is the challenges involved in the hiring process. This is a great paying job and there are many qualified candidates that want the job. The merit-based system and layers of bureaucracy make it so hard to for individuals to get the job and Cal/OSHA offices are often understaffed or put up with bad employees because it is so hard to hire new ones. My idea would be to designate a position in each individual district office to act as a liason with CAL-HR and give offices Autonomy top hire independently

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-27722

    Lack of "Promotion in Place" opportunities. I completely understand the need to be transparent in our hiring practices. I just understand why there is not a career path available though promotion and good standing. Why does an employee who deservedly has worked towards the next level up, have to leave their department or agency to chase that next position instead of just having opportunity to promote? In my opinion, it seems to foster more job- hopping and loss of talent for agencies, where retention would be a lot higher. just my thoughts for today :)

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-27981

    California government spends money on things that do not improve our lives. For example spending billions of dollars on a high speed rail line to no where and not adding traffic lanes to I 5 and US 99. We are Californians and our cities are not designed for rail service.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-27513

    Right now, there are a lot of departments with good best practices, good processes, good policies, good technologies. The departments that don't have those things are too prideful to ask the other departments to share their secrets. We need a process where we share information more generously.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-27760

    The practice of posting a job position at the DLSE, which is overseen by DIR HR, is inefficient, and damaging to the agency. Often, a position is posted, when the current employee wants to transfer to another office. When this happens, members of the public apply, and none of them are selected because the reason for posting was just a transfer. This is a huge waste of government resources. The employee should be allowed to transfer without applying for a new job. Also, the public feels angry when they are not selected despite their qualifications, and they decide not to apply again because they know that internal candidates will be selected. The same thing happens when promotion in place is difficult to gain as has happened at DLSE. HR spends dozens of hours reviewing resumes, interviewing candidates, only to turn everyone external down. Qualified candidates refuse to continue engaging in the hiring process because they get turned down so frequently. I have seen this happen, and because I am an internal candidate, I know that the internal employee gets hired. We would save hundreds of hours of work if we eliminated these practices. We would also increase the pool of qualified applicants if the hiring process was genuine.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-27800

    Macro: The primary problem is the cost of running a government that has to balance the needs of the population it is serving without running out money to do so. Micro: - Management not promoting people who are competent. - No proper training guides for new employees and/or training for people who are about to take over from those retiring or moving to different positions - micromanagement. especially with telecommuting

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-27659

    I would address telework as the priority issue for CA state employees. The CA State Auditor stated in August 2025 that a one-size-fits-all telework policy does not work for employees or the state as whole. As a state employee whose work is entirely conducted online, I'd love to see CalHR and department management prioritizing a telework schedule. It allows me to get my work done in a timelier manner and prevents carbon emissions from commuting and powering office spaces. I believe it would also allow the state to be a more competitive employer and retain employees long-term.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-26180

    Training, in the department I am working in we went from person to person training to computer training. For the type of work, I do you need to be able to ask questions, hear experiences from someone who has already done the job. The newer employees really struggle with the workload and the state goals due to lack of knowledge. My suggestion is to bring in person training back. It helps provide superior customer service when the employees are knowledgeable and confident in the work that they are doing.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-26898

    We need to continue to address racial and gender disparities in the workplace by recognizing the harms of diversity indifference in the forms of colorblindness, gender-blindness, and diversity blindness. Required reading can be assigned to state workers with a short paragraph essay on each chapter written by the state worker to acknowledge what they have learned. Acknowledging the history of vulnerable demographics such as Women, Black, Asian, Hispanic, LGBTQIA, and the disabled can help mitigate colorblindness, gender-blindness, and any feelings of resistance against understanding race and gender vulnerabilities. Required reading and writing can also maintain reading comprehension and critical thinking qualities which are rapidly declining even at managerial levels. Required reading and writing about diversity can also help to heal the injustice of native American genocide, racial indifference (and racism) against people of color, and gender discrimination that were unfortunately inherited from the beginnings of the USA nation and ensuing statehoods, California included. The book New Jim Crow by Michelle Alexander is a good start. Bury My Heart at Wounded Knee is another recommendation. By remembering the history of how the USA and CA statehood came to be, we can repair and heal these centuries-old traumas and the recurring injustices that continue due to ignorance of and indifference towards marginalized communities and state workers.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-27672

    As a field inspector, I ask the State to consider satellite office provision and telework arrangement. Commute consumes energy and time in Southern California. One hour forty five minutes one way is not sustainable. In order not to lose experienced field inspector, please keep some flexibility on reporting to office in the coming 4-day a week schedule. Management can design a mechanism to evaluate employees' work and performance by quality and quantity of the field work, case load, etc.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-27667

    Balance and falls. Exercise, community services that include exercising, and accessibility to physical therapy services (outpatient and home health).

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-26492

    As California state worker once issue is parking and finding street parking take the risk or pay for expensive garage which double the price. To solve this problem I feel employees should get some benefit for parking when you can’t find spot to park.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-26603

    I would like to see state agencies draw on talent from across the state. Move to a model that emphasizes telework.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-26721

    It is time to update old platforms and systems. There is no reason Fi$cal should be so hard to navigate. We need a more user-friendly system with drop downs and not tabs within tabs within tabs. The systems in place are hard to navigate and to learn.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-27130

    Redirect a portion of the state work force within each agency to serve as a point of contact or dedicated case manager/worker to help program recipients that reach out for assistance.

    October 15, 2025
  • Guest-21631

    A primary way to become more effective and efficient is to meaningfully invest in recruitment and retention. Like many here, I've worked at different departments over several years and it’s become incredibly challenging to recruit and retain talented professionals. Telework and statewide hiring in the early 2020s helped us recruit skilled professionals from across the state. But now, with pay falling behind the private sector, few opportunities for advancement, and new in-office requirements, we lost dedicated employees who have years of experience and institutional knowledge, and try to replace them (if we can), which is expensive and slow. One of the biggest reasons people choose state service is PSLF. Recent proposed federal changes that raise monthly repayments upwards of 500% and will likely disqualify employees from certain states have taken that recruitment/retention tool away. I believe CA should create its own Loan Repayment Assistance Program (LRAP) for state employees. We already have successful models with the CA Legislature authorizing an LRAP through CalATJ for legal aid attorneys, and CalHealthCares using Prop. 56 funds to offer up to $300,000 in loan repayment for physicians who commit to five years of service. Building on those for a broader program that is for all state employees would be a powerful tool to recruit and retain talented professionals, keep expertise in-house, and strengthen the quality and stability of public service across the state.

    October 14, 2025
  • Guest-24627

    I see a lot of State employees still do not have direct deposit, which means a lot of man power and trees are being used. A partnership with a bank or credit union with benefits to the State employees. A drive for the employees to sign up for direct deposit.

    October 14, 2025
  • Guest-27207

    The biggest hurdle is the length of time it takes to implement plans and/or strategies for change and the lack of consultation or corroboration with the staff who actually perform such duties are not included before the policies or plans are implemented.

    October 14, 2025
  • Guest-27423

    Rigidity! Combined with Variability... I think that are so many great things about being a public servant in California. That said, we are often hemmed in by overly rigid departmental policies that are too variable within the regulations that guide state administration. I think the regulations provide ample framework for control agencies to tell departments that their policies do not align. I think too often we allow great variability that becomes a liability when someone finally takes a department to task. And yes, I have examples/receipts.

    October 14, 2025
  • Guest-26879

    Inequitable hiring processes, such as differences in examinations and exam administration. Exam questions that no longer work as intended; they do not evaluate experience and knowledge, but only self-assess experience. I recommend removing examinations from the civil service process. They are no longer necessary. Focus instead on better screening (and I don't mean MQ determinations). Another problem is outdated classifications. Solution is to continue to consolidate classifications. There's also lot of management, some new, that are not trained how to manage within state regulations/practices. There's a lack of understanding/balance of being engaging, caring and yet firm, setting expectations and keeping employees accountable. (Or for some, to not be so caring, that they fail to hold employees accountable.) It's not modeled by managers that have been in state service for years. Sometimes, we push things like performance evaluations for compliance, but the quality and engagement are severely lacking. One solution is to update the 80-hour training to be "real talk" and not philosophical / general principles. New managers need more performance management and employee engagement training. It shouldn't be self-paced where we know some just click through e-learning without really learning.

    October 14, 2025
  • Guest-24637

    As the State Auditor's Office showed recently, telework is effective. It is a great tool available to the state to foster collaboration and retain highly skilled staff. In my role, I engage with community based organizations, researchers, and local government all across the state. I would not be able to do that without teleconferencing tools and support for telework—even if we had the funds to support travel, I can't be in multiple places at once, and it is more effective to have everyone in conversation together. The State government of California does not only serve Sacramento, yet without telework I am less effectively able to serve communities outside of the Sacramento area. Telework saves money, reduces pollution and traffic, and makes state workers more efficient. I am able to do more work in the same amount of time if I don't have to run around a poorly planned building to find a bathroom or navigate a lengthy commute. When working at headquarters, my colleagues frequently have to interrupt meetings in order to move their cars because we don't have the infrastructure to actually support employees working in the office, even on a 2 day/week in office schedule. Additionally, I am less likely while working remotely to experience microaggressions and other behavior from coworkers that could lead to a lawsuit against the state.

    October 14, 2025
  • Guest-24637

    Over the years I have identified two major inefficiencies in stat service: siloing and mismanagement. Siloing leads to duplication of effort across state government. When we conduct duplicative engagements with external audiences, it wastes their time and makes clear that we aren't coordinating internally the way we should. Collaboration and breaking down silos would lead us to be more efficient and make better use of expertise within and across agencies and departments. Mismanagement is another very common problem I observe in state service. There are very few effective managers who support and empower staff to do their work effectively. Others are either totally checked out and non-responsive, or are some of the worst micromanagers I have ever encountered. So many times I see the state put forth goals for improving state service that ignore these issues. Hiring the right people won't matter if they don't get the support they need to do the work. Managers and supervisors need training to help them develop self-awareness, recognize signs of mismanagement in themselves, and grow to become supports rather than barriers for their staff.

    October 14, 2025
  • Guest-26881

    I wish there was an easier way to navigate the Cal Careers website and explore job opportunities that match your qualifications. It would be helpful to have a feature for current state employees, in which they could enter their current job title, skills, and areas of interests, then the system could filter and suggest positions that align with their interests and experience. Especially, if the roles fall outside their current classification or career series. This would make it much easier to find roles that are a good fit, especially for those of use looking to transfer into a new field at a similar skill level.

    October 14, 2025
  • Guest-24493

    Better training is needed on all levels. An online course with a quiz is not working for real life situations. Telework is great, but making sure employees are connected and feel safe to ask questions is important. New employees and counselors feel overwhelmed when trying to learn the job while also doing the job. Staff development should assess each person' learning style and have a safe and/or test environment before they are expected to do the actual job. And our structure has manager that have no idea what the role of support is. How do you manage and train staff if you do not understand their job? Our previous structure had management for clerical staff and management for counselors. It was better then than now.

    October 14, 2025
  • Guest-26849

    The problem I continue to encounter is that when purchasing items for my factory, each time I request a quote, I’m required to obtain three documents: the recycled content certification, a signed RFQ, and the No Russia business form. This process becomes unnecessarily repetitive because these vendors are already approved suppliers that we’ve worked with before. It doesn’t make sense to require the same paperwork for every purchase when the information and compliance status haven’t changed. The redundancy creates excess paperwork, slows down procurement, and adds unnecessary administrative burden for both us and the vendors.
