Additionally, requiring a signed RFQ seems redundant when the vendor is already providing an official quote on their company letterhead, which inherently serves as their formal offer. A more efficient approach would be to have vendors complete these forms once per year, or only when there’s a material change to their status, rather than for every individual quote.

    October 13, 2025
  • Guest-27181

    The State could do more to enforce recycling in public spaces. There is also area for growth on installing solar panels and upgrading charging stations throughout state-owned buildings and property.

    October 13, 2025
  • Guest-26939

    I wish that those making the "decisions" would come to the realization that the option to telework has been so beneficial for state workers. People are more productive and the work life balance is almost even. We spend so much time at work giving our all, the ability to do so at home without having to make a drive in a few times a week is very appreciated.

    October 13, 2025
  • Guest-27414

    For State contracting, it is challenging when the Department of General Services has not provided any templates for contracts and solicitations. There should be standardized templates that state employees in a buying role can use for their contracts and solicitations. Similar to the CMA Model Template that the CSU and UC universities use. It's a required template used for all contracts and grants with CSUs and UCs. DGS should develop standard templates for contracts and solicitations that can be used by all departments. This would streamline the contracting process tremendously. It creates uniformity, and will increase the review and approval process by departments internal processes, as well as DGS' Procurement Division and Office of Legal Services. It would also impact the prospective contractors that are interested in bidding on our solicitations. If I build an RFP today, it likely looks drastically different from another department's RFP. The content may capture the same info - however, where the information might be found in the solicitation, and the formatting of the solicitation, would be drastically different from others. RFPs can be hundreds of pages, and can be overwhelming for contractors. A standardized contracting template, and a standardized solicitation template for things like RFPs and IFBs - would save the State time and money across the board. DGS, as the business manager for the state, should implement these templates in partnership with other teams.

    October 13, 2025
  • Guest-26528

    Ways to upgrade and repair all old infrastructure in CA State Parks. Ideas: 1. Engage private sector for sponsorships/donations 2. Privatize certain aspects of CA State Parks (housekeeping, contracting, entrance station/fee collection) 3. Remove the 5 levels of internal review and approval prior to submitting to Control agencies for authorization and approvals

    October 13, 2025
  • Guest-26430

    Lack of direction, clarity of job duties. I asked for training in my new post, as the training I had for this new position was over 10 years ago. I was told " you will have a partner to help you" My partner has now been off 5 of the 10 weeks we have worked together. I am often left to figure it out on my own and cover her area as well. Then am reprimanded for not doing something that I didn't even know I had to do. It's so frustrating. I suggest allowing time for this training. Properly train all staff and if staff are asking for help then help them.

    October 12, 2025
  • Guest-26666

    One of the guidelines stated that we should not discuss telework or return to office policies; however, I noticed that others have posted about it already so I would like to add my request as well. As a state worker for more than 25 years I have enjoyed working in person alongside my peers and building relationships with people that have shared the workload struggles, so I am not arguing against the benefits of having in person collaboration. However, through the years my family situation. Currently, my greatest need would be to have an option for those of us that are caring of very ill and elderly family members or that have other family situations that make it difficult to commute into the office to be able to be exempted from the in-office requirement. We have programs like Paid Family Leave that allow for paid time off to take care of family members, but the needs of aging or ill family members do not always impede our ability to meet our workload commitments and do not meet the requirements for PFL or an RA request. Personally, leaving my mom in the care of a stranger so that I can travel 50 miles to go sit in front of a computer is a big mental, emotional, and financial burden that doesn't make sense when the workload doesn't demand it. Please consider it. I know there are many state workers that would greatly benefit from having an option.

    October 11, 2025
  • Guest-27376

    Poor staff/employee performance, excessive departmental fraud, and excessive waste of state/government funds! We currently have two - three departments/entities who handle the same duties and responsibilities, while some even work from home, where some of these departments require a physical presence in order to complete their actual job duties or patient care!

    October 11, 2025
  • Guest-26140

    The hiring process. The process takes too long to jump through the hoops to bring on quality people when needed. Reduce the number of hand offs that paperwork has to go thru during the process.

    October 11, 2025
  • Guest-26506

    bureaucratic waste

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-27132

    WE are locked into too many limitations, when a decision needs to be made. There exist certain rules or preset standards that limit the best product from being achieved. The only way to correct this is to abolish these internal standards or rules, and run this organization as a true business, and not as a state expenditure. Another problem is the limitation or employees not being able to be held accountable at work, since they are protected by the union, and we all know how to correct that.

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-26841

    Recruitment and retention. Newer generations that are not already heated and close to retirement seems to have little interest in applying for State jobs. If they do, they move on the other industries quickly. I feel like the State ends up training the workforce for other companies rather than retaining the talented staff hired. I believe the State would benefit from focus groups to identify innovative ways to recruit new staff and improvement opportunities that would increase the retention of those staff longer term.

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-26247

    Here is a crazy idea: we actually promote people instead of forcing individuals to interview for other positions to obtain a "promotion." What is the incentive to perform well except to not get fired? I have witnessed people "quiet quit" because they do not interview well, and therefore cannot promote despite being exemplary employees.

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-27043

    There is much too much management that has nothing to do with the job we do here regarding actual patient care here in Atascadero state hospital. It is a huge waste of tax money. There are a lot of overpaid management positions I would eliminate or streamline. Maybe it is time for AI to make this place efficient. Also, we need to actually switch our records to digital after all these years. We still have paper charts if you can believe it.

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-27122

    I think a lot off staff in many state departments and agencies are bored. The work is very hierarchically driven, and much of the work load involves pushing products or to--dos up or down the chain. I would like for innovation to increase in departments as needed. Also, sometimes staff training is hampered by fiscal constraints. Departments should never hesitate to train quality staff as needed. Also, Departments should hire top talent. Also, there should be better cross collaboration and flow of ideas and research between our state universities (UC and CSU) and state departments. State employees should also have access to either the UC or CSU library catalogs to foster the advancement of knowledge on particular topics. Also, core staff need to be paid more to compete with corporate entities.

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-26251

    Diet quality within California run state institutions (State Hospitals).

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-26058

    The need for remote work nationwide. My restricting state employment to California and an in-office requirement, we are immediately limiting the ability to recruit the best and the brightest. Although California is a great place to live, we exclude individuals who would be great assets to the state, both in mind and talent, if they don't want to relocate. This creates an unnecessary barrier to recruiting the best, which only helps our state succeed. My suggestion would be to identify classifications and areas which require unique and specalized skillsets and expanding the ability of those classifications to have more flexible remote work option.

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-26551

    I'm brand new to the state. My interests are in employee rights, benefits, training, and upward mobility.

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-27128

    The primary problem that I would address is the homelessness and more advocacy for people with disabilities. The ideas that I have to solve the problem that I shared would be building more homeless shelters for people in the state, and having more advocation for people with disabilities here in the state as well. Plus the lack of training and guidance for staff.

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-26541

    I've noticed that several agencies are outsourcing tasks like accounts payable and travel coordination to other states. This reduces the number of Californians who work for the state and puts a burden on the remaining staff to train those workers from other states. If I could I would like to have these contracts revisited, they are not saving the state money and from what I see are costing it more.

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-23895

    Let's update Non-Industrial Insurance application to be able to complete online as SDI is. This hurdle of printing and mailing the paperwork is historic and leaves it open for lost applications.

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-23895

    Efficiency. Working in different departments I find many departments need an overload, reorganization, and current policies and procedures. I would love to join a team to review each department and find the state savings. Salary and Benefits is a large budget in each organization; savings here would do much for the state.

    October 10, 2025
  • Guest-27082

    Flatten the organization. Multi-tier mgmt hierarchies tend to result in excess procedural steps thereby slowing the overall process. Eliminate "red tape" where none are needed. Review steps in a process to assess value in the overall procedure. If step(s) don't serve a legitimate purpose then the procedure should be tightened up to improve & fast track the execution of the procedure. In short, streamline by investigating the overall process for value & timeliness. Pare down where there are redundant or no-value steps/processes. If there is an opportunity to automate a process/procedure, empower knowledgeable staff to explore/implement solutions even if there is an intended enterprise-wide solution targeted for deployment downstream. There is value in short-term solutions which, until a potential future large-scale solution is implemented (which can sometimes/often be unavailable for years), can greatly reduce considerable man-hours that would otherwise be necessary. Create bridges between units that share knowledge, require similar experience/tools, feed information across units, present opportunities to consolidate & standardize output either for internal or public use. There are current silos of units that have very similar processes but are handled/implemented differently. Normalize processes across these silos. Establish inter-division training plans to retain staff expertise. Grow/support internal advancement plans based on org job forecast. Hire from within.

    October 09, 2025
  • Guest-24062

    Cost of Living in our state. There is a real gap between wages and/or benefits and housing / utility prices. Young adults just entering the workforce, low wage earners, people who cannot work due to disability and older adults leaving the workforce simply cannot afford a basic standard of living. We need to pay better for typical "blue collar" jobs, put money into paid apprenticeship/internship positions and target high school students not on the college path for those positions early, and last, put less money into politicking/campaigns and more into more subsidized housing. Set limits on the monopolization of companies that buy up properties and airbnb/vrbo type companies only to have properties sitting empty for long periods of time. Incentivize commercial real estate holders that have empty buildings to convert to shelter/housing units.

    October 09, 2025
  • Guest-27236

    Tax Auditor Training. Regular, relevant, and frequent training about auditing different industries and office procedures.

    October 09, 2025
  • Guest-26006

    As someone who does a lot of state purchasing I believe that Cal PIA and the purchasing system around it creates massive unnecessary cost overruns that in turn not only hurts our budgets but also the wallets of the taxpayers. I understand the original intention of Cal PIA but I believe their reach has extended to far. Because of this many of my hours are spent trying to get purchasing waivers or thinking of way to get purchasing waivers because purchasing through Cal PIA would cost me up to 10 times the amount that a state certified small disabled veteran owned business might cost me. To fix this I believe we need to look at what Cal PIA currently produces and remove any items that are not at least closely competitive with these outside businesses prices.

    October 09, 2025
  • Guest-27253

    I have noticed many managers do not have a touchstone for what they do. They do not have a structure for decision making and do not have a written process. When a process changes, the information is dispersed word-of-mouth, take your own notes, no email. It's like managers are chasing plausible deniability rather than efficiency and uniform process. We have processes that make no sense AND what's worse, when a senior employee moves on there is nobody to explain a process - because it was made up of whole cloth or because it is genuine and correct but not written down. I know there are other units that check our process to a point they never seem to notice these issues.

    October 09, 2025
  • Guest-26351

    The primary problem that I would like to address is ensuring that proper preventative maintenance is completed on State Park infrastructure to avoid costly repairs and replacement.

    October 09, 2025
  • Guest-26424

    my main problem i would address is to continue to minimize our website to make more friendlier for example ID me. I love the pizza effect we have with edets. I would love to see more offices with claim reps in them to process claims as well.

    October 09, 2025
  • Guest-27322

    I understand that bureaucracy can be problematic pretty much wherever you go but it is very disappointing to see how wasteful and inefficient things can be. I am somewhat new to state service but I hope to help this problem along once I put more time in.

    October 08, 2025
  • Guest-26411

    This state is too big to manage under one roof. Too many residents and too many resources to manage effectively and efficiently. While both geographic entities can remain under one banner of 'State of California", serious consideration should be given to splitting this state into two separate regulatory bodies, with separate budgets and separate oversight.

    October 08, 2025
  • Guest-27044

    In my department, the restrictions on classifications and hiring can limit the upward career mobility/development of individuals without bachelors degrees. An individual with many years of experience can have a difficult time qualifying for a certain classification if they don't have a bachelor's degree, but are otherwise qualified. This is a barrier for entry for many people who want to come into state service and could contribute to the important work we do.

    October 08, 2025
  • Guest-27044

    System software is often very outdated and we either can't access or can't *afford* to access updated software that will allow us to work more efficiently. Processes often seem cumbersome and disjointed. An example of this is submitting a travel reimbursement claim through CalATERS. I can only create part of my claim in the outdated Java program, but then have to email or mail all of my receipts and documentation. We are also in desperate need of access to quality project management software (such as Monday or Asana). There are many more examples. The outdated software we are required to use slows our work flows and wastes valuable staff time.

    October 08, 2025
  • Guest-27412

    The amount of paperwork required to obtain any service of the state should be streamlined. Forms should be revised constantly and redundant forms or request for information should be eliminated, making the way our constituents ask for state services easier to understand and the form easier to complete. State departments should be able to share information as to avoid duplicate requests from the constituent. In this age of technology constituents should be able to download, complete and submit forms electronically and these forms should be placed in a repository where all state departments can have access.

    October 08, 2025
  • Guest-26522

    The procurement process is complex and difficult for contractors, business owners, and employees to understand. A review of the procurement process is needed to remove unnecessary forms, protocols, and monetary spending limits that have not changed with inflation. These issues have had the indirect result of hindering necessary purchases for needed services and repairs that keep our facilities open and operational. It has also resulted in the loss of business opportunity for many business owners and local communities that rely on government contracts.

    October 08, 2025
  • Guest-27334

    Because of inevitable turnover, all agencies should prioritize the creation of task specific guides, or 'desk guides', that describe how the employee actually carries out one of the many regular/repeated tasks; this is also where to share best practices (navigating software, interdepartmental timeline needs, formatting preferences, etc.). Too often, the department is forced to burden the one person who knows how to do a certain task with training every newly hired employee on every step of that process, and field all follow-up questions, only to have the new staff member move on and be replaced by a new-hire and do it all over again. The desk guides also function to keep processes consistent from one employee to the next, and remind staff how to perform parts of the task when the task is performed infrequently.

    October 08, 2025
  • Guest-24446

    Our data systems and workflows are disconnected and outdate. It's like we wanted to start doing everything electronically, but just we're using the same paper forms. We're not taking advantage of current integrating technologies. I believe cloud-based systems that bring together all the different sets of data would be helpful. Instead, we're still using SharePoint, opening the same unwieldly Word docs and PDFs that were used back when we were using paper files, and doing Print-to-PDF when sending documents to other colleagues. I feel time and resources could be better used.

    October 08, 2025
  • Guest-27313

    CA's govt might be more efficient if all organizations had quick and thorough onboarding and training of new staff. Often, hiring managers want staff to "hit the ground running." But some positions really should allow for ramp-up time. If there is no time to spare, a willing and capable mentor could be helpful. Related to this is lack of succession planning, which is a massive issue in some state organizations.

    October 08, 2025
  • Guest-21741

    State government often has excessive bureaucracy and administrative hoops. Examples include multiple forms and years of work to replace a vehicle, needing to work through multiple layers of staff to get approvals for things, or spending dozens of hours of staff time just to make simple purchases. Much of this is expected for large organizations, and is cliche in its predictability, but nevertheless these inefficiencies hinder state employees ability to get their work done efficiently and slow responses to public needs. Often the extra forms, steps, etc are in place to prevent fraud, waste, and abuse, but have the opposite effect in needlessly wasting resources. An effort to consolidate processes, particularly around purchasing, would be very helpful.

    October 08, 2025
  • Guest-27314

    A primary problem is the lack of data integration between state systems that serve California's industries. Businesses are often required to submit the same core information, legal name, address, corporate filings, to multiple, separate departments. This creates a fragmented and inefficient experience for them and for us. This data silo effect forces state employees to spend significant time manually reconciling information from different sources, increases the risk of data entry errors, and prevents us from having a clear, holistic view of industry interactions with the state. Solution:My ideas focus on creating a "submit once, use many times" data architecture through two main initiatives: A Unified Business Portal: We should develop a single, secure online portal to serve as the primary digital entry point for any business interacting with the state. A company could manage its profile, permits, and filings in one place, and that verified data would automatically populate across all relevant agency systems. A Standardized Data-Sharing API: To support this, we need to build a modern, secure Application Programming Interface (API). This tool would act as a universal translator, allowing our diverse departmental systems, both legacy and new, to communicate and share common data automatically and securely, eliminating the need for redundant data entry. To make this actionable, we could launch a pilot program integrating the systems of two or three key departments to prove the

    October 08, 2025
  • Guest-26400

    One primary issue j would seriously want addressed is routine employee reviews. Management needs to be more involved in how we handle ourselves when we speak to our customers. Some employees have 0 interaction skills to be helping a state agency or work with the public and these bad apple(s) make the the rest of the crew look bad. A QR code should print on all customer receipts so they can, within pressure, submit their our reviews of their office visits and is a customer asks for an employees name the employee should not hesitate to give their first name only for the purpose of a review, this way management can use this data for routine employee reviews.

    October 07, 2025
  • Guest-26143

    The State of California should implement statewide enterprise-scale master service agreements for common software/services used by multiple departments, saving a lot of time and money by eliminating the need for each department (and sometimes different divisions within the same department) to go through the contract/bid/procurement process individually for identical services, which is cumbersome, inefficient, and expensive. With a statewide service agreement, departments could easily procure needed services off an approved list.

    October 07, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-26143

    State of California should eliminate form/contract requirements for services under a certain threshold (e.g., $1,000 a year). This will reduce paperwork as well as allow more vendors who provide low-cost services but do not want to/cannot sign a contract for a minimal dollar amount.

    October 07, 2025
  • Guest-27032

    Problem: procurement process takes too long. Fix: Have more contracts similar to the CALNET model, where the state does the contract and other agencies get to use it with quick ordering process. Problem: Long wait times at state departments Fix: hire more entry level position for intake and help customers fill out simple paperwork Problem: personnel spending Fix: some departments have too many management positions and not enough staff doing the actual work.

    October 07, 2025
  • Guest-22585

    California's permitting system is inefficient and often tilted in favor of large agencies and well resourced companies. Small landowners, community groups and individual applicants frequently face overwhelming barriers- long delays, unclear requirements, and inconsistent communication. Everyday Californians struggle to participate meaningfully in this process and many simply give up. Meanwhile, agency staff- stretched thing and bound by unclear, outdate (some Basin Plans) - often seem unable or unwilling to help. The system is not just slow it is inequitable. There are major structual issues driving the dysfunction: fragmented authority, a legal inablity to rely on sister agency reviews, and a risk-blind permitting process ( small projects get similar requirements to big projects). These isuess can't be solved overnight but having a way to measurer the effects and inequities would be a start. There could be a few steps to start: -Create share reivew systems across agenices so they can work together instead of having separate sources of data on permits. Identify repeated work across agencies. - Change/update laws to let agencies rely on high quality review done by partner agencies. - Build tiered permittig process that matches level of review to actual risk of the project ( driveway, replacement of infrastructure without increased foot print), versus new industrial center). - Invest in accessible guidance and staff support. Similar to CDFW have scaled permit fees based.

    October 07, 2025
  • Guest-27083

    An extremely complex and expensive current problem is the over, unnecessary, and excessive use of the workers compensation system by state employees. Significant funding should be allocated to provide for streamlining the process, investigating fraud, and limiting benefits for questionable and unfounded complaints.

    October 07, 2025
  • Guest-26270

    One of the problems we have in our department is that we dont have the ability to purchase simple items such as counter pens for customers to use or clipboards. Its embarrassing for us to tell customers, we dont have pens for them to use or to bring a pen back to me, because its my only one. It also takes us even longer to explain that we dont have tools for them to use than for us to intake them into our department. Also training is so expedited, once we onboard and employee is to be cross-trained right away so our stats look good, but they don't get thorough training to make them efficient. Its all about looking good and having the rights stats, but stats can be manipulated.

    October 07, 2025
  • Guest-26291

    Under the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act (WIOA), every state has to set up not only a virtual labor exchange system, which in the case of California is CalJOBS, but under Title I, a One-Stop Center, which in California are the America's Job Center of California (AJCC). AJCCs provide many wonderful services to the public including connecting jobseekers and employers; case management on job-related services such as resume writing, job applications, interview preparation; on-the-job training and apprenticeships; secondary and post-secondary education attainment; veteran services; assistance to migrant seasonal farmworkers; and, much more. Unfortunately, whenever people ask me what I do for a living and I tell them about the services we provide, they are oftentimes shocked that all of these services even exist. When I tell them I work for the EDD, they automatically think I work for the Unemployment Insurance Branch or Disability Insurance Branch. The primary problem is that I don't think the state does enough to spread the word of our programs and the AJCCs and their purpose. The idea I have to solve this problem would be more advertisement whether that be pamphlets throughout state buildings or TV advertisements. I haven't really thought about the logistics or cost of all this. I just think that there needs to be more information given to the public.

    October 07, 2025
  • Guest-26288

    I think that a problem that might be able to be improved is the efficiency of timekeeping. Currently, we are using a form, and it does take time to fill it out and investigate how to use it. Moving to a streamlined online system where we can see all of our paid leave we have and be able to complete our timesheet online and use our leave (and see how much we have left when using it) would be really helpful to speed up the process. Another problem that I wish could be addressed is teaching employees how to use the PSLF program. I think if people knew that by working in public service it would help with their schooling -- then they will work harder and try to advance in their careers (helping keep valuable, educated employees).

    October 07, 2025
  • Guest-26824

    I think we need to streamline our processes. It seems like we keep adding additional steps, systems, rules, etc. to everything we do. I think the steps we need to take to make our government more efficient and effective are: 1) question every requirement, 2) Delete any part or process we can 3) Simplify and optimize 4) Accelerate cycle time and 5) Automate.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26457

    I like to say I prefer Towers to Silos since it makes for better cross-work with other skill areas. Towers have doors and windows to reach across lines. Silos are meant to keep things stored inside. Don't be afraid to reach across divisions to generate the best workable ideas.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-27060

    Public works and environmental projects often require multiple permits and notifications from different agencies—each with its own application process, timelines, and conditions. This fragmented system wastes time, creates conflicting requirements, and makes compliance tracking burdensome for project managers and contractors. I propose a statewide, centralized permitting portal where applicants submit one unified application describing project scope and location. The system would: Automatically identify which permits and agencies are triggered. Generate a single project ID to track progress across agencies. Push project information to agencies in their required formats. Host shared communications, map uploads, and condition tracking. Consolidate agency conditions into one coordinated, conflict-checked permit package. Enable online fee payment in a single transaction. This platform would streamline environmental compliance, improve inter-agency coordination, reduce redundancy, and ensure consistent, transparent permitting for all public projects statewide.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26694

    I work in state law enforcement and I work with many other state law enforcement agencies. The state of CA operates well over a dozen LE agencies and from my experience it greatly inefficient and the communication and partnerships with these agencies lack. Looking at other states, they have one single state law enforcement agency with multiple bureaus and I think this is a better model. Less chiefs, less administrative staff, less police officers, better communication and teamwork and producing better outcomes. I know this is the better way because currently we have over a dozen agencies who don't communicate well and especially don't even have the ability to communicate their locations or talk over the radio, so it' similar to walking through a house in the dark playing 'Marco Polo'. Look at Texas, Tennesee, Colorado, and Arizona. They have a department called "The Department of Public Safety". Highway Patrol, State Detectives, Rangers, Wardens, etc. are all subdivisions of one agency and they communication and collaborate well to better serve there citizen's well-being and tax burden. I am citizen, tax payer, and public servant. Everyday I come to serve the people. I would like to give the people the best service and I know it can be done better this way at a better price.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26186

    Nepotism and the associated negative consequences to other employees in the unit should not be allowed, and should be investigated and corrected.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26581

    There's no inter and infra agency standardisation of operations, networks, software, etc

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26232

    As a Californian, one issue I notice is the amount of ongoing road work happening at the same time across different areas. While I understand maintenance and improvements are necessary, it often feels uncoordinated and can lead to long delays, wasted resources, and public frustration.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26712

    Need to reduce law enforcement spending and get the currently employed people in public safety sector out doing the actual job. too many of them are gaming the system with overtime, 3 or 4 differentials each and retiring early. The system allows for the abuse. Reduce the overtime by scheduling staggered shifts and don't allow them to get paid to drive patrol cars to their residences and back (2 hours each day in drive time + fuel x how many people doing it?).

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-27248

    I would say a primary problem is the lack of uniformity. There are times when a customer can go from one office to another and be told they are required to have different things, and in some instances, they can go from one employee to the next in the same office, and again, be given different requirements. The lack of uniformity shows that our online training, and on-the-job-training is not being done properly. People are training THEIR ways of doing things, instead of the way the state has drawn out. We should have more of a "one office concept" where customers will get the same exact response from every employee, in every office.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-27088

    for my department I feel we need to restructure the way we help customers get into our office to eliminate the lines, we can provide assistance more efficiently by having employees interact with customers on how to navigate our website, redesign our website to more user friendly, also provide a self-check in kiosk with information as to what forms customers need when coming in our office, have our monitors display our appointment list and wait times as well as how to videos

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26712

    Purchasing is way too complicated. most purchases have an added staff processing (staff processing) cost that is expensive to tax payer and overall uses time that could be doing something more productive.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26358

    Many of our processes are outdated and rely on paper copies and wet signatures. It's time to move everything to online and digital signatures, which reduces paper waste and printing costs. It also helps with efficiency in filing/finding documents, etc... We have access to the Microsoft Power Suite and should be utilizing it to the fullest. SharePoint/Power Apps/Power BI/Power Automate etc... all lead to cleaner data and less time wasted. While there is an effort by CalData to move departments into the future, I think that there are people within those departments who are already making inroads into that space and that departments should tap them to help others in their departments get ahead.

    October 06, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-26712

    Staff retention is a issue that comes up a lot, and back filling those vacancies can take up to a year. Its cheaper and more efficient to upgrade positions to proper level that we keep the good employees.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26728

    Profit over people. There may be guidelines on discussing telework, but it is important to address because it affects not just state employees but all workers in California. Employees are more productive and effective when they have a better work life balance, and this should be recognized across the state. California should be innovative, technology driven, and worker first, leading by example. Stronger protections are needed for workers, tenants, and consumers. Citizens of California need safeguards against profit driven motives in all areas. People over profit for everyone everywhere in the state.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26840

    Not giving clear instructions on websites

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-27248

    I feel that our online resources are not very senior citizen friendly, or even ADA compliant. There are so many options, so much small print, and all the information can be overwhelming. I have actually brought it up to our IT department and even informed them of our website not being ADA compliant, and I was unfortunately just brushed off. I warned them, they will end up having a lawsuit, especially since there is a new law requiring the ADA compliance.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-22683

    Bring processes and products into the 21st century. No one should be filling out a paper timesheet. No process should require wet signatures. Similar, software licensing. May be cheaper for the State to have a MS Office license, instead of individual agencies or departments having licenses. Same with ArcGIS, timekeeping software, or anything that multiple State agencies use. The legislature should also take a close look at what reports they are getting and what they still want. There is an overemphasis on reporting, and many of those reports may not be relevant anymore. Reform the hiring process. Exams are outdated and do not determine whether someone meets the minimum qualifications, HR does that. The exams often can only be taken over 12 months. What if someone is just a month short of meeting minimum qualifications, but they have to wait a whole year to take the test again? That does not seem fair or equitable. Make it easier to apply for positions. It is so cumbersome.

    October 06, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-26677

    We have a terrible habit in State employment of withholding info. There is ZERO reason that anyone working with or on a case to have all the information. Everything should be visible, and we should have better and more ACTUAL training

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-27006

    Every office processing “office policy” the way once office or region processes something is completely different, than how another office or region does. It’s inconsistent and make it look like one office doesn’t know what they are doing.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26512

    I would like to see an effort to have independent audits for effective use of PYs for departments.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-27240

    Legal residency and fear of deportation is a rising fear amongst many Californians. I would want to address helping non-citizens gain citizenship and/or permanent residency. In my opinion, there should be a process developed to fast track persons who are already residing here who are gainfully employed and not reliant on public benefits.

    October 06, 2025
  • Guest-26435

    I think one of the problems is those who telework. I mean if you have the ability to work from home then I believe the employer/state of CA should offer them less salary for the sake of convenience from working from home. This way, the employee can have the option of either or but if they elect to work from home, then the state will be effectively paying them less per month than an employee who "clocks in everyday" at the office. If this option were available for those who choose to work at home this would save the state in hundreds of thousands in salaries per year. I don't telework nor have the option since my job requires me to be physically there.

    October 05, 2025
  • Guest-25489

    The hiring process for Park Interpreters is absurd. Interpreters must take a "test" that does not gauge interpretive skill. Analysts that do not understand what interpretation is then grade this "test" and qualify people for a job that is the opposite of analytical. Interpreters benefit from a varied background and utilize many artistic skills and abilities to convey an impactful interpretive message. Limiting qualifying degrees to science and history fields makes for a less diverse interpretive team and misses opportunities to connect with a diverse audience. Interpreters should be consulted for the interpretive exams, as this is not an analytical field, and an analyst will have no idea how to properly grade or qualify interpretive skill.

    October 05, 2025
  • Guest-26870

    Maintaining cultures where everyone is given meaningful work and a sense of accomplishment. I've experienced this and witnessed this at three different departments where people are not in hurry to get things done, and/or the opposite where managers keep work for themselves and don't delegate. If you look around and everyone is on their phones, there is a problem - low expectations or not given enough work. My experience is that if you are stuck in this situation, the only remedy is to move on and find another job, but switching State jobs is no easy task. Promoting and transferring should be much easier, but that is a different post.

    October 03, 2025
  • Guest-25846

    I think that more support should be given for managers on performance management. There are far too many staff out there that skate by with bad behavior, poor work ethic, and a lack of technical skills because managers simply do not have the time or capacity to document, coach, and turn things around. Years of position sweeps, ongoing furloughs, etc. has resulted in so much work that managers don't have time to get the work done in addition to dealing with a problem employee. Unfortunately, in state service, there are some staff that have had YEARS to perfect their craft of skirting work, spinning up protections such as discrimination complaints/workplace violence complaints into weapons. For managers that are barely keeping afloat with the work and the rest of the team, it often ends up that the bad employee gets to continue with their ways.

    October 03, 2025
  • Guest-26019

    I work for WSB in an AJCC. We are a designated MSFW significant office with growing traffic on a yearly basis. This customer really needs a lot of hands on and help with navigating all EDD programs. The current format works fine, but increasing access to claims of all types would be extremely helpful for us at the local level. We spend too much time waiting on phones to connect and get questions answered for our clients. Access to UI and SDI claims to more directly assist customers would be very helpful, some of us had this during the pandemic. Call centers are stressed as well. This would help both parties. Increased staffing levels at crucial layoff periods would also be helpful. Non-MSFW offices should be shuttered, and those should become RESEA home based telework offices, needing limited office space.

    October 03, 2025
  • Guest-25846

    I think that the state would be well served to do a statewide desk audit, to ensure that classifications are appropriate for the work being done. There are MANY positions out there that have been "upgraded" for what I like to call "social promotions". People are promoted instead of Positions based on the work being done and technical skills required to support the mission of the department. Once a position is upgraded, it is very difficult to get it downgraded again when the person moves on, because there is a general perception that more money = better staff. This is not the case. I have seen staff in very handsomely paid positions doing the work of an AGPA. It is also devastating from a succession planning standpoint, because everything becomes so high level that there are no entry level positions to feed the higher-level vacancies. As a result, all new hires are new to the department, and no institutional knowledge is maintained as the more senior folks leave for even further promotions. Not to mention that the more positions are upgraded inappropriately, the more expensive it is to support. This cuts into programmatic funding meant for carrying out the mission of the department. Unfortunately, this is not a for profit enterprise. We don't have the authority to give financial incentives for loyalty and hard work.

    October 03, 2025
  • Guest-27243

    I believe that there are many rules and regulations to protect the environment. It is my opinion that many of these regulations are duplicative and difficult to understand. Additionally, I do not believe that state workers in some of these regulatory positions have the proper support and guidance to protect the environment. I have continued concern that state agencies are top heavy and have excessive amounts of management staff that are disassociated with the day-to-day tasks. Additionally, political will is often lacking in the higher levels of management to enforce regulations as they are intended. I think the public would be disappointed if they truly understood how water was regulated in our state. I think that we need to have a real discussion on how regulations will be applied to all equally, to be most effective at conservation on a statewide level.

    October 03, 2025
  • Guest-21781

    Constituents do not know what services they are eligible for so they do not apply on time or at all. Make TikTok or Youtube, whatever, videos to instruct Californians on their eligibility for Unemployment, SDI, PFL. How to apply, how to NOT quit or be fired. That wage theft or unsafe work conditions can be reported, that services for the disabled are available. Have the Governor's media people do it and get the word out.

    October 03, 2025
  • Guest-26628

    Many of our customers are having issues using our online services and self-service kiosks. A better organized website would help our less tech savvy customers get used to using our online services.

    October 03, 2025
  • Guest-22899

    Tax revenue would increase dramatically if California taxed the extremely wealthy the same way it does the middle class.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-25602

    A problem I am seeing more and more is that we are receiving the employer's protest letter (for an Unemployment Insurance claim filed) and a note gets entered on the claim but no other action is taken - even when the employer clearly states the claimant quit or was fired (and the claimant told us they were laid off due to lack of work). There needs to be an action list that the employer's response gets added to so someone can address the issues that the employer has raised.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-26236

    Micromanaging has to be the biggest problem. My department has supervisors that got promoted more of who they know over knowledge. These type of people constantly are in people business because they don't understand what is going on. If we train our staff correctly they should be able to do their jobs without constant supervision. As a veteran, this is how it was done in the service. Train your staff fully, communicate what needs to be done, then let them do it. These steps are missed in my department as people don't know the job themselves so they cant train the staff nor can they trust that they get the job done without a boss over their shoulder.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-27041

    Another problem I see is some workers are not serious with work. They do not communicate with peers

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-27134

    Currently every department has duplicative workloads. Every department has an HR team, every department has a Tech team, every department has a procurement team, etc. It would help streamline and optimize our overall performance if these could be consolidated down to at least an Agency level, or to have actual departments (CalHR, CDT, DGS, etc.) run these functions for every other department, and allow them to perform the functions that they need to.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-26276

    Wasteful spending and offering practical solutions to each problem.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-27041

    I would say hire more employees. Also, provide more training to the staffs. And offer more promotional opportunities.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-27041

    I would say we have more workload than resources. At times, we have lots of work with not enough staffs. And product output is expected of high quality and at times work is not balanced with peers properly. I will say balance each staff's workload and hire more staffs.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-23523

    Update Cal Employee Connect to be user friendly. Managers spend a lot of time correcting timesheets and answering questions about leave balances, instead of working on program goals. I suggest enhancing the Cal Employee Connect platform at SCO, to identify what payroll codes stand for and how to use them. For example someone could hover over the "HOL ITO" and a pop up would appear that spelled out what the code means, and state either how to use it, or link to information about how to use it. The entry for "PH" has an information symbol that states, "This entry is measured in Units and must be used in full Units increments." This is helpful to know, but many employees may not know what "Units" or "full Units increments" means. "Units" is capitalized, indicating there is a definition somewhere, but that information has to be hunted down. While I can find this information, it would save me time if employees could easily find this information themselves, and understand how to use the different leave categories, at the same time as checking their leave balances. While the information is out there, it could be provided in a much more user friendly/smarter way. I'll add that Cal Employee Connect is already a great platform. I think it can get even better.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-27028

    The lack of collaboration between and within departments. I see overlap in projects but no collaboration.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-27399

    The hiring and specifically the promotion process is broken. There is zero or next to zero feedback. There is no way to know how you 'scored' on the answers to the interview questions. There seems to be favoritism all over the place. That is my experience anyway. The system for promoting employees needs to be more transparent and feedback, coaching and development for promotion has to improve. Ten fold. I say this as a former coach, manager and leader.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-26535

    Creating and soliciting contracts seems inefficient. I am not a supervisor and haven't had to go through the process, but all I hear from people who do have to fill that role is it's challenging. We may find a vendor that the District likes to work with but you can only use them for one job. Or one of their subcontractors didn't fill a requirement (veteran owned business) and so the whole project falls apart. It is not efficient.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-26229

    It appears that we don't have the options to purchase non EV vehicles. However, the charging stations at our office never work due to the fact our parcel has maxed out our electrical capacity. So we keep getting more and more EV cars and trucks with no easy way to charge them (quickly). Throw in the fact we are mostly rural, and fast public charging stations are few and far between. This could be solved by allowing state agencies in rural areas more exemptions to purchasing EV only vehicles.

    October 02, 2025
  • Guest-27216

    I see an opportunity to help all staff understand the customer they serve and to keep the customer's needs as a focal point as we do our work. I also see an opportunity to include our customers in the refinement of old and the development of new policies / procedures. One idea for addressing these opportunities: a simple communication campaign to raise awareness and, in some cases, change people's minds.

    October 01, 2025
  • Guest-27339

    Primary problem with the department that I work for is that it is a "reactive" department and not "Proactive" when it comes to decision making. There are so many things implemented based on what upper management thinks is right but then changes when someone gets injured or something goes wrong. If upper management would work cohesively with their staff, they would be able to implement better policy and procedure. There needs to better communication and work ethic with upper management and staff.

    October 01, 2025
  • Guest-27079

    Main Point: Need to Encourage and retain talent to improve effectiveness and innovation Problem: Advancement often depends on tenure, not performance or innovation. Hard to reward high-performers and limits merit-based growth. Doing the bare minimum is often “safer” than pushing for change.Career stagnation becomes normalized. Solutions Incentivize impact, not just time served. Recognize and reward problem-solving, not just process-following. Build a culture of accountability, growth, and innovation.

    October 01, 2025
  • Guest-26387

    The lack of personnel. We have last many positions within our unit as the positions were abolished. We are expected to the same amount of work, if not more and we are poorly compensated in comparison to the private sector. We constantly loose employees as they look for elsewhere as they’re compensated more in other companies.

    October 01, 2025
  • Guest-26290

    One problem I've observed is cultural. I think many people say something is "not their problem" and so we end up in a tragedy of the commons situation, where nothing is anyone's problem. In truth, I think each team needs to exercise greater ownership over the problems we can all see. Furthermore, I think people need to be willing to accept more responsibility when systems go down or when problems occur.

    October 01, 2025
  • Guest-26952

    A) Clearer more streamlined CAU, SCO, and HR activity directing ETR leave synchronization, CalConnect payroll changes and confirmations B) more accurate handling of State Disability Insurance and Paid Family Leave cases. C) Better collaboration of Federal Labor Law organizations at the county and local level to address complaints, violations, and accountability. D) Assistance with state employees leadership and local Boards when relationships falter. E) EDDNext into an app that improves customer self-service.

    October 01, 2025
  • Guest-24477

    I would address the RTO mandate and evaluate the need for for RTO based on the job description

    October 01, 2025
  • Guest-27263

    Lack of feedback from the field offices and the central office

    October 01, 2025
  • Guest-23431

    One of the simplest ways to immediately make government run more efficiently, is to institute full-time telework across state agencies. This would increase efficiency by enabling the ability of employees to get more done, decrease greenhouse gas emissions from personal vehicles, and save the state money by getting rid of excess office space. Many state agencies also make the claim of wanting to increasing employee personal wellness, job/life balance, and spending more time with family. Full-time telework would solve these issues, and many similar ones as well. It's time to move forward and embrace new ideas, and more efficient ways of doing things. This takes courage and leadership from the top, which has been in short supply these last few years. Full-time telework is an answer to more government efficiency at all levels, and California should act as the bellwether for all other States to follow on this issue.

    October 01, 2025
  • Guest-27385

    Outsourcing, private contractors, temp employees and or trying to implement an off the shelf product or project from the private sector. The state has many requirements and regulations that are designed to protect, inform, safety regulations, IT, laws, privacy, etc. The private product or program and or staff does not understand and trying to get it to work and it does not. The state in the future no matter what the need is, pay no matter the cost for the ground up projects and ask the state employees how it is to functions and or work. Buildings, Software, Computers, Desks, vehicles, tools, etc. No outsourcing of any kind at any level and quit going with the lowest bids, it costs us and the taxpayers money over and over again.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26890

    State is not proactive, it is reactive. Consider DMV. The DMV is too small in most Metropolitain areas. No foresight in planning. No parking, nowhere to sit, not enough people working. The workforce is discouraged by the inefficiency of the decision makers. Most of these decision makers have made changes based on outsourced companies' evaluations. The changes made are creating hardships and do not work. The recommendations that would work are not followed.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26146

    Idea: Reform or eliminate CDT’s duplicative IT project procurement oversight process. Rationale: Inefficient use of resources: Current CDT oversight adds layers of redundant review and documentation that consume millions in staff hours and consulting costs, without measurably improving project success rates. Delays delivery: The additional procurement checkpoints extend timelines, often by several months, discouraging innovation and responsiveness. Limited accountability: Oversight bodies rarely bear responsibility when projects fail, creating a misalignment between authority and accountability. Alternative Approach: Shift to agency-led, outcome-based accountability: Empower departments to own their projects end-to-end, while holding them accountable, to iterate, hold departments accountable, for cost, schedule, and performance. Replace CDT’s checklist-style oversight with independent milestone reviews (e.g., external auditors or peer agency reviews) tied to measurable deliverables. Adopt modular, milestone-based procurement (similar to private sector best practices) that enables course correction without massive sunk costs. Expected Impact: Faster project initiation, lower administrative overhead, clearer accountability, and better use of taxpayer dollars.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-25100

    Executive decision making. Control agencies create guidelines, guidance, guardrails, etc., but then allow each Department to interpret it differently. Why is hiring easier at one agency than another? Why can a contract with CARB get executed in 2 months and a contract with Caltrans takes a year? They cite the same contract code, DGS rules, etc. but have completely different interpretations of the same thing. How is that possible? What's the point of this guidance or having a control agency if there is no standardization across the State.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27040

    Problem 1 – Customer Experience From a customer’s perspective, the State’s reliance on mailed notices and mailed responses is outdated and inefficient. Californians expect the same level of convenience they experience with banks, healthcare providers, and private businesses. Mailing forms back and forth creates delays, risks lost documents, and forces residents to rely on slower communication when technology could streamline the process. Proposed Solution: Require high-volume departments to offer an online option for submitting responses or uploading documents. Provide funding to ensure these systems are secure and user-friendly. Gives Californians faster, more reliable ways to communicate with the State. Reduces dependence on paper, manual scanning, and case management bottlenecks. Aligns government services with modern technology standards and customer expectations. Problem 2 – Employee Retention and Knowledge Gaps State programs are highly complex, requiring years of training to master. While it is positive that employees advance through promotions, frequent turnover combined with limited pay results in ongoing knowledge loss and operational disruptions. Proposed Solution: Leverage technology and AI to improve knowledge retention and training: Real-time guidance systems for staff when processing cases. AI-assisted training that adapts to an employee’s pace and learning style.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26384

    I like to see improvements in data sharing between state agencies. Most state agencies are cooperative, but the contact and data sharing process can take a very long time. State of CA is the owner of the data, not the individual agency. I like to see more of the data integrated for better use.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26333

    Inefficiency. One major source of inefficiency is the printing of documents that are already in our system of electronic documentation. I am not familiar with others, but I do know that it is required for me as a Licensing Program Analyst (LPA).

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-24536

    I feel there is too much bureaucracy. To get anything done, there is so much paperwork and red tape, leading to inefficiency and time wasting. I understand the need to follow the chain of command, but sometimes this leads to delays, missed deadlines, and loss of funding for projects. the number of forms that need to completed for ANYTHING is ridiculous. Time can be better allocated to doing other things.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27411

    We need to significantly improve efficiency. As someone who worked in the private sector for decades before coming to state service a few years ago, I continue to be shocked at the amount of inefficiencies. Processes take too long, resources and time are wasted continuously. If we were a private business, we would have declared bankruptcy long ago. We need more innovation and get over the idea that we've always done things this way. We need to do better at keeping up with the times, learn to be more flexible and adaptable. The stereotype that government works slow is completely accurate, we often joke about it, but it should be unacceptable.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27215

    As a newer state employee, I was shocked that I did not have a sit-down meeting with someone in HR to help explain the benefits and at what level I was eligible for. I didn't even know what type of employee I was, and I could not talk to anyone, I had to ask co-workers what the differences were and how it would affect me and my future. It seemed like this was so secretive. I would think that would be an essential part of onboarding. In addition to overall better communication, access to information needs to be improved.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27310

    Requiring employees to return to the office for an antiquated manner of managing staff is absurd. We have been actively completing our duties and flourishing with remote opportunities. Our agency has been able to complete more projects during remote work than ever before yet there is still a desire for us to return in-person. We should be allowed to continue remote work.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27310

    There is an urgent need to make all aspects of my agency electronic and remote. The effort does not take into consideration human knowledge and the multiple scenarios a customer’s record could have. There is not always an automated way to handle all transactions. And instead of realizing this, we continue to push out bad products causing confusion and frustration for both the customers and employees.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27337

    As a California resident, one problem I see is that many Californians see State agencies in a negative light. To address this issue, I believe agencies need to be a little more transparent and also staffing issues seem to be a concern with the public as well. I also, utilize agencies for personal needs as required, and I notice that alot of the agencies have a negative attitude toward the public if they are not educated on what options or needs they need addressed, that being said, the state workers are not very transparent in explaining or assisting the public in that regard.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26890

    Staffing shortages need to be addressed. Every public facing department does not have enough staff to meet customer demands.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26818

    Communication and training are the biggest issues.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27283

    I believe training is a critical issue that affects both new hires and experienced employees. To ensure consistency and clarity, we need centralized training guides that are regularly updated with real-time changes. Too often, I’ve approached different SMVTs or managers with the same question and received conflicting answers. Ongoing training, regular reminders, and open communication between managers and staff should be a core part of our Wednesday meetings. Currently, these meetings often feel unproductive. However, they present a valuable opportunity to conduct reviews, discuss updates to tasks or procedures, and align on best practices.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26409

    Overall, I think the state training program is woefully inadequate. The two-week new supervisor/manager training is a waste of time and doesn't cover most of what managers actually face. There are so many leadership development and coaching programs in the private sector that could benefit state employees, like Harvard Business Review or Stanford. Lareg companies often have leadership development tracks. VSP, for example, has two types of tracks you can enroll in -- one for those wanting to become executives and a second track for those who want to be subject matter expert leaders (but not necessarily an exec). They provide mentors, hold classes, and you feel encouraged that you're proactively growing professionally. Employee management training, specifically, is reactive instead of proactive. I've worked closely with performance management units to discuss personnel issues and sent emails or disciplinary plans for their review. Rather than being trained ahead of time, before issues start, I'm stuck with escalating issues waiting for PMU to review emails and provide guidance. And then their guidance changes frequently depending on what they've recently experienced in court. It's immensely frustrating.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26673

    Yes, the bureaucracy is excessive and results in things costing more than expected.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26702

    Innovated technology. Update the systems that are causing unnecessary delays. The State's method of communication with the public is antiquated. For many wage replacement programs, the only way to submit required eligibility documents is by mail. We need to simplify the process for the public and create State service centers.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27317

    As a Californian, one of the biggest problems I see is the inefficiency in how government work is structured. Too much emphasis is placed on rigid rules about where employees sit, how many minutes their breaks are, and other political battles, instead of focusing on improving programs and actually saving money. For example, cutting back on telework only forces more cars onto the road, increases congestion, adds to air pollution, and wastes time and money on commuting and parking. It lowers employee satisfaction and productivity. A better solution is to invest in flexible, results-oriented work structures. Telework should be preserved and expanded where possible, with clear performance metrics and accountability built around outcomes rather than physical presence. This reduces costs tied to office space and commuting while allowing employees to devote more energy to meaningful work that directly benefits Californians.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26053

    It is challenging to give too much autonomy to any department when it comes to statewide issues, such as recruitment practices, policies and administrative programs, pay systems, etc., when we are all ONE EMPLOYER. It leaves a lot of room for error when we are all subject to the same laws and regulations but are allowed to interpret and apply that interpretation differently. It would be most helpful to actually have our CONTROL AGENCIES truly control, provide THEIR interpretation to laws/rules/regs that should be applied across the board, and have them develop the statewide policies (not just as a model) and practices that are most defensible to create standardization and consistency across agencies.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26470

    The amount of approvals needed to get things done. I understand the necessity for such complex oversight, but it also slows down many processes and delays things like payments, grants, etc. Additionally, many of our systems are outdated and requires people with expertise to use it to the full advantage, which is also lacking.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27257

    C alHR does not pay state workers enough, and is constantly nickel-and-diming us. I would like to see wages come closer to a livable wage for Californians.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26866

    Making the websites easier to navigate. Make it easier for the customers that are not as technology savvy navigate the websites

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26866

    Consistency! Every office has their own interpretations of laws and policies. It could be frustrating to the customers

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27071

    There are too many levels of management and things are lost. I started about 5 years ago before the salesforce platform. Now, with salesforce, the ONLY thing that matters are numbers. These numbers did not exist a few years ago and now they are the only thing that matters. The employees don't matter. JUST the numbers. Managers are very secretive and things are kept from everyone.. very us against them mentality

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26902

    What can we do to foster employee engagement at all levels. Culture is hard to build and particularly during these times people are seeking community. We need to find ways to help teams from the agency to the unit level bond and connect over common goals.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26908

    The budget always seems to be the topic that is brought up when it comes to asking for funding for different projects. We work in a huge state that covers many different areas with a wide variety of assets. It should be easier to reassign assets from one area to another within the state. These assets have already been purchased with state money. If there is a department / agency that could use a piece of equipment that is no longer needed by another, it should be easier to reassign it. If one agency no longer requires it or is due for an upgrade, another agency should be able to utilize it instead of trying to go through an entire procurement process that there is no funding for.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-23077

    As a Californian with almost 30 years in state service, the primary problem I would address is redundancy in process and clarity of procedures for staff. Streamlining duplicative processes and procedures to gain efficiencies in processing times while also helping clarify/simplify processes for staff would be helpful to both the public we serve and the hardworking staff of this state.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27119

    Procurement is unnecessarily slow and tedious and filled with a litany of rules and subjective opinions on how to do business. It is quite inefficient to get things done and, when you can finally move on the work, you are left to deal with the lowest bidder. These practices are not in the best interests of the people of California.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27119

    California Government Code requirements around maintaining a former employee's old position as limited-term while they electively go explore another limited-term position elsewhere in State Government. The idea that we have to "keep their seat warm" while they explore other options at their own discretion (for up to 2 years or more!) is nonsensical and does nothing to support the dedication and program cohesion of the employee who fills their old job. If the job is originally funded as a permanenet position, it's status should not change nor should any preference or "reinstatement rights" be granted an ex-employee who left of their own choosing.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26045

    There are many positions that can stay hybrid or remote. If yoy are going to force workers back to office, please also compensate us more so thst we can afford the additional gas and parking costs

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26045

    I feel like one major point of contention as a state worker is the RTO policy that the governor tried to implement this last year. It has been proven that we are capable of doing our jobs while maintaining a hybrid schedule. And as someone who does purchasing, watching my agency waste money preparing for workers to return to office seemed like such a waste of money. There is such a huge expense to bringing people back tomoffice that includes leading office buildings, the additional cost to workers and the additional traffic. Please keep workers well being in mind

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-23336

    My agency is chronically understaffed and people are asked to take on extra workloads with no solution in sight or any compensation for the extra work. I think this is contributing to burn out of staff and high turnover rates that make amplifies the staffing issue over time.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-23336

    The strict hierarchy in the state does not allow for innovation. Supervisors preserve the status quo instead of encouraging staff to find streamlined solutions to problems that affect the lower staff levels.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26377

    Mentoring new employees on the important aspects of government work. Some of us joined the state as seasoned professionals, bringing a wealth of specific expertise, but are not contributing to the fullest extent of our knowledge, because the bureaucracy gets in the way. Understand that some redundancies are intentionally baked in our processes, which is why I feel that a mentor with a longer state employment could help us navigate the barriers for better efficiency and reduced frustrations.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26498

    Purchasing and procurement! I regularly have to manage the purchase of items that are distributed to youth. These items and their related youth program are a widely recognized public good with broad public support. To purchase these items I have to spend months submitting and waiting (and waiting, and waiting) for first quotes and then waivers from CalPIA and from OSP; if I am required to work with either, I win the privilege of having 10 times the turn-around time at almost twice the price available with commercial producers. I spend countless taxpayer-funded hours navigating statewide contracts and multi-tiered purchasing requirements, all with minimal administrative support. I have to ask proprietors of small businesses to submit mountains of forms, including one that confirms they never supported a 20 year old international atrocity, and then I have ask them to float their own overhead costs before I can pay them for their services. I loose weeks, if not months, of staff time and project delivery timeliness when vendors are unable to comply with our cumbersome requirements, especially when I am required to have multiple quotes. As it stands, I see almost no benefit to the people of California or the budgeting of their tax dollars.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26498

    Our exams/hiring process. First, there are extremely limited resources available to applicants to help them navigate a hiring process that is fundamentally different from most others. This means the most successful applicants are ones who either receive direct support from State Workers or who have the luxury of time and money to learn the slow way. This is a major impediment to hiring equity. Second, the almost cartoonishly strict interpretation of MQs regularly disqualifies intensely qualified applicants. The staff responsible for interpreting MQs are not experts in the fields they are judging and frequently discredit intensely relevant work experience simply due to the use of a synonym; I have seen applicants with decades of relevant experience have it all thrown out for using a slightly different word on their applicant than what the exam/application reviewer was looking for. Same too for calculating time of relevant work experience. Again, this only serves to exclude marginalized applicants.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26565

    Telework is not utilized to its full potential. It makes zero sense to force people into an office when much of the work many state employees are doing can be done from anywhere across the state. Why spend millions on maintaining office spaces when Telework has been proven to work?

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27441

    Telework - The need for it to be widespread and standardized. Telework should be a given for a lot of positions not the exception.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27377

    Process documentation to ensure continuity of services and processes at every level. It requires governance and monitoring within the organization to develop the programs and the successful execution from the top down. It is also important to communicate successes to build confidence and employ a change management strategy.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-27011

    Modernization of existing infrastructure and processes to increase efficiency

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26335

    Government should design public programs to help communities respond to the needs of their own community members, rather than government programs replacing the way individuals rely on community. Therefore, every public program should rigorously evaluate the extent to which programs, and the public resources they administer, strengthen real connections between communities, families and individuals who are in need. One area of policy this idea could be applied to is homelessness services. Every homeless person has people who love them and want to help them, but there is often no safe way for family and friends to stay connected and engaged. Government spending should always start from a place of prioritizing ways that it can strengthen/rebuild a vulnerable person's connectivity to their family and community.
