That could include creating pathways for a caring family member to make financial contributions to a family member struggling with mental illness or addition that are dedicated to housing, to food, to pro-social activities. Or to create safe environments that are dedicated to helping people in a mental health crisis reconnect to people who love them. People only heal in the context of community and belonging. Our programs should prioritize paying for things that rebuild those actual connections.

    September 30, 2025
  • Guest-26426

    Allow employees to work remotely. Also create a budget for employees to celebrate wins, instead of having managers pay out of pocket for employees.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-27029

    I would address the EDD Unemployment Benefits program. At a time when individuals need their benefits the most, accessibility should not be a factor. The system used to manage unemployment benefits is antiquated and the whole infrastructure needs to be modernized. Individuals having issues applying or accessing information about their claim are unable to speak to a live representative because their call center hours are extremely limited making it more difficult to reach a live person. Why can't call center hours be extended? With disability and paid family leave benefits, an individual is able to go into a local office and speak with an employee. Why was that option removed for unemployment benefits?

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-26752

    In a nutshell, understand what is already in place creating new solutions. There is so much overlap between agencies that creates a great deal of duplication and confusion. This is actually pretty simple. Do your homework first. Know all stakeholders, internal and external. Create meaningful engagement upfront. I have been a state employee for 25 years and worked for 4 agencies. I have been through a great deal of change, often mandated by legislation. The implementation is always an afterthought. Implementation is essential to success. Employees in the trenches too often are not identified as stakeholders, their expertise overlooked. This is not good stewardship, and all too often results in program duplication and implementation failure.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-26862

    The primary issue I see is ineffective individuals in the same job for decades without being held accountable for the work they do or don't do. I believe that mandatory and semi-annual performance evaluations should be implemented for all roles. Continuing employment and pay should be based on those evaluations. Two under-performing scores in a row would initiate a Performance Improvement Plan with specific and measureable goals to meet by the end of the PIP period. If these goals are not met, depending on the severity of the underperformance, either a salary cut or termination.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-26003

    As a Californian and state employee, one primary problem I would address is how we support and retain the state workforce. Too often, employees face challenges such as outdated classifications, pay that does not reflect the cost of living, and limited flexibility in how we work. To solve this, I would advocate for three key improvements: first, expanding telework options where appropriate, since telework has proven to increase productivity, save costs, and improve work–life balance; second, raising pay scales to be more competitive so that we can attract and keep skilled workers; and third, ensuring proper classifications so employees are recognized and compensated for the full scope of their responsibilities. By addressing these issues, we would not only strengthen morale and retention among state workers but also improve the quality of service delivered to Californians.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-27049

    A primary problem I would address is the gap in support for Californians who work hard but earn just above the eligibility limits for food or housing assistance. Many working families face food insecurity or risk of homelessness because their income technically disqualifies them from aid, yet their wages are not enough to meet California’s high cost of living. To solve this, I would propose expanding tiered or “bridge” programs that provide partial assistance on a sliding scale rather than an all-or-nothing cutoff. For example, a working family over the income limit for SNAP could still receive reduced benefits to help offset grocery costs, while rental support could offer temporary subsidies based on local housing markets. Additionally, strengthening public-private partnerships with nonprofits, food banks, and community organizations would stretch resources further. By reimagining assistance as a continuum of support, rather than a strict threshold, we can help stabilize California’s working community and reduce the cycle of crisis many families face despite being employed.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-26265

    State employees should be able to give evaluations on their supervisors. Many supervisors are clueless on whether they are doing their job effectively from the people that work under them. Employees are stuck just commiserating and vent amongst themselves while the supervisors are blind to their weaknesses and ways they could potentially improve.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-26845

    Make downtown Sacramento parking more affordable. Make it part of the compensation package and provide free park-and-ride programs around the downtown area. In Oceanside CA, (near San Diego) they have FREE on demand rides to a location you need to go to. The rides are on electric large golf carts. Its clean, easy, safe and FREE. Downtown Sacramento parking must be addressed to bring people back to work.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-27403

    Problem: For state financed construction projects, even state & local govt. managed/owned 3rd parties (water districts, city roads, utilities, Caltrans) lack alignment on fast & cost efficient delivery. Permits & clearances are unnecessarily delayed causing cost & time overruns. Possible Solution: All state agencies that need to work in tandem with other agencies should have a part of their budget dependent on achieving inter-departmental synergies.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-27135

    Instead of writing a mail station number on an inter-department delivery envelope, and then continuing to scratch out the previous one and write a new one below it, we should all simply just place a sticker with our intended mail station number ON TOP OF the previous sticker, and that way it would make the inter-department delivery envelopes LAST FOREVER, thus saving the state money by NEVER needing to purchase more inter-department delivery envelopes ever again. Think about it. If everyone keeps hand-writing mail station numbers below the previous one, and scratching out the prior one, eventually the envelope becomes full and has to be thrown away and more envelopes get purchased which wastes money. BUT if everyone were to simply place a sticker with the mail station ON TOP of the previous mail station rather than below it, then those envelopes would last forever without ever needing to get replaced.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-27326

    Not enough training available for State employees in the areas they are interested in.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-27326

    Conduct a survey of all State employees to identify what training they are interested in that would assist them in promotions or just doing their jobs.

  • Guest-27326

    After conducting the survey, examine the results and determine what training is feasible that could be created by that State agency or another State agency.

  • Guest-26546

    Misappropriation of funds and the lack thereof for California Department of Fish and Wildlife and the hiring practices of the department

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-27289

    The state budget allocation system is broken. Programs are given an annual budget - which is commonly based on prior year allocations. When a program underspends its allocations, it can expect a proportional reduction in the next year's allocation - this leads to programs "spend it or lose it" mentality which often leads to unnecessary or wasteful spending.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-26448

    The State Governments' resistance to innovation, technology, and change. COVID-19 forced many state governments to automate outdated processes however we are still behind. Why doesn't state government spear head the forefront in innovation? This statement is inclusive to a majority of departments not all (i.e. departments that still utilize paper time sheets). California state departments vary greatly on the spectrum of technologically adaptative, there should be a baseline. California is known for its innovation it's time we embrace the technology we have and collaborate with our citizens (i.e. Google, Tesla) creating government contracts.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-26837

    I've been with the same department for 20 plus years. My biggest critique is the favoritism and CYAness of many managers. Also despite giving lip service to the contrary, building morale and encouraging old timers to take on leadership roles is nonexistent in my department. Hoping this group elevate mine (and others') concerns.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-26388

    This is an issue in my division specifically. My division schedules in-person hearings upon request, which requires staff to travel hundreds of miles at the cost of $300-500 in travel expenses. Oftentimes, the public does not show up, wasting the staff member's time and needlessly costing the state hundreds of dollars in travel fees. Eliminating this in-person hearing requirement would be a simple solution to save time and money.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-25846

    I would suggest that there be a new contract type specifically for Tribal Consultation. Rules would be tailored to their unique circumstances, and not require things like "competitive solicitation". I wouldn't mind seeing some standard rates coming out of it that accounts for their travel/time/expertise

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-22904

    I would like to continue addressing the need for Californians with lower incomes (e.g., main source of income is Social Security) to have easier access to quality assisted living care when needed. My ideas for helping with this would be: - An Assisted Living Navigator who can personally work with individuals and their families to help them take the steps needed to access funding for care. - A one-stop portal (one-doorway) to allow individuals and families to make the linkages necessary to access funding for care. For example, an individual who qualifies for Medi-Cal and Medicare who wants to qualify for the MasterCare assisted living program needs to work with county Medi-Cal, their insurance, and a healthcare organization, and MasterCare to enjoin their insurance to access benefits.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-26373

    Accountability for our appointed executives may be an issue. The politically appointed executives answer to no one except for perhaps the Governor's office, which may be too busy dealing with other issues.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-26580

    Combating State worker burnout and abuse at the hands of poorly trained Supervisors, I propose stricter sanctions on supervisors, better training for both, and all employees adhering to a code of conduct.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-26369

    "Interdisciplinary" Collaboration and Communication. Engage "interagency" and other outside agencies/entities' participation and collaboration to address previous and current tasks, issues, problem-solving, etc. through meetings and discussions to reach a common and/or time-tested solutions. This eliminates previously failed endeavors and duplicative work.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-27235

    As a skilled trades worker, the problem I would like to see addressed is obtaining and retaining other skilled trades workers like me. The State of California has and does promote individuals from non-skilled trade roles to skilled trade positions. While the opportunity for one to promote and improve their wages is a great thing, the flip side is that the non-skilled staff create a dilemma that highlights the destitution of knowledge which hinders the efficiency of skilled trade workers. Skilled trade staff are forced to pick up the slack, figuratively speaking, by correcting bad work or having to train individuals on how to correctly administer trade work, things that are usually taught through apprenticeships or trade unions.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-25537

    The Lottery's rigorous SmartyStreets address system is much more detailed and rigid than the USPS's, which means we spend a lot of time investigating discrepancies on addresses we get from ID's. If the DMV were to use SmartyStreets I'm sure all state agencies would save time from the elimination of so many errors that are generated there.

    September 29, 2025
  • Guest-27114

    I think the State could benefit from standardizing the processes that all departments follow. Consistency is key and there are many missed opportunities for efficiency and greater effectiveness in our shared processes. Areas to consider include onboarding of new State hires and the hiring process. Of course, a key piece of doing all this would be to make sure that we have one efficient and effective process for everyone. Some changes to the current way of doing things would likely be required.

    September 27, 2025
  • Guest-26597

    The primary problem is low salary scales. This isn't just my opinion: The California Legislature's Bipartisan Fiscal and Policy Advisor released a report with data that shows average monthly salary figures have continued to fall behind market averages even calculating for inflation, and top step for salaries has also been in decline. An employee will abide great discomforts if they're compensated handsomely. But, that same employee will pursue other employment if neither salary nor working conditions reach respectable levels. Getting "paid in sunsets" is insufficient to recruit and retain top talent. Field staff are repeatedly told we're the most valuable members of the organization. But, HR, finance, management, and safety policies loudly declare the true narrative which contradicts all niceties.

    September 27, 2025
  • Guest-27305

    Primary Problem Sales and use tax regulations are complex and often contain gray areas. True expertise usually comes from auditors who have dealt with specific, real-world applications of the law. However, there is no easy or safe way for auditors to share their knowledge across the department. Getting clarity on how to apply a regulation often requires layers of approvals, and managers may hesitate to elevate questions up the chain. This slows audits, wastes staff hours, and delays clear guidance to taxpayers. Proposed Idea Create a safe, department-wide collaboration channel where auditors can directly share and discuss regulation questions with a large group of experienced peers. For example, an internal email group or moderated forum could allow auditors to submit questions and receive input quickly, without having to navigate multiple levels of approval. Faster feedback would reduce audit hours, improve consistency, and give taxpayers clearer answers sooner, all while fostering a culture of shared expertise and professional growth.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-27148

    Stop buying so many computers. We waste hundreds of thousands annually to replace good, working equipment. Not to mention all the e-waste.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26166

    A recurring concern I encounter is the continued reliance on paper documentation in environments where the vast majority of records are already managed electronically. In many cases, hard copies provide little added value, yet we continue to utilize paper and intra-office mailing systems for processes that could be handled securely and more efficiently through our encrypted email systems. The ongoing costs of paper, printing, and postage are considerable and present an avoidable expense.

    September 26, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-26931

    Within the department I work, we experience a significant amount of extra workload from secondary and tertiary duties. These are duties that fall completely outside of a person's duty statement and go far above the standard "5% of other duties as assigned." clause in the duty statement. This includes assignments such as EEO coordinator, Skelly officer, Peer Support Specialist coordinator. It seems these types of assignments get handed to 1 or 2 people instead of split among several to lighten the burden.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26422

    I would say management in CMF does not communicate well and needs to follow policy more.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26976

    I've been a state employee at the prison for more than 10 years. These are the most common problems we see there. -Overriding Inmates to a lower level when they don't deserve it. By overriding youth offenders, medical Inmates or non-violent offenders to a lower-level causes disruptions in positive programing. Inmates should be placed into an appropriate security level based on their security points only. -Promoting and Pushing the Suboxone program to receive time off makes Inmates that would normally not take it, sign up for the program just to get time off or sell the Suboxone in the dorms/housing area. Don't give time off for the program and you will have only the Inmates that really need it on the program or don't give out Suboxone because by the time they get to State prison they should have already gone through withdraws and be clean. -The overuse of medications to Inmates is out of control. We are no longer a prison but a medical facility. Most Inmates take medication in hopes to get time off of their sentence or get on Social Security when they get out. Thousands of pills are spit out or sold in the dorm. Have doctors not medicate Inmate so easily.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-27200

    Nursing is grossly understaffed. Upper management is rarely if ever in the clinics engaging with their medical staff. Nepotism and favoritism are rampant. An SRNII told an LVN the other day that she should stop bringing up issues regarding safety because she, "won't get hired when she gets her RN license, because management doesn't like people bringing up issues." Education relies too much on staff to train new employees. There's zero continuity in the clinics, so that plan is fraught with issue after issue. Custody has become too complacent in regard to safety and security. A resident in C Yard was caught with eleven diabetic syringes/needles. That particular resident gets insulin twice a day, so they were able to acquire all those needles over a period of time. Residents bring their tablet to the pill window and in the clinic. The tablets aren't allowed to leave the housing units for obvious reasons. A patient reported to an exam room to complete their appointment with me this week with a hot cup of coffee. I didn't notice he was holding it until we were in the room together. The porters/residents in our clinic are allowed to "clean" without anyone directly watching them. The porters/residents are given access to areas they should never be given entry to.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26671

    Big Idea: Streamlining Student Internships at State Agencies Right now, if a college student in California—say, someone attending CSU Fullerton—wants to do an academic internship at a state agency like California State Parks, they face a long and complicated process. Their university has to negotiate a legal agreement (a MOU) with the agency, often involving lawyers on both sides. This process can take months and discourages students and agencies from even trying. My proposal: The Department of General Services (DGS) should create a blanket MOU that covers academic internships between all California public college systems (Community Colleges, CSU, and UC) and all state agencies. This would eliminate the need for each university and agency to negotiate separate legal agreements every time a student wants to intern. Agencies would still define the details of each internship—like duties, schedules, and supervision—but the legal framework would already be in place. This would: - Make it easier for students to gain hands-on experience in public service. - Reduce administrative burden for both colleges and agencies. - Create a clear recruitment pathway for state agencies to identify and develop future talent. - Expose students to careers in public service, helping build a stronger, more diverse pipeline into government work.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-27226

    I believe the direction of CDCR will infuriate the public by the state policies enable the negative behavior inmates perpetrate on each other and to staff. The enabled behavior contributes to higher recidivism rates and does not prepare inmates for when they become discharged. It is disheartening to see bad behavior bombarded with wasted resources for inmates versus hard working citizens and their needs.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26691

    With 23 years working for the state I have noticed state workers do not have passion for what they do. Those who run state departments just do it for the money and power but not for love or passion for what they do. I was told to promote to make the change happen but the I realized that no matter how many time I promote there is always someone on top who works “by the book” not looking outside the box to see what is best for the the public and the employees.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26010

    Telework needs to be considered more gracefully and as a solution to saving money. I work in a prison as a teacher and there are many times the work we do can be done at home. For instance, the first Wednesday of the month is a staff training day. Why not save resources and have these trainings from home? When looking at classroom instruction, there is increasingly a push to have students use technology. Why not use the tablet the prison population has now as a tool for education rather than just a tool for communication and entertainment?

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-27299

    As a state employee, my experience has been that the merit system has very few checks and balances and has largely failed. In my agency, cronyism, nepotism and favoritism run rampant. The rules speak out against these practices, but it seems that the system has slowly evolved to where it is not designed to encourage them. One of the reasons this happens if SPB has a rule that examination results are kept private, so there is no way to know the exam results of others who have taken the same exam, and it becomes extremely difficult to prove when others rig and/or cheat the system. Another reason is the heavy reliance on job references, which seem to these days be more important than actual work experience and meeting job qualifications. Get a hold of all of the dishonesty and restore actual merit in the merit system and you will have better, more qualified, reliable, knowledgeable and more efficient state employees. I am tired of having to work for "the kids" of former executives who do their absolute best to ensure their next of kin get out of doing actual work.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-27227

    The Red Tape that management needs to get through in order to improve processes is very tedious. Good ideas come out of Brainstorming committees, but it seems to get bogged down at the executive level at Headquarters. Although, management requires policies and procedures to be followed on a statewide level, local leadership needs to have more autonomy to customize policies at the local level.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26061

    I had both a valid real ID and CA DL. Upon time for renewal of these, I received 2 separate renewal notices which could be processed via mail BUT I wanted to combine these into one and was unable to do so without a DMV visit. I had to make a DMV appointment and go through the process of getting a real ID again by providing all the appropriate paperwork although I already had a valid real ID. Would like to see the option of 1. combining valid forms of identification 2. if offered renewal by mail for separate forms of IDs, these IDs should be able to be renewed via mail if combined 3. not needing to provide documentation again if there is a valid ID

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26080

    I feel the institution staff should mirror the HQ staff, work wise, pay wise, and classification wise. The institution staff are the boots on the ground and I feel have more complex cases and more interruptions, but we are in a lower classification which means we are compensated less, and we are not allowed to telework like HQ staff(which would allow us to focus and dive more into our cases without interruptions).

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-27346

    Before we can provide lasting solutions to houselessness, we have to address the immediate problem of housing the unhoused. I see sprawling self-storage businesses that contain hundreds of garage-like storage rooms. It seems to me that these could be converted inexpensively to provide temporary, secure housing for people living on the street. Once in these converted shelters, service providers could be contracted to provide healthcare and social services to the residents. The state should consider purchasing some of these facilities and converting them into shelters.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26444

    It’s so expensive to live in California. So many things, like licensure for example, are so much more affordable in other states. I am a licensed psychologist in California. I pay $850 to renew. I got licensed in Virginia for only $200. That’s just one example. The price of gas is another. It’s so much cheaper in other states. Car registration is another example.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-27146

    Better pay and getting paid every 2 weeks would be great!

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26999

    Contracts seem to be done without much public knowledge or oversight. There are significant pay gaps in contract positions vs state employee positions. It’s feels demoralizing to have contractors making three times the amount with better benefits. I no longer suggest that others work for the state. I also suggest that new grad work as contractors first since the state will not allow us to transfer into contractor roles. Also, the benefits are not keeping up with the rising costs of healthcare, which makes paychecks lower ever as pay gets higher.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26782

    I lack of onboard training and lack of supervisors knowing the roles of their staff. I think that each desk should have a desk manual and if each desk had a back up staff member who knows the desk work or is rotated to that desk work on a routine basis would assist when staff vacate the position and a new staff member is hired for training purposes.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26000

    With the use of Tables among the incarcerated population, I would reduce the amount of paper used and waisted. Make CDCR more digital. From incarcerated persons 22s and medical requests as an email to the person of request, to emails from the state employees to the incarcerated person notifying of changes. This allows for tracking and accountability on both ends and reduces the paper usage. we have become a digital society, and in part of rehabilitation, we can integrate the incarcerated persons into the digital eras as well. creating a dashboard/portal for communication between staff and population. Metrics can be used to track responses back and forth.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26017

    The underutilization of the pharmacists. Pharmacists can play a transformative role in California Correctional Health Care Services by expanding their clinical, operational, and educational impact. They can contribute more effectively through a Collaborative Drug Therapy Management: 1- Work closely with physicians and other healthcare providers to optimize medication regimens for chronic conditions like diabetes, hypertension, mental health disorders, and substance use disorders; 2- Patient Education: Provide direct counseling to incarcerated individuals on proper medication use, side effects, and adherence strategies, especially for complex therapies; 3- Protocol-Based Care: Use approved clinical protocols or collaborative practice agreements (CPAs) to manage drug therapy independently in certain cases, improving access and outcomes.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26980

    Not a primary problem, but my most recent thought: CDSS has janitors coming to our floor every day and emptying wastepaper baskets that have maybe one item in them (and, as well, waste time passing by empty cubicles several days of the week). Office janitorial work is still allocated as if we don't recycle or compost our waste (I noticed this excessive janitorial wastepaper basket effort pre-Covid), much less take into consideration hybrid schedules. If we merely asked employees not to put anything smelly in their trash (place it in the kitchen trash to be emptied daily), the janitors could empty the office wastewater baskets weekly. Or, as CalEPA does, each person is responsible for emptying their trash, as needed, in a central hallway receptacle and the janitors merely empty that one, as well as the separate receptacles in the kitchen (compost, recycling and landfill) on a daily basis.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-27284

    I think efficiency is a huge part of things for me. Being able to appropriately convey expectations in an efficient and effective manner is a challenge that I think we can all relate to in some aspect. Whether this means streamlining systems or revisiting process steps, I've long felt the impact of circuitous actions which, especially in repetitive tasks, limit the potential for more meaningful task completion, learning, and even have negative impact on employee engagement and morale. Proper education in evolving with technology and developing less complicated means to accomplish required tasks enables big changes to be handled in a more incremental manner, which, in turn, allows more adequate training and mitigates the inherent aversion to change by offering what appears more like help, rather than a potentially overwhelming or burdensome addition/change to one's workload or processes. This ultimately improves job satisfaction, thus improving morale and even reducing costs as work completion times not only reduce, but the ease and desire for employees to complete more work increases. Supplementing this with relative and engaging training invites staff to become more invested in not only the evolution of the process, but the overall objective(s) of the larger department/industry.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26380

    Roles of support staff working from home doesn't seem to make it to those of us with boots on the ground.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26380

    Staff should be designated on site to directly impact the support they were designed to do.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26452

    Downsizing State agencies. Combine like agencies and departments. For example. Cannabis could be merged with ABC. Stop creating new departments and agencies.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26283

    consistency in guidance

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26319

    This is specific to the California Model of incarceration. The California Model is not about money and it is not about modifications. The California Model is about mindset! Change the mindset of the leaders and, through the principle of Extreme Ownership, we can collectively change the mindset of the staff and the incarcerated. At this time, insufficient effort has been made to change the mindset of the policy makers and stakeholders at the top, so, as a result, we will never success on the front lines.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26442

    I'll admit I'm no expert in organizational governance, but it has always struck me how fragmented CA's agencies/departments are. I think it's important to have decision-making independence for some kinds of agencies e.g., it might not be a good idea to have a Commission regulate itself, but does the staff need to be as separated as they are today? Maybe it should be easier to work on projects or tasks across agencies so good ideas can spread more quickly and agencies can have a better understanding of what their partners do and how they do it. This might be more true for the departments and agencies working on building things or managing natural resources.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-26507

    I would address the inability to take actions towards micromanaging supervisors. I had a supervisor who was not maintaining confidentiality and was intentionallg bullying, lying and minimizing my skills. I complained to supervisors and they rather blamed me despite that they had no evidence against me but I had many examples of her behaviors along with other team members. I recommend there needs to be a more streamlined process to address bad supervisors based on teams feedback. Current methods are not used due to active retaliation.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-27036

    Deregulate permitting agency and increase enforcement and education. Trust the public will build according to standards by increasing public awareness to agency standards and enforcing when compliance is not met. Too much red tape is working against necessary projects to combat climate change and incorporate multi-benefit solutions. Work smarter not harder endorse hybrid work solutions like telework.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-27341

    1. Competing interests makes it difficult to prioritize resources. 2. Inertia and institutional thinking cause tremendous delays. 3. Hiring practices are slow and antiquated. A possible solution is to implement more practices of responsibility/accountability.

    September 26, 2025
  • Guest-25254

    This is a big question! A problem I would like to address is that bans on harmful practices, products or substances often are passed with enforcement beginning years later. These delays often seem too long to me, and when it comes to practices / products / substances that harm the environment and / or public health, to prioritize the need of industry to transition slowly is to de-prioritize public health and the environment. Things that are harmful, like dragnet fishing, or PFAS in dental floss, to give actual examples, should be banned as soon as possible, rather than as soon as is profitable. If it were up to me, I would have banned dragnet fishing immediately and PFAS in dental floss within 6 months. Both were passed with several years for implementation.

    September 25, 2025
  • Guest-26209

    Is there a State translation service available? If there is, it needs to be easier to find and if there isn't... well there should be one. I have been working on getting public safety signage translated and have had to scrounge around for native speakers. This seems like something that any agency that interfaces with the public would need access to.

    September 25, 2025
  • Guest-24873

    There is a huge range of income-qualified programs (IQP) and subsidies offered by the state. However, each one often has its own individual requirements. Centralizing coordination on streamlined/simplified IQP requirements would save a huge amount of work in terms of verification and avoiding having to replicate similar work across departments. Here's the vision someone who is qualified for a subsidized electricity rate may also be qualified for CalFresh. However, the requirements are not aligned, which means that these two programs cannot automatically "talk" to each other. Ideally, it would be good to have a universal crosswalk of IQPs so that if you sign up for one, you are offered access to all of the others at the same time. This might look like a central hub for accessing all social/welfare benefits run by the state where you fill out an initial intake survey (limited to 15 questions *max*) and then have all programs you qualify for presented to you for sign up, in such a way that you can just check a box and batch-enroll in multiple at once. Undoubtedly this would require some legislation to fully align, but doing the groundwork to understand all IQP requirements and set up the crosswalk is the first step. But that requires someone who can work across all departments that offer some kind of IQP.

    September 25, 2025
  • Guest-21434

    A more efficient way to spend small amounts of money. There are multiple ways for me to spend my budget, and some of them are quite quick and painless - so I know it's possible. But just to order pizza requires a whole process and huge packet of forms. The pizza place even has to sign a form certifying they aren't associated with Darfur. This makes sense to spend 20 million dollars. But a few hundred bucks for pizza? There should be an easier process for small purchases.

    September 25, 2025
  • Guest-26209

    Do we have access to a translation service? I have been trying to make our signage more understandable to more Californians, but have been having to scrounge for native speakers in various languages (Russian and Dari specifically).

    September 25, 2025
  • Guest-25986

    The overwhelming amount of repetitive paperwork. I can only speak for my department when it comes to solutions (or possible solutions) but my attempt to even suggest some options seem to hit brick walls.

    September 24, 2025
  • Guest-24595

    Wasted and/or discarded resources.

    September 24, 2025
  • Guest-26094

    One idea to make California’s government more effective and efficient: "LEAN" Reducing the number of steps to get the job done should take top priority for every department. Investing in training every employee in LEAN would be a starting point to transform how the State does business more efficiently and serve the public more effectively.

    September 24, 2025
  • Guest-26142

    Environmental compliance laws and policies exist because there is a history of resource degradation without regulations, and corresponding protective measures, like best management practices, have a proven track record of effectiveness. However, when environmental restoration projects are constrained by environmental regulations such that the project must reduce the scope (size, duration, etc.), or they significantly drive up the costs (e.g. engineering, compliance, planning) of said projects, the public funds (State and Federal) go less to the intended use, and more to the churn of bureaucracy. There should be more streamlining of statewide permits or more exemptions for restoration projects, and these should inherently recognize long-term benefits of restoration projects outweigh short-term impacts that follow standard BMPs.

    September 24, 2025
  • Guest-26675

    Too many managers. I don't understand why we need 4 levels of managers under the Executive Officer. With all of this management everyone is always blaming the level above them. If my department questions something new that is being implemented or we don't agree with it, our direct supervisor blames the person above them and so on and so forth. It's really discouraging and a waste of resources to have that many managers.

    September 24, 2025
  • Guest-26479

    I would like CDCR to look for management skills and abilities when hiring or promoting people to management positions. It seems the state tends to promote people into management roles who may be good workers or supervisors but lack management skills or training. A lot of the errors and roadblocks encountered by my peers and myself could be attributed to poor project planning or management principles. One glaring problem I routinely see is the lack, or even refusal, to have stakeholders involved with project planning. (Just a hint, stakeholders are almost never management employees.) I think looking down for answers, properly identifying stakeholders in projects, and management staff learning how to manage would greatly help California.

    September 23, 2025
  • Guest-25947

    Effectuating change feels impossible due to how long rule-making, procurement, and other processes take. We should consider removing some of the bureaucratic and unnecessary steps to these processes to allow them to be accomplished in a shorter amount of time.

    September 23, 2025
  • Guest-27333

    There should be blanket licenses for similar departments. For example, why aren't communications teams all using the same email client, or social media services, or editing and graphic design systems? The same goes for IT, data teams, etc. etc. The licenses are very expensive and many departments have the ability to invest in that. It's a cost savings idea and one that makes it seamless if/when a staff member transfers to another department or team.

    September 23, 2025
  • Guest-26101

    The functioning of FI$CAL is slow, clunky, and inefficient. When entering line items for POs the number of fields to complete is excessive, and when you move from one field to the next there is a lag. The system also seems to slow when during heavy use periods during the work day. Reducing the number of fields required to complete for each line item of a PO and improving the system's speed and ability to handle high number of users simultaneously would be helpful.

    September 23, 2025
  • Guest-26542

    Often improving efficiencies often end up increasing cost. Efficiency becomes a box checking exercise, not a true goal. Efficiencies should not just be about systems and processes, but about delivering better outcomes for the public at sustainable cost.

    September 23, 2025
  • Guest-26661

    Each agency has a Mission, Vision, and Goals. It makes us strive for a purpose. However, some of these goals need to be cross referenced. A goal for one agency maybe in direct conflict with another agencies goal. There are countless examples of this. Especially when it comes to Environmental. We (the taxpayers) want a healthy environment, but at what cost? We (the State Employee) want to do the best we can. However, typically, low level employees are creating processes that increase regulations, aren't customer friendly, and drive-up cost for everyone. They are not looking at the consequences of their decisions. In State government, "everyone has a boss". Just because we have Directors and other leadership with a goal, doesn't mean they have the authority to dictate regulations. Many of them make decisions on experience and "the way it's always been done", and that's wrong. They need to check the big picture. Sometimes decision makers require unnecessary actions and these cause issues for others. A great example are Governing boards, such as ARB. They have good intentions of cleaner air for CA, but at what cost? Should CA pay $10/gal when the rest of the world is creating global warming. We are not the problem. It seems like CA always has to lead. It would be fine if the taxpayers voted on those regulations, but not when decisions are made by unelected officials. They have too much power and are just doing what their boss wants them to do.

    September 23, 2025
  • Guest-23633

    All boards and bureaus under the Department of Consumer Affairs (DCA) share the same goal: to protect Californian consumers. I often see representatives from various DCA entities at community resource fairs, senior fairs, etc. Outreach teams from different agencies are constantly searching for these events and trying to join them. We pay registration fees, go back and forth with organizers to arrange everything, and travel to different locations. Why don’t we organize a DCA Consumer Protection Fair ourselves? As many DCA boards and bureaus as possible can join and set up a table/booth. Members of the public can attend and learn about all the resources available to help keep them safe, from how to find a trustworthy accountant to staying safe online, what to do if they were sold a bad car by a dealership, etc. A big issue facing us is that consumers simply don't know that our resources exist until we actively seek them out and inform them. An Annual Consumer Protection Fair would be a great event for the public and help bring together the work that the Department of Consumer Affairs does in an efficient and targeted way. We could host it as part of National Consumer Protection Week (March 1-7, 2026).

    September 23, 2025
  • Guest-26990

    We need to find a way to make State government more efficient. I’ve seen more and more rules, regulations, oversight, and impediments added over the course of my 20+ year career. We need to be allowed to do our job, yet it seems like at every turn there is a roadblock— from hiring, to purchasing, contracts, etc. All these regulations are not making us better, more honest, or more effective. We have to find a way to reduce the oversight and allow us to do the jobs we were hired to do— to benefit the public and gain their trust

    September 22, 2025
  • Guest-27307

    Modernize the civil service hiring process to be more equitable, including an examination of underpaid and unprotected non-civil service seasonal and part-time help. Address the strict, rigorous, confusing, selective, lengthy, maze of procedures that leads to possible civil service employment.

    September 22, 2025
  • Guest-26661

    Have the Agency do a cross check on their policies and procedures. Each policy references a law. Does it exist? Has it been modified? Are we over regulated? Many laws date back to the 19th century. It's time we double check our policies.

    September 22, 2025
  • Guest-26661

    The biggest problem in CA government is authority. Authority is going unchecked. I know several occasions where decision makers are overstepping their boundary. Even worse is when low level (say AGPA) is trying to do the best job they can, but don't realize the negative impact they are creating or the laws that they are breaking. For example, a AGPA needs data from another agency. They are blanketly ask for the information. The other agency, doing the best job they can do, says Ok and collects the data. However, collecting that information could have taken 80 hours, or require a "system" to be built to accommodate the request. When looking deeper into "the information", it was never required by law to collect the data. This is unnecessary waste. Times this by 1000's of request, and we're all working for each other, and not the people of CA. State employees are very hard workers and try their best. Sometimes, we need to step back and make sure we are doing what we should be doing, and not what we want to do. This wasteful work leads to wasted time spent, delays in the work that should be done, and selfish pride. Laws - we all have to abide by them. However, many laws have "sunset dates". it would be nice if the State of CA did a cross analysis of what policy was created for what law. if that law was changed, modified, or unsetted, then the policy needs to do the same. Laws aren't typically created from scratch; they take an existing law and totally rewrite it.

    September 22, 2025
  • Guest-26397

    Less paperwork for every day functions. For example, we have fuel pumps and paper sheets to track the fuel usage. It is subject to human error, and extra time balancing the sheets every time we make a fuel purchase. If the fuel pumps had electronic sign in for unit number, and PCA code option, we would be saving lots of headaches and potentially recover lots of money that otherwise gets lost from the emergency fund. if you had to punch in your unit number every time you filled up, through some kind of key pad, it would streamline that aspect of our department. Look in to a company called Link2Pump. Another note I don't understand why the process takes years for me to get a contractor to fix up my facility. Our building is from the late 60's, and If I ever want to get something done or fixed, the easiest option for me is to do it myself. I dont see why having the lowest bid contractor shouldn't be able to start work in a few months. Instead im told it takes 3 years. It is causing me more work in the long haul instead of letting a licensed contractor do all of the work.

    September 20, 2025
  • Guest-23149

    To improve efficiency and accountability, state government should pair top-down evaluations with structured bottom-up feedback. Currently, employees are reviewed by supervisors, but managers rarely receive input from the staff and programs they oversee. This creates a blind spot: how can managers become better leaders, improve efficiency, and address challenges if they never hear from the people most affected by their decisions? Once someone becomes a manager, the department’s instinct is often to protect them from criticism. While meant to support leadership, this practice can unintentionally foster waste, fraud, abuse, and inefficiencies. Without meaningful feedback, even well-intentioned managers may not know what needs to change. A balanced system would introduce safe, constructive ways for staff to share input—such as anonymous surveys or 360-degree reviews—while preserving fairness and professionalism. The goal is not blame, but growth: identifying strengths, addressing weaknesses, and building stronger teams. This feedback loop would improve trust, strengthen programs, and ensure managers are as accountable as the staff they supervise.

    September 19, 2025
  • Guest-26730

    Overlapping and sometimes duplicative permitting processes across different departments create frustration with the permitted community. Specifically, the California Department of Fish and Wildlife regulates substantial alterations to or diversions from streams. Similarly, the Regional Water Quality Control Boards regulate fill discharge to Waters of the State. The State Water Resources Control Board regulates water diversions. Sometimes all three departments are involved in permitting a single project, sometimes it is only two of three, or only one. But in the case where all three departments are involved, there are three completely separate and independent processes for submitting the project description and receiving state authorization (not even mentioning the local permitting requirements that are also typically involved). Combined permits may not be possible, but having a single hub for applying for state permits could be a start. Alternately, creating better mechanisms to collaborate across departments (maybe shared office spaces and shared virtual teams) would be extremely useful. Sharing resources across departments in general feels like a win for the state and the various departments doing similar work.

    September 19, 2025
  • Guest-23149

    People often blame CEQA for slowing fuel reduction projects, but the real barriers are limited capacity, planning inefficiencies, and overlapping regulations. Even when CEQA has been waived—such as the 45-day priority projects or the 2025 Secretary’s suspension—we still must comply with all other environmental laws. That often results in more paperwork and delays than if we had simply gone through CEQA. For example, CDFW frequently requires a take permit, which adds 90+ days to a project, making timely fuel reduction even harder. A more effective solution would be to create a 4(d) rule under the California Endangered Species Act (CESA), modeled after the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service’s approach under the federal ESA. These rules allow targeted exemptions or modifications to take prohibitions for threatened species, so beneficial projects like fuel reduction can move forward without lengthy permitting when impacts are minimal or positive. This is especially critical for California’s fire-adapted species, which often benefit from vegetation treatments. Currently, CDFW offers Memoranda of Understanding for take, but these are case-by-case, drafted after listing, and far less efficient. A 4(d) rule would instead be established at the time of listing by the Fish and Game Commission, ensuring consistency and streamlining the process. Where federal 4(d) rules already exist, the Commission could adopt them directly, avoiding duplication and aligning state and federal protections.

    September 19, 2025
  • Guest-26239

    I worked at California Department of Water Resources (DWR). The department depends on accurate, timely data to manage California’s water, yet many workflows remain manual, siloed, and slow. For example, the current processes for generating critical data—like ETaw used in water budget calculations—span multiple teams, take up to 12 weeks, and involve redundant steps such as manual file transfers, duplicated analysis, and outdated tools. These inefficiencies limit transparency, slow responses to urgent requests, and create inconsistencies across programs. I proposse Land and Water Use Cloud Pilot aims to address these bottlenecks by demonstrating how a modern, cloud-based data stack can streamline workflows. By automating repetitive tasks, integrating land use and water use analyses, and leveraging scalable platforms such as Google Earth Engine and Snowflake, the pilot will reduce turnaround times, improve collaboration, and ensure results are both accurate and transparent. This proof-of-concept will show how moving from fragmented, manual systems to an integrated cloud environment can cut processing times dramatically (e.g., reducing ETaw delivery from 12 weeks to 4), while laying the foundation for broader adoption of modern solutions across DWR. Ultimately, this approach strengthens our ability to conserve water, respond quickly to partners, and deliver high-value information to millions of Californians

    September 19, 2025
  • Guest-22880

    CALFIRE receives massive amounts of funding to manage/treat vegetation on State lands for fuel reduction/fire management purposes . They are experts in fire and fire fighting, but not experts in vegetation management. Public agencies that own and manage land (including State Parks) should receive the funding to manage/treat the vegetation on their lands. These non-CALFIRE agencies have the expertise to manage vegetation to meet fuel reduction goals while minimizing harm to natural resources and natural processes.

    September 19, 2025
  • Guest-26348

    We are having issues with staffing firefighting crews in CA; we should explore exclusive use fire crews as we do with Firefighting helicopters.

    September 19, 2025
  • Guest-27077

    Deep and miring bureaucracy. I know this goes with the territory, but it's really hard to take sometimes. I often feel like I'm wading through molasses trying to do BASIC parts of my job. This manifests in too many ways to count, but a couple issues are top of mind: 1) The regulatory environment limiting restoration-- hell, even basic ongoing maintenance-- is stifling work when the need for urgent action is so high. The "cutting the green tape" initiative was good, but didn't really fix structural issues. For example, replacing a minor culvert under a remote road in the middle of summer when it's bone dry still costs $10,000 in labor before ground is even broken. Surely there could be a streamlined (no pun intended) process for this-- perhaps a straightforward checklist or rubric to determine if there are major concerns or perhaps statutory exemptions for which you qualify. 2) Basic tasks like purchasing and timesheets are so complicated that we literally need a TEAM of administrative staff to handle navigating the esoteric, bureaucratic backwaters of Sacramento. Even with their help, it's frustrating, time-consuming, and hilariously dysfunctional. This is don't have solutions for. Ours is a large and complicated state. I've seen too many failed attempts at systems integration to believe there's a single fix for this. It may not be possible to put humpty-dumpty back together again.

    September 19, 2025
  • Guest-26575

    Working in a decentralized department, I believe greater standardization and improved tools for managing budgets, purchasing, and routine statewide assignments would strengthen efficiency and consistency. Providing shared systems and clear processes would reduce the need for individuals to create their own methods, improve program management, and ensure more reliable data reporting back to headquarters. Department has started implementing better tools and more standardization in Human Resources program, but other programs still lagging behind - budgeting, accounting, procurement, contracting asset management, leadership training and much more.

    September 19, 2025
  • Guest-26414

    The primary issue I have experienced is ineffective management. Required oversight of hiring for management positions and required continuous management training would go a long way in reducing staff turn over for key field and office positions actually doing the important work. Once a manager is in place, they can promote and hire people without any accountability for hiring the most qualified people. It is also nearly impossible to get a manager demoted or removed from their position, even if they are unqualified and promote a toxic work culture.

    September 19, 2025
  • Guest-26575

    In a decentralized department, stronger program management and tracking systems, greater process standardization, and more comprehensive leadership training (beyond online courses or webinars) would add significant value. Reducing the expectation for employees to be ‘jack of all trades’ would also help ensure that backlog work is addressed more effectively and that staff can focus on their core responsibilities.

    September 19, 2025
  • Guest-22157

    "bulk" training procurement by state department. For example, if I want to take a HAZWOPER course as a staff at an agency under CNRA, I have to go out and individually procure the training through my departments procurement protocol, which is slow and cumbersome. If CNRA could contract and offer trainings that a large percentage of staff may be required to take, staff would simply have to sign up through some interface, rather than every staff person or department having to individually procure the training. Due to my workload and the cumbersome procurement process, I have avoided taking trainings that will advance my ability to support the state in emergency situations, in this case.

    September 19, 2025
  • Guest-26801

    Revising the classification system for the modern work force. Many of the classifications are outdated or don't include current roles. It can be challenging to recruit talent without more specific classifications.

    September 18, 2025
  • Guest-24885

    There is a lack of cooperation between agencies that causes frustration on all sides. And there are not clear set guidelines on what procedure is, so we all have to navigate personal preferences with each person we need to work with. It becomes frustrating when dealing with items that need approval from other agencies. Items will sit for weeks just waiting for a waiver from them. It makes completing our jobs in a timely manner impossible.

    September 18, 2025
  • Guest-25983

    As a Californian state employee with expertise in enterprise architecture, one primary problem I would address is the slow execution of projects due to repetitive and lengthy approval processes. A key improvement would be to implement a "Smart Approval Framework" that leverages a centralized repository of previously approved use cases. This system would use metadata tagging and AI-driven similarity analysis to identify when a new request closely matches a previously approved one. If a match is found, the system could auto-recommend fast-track approval, reducing redundant reviews. This would not only accelerate execution but also promote consistency, reduce workload on review boards, and encourage reuse of proven solutions—ultimately driving more agile and responsive public service delivery.

    September 18, 2025
  • Guest-21907

    As a manager with many years of experience, the first problem I would address is hiring. People are our biggest asset. The process has improved over the years with online exams but there is still a long way to go. It's difficult to understand how to get a job with the state. Many people apply for positions that they are not eligible for. This takes HR and the hiring managers time to weed through applications. Fortunately, my department now has a recruitment team to help with the process. Maybe each department should have one or share one.

    September 17, 2025
  • Guest-25429

    In every position I have been, the 998 timesheet is an incredibly un-user-friendly document. This is a document that we all must use regularly, and it is so inefficient in its current condition. As I am not an accounting or Excel expert, my only idea to solve this is to RE-DO it so it is as clean and simple as possible. A bit of work on the front end would decrease enormous time on the backend.

    September 17, 2025
  • Guest-24885

    Communication between departments can lag and often create barriers to completing work in a timely manner. There is also a lack of cross training on what is necessary to complete tasks as proper training tools have not been created for newer employees.

    September 17, 2025
  • Guest-24885

    1. The program Fi$Cal struggles to operate under large loads. It needs better infrastructure support to the amount of users that operate this program. 2. Increase the size of the IT force throughout the state. With so much of our jobs requiring the use of computers, there are too few IT specialists to manage the needs of everyday users. 3. Allow for POs to be pulled back from dispatch to correct errors that were not caught right away. 4. Create a blanket waiver for over per diem on hotels during emergency responses for incident personnel.

    September 17, 2025
  • Guest-24021

    Would like to see more long-term entry level positions or job extensions. It's difficult to promote and grow within my position when I have a limited term.

    September 17, 2025
  • Guest-25815

    Less Paper: I would love to be on a team where any office who is using paper to officially transmit records is given an efficiency audit where the team determines if there is a better way to do the data transfer. For example, I have seen entire jobs where one staff completes a piece of paper to give to another staff who then retypes the paper into a system. The entire job consists of hand entering data already completed by staff from the same department. This is true for the public giving the State data too. Today in my public citizen life I had to complete a paper to a state agency I know will be entered into an online portal. I assume that data is being hand read and entered based on my experience of 15 years and 4 agencies.

    September 17, 2025
  • Guest-25722

    I haven’t been a state employee long, but I work with a lot of people that have so I’m basing this on coworkers’ explanations/comments along with what I’ve seen so far. I would say address the repetitiveness/excess/disconnection. How many departments provide the same service to the public/have the same or similar program? Does that need to be provided in multiple departments? Do those departments work together or at least know of the other programs and departments? Are the processes as efficient and streamlined as possible? As mentioned in other comments, the purchasing process for example. Do we really need 3 bids for all purchases? Is that an outdated requirement? There seems to be a lot of oversight/"red tape" in some areas, yet huge contracts can be awarded with little to no oversight of the program/department/funding disbursements after.

    September 16, 2025
  • Guest-25593

    One issue I have consistently encountered is the challenge of working with state procured contractors, especially in programs that involve direct public service like case management. In my experience, onboarding and training contractors can be time consuming, and often their staff do not fully understand program policies or the scope of work. This can lead to inconsistent messaging and confusion for the Californians we serve. Many of these vendors are also based out of state and may not fully grasp the unique needs of our communities. I believe we should explore bringing more of this work in house. For example, having state employees handle case management could improve consistency, accountability, and transparency. It would also allow us to better control outcomes, reduce excessive billing, and build internal capacity by developing staff skill sets. While contractors can play a role, I think a more balanced approach with a stronger in-house presence would ultimately lead to better service delivery and public trust.

    September 16, 2025
  • Guest-22479

    Many state employees in lower-wage classifications struggle with housing costs, and some are even homeless while serving Californians every day. A state-facilitated housing match program could safely connect Californians who have rooms or rentals with civil servants who need affordable housing, with the state verifying employment (with consent) to protect both sides. This creates a win-win: Californians gain reliable tenants, and state workers gain stable housing so they can continue serving our communities.

    September 16, 2025
  • Guest-21946

    Problems: - Lack of vision and commitment to achievement, growth, and excellence. This saps motivation and can lead to a public service workforce that's just "going through the motions" to get the paycheck / no longer spurred by desire to serve the public. - Hierarchical org structure that minimizes the ideas and opinions of rank-and-file employees. - There are few "senior staff," non-management positions. This results in many people being promoted to manager roles that they're either unprepared or not suited for. Solutions: - Opportunities for people at all levels to participate in developing organizational vision and goals. - Foster a culture where staff are encouraged to share ideas even if/when they're contradictory to "direction" from above. - Creation of more senior staff positions where people can move up in agencies without becoming managers.

    September 16, 2025
  • Guest-23088

    Active outreach to private industry for recruitment and the potential for a second career in government would benefit may state agencies and departments.

    September 15, 2025
  • Guest-23088

    State agencies and Departments should focus more on recruiting industry subject matter experts in their respective fields of oversight and regulation. It is all too common for staff to be disinterested or lack knowledge of industries they regulate.

    September 15, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21302

    The cost of maintaining office buildings and the cost to the state worker with traveling from home to work in office. This would be solved with allowing workers to again work from home full time. Downtown buildings should be converted to affordable housing which could in turn help sustain downtown business. The people who have worked from home can in turn continue to support their local businesses.

    September 15, 2025
  • Guest-26479

    I think California is a great state to work for and I believe that my department truly wants to improve. I work for CDCR and I have observed some areas where improvements can be made. From my anecdotal observations, we have a poor hiring process for managers and tend to promote people into management roles without the skills, knowledge or abilities to be a manager. I noticed we promote those who may have good supervisory skills into management roles, but not necessarily management skills. I would prefer CDCR to look outside the department to staff management roles. We currently tend to have an unofficial requirement to come "up the ranks" to staff management positions when getting someone "off the streets" may be of better value as a manager. Supervisors and staff must be technical experts; managers need to manage. Additionally, we tend to undervalue education in this department. I believe having a relevant education should be strongly considered for promotion. Many studies have proven the value of education, and I never understood why CDCR's hiring practices put no value in having an education. If I were able to make the decision, I would require a BS degree and experience to be interviewed for a management position. I am not implying all of CDCR's managers are horrible people, but I have observed a lack of critical thinking and poor management skills with many of them.

    September 15, 2025
  • Guest-23870

    Coming from the military into the state, I have noticed a lack of proper training and materials to aid in job support, roles and responsibilities, as well as understanding the state rules/policies. When I met with HR, I explained being new to state and was handed a document with links and was told to go research with no explanation. For my job, I was told it takes about 1-2 years to grasp even the lingo in our unit, let alone how we operate. There were no procedure logs or training as there isn't any turnover for the last employee to show a new one what it was they did. The structure seems a bit unorganized and the chain of command doesn't seem to be followed.

    September 15, 2025
  • Guest-23524

    I agree that hands-on training needs to be much better. I have moved around in the same Dept. at the same level now for 20 years and I find it difficult to understand why there isn't consistency from one are to another. I usually have to spend a lot of time trying to put things in order in order to find what I need, find what others need, find the processes to do the job, find the resources to do the job, all of which is time consuming and shows a lack of consistency. I agree with the person below, the training, not the annual training we all get, but the hands-on training we get is poor. I get the impression that the supervisor doesn't really know what the person in the position does. Instead of help, it's criticism and sometimes yelling at a person. I come from years of private sector work and the environment between state and private is so different. I'd like to see more cooperation and less dictatorship and egotism. We all have a job to do, and we do it better when we cooperate instead of arguing or putting each other down. I'd like to see consistency from one area to another within the Dept. There was an attempt to make all institutions the same and that is a great idea, but it didn't really happen like it was intended to. I still see a lot of confusion and inconsistency which makes it difficult to get things done. I would like to see a real retention schedule that gets reviewed by a committee and updated; it surely helps when getting audited.

    September 15, 2025
  • Guest-25979

    As a project manager, I want to see the state deliver projects more quickly, enabling earlier resolution of issues, faster realization of community benefits, and a quicker path to innovation. To address this, we can reform our current processes that create bottlenecks or unnecessarily extend project lifecycles.

    September 13, 2025
  • Guest-25387

    I'm finding that the leadership within my organization hires management that does not know the agency, its goals, and processes. We, as staff, have to train our managers.

    September 12, 2025
    • Guest-25841

      This is so true, I can't even count how many times I've been denied a promotional position to an individual outside of the organization, only to be told by the hiring manager to train the new person when they start.

  • Guest-26303

    The state's hiring process is difficult to navigate and may prevent candidates from applying. It also takes too long. Qualified candidates may be hired elsewhere before an agency is allowed to make an offer.

    September 12, 2025
  • Guest-24630

    Our state prison system - the California model is a great foundation but many of the prisons lack basic services for rehabilitation (groups, etc.). This only happens by scaling up staffing. Also, homelessness - we need a lot of affordable housing

    September 11, 2025
  • Guest-25692

    The public perception of state service. It needs a glow up. Right now the sentiment feels like we're not effective or efficient - perhaps because that is partially true - but it does not invite others into state service like other entities that define their place in the world. Look at Meta's recruitment page, or Apple's, or Google's. It's a whole different feel. The psychology that's applied to tailor that feel is wholly different, hits different, and carries a different message. Just a step above public view of the DMV.

    September 11, 2025
  • Guest-23095

    I agree with other comments about training could be better and that some of our platforms and programs are outdated. I also agree with "too many cooks in the kitchen" even for small tasks it seems to have to go through multiple levels of management, which seems like it uses too many resources and takes too much time.

    September 11, 2025
  • Guest-25325

    The State of California has many Agencies that do the same work, but in a different way. The Governor has talked about consolidating some of those departments, but that's not really being done according to the mandate. The main issue is that those that are in charge of making changes have a big ego that gets on the way of serving California effectively. For example, in the homeless issue, sometimes it takes 3 to 4 months for a homeless to be referred to an apartment and receive all the medical, mental and housing needed at that time sometimes it's too late.

    September 11, 2025
  • Guest-22068

    Executive Leaders/Managers should be subject to 360-like, anonymous reviews and required to implement changes proposed in those reviews that could result in better processes and procedures. A simple thing like having less meetings (because so many are unnecessary and add to the bureaucracy), or delegating responsibilities more effectively, or identifying and establishing SOPs to resolve gaps. Again, this review should be required. Even well-intended leaders/managers can be blind to their short-comings or lack self-awareness or act defensively when given critical feedback.

    September 11, 2025
  • Guest-22068

    There should be an agency liaison for every state agency and every board, bureau, or commission within that agency, who can be relied on to facilitate information or communication between agencies. Our office has overlapping responsibilities with other agencies, but we often hit a wall trying to find the appropriate person to assist with an issue. It's a nightmare.

    September 11, 2025
  • Guest-25904

    Document/form management is a statewide challenge that could more effectively be coordinated centrally. In the previous century, everything went through the Office of State Publishing, and the State Library had catalogued copies of everything that was published, but now each agency has its own content management system and publishes to the web with no centralized coordination. Retrieval largely depends on private 3rd party services. Index each web address and tag with metatext; leverage librarians' expertise to structure a retrieval system facilitating public access. Track revision dates, ownership, and language translations within the same platform. Use AI document translation with a common lexicon, have a disclaimer and feedback mechanism to prioritize review (e.g. vital docs), ID bad translations, recommend improvements. Also flag items past a certain revise date. At present we have active standard forms owned by agencies that no longer exist and historic documents posted that pop up when searching for current policy. It is not always clear what is current and what is outdated. Systems like WorldCat and GoogleBooks provide models, and there could also be a concierge function for the public to punch in what they need and receive instructions along with any necessary forms and guidelines (perhaps compiled by AI and verified by human staff, moving to spot checks as effectiveness of the tech is demonstrated).

    September 10, 2025
  • Guest-21761

    As a state employee, the primary problem I would address is to promote employees that really do the work on the daily basis. unfair promotions in those state departments. Those are doing the real work are left aside.

    September 10, 2025
  • Guest-24086

    Increased efficiency in State procurement for information technology needs. The two control agencies (CDT and DGS) need to simplify and streamline IT goods and services approaches. Many of these are antiquated. CDT could provide cost savings by being the front of many basic IT goods and services procurements, then leverage this out to state entities. Also, although the focus on small business is important, for complex IT goods and services, most small businesses need additional support from DGS to develop relationships with vendors, skill sets, and expertise to support technical procurements. DGS could rely on technical staff to develop enterprise and mandatory agreements (with CDT) to reduce overall risk. Another area is GenAI and AI procurements. We need to get faster on understanding risks and benefits. Reliance on private data centers, as well as the global impact of GenAI power consumption, need to be examined.

    September 10, 2025
  • Guest-23004

    Yesterday I attended California's 175th Birthday Block Party. I thought it was an awesome idea for the Capitol to host that type of event for the community. It would be great if we could continue the tradition and invite state employees along with the public with more advance notice/communication. It would be a fun way for state employees to network with each other and the public as well as promote all of the great services/hard work we provide. I think it is a great way to boost morale and pride working for the state. It would also be nice to have a speaker or a special guest come out as well to showcase California's talent!

    September 10, 2025
  • Guest-24389

    CEQA reform. Too many good-for-the-environment projects are stopped due to CEQA. This needs to start with an information campaign, because it sounds like a bad idea initially. Too much "bean-counting". Yes, it is good to people and the state accountable, but when the process of holding-people-accountable uses up more time and money then that actual project, it is inefficient and wasteful. There should be accountability on the bean-counters themselves! The hiring process is biased when questions are limited and have to be pre-approved. The current process stifles follow-up questions, and this is inherently unfair to people who speak english as a 2nd language, or who come from a culture where that is more demure and respectful. The loudest people who overshare and not your best candidates, but speaking for too long after each question is an effective interview strategy (and it shouldn't be).

    September 10, 2025
