  • Guest-22055

    PR. There is such a deep misunderstanding of why working in the public sector must be different than working in the private sector. All people hear about are the slow wheels of bureaucracy and government waste, not realizing that the design is purposeful. Obviously there ways to make things more efficient, like digitizing forms, modernizing ways to make payments, etc. However, my experience is that most agencies are working on these issues. Just look at the DMV, or being able to pay a parking ticket on your phone, amazing! However, due to the nature of our work, sometimes it's important to work slowly and methodically, with standard procedures. When we "move fast and break things" or suffer from burnout trying to "optimize" our workday into hyper-efficiency, those things effect people's lives very deeply and if we can be thoughtful, we can make them better than the original concept. When we do that, it always pays us back, fiscally as well as socially. Because the public is so focused on waste, instead of hiring internally, we hire contractors, and instead of hiring the best contractor, we have to hire the one with the lowest bid. This creates real waste that hurts the tax payers and frustration of employees. There's a reason why most private tech companies crash, and that's no way to run a government.

    September 10, 2025
  • Guest-24512

    Environmental and financial stewardship. Our Sacramento area freeways are crumbling under the weight of too many vehicles generally and specifically during business hours. Invest in urban public transportation. Locate State offices near transit hubs. Stagger State worker schedules to minimize our impact in congested areas.

    September 10, 2025
  • Guest-22480

    Waste. Regarding departments I've worked in and in talking with at least a half dozen other departments and heads of departments it seems a common theme. I was picking up something inter-office today and heard finger pointing on a department meeting that could be avoided with simple process improvement protocols. There's a lot of wasted time on meetings that have no clear goals/agenda/template/time constraints, redundancy in process due to lack of process awareness and documentation, no standardized document management protocols, no clear mission of waste reduction, hierarchical structures are preached rather than going direct to a solution which wastes enormous time and hinders solution iteration/revision/implementation, not offloading to contractors the busywork related to their contract housekeeping, etc. Lean culture needs to be implemented asap with everyone trained in Lean methodologies so the mission is clear and the Gemba is understood to be open and the org chart flat for these purposes. Many don't want to rock the boat but it seems like the dirty little secret of water cooler talk. Lean initiatives would save the taxpayer millions each year if done well.

    September 09, 2025
  • Guest-24089

    I am newer to the state, as are some of the commenters below, and I have to say I wholeheartedly agree with there needing to be more direction and guidance. We know that some people learn and work differently (it is standard practice to identify our "types" when taking entry community college courses) and that should be applied in the workplace. The bureaucratic comments may be to blame for a lot of the unwillingness to step outside of our role to assist a coworker who asks for help or something like that. I have always been like "we are on the same team," and will help any way i can but quickly learned that others are not like minded. Only in my state jobs though. I think rank-and-file employees have first hand experience and are kept far from the decisions about their own roles and what processes should be changed to save money.

    September 09, 2025
  • Guest-25952

    There is too much waste, redundancy and inefficiency. A very visible example is managing the California coastline. There are at least 6 agencies in the mix which include: 1. California Coastal Commission 2. California State Lands Commission 3. California Coastal Conservancy 4. California Department of Parks and Recreation 5. California Department of Fish and Wildlife 6. California Ocean Protection Council (OPC) Additional government organizations with narrower or supporting roles include: • San Francisco Bay Conservation and Development Commission • California Environmental Protection Agency Board • Regional Water Quality Control Boards I suspect that there are many more that I am not aware of.

    September 09, 2025
  • Guest-21397

    Lack of efficiencies and funding for efficiencies. It would be nice if we had a modern, centralized system for reporting and tracking that would cut out the back and forth that slows things down

    September 09, 2025
  • Guest-26280

    I see that there is a huge waste in spending with the ISUDT program within CDCR. The counselors that are hired are only required to run their counseling classes Mondays, Wednesdays and Fridays. Tuesdays and Thursdays they do not hold classes so they can "write case notes." However, you can see these counselors using their time to take breaks, walk around the institution for exercise. I urge the state to evaluate the data and see if this program is genuinely cutting down on the substance abuse problem amongst the inmate population. The funds for these programs would significantly cut expenditures for CDCR.

    September 09, 2025
  • Guest-27384

    Some of the laws and procedures that have been implemented with the intent to fight corruption and waste have been counterproductive and have in fact increased waste. This is a complicated subject that is beyond the scope of a simple comment and will require involvement with the legislative process.

    September 09, 2025
  • Guest-22709

    Lack of effective communication and transparency from management to staff and vice versa.

    September 09, 2025
  • Guest-26584

    As a former Federal Employee, and current state employee, I think there is a gap when it comes to holding employees and leadership accountable to their responsibilities. While there are a ton of dedicated and competent employees, there are also those that utilize the protections afforded to public employees and those in unions to not meet even minimum potential. To solve this problem, I think we should ensure that employees and supervisors are being periodically checked in on. I have had three roles within the state government since 2021 and have never had a performance review or worked with a supervisor on a plan for trainings or moving forward in my career. I also think supervisors should undergo a 720 degree review - where they are appraised by their supervisors, employees, and themselves.

    September 09, 2025
  • Guest-22932

    Lack of training in all staff, including management. Abuse of time because documentation on staff takes years, and years, and is a lot of work. Promotion of incompetent staff, because they are great talkers. And because their opf is clean due to the fact that managers do not want to be the bad person.

    September 09, 2025
  • Guest-23016

    Something I've noticed from working for several years in local and state government is that living in a constantly changing digital world, we need updates to some of the software we use every day. For example, when using our system's database, we should be able to customize and refine searches with filters, run reports that show only the data we need, save/edit groups of favorite sections when we cover other desk areas, etc. I believe there is a need for a tech or IT committee composed of one person(s) in charge of collecting ideas from his/her department(s) and discussing strategies with the IT department in meetings. This could lead to discussion about how to optimize or implement more efficient and effective use of software, websites, and useful settings. Even further, this could result in more communication and transparency from IT in the form of occasional trainings for those interested in solving their tech needs.

    September 08, 2025
  • Guest-25660

    California’s government must recommit to a centralized IT strategy—one that prioritizes shared services, specialized expertise, and fiscal responsibility. The current decentralized approach, where individual departments manage their own IT infrastructure, is inefficient, costly, and misaligned with the state’s legislative intent. Many state agencies were created to fulfill specific missions—public health, transportation, education, and more. Yet today, these departments are investing significant time, energy, and capital into building and maintaining their own IT systems. This diverts focus from their core responsibilities and leads to duplication of effort, fragmented infrastructure, and inconsistent standards. The California Department of Technology (CDT) was established to solve this problem. Assembly Bill 2408 and the resulting Government Code provisions laid the foundation for a centralized IT model—one that consolidates services, reduces redundancy, and enables departments to focus on their primary missions while leveraging CDT’s expertise. Despite this mandate, many departments continue to operate isolated IT silos. This not only undermines the intent of AB 2408, but also wastes taxpayer dollars and slows statewide progress. The result: overlapping systems, inconsistent security protocols, and missed opportunities for innovation.

    September 08, 2025
  • Guest-25071

    Full time telework solves so many problems. Many private leases can be cancelled to save millions for the State. For staff that still must come into the office for a legitimate in person requirement (processing mail, etc), multiple departments can consolidate into existing State owned buildings. The Franchise Tax Board campus would be a great place to consolidate these staff. There are multiple buildings to house different departments with free parking. Staff that get to telework 100% would be much happier and more productive at work and at home. It was proven to work during the pandemic. There was never a problem with full time telework and it didn't need to go away. Staff in positions that can't telework would have something to strive for in future positions. The State government would be able to obtain the best talent away from the private sector and offer a new incentive to work for the government. Taxpayers would be happy to see the cost savings. I suppose the only people unhappy with full time telework would be urban business owners. It is said that urban areas hurt when people telework. But there are winners and losers in everything in life. For every downtown business owner that is unhappy, there is a business owner in the suburbs that is getting more business. Let people live and work where they want to. Less commuting is less polution and traffic, which also benefits everyone.

    September 08, 2025
  • Guest-21673

    A primary problem I would address is understaffing. At the district where I work, we have been chronically understaffed for as long as I've worked here and long before I started. Presently, we are short two supervisory managers that are needed to help direct key program areas and many full time positions in our district remain unfilled. At the same time, we are told that we need to provide the same, if not higher level of service to the public. This leads to existing staff being worn beyond thin working multiple jobs and duty statements that do not accurately reflect their workload. The situation just feels like a crash happening in slow motion with me as a rank-and-file watching it happen with little that I can do about it. If I had a magic wand that could easily change things, I would focus on ramping up hiring and retention. I understand there are budgetary considerations, but it is not possible to recruit and retain long term employees if our district does not have any growth opportunities for seasonal staffing. An overreliance on seasonal employees seems to be the crutch that my department relies on, forcing interested seasonals to endure years of attrition and competition before landing a permanent position. By retaining more long term employees, they will have a better chance of learning how state systems work and managing them efficiently and effectively. Longer term, this will be more sustainable than relying/spending on technology centered "efficiency" campaigns.

    September 08, 2025
  • Guest-25948

    I've been a civil servant for over a decade, and having come from the private sector, the biggest improvement that we can make is removing unmotivated staff and sub-par performers. Managers need to jump through far too many hoops for progressive discipline with potential legal backlash, and even that doesn't provide sustainable results for improving staff productivity. We need the ability to terminate staff that continuously fail to meet the expectations of their duties. For example, if there are at least three documented conversations about the same issue, managers should be able to move forward with termination instead of issuing a Letter of Instruction (LOI). The truth is that a lot of managers are hesitant to issue LOIs because these can hinder that staff's ability to leave their current role for another position. This means that the problematic staff won't be moving anywhere and will continue to be a problem in the current unit. Staff like this bring down morale and create more work for others on their teams. This inefficiency is costly and fiscally irresponsible to State funding, and hence, to California taxpayers.

    September 08, 2025
  • Guest-25676

    I would love to bridge the cap between being about to Telework and keep loyalty and culture. While I am old school and love the in-person culture, I am so much more productive when I telework (most are). We need to find a way to balance it. Keep culture by improving TW communication tools. Can't wait for the Star Trek days where we can join meetings by visually adding our image into a holodeck and it will feel like we are meeting in person. 🤣

    September 05, 2025
  • Guest-25714

    I believe one of the biggest issues in most of the departments of state employment is the low morale. Of course, it may vary in the workplace and institution, but I believe it impacts directly the efficiency of our work. Low morale could be the cause of a variety of reasons and would be hard to condense it to a specific situation. Maybe if there was a department which would monitor the level of morale in problem areas, it would help us want to go the extra mile in our work areas. if we really felt appreciated and that our work is making a difference.

    September 05, 2025
  • Guest-21913

    Thin out middle management.

    September 05, 2025
  • Guest-25043

    I've been with the same department for 11 years, before that, I served in the military for 25 years. The primary problem I see has been the use (or lack of) technology. There are so many things that would be more efficient if technology was utilized. We manage millions on excel spreadsheets, which I find unbelievable and a risk. When new technology is procured, the IT branch decides what to turn on or not and it's one thing at a time. For example, we have two IT units, one oversees Outlook and allows us to use CoPilot, but the unit that oversees Teams, won't allow us to use CoPilot. It makes no sense. I wish I had a solution to recommend.

    September 05, 2025
  • Guest-25694

    I would address is the breakdown in communication, specifically how long it takes to report and resolve operational problems. In my experience, communication often functions as a buffer that insulates leadership from the daily realities faced by frontline staff. When issues arise, responses are frequently delayed due to layers of approval, bureaucratic hurdles, or vague assurances that “it’s being looked into.” This delay not only prolongs the problem but can exacerbate it to the point of creating liability or safety risks. Unfortunately, when these issues escalate, leadership may downplay their prior awareness, further eroding trust and morale. To solve this, I would advocate for a transparent, time-bound communication protocol that includes: • A tiered reporting system that tracks issues from initial submission to resolution, with clear accountability at each level. While some systems are already in place the emphasis should be on ensuring managers cannot minimize their role when issues persist. • Mandatory feedback loops where leadership must acknowledge receipt and provide status updates within a set timeframe. • Consistent process audits to identify bottlenecks and recurring delays, followed by actionable reforms. • And most importantly, a culture shift that values timely action over bureaucratic deflection, recognizing that unresolved issues create stress, inefficiency, and risk for those doing the work. This should be done while respecting existing labor rights.

    September 05, 2025
  • Guest-24658

    Waste is a HUGE problem. There should be independent 3rd party audits of state agencies and programs. Release these audit findings publicly for accountability and the pressure to act. We should increase public transparency and accountability. Use an open data dashboard to show where our tax dollars go. Allow citizens to track state spending in real time. We should engage our citizens in participatory budgeting in certain areas like locally. Let citizens propose and vote on local budget priorities. This builds trust, sheds a light on new ideas and adds a check on wasteful spending.

    September 05, 2025
  • Guest-25621

    Telework solves so many problems and I would like to see it be made permanent rather than live with the constant threat of it being taken away. What problems does it solve? It reduces state expenses, improves morale, makes state hiring more competitive, helps the environment, helps families, provides a highly desirable work-life balance, streamlines communication amongst coworkers, fosters innovation and creative thinking... I could go on and on. I can think of many problems I would like to address but telework is above them all and serves as a solution to most of them.

    September 05, 2025
  • Guest-23263

    Within our state workforce, stress, divorce, marital issues, money problems are present and affect employees work perform, attitudes and relationships on and off duty. Police officers, firefighters, EMS workers often commit suicide as a result of the issues mentioned above. My solution to help state workers reduce or remove stress, divorce, marital issues, money problems is to provide EveryDollar as a benefit to all state employees. EveryDollar is a highly effective tool that focuses on controlling personal finances which then carries over into all other aspects of life, resulting in tremendous growth in confidence, awareness, financial freedom, relationships, mental health, and much more.

    September 05, 2025
  • Guest-23125

    Convoluted processing of everything (contracts, grants, hiring, purchasing). Instead of making it a way to ensure funds are being used correctly, it results in tons of wasted time and money.

    September 04, 2025
  • Guest-24982

    In the private sector, there are roles (IT Business Analyst) that sit between IT and the business that help identify opportunities to advance the mission via improved technology tools and/or processes, and serve to translate the business needs to IT and the technology constraints/opportunities to the business. This role has been absent in my government organizations, and as a result I see a reactive IT force and outdated tools serving the public. Agencies/departments should have such a role to proactively identify opportunities to better meet the mission/serve the public via technology tools; coordinate implementation, drive efficiency, overcome silos, and save on costs. One example: CNRA purchased a GovDelivery contract and negotiated so that all departments could use it - great! But when DPR (Parks) communications adopted it, they did not identify other needs within DPR which was using at least 4 different email services, and they didn't set it up well. The public was signing up for GovDelivery mailing lists that were never used, and some that were being used (i.e. State Parks Commission comms) were still being managed in an Excel spreadsheet list copied into Outlook (yikes). It was poorly implemented and misused because there was no role responsible for identifying the department-wide needs, developing an implementation plan, executing the implementation, training users, and staffing a department-wide administrator to manage the product after launch.

    September 04, 2025
  • Guest-24982

    Specifically in Parks, I don't think anyone should be able to become a Chief in HQ unless they have worked a minimum of 1 year in the field. This concept probably applies to many other organizations that have large field operations.

    September 04, 2025
  • Guest-25809

    PROBLEM There is no real-time visual summary of statewide sources of funds (revenues) and uses of funds (money spent on programs and services). The absence of real-time data on revenues and expenses may make it challenging for lawmakers, control agencies and constituents to manage/adjust their budgets based on the latest info. SOLUTION Create an online visual tool with real-time data of sources of funds (from CDTFA, FTB, etc.) and uses of funds (to the General Fund, special funds, and locals). This would enable lawmakers, control agencies and the public to have a better understanding of the current financial state of revenues and expenses overall as well as to individual programs. A real-time (perhaps updated weekly) tool should also enable the State to better forecast revenues, expenses and surplus/shortfalls.

    September 04, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-25794

    The states contracting methods and purchasing are too bureaucratic ,are difficult to navigate, and are too time consuming. Make them more efficient by streamlining the process and allowing public works and service master contracts to be awarded. Once that is done time efficient work orders can be implemented quickly and efficiently.

    September 04, 2025
  • Guest-22601

    I am particularly concerned about the financing of Public Health services and programs in California. Local Health Jurisdictions (LHJs) rely heavily on grant money allocated by the CDC or the State. Most of these grants appear to be formula-based, while some are competitive. Problems: 1. Funding public health by grants is not sustainable due to factors such as the end of grants and new policies. 2. Allocations are based on factors that don't accurately represent the real needs of each LHJ. 3. LHJs are not usually part of the decision-making process on how to allocate funds to address the diverse and unique needs of LHJs. 4. Strict guidelines on usage, limits the ability of LHJs to address the specific needs of their LHJ creatively. 5. Not all LHJs possess the skillset and resources to compete fairly for competitive grants distributed by the CDC or CDPH. Proposed solutions include: 1. Updating allocation methodologies with modern economic models that incorporate factors representing real needs and adjusting for population variations. 2. Making the process participatory, allowing LHJs to contribute to the construction of formulas and the utilization of funds. 3. Building local capacity to secure merit-based grants. 4. Shifting funds from clinical services to Public Health, benefiting both Managed Care Plans (MCPs) and LHJs. I would need more space to elaborate on the details how #4 can be operationalized.

    September 04, 2025
  • Guest-23405

    The primary problem I would address is flexible work schedules and the ability to work from home. This means allowing employees to work in a format that is best for them which in turn would allow them to reach top productivity. Also, automating most of our contact with the public would ensure faster and smoother interactions and improve morale for both sides.

    September 04, 2025
  • Guest-25803

    Different departments that perform the same type of work at various stages use different systems to house records. Records have to be sent to different systems, creating inefficiencies and information silos. I am speaking specifically about Medi-Cal fraud investigations.

    September 04, 2025
  • Guest-25258

    As a tenured state employee with over 10 years of experience, one of the biggest problems I've seen is the retention of employees who don’t contribute to the mission, goals, or vision of the department. It’s frustrating to work alongside individuals who consistently underperform or show little motivation, especially when dedicated employees like myself are paid the same or often less. This impacts morale, slows progress, and weakens the overall effectiveness of the team. To address this, I’d advocate for a more meaningful and transparent performance evaluation process that includes peer feedback and self-assessments, not just supervisor input. Raises and promotions should reflect both tenure and performance, so that strong employees feel valued and motivated. I’d also propose a peer recognition program to highlight those who go above and beyond, and development plans for employees who need improvement. Finally, training supervisors to actively manage performance issues would help ensure accountability. These changes would support a healthier workplace culture and better public service. By recognizing effort and addressing complacency, we can build teams that truly reflect the values of public service.

    September 04, 2025
  • Guest-25723

    A single tool for Emergency management, a tool that has been approved and aligned with all agencies to be the true source of real time data.

    September 04, 2025
  • Guest-25962

    For workers in Sacramento who are visiting other offices and who don't have a downtown parking space, we should be able to reserve and use empty parking spots that sit unused because they are reserved for people who are not in the office every day. There should be shared/hoteled spots, and then there should be spots that can be used by any state employee who needs to drive to visit another office.

    September 04, 2025
  • Guest-22926

    A lack of formal training, training manuals, no consistency through departments & offices in streamlining functions. Every office needs to be audited. Training manuals should exist for every job. The Hiring Process & HR takes entirely too long. Great candidates are lost due to the time it takes between their interviews & follow up.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25059

    Individuals who are hired to carry out a responsibility should have the authority to do so. Excessive oversight and approvals make delegating responsibilities useless and lead to hiring more people who are unable to actually do their work and to delays in processes and product delivery. Employees at all levels should be empowered to take initiative to carry out the responsibilities of their role and be held accountable for their work rather than placing responsibility and accountability only in those at the top of the chain of command.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-22533

    I would like to see the State of California use less paper, and decrease the cost of printing out reports. Other costs to consider: maintenance of machines, power used to keep printers online, building space to archive documents. One way to solve the issue is to leave the already printed files alone, and start where we are now, creating a file system on the one drive that other teammembers can assess.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25826

    Costs of doing business that are not financially responsible to the State or the taxpayers. Payroll failures. Let's pay our employees on time, and correctly. Make it easier for an employee to look at their paystub and be able to clearly see they are getting all the pay the are due.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25788

    Recruitment of the most qualified employees for each job should be a state priority. Recruiting candidates throughout the state allowing for telework can put us closer to that goal.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25788

    There is a wonderful opportunity through a COVID bright spot to redesign how state employees engage in their work. The recent legislative audit outlined several ways telework is a benefit to all Californians. I urge all state agencies to build on the efficiency, financial savings, recruitment benefits, and environmental protections telework affords. Don’t we want state agencies to recruit and retain the best employees from throughout the state? Telework is a golden opportunity to move forward to create the most efficient, productive, and inclusive workforce to best meet the needs of Californians.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25788

    Lean into the possibilities of telework by creating efficient and inclusive work opportunities for employees with disease and disability. In addition, examine which job duties are enhanced or prohibited by in person work.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25905

    The State's haphazard approach to RTO which includes dismissing telework is certainly a primary concern with my colleagues. The State's attempt this year to needlessly change to 80% RTO ended up with a huge amount of waste. Departments were moved around for ultimately no reason, and without proper research done. When an actual thorough study did come back, it was after things had already been postponed, and the study showed that telework saves taxpayers millions, and falls in line with California's goal of reducing carbon emissions to combat climate change. How many thousands of hours of State Employee time, and consequently, millions in taxpayer dollars, were wasted by this rushed and mis-informed decision? How many thousands of Employees lives (daycare, commute, carpool, home office investments, etc.,) were financially & physically disrupted, changed, and then changed back? All this because the State did not do proper research or go through proper channels. I certainly hope we've learned some valuable lessons and approach this issue differently next year. If not, there will be an exodus of talent, because a primary reason people come to state service is the benefits, and telework is a big one.

    September 03, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-25606

    I would like to see clarity returned to remote work options for CA State Employees. I know the uncertainty over return-to-office mandates has caused me additional stress and uncertainty. The promised work-life balance was a big reason I chose to work for my department.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25755

    As someone familiar with California's education system, I'd identify the fundamental disconnect between compliance and student outcomes as the primary problem. Currently, we've created a system where districts spend enormous resources crafting grant narratives that sound impressive rather than implementing practices that actually work. A small rural district with a proven intervention might lose funding to a larger district with better grant writers, even when the rural district's approach would deliver better results for students. The Core Problem: We're funding narrative ability instead of educational effectiveness. Districts hire consultants to write compelling grants while the money that should go to students gets trapped in a bureaucratic approval process that prioritizes compliance documentation over evidence-based outcomes.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25882

    If we have a huge deficit, why fix something that isn't broken. The money it would cost to bring state employees back to office is extreme. We downsized to save on rent and maintenance as well as cost to the state. RTO will not promote collaboration because staff will still be meeting via Teams. RTO also means more interruptions with staff walking to restrooms or breakrooms. Noise levels will return. There is no flexibility with RTO and managers/supervisors will wind up just micromanaging which takes away from better solutions and better results. Gas is already expensive, and we took a pay cut for two years. How is RTO going to improve collaboration, innovation and accountability when we are already doing it remotely?

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-24508

    Any plans to return to office more than two days per week is an economic drain on the state and a demoralizing prospect for hard-working, family-oriented civil servants. I propose a permanent two days in office policy be put in place (although one or zero days would be even better).

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-22090

    A phenomenon amount of bureaucracy and wastefulness. If a private practice was to operate like the State, it would bankrupt itself within a very short period of time.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25987

    For our unit we work with foreign vendors to create trade shows or hire consultants in other countries. Our payment of a physical check has presented many issues and in some instances is not a legal payment (i.e., South Africa). Being able to pay via wire transfers is something the Golden State who prides itself in innovation should be able to offer.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25830

    One of the primary problems I would address is the outdated and fragmented database systems we rely on as state employees. The current infrastructure is antiquated (over 40 years old and is DOS), and because so many departments and individuals are involved in each process, with systems that don’t communicate with each other, it often creates delays and confusion. Even when tasks are completed promptly on our end, we’re left waiting for other departments to act, which can negatively impact the employees we serve. I'm speaking from within Human Resources. I understand that there are efforts underway to create a new system, but past attempts have fallen short due to the complexity of the project. Training has also been limited to overly basic scenarios, leaving staff unprepared for real-world complications. When unique or difficult cases arise, there’s often no clear guidance or consistent answers. To address this, I believe we need: A centralized and integrated system that reduces dependency on multiple platforms. Collaborative development of the system with feedback from front-line users during the build phase, not just after rollout. Advanced scenario-based training that includes real-life, complex examples. A dedicated support team that can provide timely and consistent answers when issues arise. These changes would not only improve internal workflows but also ensure better service and outcomes for the public and our fellow employees.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-24071

    Another ongoing issue is working with personnel. It seems like no one really knows how to do things, maybe it's high turnover, but different people give different answers. Being out stationed, we don't really have a contact person. Going out on leave is super complicated with mis-information, or changing processes, and it shouldn't be that difficult.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25545

    Telework is highly effective for retaining state employees, saving taxpayer money (as demonstrated in the August 2025 California State Auditor report), and reducing carbon emissions—a priority consistently emphasized in state climate policies. The State of California recognized these advantages when it touted telework's benefits during the pandemic and confirmed no loss in productivity during that period. Given these facts, it should be made more widely available and implemented whenever possible for all state employees.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-21482

    I have been a buyer, contract analyst and purchasing manager for some years. I think some of the excessive purchasing laws and policies make the State purchasing/contracting procedure unnecessarily complex. Simplifying the process and reducing the number of forms needed not only expedites the process but also saves taxpayer money. One example I suggest removing is the DVBE participation requirement in formal solicitations. Almost, 99% of the times, it's been waived due to highly specialised services or other factors. These types of laws and policies do not help DVBEs; they just add additional paperwork and delay the contracting process. Other examples are forms like Darfur, California Civil Rights Laws Attachment, and Iran Contracting Act Verification that need to be submitted by bidders/contractors, or 113 pages of Telecom General Provisions included in telecom contracts. The lengthy provisions and forms that are never looked at or used for anything waste taxpayer money and complicate the process for companies. Some vendors, especially Small Business suppliers they do not have a lawyer on board to help them understand the State contract provisions and for that reason do not participate in bidding opportunities. There are a lot of requirements during the purchasing process that could be removed or simplified. I'm referring to the laws and policies included in State Contracting Manuals Volumes I & II and State Administrative Manual (SAM).

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-24029

    A large problem is the manual redundant processes for state mandated reporting. State entities must report quarterly and/or annually to oversight entities. Many of these reports are manual excel spreadsheets or PDF documents that incorporate/reiterate information already available to the oversight entity, putting an undue burden on the workforce to reproduce . Processes could easily be streamlined with a shared platform for reporting and monitoring. Workflows could be automatically initiated at a set frequency to ensure timely updates are provided by the entity in accordance with requirements. Data could be prepopulated for ease of completion.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25629

    As a state employee, I am writing to propose an innovative idea to standardize specific statewide policies across all departments. Currently, many departments independently develop similar policies in operational, human resources, and administrative areas. Centralizing these policies would create significant efficiencies and result in substantial resource and cost savings. This proposal includes leveraging technology to automate this process where feasible, developing comprehensive templates, and providing statewide training to ensure consistent implementation across all departments. It is recommended that the state centralize this effort for all general policies within the California Department of Human Resources. Additionally, there is some statewide centralization for Information Technology (IT) and security policies. However, IT and security policies should be reviewed to identify if there are more opportunities for centralization. While many IT and security policies are proprietary, there may be areas where streamlining is possible. Statewide IT and security policies should continue to be centralized within the California Department of Technology.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-25997

    For agencies with rulemakings, it would greatly improve efficiency if we have a more modern way to notice service lists about workshops, events, or other provide other critical updates that isn't just copying from an excel spreadsheet into email. It's inefficient and hard not to make mistakes.

    September 03, 2025
  • Guest-22836

    California's state agencies often have different interpretations of the same policies set by entities like CalHR, the Secretary of State, and DGS. This decentralized approach creates a lack of uniformity in business processes across departments, particularly in areas like Human Resources. A recent project aimed to identify a "golden image"—a consistent, shared process—for the departments within our agency. However, we discovered that each department follows a different process for the same core functions. This forced us to develop a separate, customized IT system (SaaS) for one department and then hire a consultant to modify the code to fit the next department's unique needs. This constant customization is expensive, with each new system integration costing between $250,000 and $500,000. By mandating a single, consistent interpretation of state policies, we could achieve significant efficiencies and save millions of dollars. A standardized approach would allow for easier cross-training of staff and eliminate the need for costly, department-specific customizations of IT systems. It would ensure that technology solutions developed for one department are readily usable by others, creating a more cohesive and cost-effective operational model for the state. Furthermore, it may be beneficial to centralize human resources at the agency level and have one HR system across the whole agency to save even more money. This centralized idea can work for other admin areas as well.

    September 02, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-24560

    The state is behind the times in use of technology. Implementing simple technology solutions would streamline some of california's biggest problems, save money, and improve efficiency.

    September 02, 2025
  • Guest-24907

    As a Californian and with my expertise as a state employee, the primary problem I would address is the lack of urgency and care in responding to public, as well as internal inquiries (e.g., a penalty abatement request to the IRS taking 60 days to process or internal budget approval requests interfering with immediate needs, such as the need to dispose of hazardous waste within regulatory timeframes, due to long processing times). My ideas for solving the problem I shared includes creating expectations for higher communication standards, such as through departmental policies or other methods, to encourage faster processing times and responses to email and other typical communications methods.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-24705

    Most people associate the EDD with unemployment insurance or disability insurance. However, it is much more than that. It is a government agency that provides resources, job referrals, referral to services, trainings, etc. to help people in need. We need to do a better job at informing people that we are on their side. The EDD can be a bridge to help people not only get by but literally change their lives for the better.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-25669

    A primary problem in the hiring of state employees is having to retake exams after expiration to rejoin the eligible list. If someone has already passed an exam previously, they should not need to retake the exam to stay on the eligible list - I think instead they can just be asked to confirm yearly if they would like to stay on the eligible list. I also think the exam should not be required for current state employees looking to promote and who qualify based on their years of experience. For example, someone with 1 year of AGPA should not have to take the SSM1 exam to get on the eligible list. They should automatically be eligible for SSM1.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-22611

    The need to push forward on something that clearly isn't working or needs more time in development. Hitting the pause button, while measuring implications, isn't a sign of weakness, it's a sign of self-reflection, accountability, and the want to do things the right way. So many times we have duplicative efforts and efforts that just aren't working, but rather than taking the step back we keep trying to fit the square peg into the round hole.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-23692

    It would be good if more forms and documents could be submitted online to our department, including an online payment option, when needed. The amount of paper docs we receive in the mail is huge, when most could be sent by email or online.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-25669

    Need a state-wide, electronic portal for reporting timesheets. Some departments are still submitting Excel spreadsheets filled in by hand, which is a very time-consuming process. People also have to learn new timesheet reporting processes when switching positions/departments - if it was standardized, this would be much simpler.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-22533

    Given the urgent housing shortage in California, why aren't existing vacant or underutilized properties being prioritized? One idea is to start encouraging the state to revamp tax code incentives and penalties to maintain, occupy, or repurpose spaces that are currently eyesores or not wisely used.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-25669

    Hiring exams - after taking an exam, the eligibility shouldn't expire. Nothing has changed with that person's experience (if anything, they'll just gain more and more relevant experience). Current state employees also shouldn't be required to take an exam to get on the eligibility list when they are already in that classification path and qualify - for example, someone with 1 year of AGPA should automatically be on the eligible list for SSM1, without needing to take the exam.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-25841

    As a state employee that has laterally transferred to different Departments, I've noticed a dramatic difference in the responsibilities and level of expectations for the AGPA classification. In one department, as a seasoned AGPA I was required to have every email reviewed that was being sent to an external source. Another department that I worked for places their AGPA staff in lead roles, facilitating and leading discussions with internal and external stakeholders and the management sit back and watch. These types of inconsistencies can cause people to struggle when they believe they are taking a lateral position but their level of responsibility dramatically increases in their new role, it affects efficiencies when AGPAs with multiple years of experience cannot effectively lateral into similarly positioned AGPA roles. I believe there needs to be statewide accountability put in place in order to make the level of responsibility across classifications consistent. This cannot simply be done by reviewing duty statements which are broad and do not often reflect the management culture of the office. There should be statewide reviews of internal procedures for varying classifications to ensure that state workers are paid for the same level of responsibility, effort, and commitment.

    August 29, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-25716

    Please consider the process of awarding a construction contract on state-funded projects. The current process of requesting a lump sum bid and then awarding to the lowest bidder has several problems and ultimately costs the state more money and time in the long run. The contracts team needs to create a database and flag contractors who have defaulted, been previously terminated from a project, or have a track record of poor performance. We have been awarding projects to the lowest bidder without being given an opportunity to determine whether that contractor deserves the right to work on a project. If we can establish a resource to the project managers and contracts team to vet through contractors, we should be able to disqualify a bidder due to poor performance, despite being the lowest bidder. The current process in place is actually costing the state more money and time to handle a poor-performing contractor. In addition, bids are requested as a lump sum. This is problematic for several reasons. Once we award a contract to the lowest bidder, we have no way of knowing if that bid actually includes all the requirements of the contract documents. As the process is currently set up, it is the responsibility of the contractor to ensure their bids cover the scope, however in several cases, the contractor ends up misinterpreting the scope and/or missing essential work. Better communication is key and can set up a project for success in the long run.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-25576

    It takes an extremely long time to hire new staff. The budget change proposal must be submitted by May of the prior year and the actual hiring won't happen till the next year July. This is an extremely long timeframe and slows down the state's ability to respond quickly to staffing shortages. There must be a way to allocate a position once a need for it is determined.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-22052

    Data sharing. I understand there are all kinds of legal/privacy hurdles we would have to overcome but if someone, including myself, is approved to view all kinds of FTI as is, there is no reason we shouldn't be able to pull data in other departments may have access to in order to make better analytical decisions about our own projects. Just as an example, if I'm working on an analysis to determine how a certain enforcement action may be disproportionately impacting certain subsets of California population, I can examine my own department's data but I cannot compare that to data about California's entire population, so I end up having to look for outside resources provided by private or non-profit sector groups where I can't confirm reliability. That is crazy. If I am working on a data analysis re: insurance settlement payouts, I should be able to easily access information collected by Dept of Insurance instead of having to do a Google search.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-22052

    One of the most enlightening books I've read recently was 'Poverty, by America' by Matthew Desmond. In it, he points out how, while well-intentioned, so many government programs are not being used by those who need the benefits most because they are too difficult to navigate or confusing for people to use. This creates an impression among low-income folks that state government is AGAINST them, not for them. This creates a self-perpetuating cycle of distrust between the very people who would most benefit from our services. We need (not in order): 1. Data: Identify unused gov't services 2. Tech: Provide a unified, easy to remember, accessible access point to connect people with services 3. Reform: Review all public benefits we provide in California from a modern, poverty reduction point of view, at every level, and flag those services that may need legislative reform so we can make recommendations to legislature. 4. Project management: Task force to address poverty (different than homelessness, and not meant to replace current efforts there) in California. 5. Marketing: Make combating poverty our primary focus, drill it into every state department at every level looking for ways to address, including inter-agency efforts and private sector-style promo to draw people in. Almost like a rebrand. This would be a huge lift but when we have so many services, so many people struggling, plus such a negative impression of gov't, it seems like it would be a win-win-win for everyone.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-25053

    The state is composed of various departments that are very small in size and often must contract out to provide obtain basic services internally or externally. These contracts can include interpretation/translation, website maintenance, a/v services, cabling, etc. Sometimes staff are stretched thin in smaller offices with various responsibilities because there is not enough personnel to cover issues that a team in a larger organization would do. There is so much time spent in bringing on external providers in addition to the cost of services as well as recruiting for staff turnover due to burnout. The state could better coordinate services across departments under a certain size reducing costs and providing adequate support for staff and the public.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-21174

    One of the biggest problems with working for the state is employee morale. I have come to understand that the "state" as an institution does not care about me. The beaurocracy is created to avoid risk, not create a good working environment. One small example of this is coffee and kitchen supplies. I was told that it is against state policy to purchase coffee, filtered water, or even dish soap for employees. This is a little thing, but it makes such a big difference in showing employees that you care about them. I cannot imagine the public would think that employees can't have these basic office supplies purchased for them, when it is so commonplace in every other office I have worked in -- even non-profit organizations! It is hard to do your best work for an organization that does not care about you. This affects efficiency because it affects employees' productivity.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-25700

    Nepotism in hiring and/or promotions given to friends and otherwise unqualified persons. Maybe more review of hiring by outsiders? Like reviewing the application, interview, and selection materials by others outside the department to ensure the reasons for hiring and not hiring were fair and in alignment with the requirements management had chosen to score by?

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-23675

    One primary problem I would address is the fragmentation and inefficiency caused by too many oversight agencies in California’s government. This creates delays, redundant processes, and unclear accountability. My solution would be to streamline oversight by consolidating overlapping agencies and functions. I’d also push for performance-based funding, tying legislative allocations for system upgrades to measurable outcomes like reduced processing times or improved customer satisfaction. This ensures that public resources are used efficiently and that upgrades deliver real value to Californians.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-23675

    I’d push for performance-based funding, where agencies must show measurable process improvements and return on investment before receiving additional resources. This ensures upgrades are strategic, data-driven, and truly benefit the public. A major issue is the excessive number of oversight agencies, which creates duplication, delays, and inefficiencies. From a Lean Six Sigma perspective, this is pure waste. I’d advocate for consolidating oversight functions and holding the legislature accountable for funding only those upgrades that show measurable process improvements. Fewer layers, clearer accountability, and smarter investments lead to faster, more effective public service.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-25889

    Inventory of assets, which should actually be named office equipment: As state employee I receive multiple "Excel Asset Inventory worksheets" from different individuals/departments requesting information about the office equipment I use in my line of work. I suggest streamlining process by centralizing it, creating a dashboard, and give access to interested parties and or departments that need access the data rather than having staff completing multiple worksheets, which creates the risk of inaccurate data and unnecessary processes. Have one dedicated team in charge of managing the disbursement and disposal of office equipment as well as of the maintenance of the database/dashboard for accuracy and accountability.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-24697

    I think state departments should encourage more flexibility, in general, when it comes to employee satisfaction. This includes having exempt employees instead of hourly employees, that have flexible schedules based on their workload. There is a lot of wasted time that is used on gossip and bullying, because employees are bored and become cliquish.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-22327

    I've experienced this in the private sector also, but for some state employees that have worked for the state for decades, there is often little room for change. There is a sense of "we've always done it this way," which can be challenging and create extra work for others. For someone like me, who goes around those who aren't happy about change and makes the change anyway, a solution could be more encouragement around the work we do and the improvements we can make can be a boost of morale. Also, we can normalize change and not make it a daunting or annoying thing in the workplace.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-25707

    We are unable to hire personnel, we are very short staffed. The people that are employed are not qualified, and to terminate a uncooperative employee is not possible, making it very difficult for the hard working committed employees. The uncooperative staff are rewarded and treated better than the employees who pick up the slack and work hard and are at work all the time.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-23352

    CAlPIA and their theft of funds from other agencies, by being forced to purchase from CALPIA with extremally inflated prices. Like $731 for an office chair. Similar chair sells elsewhere for $250 tops.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-24367

    I believe our government could be more efficient by making sure funds are going to the places they are meant to go to and not drawn out of other programs to make ends meet for a current program, thus at some point making the program that had a surplus of funds at some point low on funds because the once surplus was drawn out from. I feel when this happens it just happen it becomes a snowball effect. I believe it can be taken care of by having more auditors to make hold our government accountable as to where funds are going and that the full amount of the fund was used and if there is left over that is put into the surplus so that if another program needs it it will be there for the taking. Hopefully not become unaccounted for.

    August 29, 2025
  • Guest-23068

    The underlying problem is pretty straight forward in my view: California’s government is dominated by a single party that prioritizes ideology over practicality and common sense. Sky-high taxes, crushing regulations, and one-party rule have made it hostile to business and middle-class families. Soft-on-crime policies created safe havens for criminals, while homelessness is out of control despite billions spent. The state throws money at symptoms without addressing root causes like addiction and mental illness, and restrictive zoning makes affordable housing impossible. California isn’t broke, it’s just It’s failing because its leaders prioritize woke virtue signaling, radical environmentalism, and identity politics over law, order, affordability, and common sense. Unfortunately, I don’t think that’s going to change under a one-party rule.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-23343

    The IT purchasing process for state departments is absolutely nuts. Takes forever, and most companies won't accept the terms and conditions, really restricting our ability to use software. I recommend a review of our process and getting input from contractors.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-24832

    Inefficiencies as a result of red-tape put in place be legislators that require us to take the lowest (overpriced) bidders instead of training staff to complete the work or hiring staff who have the requisite skills to meet California's needs. Also, inefficiencies due to the de-centralized nature of running administrative tasks, including human resources.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-21502

    the complexity of the state contracting process, particularly when Dept. of General Services is involved. DGS can deny authority and services to Depts. and then we are accountable for failing to meet our mission. There is a consistent lack of understanding about what is required to manage large tracts of land and run fish hatcheries. Rather than serving a purpose (not wasting CA resources), contracting rules create impossible barriers and inefficiencies to acquiring tools and services required to meet our mission. Suggest giving more expansive authorities to Depts while simultaneously doing a better job writing clear policy and statute directives to assist Dept staff with making decisions that are functional and legal.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-25065

    Increase collaboration within and across Departments (as well as reduce building costs and increase business downtown) by creating coworking spaces where multiple state departments utilize the same building. For example, DPH, DHCS, DMHC and DSS could all share one building with large meeting spaces, conference rooms and amenities such as gym, coffee shop to draw people in office to collaborate versus forcing via in-office requirements. Having community spaces would create networking opportunities for partnership and mentorship allowing state staff to grow and promote across Departments with similar visions and areas of expertise. Reinforces concept that we are all stewards of the state working for one employer, Californians.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-23466

    The primary problem I have seen across my time working state service is a lack of training and skills development among staff. Instead of refining our skills, and engaging in external trainings, we depend upon internal practices that are often out of date with the professional working community elsewhere in the natural resources management world. This lack of training and skills development puts staff at a disadvantage when it comes to modern approaches to natural resources management projects, in addition to increasing the amount of time it takes to convey processes among ranks.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-23732

    Sharing information and knowledge across departments. Everything is a part of the state, its one central embodiment, but everything is so segregated in a way that hinders our ability to serve the publics interest on a meaningful level and reasonable timeline. The biggest idea I would have is to allow ECOS and PIMS access across entities in a "view only" capacity for HR departments. Why can I not do employment A01 verifications myself here for someone in another department? Why can I not see applications provided from a candidate in ECOS to another department? Perhaps they had attached transcripts there that could save 10 days to get here. Another issue I'd like to address is the use of different documents across state entities. Many processes, policies and procedures begin with guidance from CalHR and the State Personnel Board (SPB) but each department then adapts that guidance using its own forms or supporting docs. This results in inconsistencies across departments. Establishing a standardized process that starts with CalHR and SPB guidance and carries forward uniformly would promote consistency, fairness, and equity across CA.

    August 28, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-24612

    The amount of paperwork and approvals needed to make a simple purchase of essential items for our office. Release budgets to the local unit itself, so that state BSO approval is not necessary. Local units are able to spend effectively, abiding all state policies and guidelines.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-25528

    Diminishing institutional knowledge and judgment calls allowed by management. Management are not as free as so in the past to make judgement calls and that takes the "human" part out of customer service with the people we serve.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-24891

    I would like to adjust the process for accepting contractor bids as a client agency to allow for more oversight for specialized projects.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-21837

    I think lack of 1) efficiency, 3) rigorous training (specifically of soft skills) and 2) passion/pride/ownership of the work we do, are the primary problems. The problem "efficiency" can be resolved if we all (those part of the system) put our hearts & minds together to solve this issue. As someone mentioned in this chain - it is as easy as analyzing and identifying areas were we can cut down on bureaucratic surplus - and yet we have failed to resolve this problem. Among the soft skills, "effective communication" is were we lack the most - I have witnessed both seasoned and inexperienced employees lacking this skill. Although fewer, we do have some "effective communicators" and mentoring/shadowing with these folks can go a long way in learning effective communication and boosting confidence. Last but not the least, the lack of "passion/pride/ownership of the work" is very common in government employees across the board. This problem can be addressed by hiring inspirational leadership (or having motivated/passionate individuals at leadership level) that can motivate the employees to be the best versions of themselves. Once passionate, the employees will be highly productive/efficient/confident and our state will become a role model for government efficiency/effectiveness.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-21967

    The state is spending billions each year on construction, purchase and maintenance of office space. By allowing all positions that can be performed remotely to be filled by remote workers, the state could save California taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars a year on office space, equipment, and IT services, while simultaneously expanding the applicant pool for state government positions, and advancing equity by opening up opportunities for career advancement to candidates living in economically disadvantaged areas of the state.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-21046

    The hiring process from the perspective of applicants can be quite complicated. Candidates often face numerous requirements when applying to state positions, which may discourage some qualified individuals. Removing the exam requirement could potentially attract more qualified candidates and streamline the process for all involved. This change could also lead to savings in time, resources, personnel, and finances over time.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-23020

    The primary problem is that the state tax system faces inefficiencies and risks due to data gaps, manual processes, and limited use of modern technology, which lead to: • Difficulty detecting underreporting or misreporting in a timely manner • Time-consuming manual work for employees • Limited taxpayer accessibility and convenience • Skill gaps among employees in adapting to new technology The ideas to solve the issue as state employees are: 1. Real-Time Data Matching with Other Agencies • Build secure connections with agencies like FTB, EDD and IRS. • Use automated cross-checking to flag discrepancies before audits or deadlines. • This reduces underreporting, improves fairness, and minimizes manual comparison. 2. Mobile App for Taxpayers • Provide an easy-to-use app for filing, payments, and notifications. • Improves compliance by making tax obligations clearer and more convenient. • Reduces taxpayer confusion and the volume of support inquiries. 3. Training on New Technologies • Invest in upskilling employees in AI, data analytics, and customer service. • Prepares the workforce to handle modern tools efficiently. • Ensures smooth adoption of new systems and better service for taxpayers.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-25999

    More upward mobility opportunities in other cities, not just Sacramento and have exams as open continuous rather than a cutoff date.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-21776

    Our office has a lack of CPAs and CIAs, and CalHR has not updated their incentive payment program in many years to keep up for inflation or to cover the cost of renewing licenses. Employees feel they are bearing a high cost with little benefit to themselves.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-21748

    Many of the policies and procedures established and practiced currently by the state are not the most effective, especially when it comes to procurement. For example, we could only go with certain vendors for procurement of goods even if they are not the best in the business and overcharge. Going with vendors outside of the state catalogue would allow us to be more fiscally responsible and save the state some money.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-25929

    Telework should be seen as a strategic system improvement that enhances organizational efficiency and public value, not just an individual workplace accommodation. Implementing statewide telework reduces overhead costs like office space, utilities, and operational expenses—freeing funds for critical public services. Studies show telework often increases productivity due to fewer distractions and more flexible schedules. This flexibility supports better work-life balance, reducing burnout and turnover—key challenges for the State workforce. Telework also expands the talent pool by attracting skilled workers regardless of location, which is vital for hard-to-fill positions. It enhances inclusivity by making State jobs accessible to people with disabilities and those in rural or underserved areas. Additionally, statewide telework adoption demonstrates environmental responsibility by cutting carbon emissions from commuting. Less commuter traffic means public savings through reduced wear on roads and lower maintenance costs. Telework ensures government continuity during emergencies—like natural disasters or health crises—making services more resilient. Overall, telework strengthens institutional performance and public service delivery.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-25196

    Consistancy overall, in information provided on the internet on the State Departments. Should have one format for all departments and simple language. Especially with Taxes, normal people are not lawyers who are reading the information, if anything we confuse them more. Also the image State Workers have, like they don't like their jobs so they make it bad for the public, like government employees don't care. Government sector customer service and very different from Private sector customer service, we should incorporate some Private sector practices. We should show some empathy, we care. HR practices, can't be one generic for overall state departments. Each individual state department is different and unique, let them run their own HR. Example, Tax Technician and Compliance Reps are in one big bucket and pay range is the same. But a Tax Tech and Compliance Rep in a Field office learns and knows more then one that is in a central office doing one duty daily. Field Office staff should be paid more and location should be looked at also. Ones in Los Angeles cannot be paid the same as one in Sacramento. That's two different areas and cost of living, yet the pay range is the same. My pet peeve: "We have always done it that way!" We need to change with the times and technology. We improve on the technology but its not embraced, because we have always done it that way. Not being open to new ideas and/or suggestions.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-24510

    Telecommuting. Make employees come back to the office. I know many state employees that work for different agencies and they "work" from home. They also brag about raising their kids instead of working, mowing their lawns, their supervisors not assigning them enough work to keep busy, napping during work hours, shopping during work hours, being on vacation during work hours and not using leave credits.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-21594

    (High-Speed Rail) Although I don't have direct experience with the High-Speed Rail. Admittedly, maybe I don't have enough understanding of the situation. But if there is still budget to put down some tracks. Why not start the High-Speed Rail between LA & Las Vegas. Could California not split the profits 50/50 with Nevada? I am sure that the casinos would love to assist paying for it as well since you would be bringing revenue to their casinos with the easy access between LA & Vegas. It is also a lot of desert between the two cities. At least then you would start generating revenue to continue the project. Aside from that I would imagine there would be increased revenue for the city of LA as people from Nevada may want to visit Disney Land, Hollywood, etc. with easier access. Seems like it would be a win-win for both states and possibly a revenue generator for the program to get things going.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-25576

    We need make the state contracting process more efficient. It takes forever for contracts to get approved, slowing down our ability to improve our systems and respond to changes in the public domain/markets. We need to reduce the review time by removing unnecessary bureaucratic red tape and setting stricter deadlines by when we can expect to hear back on our RFPs.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-25717

    Too many agencies seem to hire new employees without the proper training program in place for the job they are intended to do. New hires are brought on board and spend 1-2 days sitting at a computer doing HR related paperwork then join a unit within a department that seems to have no set program for teaching them their job other than "sit with someone" til you get an idea what we want.

    August 28, 2025
  • Guest-22820

    One big problem I see is the lack of geographical diversity in our state workforce. That creates enormous blind spots and group think, because the folks that serve certain areas don't know or understand the nuances or barriers/obstacles in the areas they purport to serve because they don't come from those geographical areas - thus there is a real gap there. For example, our state has 58 counties, yet most state employees (depending on the department) are concentrated in one county. We should make a concerted effort to hire statewide to better represent the state in its totality. Afterall, we know that representation matters. This would better mitigate some implicit biases that we all have.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-25922

    Other depts are using electronic timesheets but not all. I've been told it's in the works for my dept but it's been years now. Processing an excel 634 is cumbersome, passes through many hands, and requires back and forth corrections which is extremely time consuming. The electronic process would result in greater efficiency and cost savings.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-24650

    A primary problem to be addressed is the misunderstanding that UI is government aid. It is not meant to be abused and exhausted ad nauseum. UI is insurance to be leveraged while in between job opportunities and it needs emphasizing that UI works best for those who work, whether it be temporary, contract and seasonal work until they have obtained permanent work, thereby a new claim may be valid at the BYE so long as there was work lost at no fault of the employee in the most recent 12 months. The public needs to be aware that they are tapping into the tax reserves of the employers when leveraging these UI benefits and that they should not be abusing the opportunity to benefit from these resources as a matter of sustaining our economic structure. Abuse begets further abuse. Furthermore, there is a need for workforce localities to reinvest in establishing a new system for hiring that does not involve digital platforms that no longer vet job listing. Local job listing need to be verified and documented as current, valid and verifiable and then removed upon fulfillment. Human resource systems needs governance and accountability and turning over a system that is respectful of the authority of our local workforce departments could be a viable option for building viable businesses in our communities. This is how it used it used to.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-25756

    Within California State Parks, there are ongoing issues with qualifications/hierarchy that creates ripple effects. The state has tried different ways to organize the hierarchy but what we really need is a park manager in charge of each park and the programmatic leads giving guidance for each programmatic area. What we have now are people doing multiple jobs to fill needs. Since that isn't recognized on their duty statement, they are not recognized on paper for it when applying for other positions. They also aren't recognized for it with any pay increase. This creates a very low morale and discourages people to stay within state parks, as they aren't recognized for the work they had to do to keep operations going. So many parks operate off of nonprofit partners, meaning ones without them are left with very few staff working far above their pay. It also creates a huge discrepancy in parks with areas with wealthy donors to nonprofits and parks in low-income areas.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-25583

    Promote good managers who support their team and does not micromanage them. Find a way to support the people who are the face to the People. Good training programs for newer employees, help people to find ways to promote and good managers make for good morale.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-22880

    The employee housing system is flawed. Employee housing is needed in many areas for recruitment and/or because of high costs of living. Issues include: 1) Many State houses are occupied by highly paid staff. 2) Once staff is in a State house, the State should be able to evict or move staff based on a higher need or more appropriate use for the house, but that does not happen. 3) Much funding is spent to maintain State-owned employee residences. A cost-benefit analysis would help to determine appropriate rental rates.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-21311

    Explore revising or eliminating core business hours to reflect new emerging technological capabilities and consumer preferences. A lot of consumers are unable to contact state agencies for services between the hours of 8AM-5PM due to work. Allowing agencies to offer both a day and swing shift would benefit both California consumers and employees.

    Can we have an overarching State policy regarding using ChatGPT for training purposes?   • Guest-25489

    Support upward mobility from seasonal to permanent employee. There are countless hurdles that employees must overcome to get into a permanent position. Most will just go to the private sector and then we lose good talent trying to get truly qualified people "qualified" for a position.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-21311

    Rather than having each separate agency be the custodian of record for employee fingerprint clearances, should that responsibility instead be delegated to CalHR? Hiring agencies would also be tasked with notifying CalHR when an employee leaves State service and submit an NLI request for submission to the DOJ.

    Up-to-date Training Manuels or Succession Plans in place. Every time I start a new role in a new State Agency, everyone is too busy doing their actual job to train me.     August 27, 2025

    As a 10-year state employee with 35+ years of building design experience in my field, I see the private sector passing me by. Greater support (financial & time) for in-house training of design professional would be a great first step.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-24071

    Problem: Independent adoptions are often dealing with children who should have been agency cases, which would come with financial assistance, Medi-Cal, and no cost. However, because CPS was bypassed, the family has to pay over $4000 and get no assistance. Solution: Have all guardianship or relative adoptions be at a reduced cost, and/or if there was a CPS history have AAP or Medi-Cal be available for more families.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-24071

    Some of the processes have not been modernized. Everything seems so archaic. Like even just the process of applying for a job is so complicated and not intuitive.
