Our office has no clear onboarding process or training manual. A lot of things are just word of mouth and vary greatly. How each regional office does things is totally different. We don't come together as a team very often.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-22417

    At Mt. Tamalpais State Park, we frequently have lost or injured hikers/cyclists. Locating them and assessing their status is complicated by the number and length of our trails (approx. 60 trails, up to 7.5 miles in length). Our ranger staff is limited and serves a large geographic area. It would help our responsiveness to have a drone to fly along a trail to locate a hiker/cyclist in distress. Pinpointing the location would help reduce response time for search and rescue.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-25596

    PLAIN LANGUAGE INITIATIVE: The Plain Language Initiative aims to make California government communications clear, accessible, and equitable. Too often, state forms, letters, and websites are filled with jargon and legalese that create confusion and barriers. By adopting plain-language standards, we can help Californians understand information the first time, reduce duplicate inquiries, and build stronger trust in government. In the first nine months, the initiative would apply to high-volume documents such as forms, notices, FAQs, and websites, piloted in key agencies like DMV, HR, and EDD. Deliverables include a Plain Language Style Guide, staff training modules, rewritten sample documents, and a feedback dashboard to track resident experience. Key objectives are to improve readability to an 8th-grade level or below, train 500+ employees, reduce duplicate public inquiries by 10–15%, and boost satisfaction scores by 15% within a year. With a modest budget of about $250K and existing staff expertise, this project is small enough to implement quickly but large enough to show measurable impact. This initiative directly supports Governor Newsom’s priorities to modernize government, increase equity, and improve service delivery—demonstrating that clearer communication leads to better outcomes for Californians.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-23543

    I would like to see more avenues for career development and cross training in state government. One specific idea I'd love to see is a more formal opportunity for employees to take temporary assignments or "details" with their own department or another department at the next step up on their career ladder. This is (or was) common in federal government and is a great way to give an individual some experience as a supervisor or team leader while also allowing a department to fill a temporary gap.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-25597

    I do not think there is just 1 primary problem. I been with the state over 15 years, and have held positions as staff, and management. Covid put restrictions on in person training. Yes, def. bring back in person training. Not all jobs can be learned virtually, at least not the ones I did. As an employee, lack of mgmt guidance on growth, learning and promotions. They make you fill the IDP beginning of the year, and then what? It's not revisited till your next annual. Fancy document serves zero purpose. Even as a manager, no support or encouragement for growth. As a manager, you get to see abuse of time by staff clearly. Lack of mgmt support in documenting those staff for performance issues (lack of knowledge, ability, or simply abuse). The employees being written up have more rights than the manager who is documenting them for years, and years. So much time and resource wasted on documenting. Staff files complain against managers, and upper mgmt does nothing to support the manager. Then why would any manager want to waste their time and document ppl? the managers ruin their reputation by incompetent staff filing complains, and sadly upper mgmt. documents that on their annual, making it hard to promote for the manager, who is simply doing their job. And so, no managers want to document and waste time. Can't fire incompetent staff from state is the one benefits highly abused by ALOT of staff. Huge waste of taxpayer's money, and state resources there.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-25592

    Problem: there is often a lack of clear rationale provided for certain statewide policy decisions, such as the return-to-office mandate. This creates confusion and lowers morale, especially when state audit reports indicate that existing hybrid models are effective. Solution: Increase transparency by clearly communicating the data, reasoning, and intended outcomes behind major policy changes. Providing this context would help employees better understand the purpose, build trust in leadership, and support more effective implementation across departments.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-24326

    A problem I have noticed is that time is not taken to research and investigate problems and wastes that are barriers to improving processes. One of reasons for this is because managers and staff are overwhelmed with the number of improvements that can/need to be made. A solution to this is to create a Process Improvement Priority Tool where we list the process that needs improving and score it based on the impact it has on customer/patient care and if we already have a policy or procedure in place to mitigate the adverse impact. Based on the scoring we can determine what process/issue to tackle first.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-24617

    The budget appears to be the primary problem with California, as state employees are expected to take pay cuts and furloughs to offset budget deficit. State employee pay and benefits should be protected. Overspending should be compensated for by those who overspend. Accountability is a virtue, and California should adopt it as a standard operating procedure.

    August 27, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-25691

    We are leaders in tech in California, but our government agencies use outdated and clunky technology. Processes need to be streamlined, and technology needs to be updated. Investing substantial money to bring California's government agencies to the 21st century and partnering with successful non-profit organizations would be the first step in solving this issue. Then, offering training in the new platforms and technology to ensure employees maximize their benefits must follow.

    August 27, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-24323

    My ideas involve process improvement. Where are the bottlenecks in the processes and how can we resolve them. First, there should be increased access to information both internally and externally. Information is power and when we equip individuals with knowledge they may be able to solve problems. Next, reducing crime and fraud in the State systems. Implementing systems similar to those in the State Fund Special Investigation Unit (SIU) throughout the State in other agencies to spot fraud. Finally, looking for what areas we depend on the Federal Government and begin to reduce that dependency. Chart specifically where we receive the Federal Funding and begin to systematically self fund those areas. If we are not able to self fund look at reductions or creating alternative structures. As the 4th largest economy in the world we should be able to accomplish this task. Lastly, we need to create a State Insurance model for homeowners, auto and business insurance. We cannot be held hostage to national carriers that cherry pick and can chose to come and go from the California when it suits them. We can model rates and create programs that lead the way of the future in insurance modeling.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-25349

    Being new to the State, I feel that the biggest problem that I would address is misleading employees upon hire. I was informed that my position was 100% remote. There is not enough training, I'm still unclear of my duties. Lastly, there is talk about changing pay periods from monthly to bi weekly. They tend to make changes, and these changes are disruptive to employees lives, and that is not taken into account when upper management makes these huge decisions. These decisions have caused some really valuable state employees to quit and return to the private sector.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-24140

    As a long time, State employee and manager, I would like to see a more streamlined approach to purchasing and hiring. The processes are constantly changing and many times when there are "Statewide contracts" (an example would be tires), there are not enough "official locations" to make the contract viable or easy to use. We could be more efficient if DGS would delegate that authority lower to each department or at agency level. Same with hiring processes.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-25358

    I work for the California Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation (CDCR). One of the largest financial burdens of this department is the amount of movement made for inmates (i.e. transfers from one prison to a different prison). Specifically, when an inmate's mental health level of care is changed to a "higher" level of care (i.e. to EOP, ICF, Acute, MHCB), the inmate is transferred "short-term" to a different prison. This transfer takes multiple Correctional Officers driving across the state to take the inmate from Point A to Point B. As the Mental Health level of care is temporary, they are transferred back (or to a new prison) within a short period of time. The same inmates are transferred many times throughout the year, whenever their Mental Health changes. In my opinion, it would be cheaper, more cost effective, and more efficient, if each prison had the appropriate staff at that prison to treat that inmate's mental health level of care. So... don't transfer the inmate from Point A to Point B multiple times; keep the inmate at Point A, and have on-site staff treat the inmate. This would be a huge cost savings.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-25737

    Funding and more transparency. I feel money goes in the wrong places sometimes and we have people making decisions that have no idea what works and what doesn't (coming from CDCR). I also think more schedule flexibility would be nice for state employees, as I often feel time constraints in my day-to-day life that are unnecessary and could be preventable with more flexible schedules.

    August 27, 2025
  • Guest-24515

    We have noticed how redundant and wasteful it seems to have each *program* in our office have their own printers/copiers (usually multiple per program). This is not an efficient use of resources or space. Why not just have a bank of printers & copiers per wing/suite/floor that all programs in that space can connect to? This would save $ on equipment, consumables (toner +/- paper), and maintenance while getting more out of each individual machine (less environmental impact). While I understand that programmatic budgets are distinct, why can't programs pay a proportional fee for use of the printing/copying equipment like we do for the office space, IT services, etc.? This would surely also save programs $ on wasted toner purchased for machines that become incompatible, outdated, and/or redundant. Streamlining in this way would also likely make machine maintenance and replacement easier.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-23227

    I get assigned workload that managers know ahead of time requires a translator as I am not bilingual. I can't figure out why the work can't go directly to the people who are bilingual and getting paid for that. It takes double the time to complete the tasks when I have to ask coworkers to translate or call the translator. I also don't think I am getting all the pertinent information when using translators. The work that the translators are getting which is for English speakers could be assigned to me and others who aren't bilingual so as to not add work to anyone.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25501

    Making sure employees dont have concerns about financial mistakes costing them money back to the state through their paychecks. HR needs to update communication with employees to decrease costly mistakes to employees that then have to pay a portion of their paychecks back to the state at no fault of the employees. I recommend a new updated payroll system or using one that is proven to handled large organizations.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25488

    We need to improve iir K-12 public schools. Educators should be laid more. Schools should be fully supplied with everything they need to twach our kids. Parents and teachers should not have to go out of pocket to pay for school supplies.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25383

    The primary problem I see on a day-to-day basis is financial mismanagement and waste. Sometimes I wonder how much thought goes into whether my department in particular actually needs the things we contract for. I see a lot of money spent on things that I regard as questionable. I believe we really need to focus on using the talent contained within our own ranks. We contract out for services so often where I feel we can solve our own problems more effectively. Financial waste is a huge problem and I feel that we need to meet more to discuss options other than just throwing money at an issue. And we need to use the auditor hotline more frequently when we see waste such as this.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25497

    I'd like to see all state departments prioritizing/resourcing the organizational development related work that we are tasked with as mission critical. Far too often, effort such as strategic planning and business planning, State Leadership Accountability Act reporting, risk management, workforce and succession plans, engagement surveys and activities, change management process improvement, etc. fall off the plate as soon as something else is considered more important/critical. It is often left to one area of the organization, delegated to staff as other duties as required (without proper training), and can even be sent to outside vendors. If this work is required and important, then let's give it the priority and resources necessary to be successful and meaningful to the work we do in state service.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25804

    Streamlining and applying an all-digital timekeeping and payroll process, removing paper from the equation. Currently, hand transcription of attendance and payroll wastes many hours of state employee time across departments.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-23559

    To make California’s government more effective and efficient, we need to prioritize human connection in the workplace. By creating safe spaces, motivating our employees, developing their skills, and consistently reminding them of the value they bring, we empower them to become the best version of themselves. When employees feel valued and cared for, they perform better, contribute more meaningfully, and help build a stronger, more effective government. I believe in dreaming big, staying positive, and starting small, because small actions often lead to the biggest impact.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-23187

    The procurement process is overly complicated, time consuming, and often not the most cost efficient. For example, we are required to purchase chairs from CalPIA. CalPIA charges $500+ for one desk chair and then another $30 per chair to deliver. The same chair could be purchased from another retailer for a fraction of the price. In addition, the ordering time is outrageous. Purchases go through many levels of approval before getting to the vendor and many times the required vendor's turn around time is 60+ business days. Small businesses and disabled veteran-owned businesses get preference, and many times, these small businesses order from retailers like Amazon and then upcharge the State. There is nothing cost efficient about the procurement process.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-24689

    There needs to be a better process for getting duplicate pay warrants issued. I often have employees waiting a month or more just to receive a replacement warrant. I like Cal Employee Connect but I think they could add functionality to it, giving the employee the ability to request their own duplicate warrant directly with the SCO.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25877

    The State of California has a multitude of leased and owned office space that spans the entire state. Given the return-to-office mandate by the Governor (N-22-25), the state Department of General Services should allow State agencies to create a 'Hoteling' service/platform whereby office-based state employees from any agency can work 'in-office' at a State location convenient to them or for operational need. The benefits could be numerous: Reduced commute times and easier schedules GHG and emissions savings Lower commute costs Greater inter-agency collaboration Greater state representation in the State's 58 counties

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-23928

    We are very poorly trained and lack operational consistency. Every office is doing something different, every agency does the same process different ways, there needs to be a smoother process on how we do things in each agency. There are too many hands in the pot, just to get something approved as simple as office supplies, it has a minimum of 5 people processing an order, we are spending more money in labor then what the supply request cost. There should be statewide system for things like procurement, timesheets, basically any common function that is takes to run an agency. It's too hard to come up with a solution to the problem because I don't know if anyone above rank and file want solutions. There are to many EO, AEO, Deputy this and that.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25565

    Postage and shipping costs. When we are able to provide resources electronically with confirmation receipt, we can eliminate mail, i.e., Family Medical Leave Act (FMLA), California Family Rights Act (CFRA) and Pregnancy Disability Leave (PDL), Workers' Compensation (WC). This will save the state budget in time used, paper and postage used, and shredding the resources after they are saved electronically.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25186

    The primary problem is that everything is politicized. Everything we do depends on who is in office, who might offended, and who's invested. I feel like a pawn half the time. I don't have any sound advice on how to fix it, but I do know that as long as politicians are fighting across aisles with each other, citizens will always get caught in the middle.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25999

    There's a saying as a state worker, sink or swim. It would be nice to have an onboarding process, academy perhaps, that trains newbies on not just policies and their role, but all of the amazing benefits offered to state employees. A lot of the times, new state workers are not aware and don't take advantage. It would also be nice for in depth training from staff members assigned to train and not take away from their regular duties.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-22422

    There are very few state employes that work in my job classification, so I think there is a lack of understanding of what we can do under our scope of practice to provide safe, timely, effective, efficient, equitable, and patient centered care. In my role, I have developed new policies and protocols, but without proper understanding of what we can contribute, we will continue to be underutilized.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25999

    Lower level staff being used as pawns. Some agencies, not all, have too many executive roles. Also, it would be nice to practice less waste by implementing electronic files/work and not a need for paper files. Implement docusign more if a signature is needed. Update policies where a "wet" signature is not needed and docusign can replace.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25962

    I think every department/office/unit should have someone who answers the phone and can talk with constituents to either answer their question or direct them to the right person or another agency. We answer our phones, and people are always so grateful and somewhat surprised. Then we refer them to another office that maybe isn't so helpful and doesn't have a person answering the phone, and they are disappointed. I know I personally get very frustrated going through phone trees when I have to call a retailer or other service provider. State agencies are supposed to serve our citizens and we should do that gladly and always. Phone numbers (and emails) should be up front and easy to find on all state websites.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25575

    Hiring the right people is greatly limited due to workplace headquarter designations. Ideas only come from major cities and not tribal lands and other diverse communities. Hiring should be opened up to the whole state and HQ designations should be available wherever you live.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25970

    I believe the core issue lies in the lack of employee responsibility and accountability. Government bureaucracy is structured in a way that pushes decision-making upward through the chain of command, concentrating authority in the hands of a few. This results in an overabundance of policies and procedures that strip decision-making power from the subject matter experts—the individuals actually performing the hands-on work. Furthermore, this system fosters a dense layer of middle management that often controls decisions without fully understanding their upstream or downstream impacts. This disconnect between decision-makers and practitioners undermines efficiency and responsiveness. To truly improve government operations, this overarching management structure must be reformed. Such reform will likely require legislative action to decentralize authority, empower technical experts, and streamline bureaucratic processes.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25097

    CDCR Fire needs a complete reorganization. Currently, multiple fire stations operate independently, answering to Plant Operations or Wardens. Each station procures its own PPE, hoses, tools, and follows local policies—resulting in a fragmented system with no standardization or unified effectiveness. While CDCR Fire Stations at correctional institutions primarily serve an educational mission, that role should not diminish their potential to be a valuable resource for the State of California during emergencies With proper structure, CDCR Fire could become a valuable asset in California’s wildfire, flood and disaster response efforts. Instead, its lack of coordination makes it a liability. A centralized command starting in Sacramento is essential. Appointing a Fire Chief and Deputy Chief, supported by three Division Chiefs (North, Central, South), would create statewide oversight. Each fire station would have a Battalion Chief supervising Fire Captains, ensuring consistency and accountability. This structure would allow Division Chiefs to standardize regional operations and report directly to Sacramento. It would also enable smarter use of state funds, prioritizing purchases based on verified needs. Battalion Chiefs would conduct assessments, Division Chiefs would review them, and Sacramento leadership would allocate resources accordingly. If modeled after CAL FIRE, CDCR Fire could evolve into a highly effective emergency response force—ready, reliable, and unified.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-22853

    I believe there is a critical underutilization of the staff talent within the State, and before hiring additional personnel, the existing team dynamics should be analyzed to see how work can be better distributed amongst those already on board.

    August 26, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22689

    Outdated technology. Our tools don't make us work faster or smarter, it makes us work harder and longer which is not doing a good service to the taxpayer.

    August 26, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-25584

    In my division of my department, we have a database to track projects that is so old it uses a platform that is no longer supported. When we tried in the past to replace this with a more functional, modern system, there were so many roadblocks that it was impossible to implement the new system. We are trying again. My suggestion is to make modernizing or upgrading old computer systems easier. While I understand that it can be costly to do so, having old barely functioning systems is even more costly to the citizens of California.

    August 26, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-25386

    The state faces many problems that telework could help alleviate. The state of CA can be a more attractive employer. We could lead in the space instead of trying follow the trends of the private sector. We have a completely different responsibility to the people we serve. Our "profits" are our efficiency and how well we manage our budgets and tax dollars. We could lower cost, by reducing footprints. We can reduce environmental impacts by reducing commutes. Department should evaluate work and task that could be done via telework and in person. With a concerted effort, managers can be prepared, trained, and supported. Expectations can be clear and departments can maintain flexibility. In Person activities should encouraged, scheduled, and deliberate in with goals such as increasing collaboration/comradery. Trainings, in person-meetings, etc. should have the appropriate facilities available. Finally, I think there should be more data available to make better decisions. Surveys for staff and managers to find gaps. Departmental specific dashboards etc.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25584

    Making it easier to upgrade computer systems so that they function better. We have many systems that are old and ineffective.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25906

    I suggest the state adapt a “classification roadmap” that guides state employees on a general career path, differing from the Upward Mobility Program, this roadmap guides from classification to classification, i.e, as a Staff Services Analyst, what courses CalHR, higher education, training events, and skills are recommended to progress to Associate Government Program Analyst. Naturally, this would lead to how to progress from AGSA, to SSMI; from SSMI to SSMII; etc. the training, courses, education, recommended reading. Another example, how to progress from Office Technician, to Business Services, etc. The classification roadmaps could include the Departments that have the specific classifications and recommendations for “assignments” as part of your career path, i.e., a new to the state employee may want to start at a smaller department, then progress as an AGPA to a medium department. Granted this would be a massive undertaking and a classification duty statement can vary greatly based off the agency and its mission, however, I think it could be beneficial to provide that general path as some employees may lack the mentorship from their current department or supervisors or generally not know how to create a path to career within the state.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25678

    We need to streamline common processes. Many levels of requirements and approvals are put in place because of past issues with corruption. There needs to be a better way to deal with the corruption without making everything more difficult, time consuming and expensive for future people. One example is purchasing office supplies is so complex it takes multiple people and hours of time instead of what should be a few minutes online. It should not require special training and multiple levels of approvals to order paper and pens.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-23678

    I want to make things more efficient and improve processes. I feel we are behind other developed countries in infrastructure and I want to help us catch up.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25579

    We can increase efficiency and effectiveness in our work by getting out of our silos both within agencies and across agencies. For example, we can be more efficient in our work and at the same time make it easier to be a business in California by streamlining reporting requirements across the environmental agencies. We can help distribute funding and make it easier to get funding by having one funding arm per agency instead of multiple groups. We could also expand our our community support efforts by encouraging remote work and embedding state workers in communities across California instead of concentrating all jobs in a few small areas, like Sacramento.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25791

    We have a lot of redundancies and yet still many inconsistencies from department to department. I often hear from staff who came from other departments how they function, and it is quite different. Some departments seem marred with duplicative red tape that makes it more difficult to function efficiently and creates additional work that takes up seemingly unnecessary employee time that is not the essential duties. Working so inefficiently and different from department to department appears to waste tax dollars.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-22880

    Purchasing and especially contracting procedures and restrictions inhibit our ability to do our jobs efficiently.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25641

    The Federal Patriot Act makes it very difficult for homeless Californians and Californians who have to live in cars, to open a bank account. However, homeless Californians still need a bank account to apply for housing, obtain services, and apply for jobs and benefits. I would do something to make sure every Californian can open some type of bank account. My Idea: If that means providing an alternate way to verify the identity of homeless people, approved by the state, I would implement that. I would not require a residential address to open a bank account if I knew someone had been homeless. This may require federal approval. Giving homeless people a way to identify themselves helps them apply for public services. This is an important barrier to ending all homelessness.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-23994

    Need to be more tech focused and open to improvements. Certain jobs in IT can be done completely from home which improves productivity and attention to detail. Open to exploring technical advancements for SCO and Medi-Cal systems.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25534

    We are still using some tools and technologies in the State Government that are very old. There are numerous innovative technologies available on the market that can be integrated into the State Government's business, enhancing efficiency and effectiveness in delivering services and projects. Explore possible innovative technologies on the market, conduct risk analysis, and take intelligent risks to implement them within the State Government. Start with some pilot projects, and if they prove useful, implement them across the State Government.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-23994

    training and communication. Mentorships and specific training classes available for each job role in order to advance up the chain and do the current job better.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25666

    A major problem in California’s state workforce is the inefficiency caused by outdated hiring and workplace policies. Rigid minimum qualifications and slow/ouutdated HR protocols block many talented candidates with modern skills from entering public service, which limits innovation and slows progress. An even larger issue is the state’s approach to workplace flexibility. Despite state audits and extensive research showing that remote work increases productivity, saves the state millions in office space costs, reduces emissions, and improves work-life balance, some leaders continue to enforce return-to-office mandates. This is hypocritical for a government that speaks of innovation, equity, and efficiency while ignoring evidence that remote work achieves those goals more effectively than traditional office models. To address these challenges: First, modernize hiring by replacing outdated minimum requirements with skills-based assessments and streamlined digital applications. Second, adopt remote-first policies that align with research and fiscal responsibility, prioritizing cost savings, efficiency, and fairness for workers. Third, strengthen protections against retaliation and ensure leadership training emphasizes equity, accountability, and inclusion. By modernizing hiring and committing to evidence-based remote-first practices, California can move past outdated systems and demonstrate it is serious about equity, innovation, and effective public service.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25495

    Budget deficit

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25623

    Strategic investment planning for state buildings. Following the same standards we make private sector landlords follow. Currently state buildings are given an allotment that is the same year after year. The allotment is not calculated by taking any factors into consideration like inflation, rising interest rates, cost of living increases. The process has slowly driven state buildings into the ground. Old carpet, failing and obsolete building systems all adding up to energy waste and pollution. If you owned a home and rented it to the same tenant for 25 years and put 2% of the rent back into the property in that time, what would it look like? Not good is the answer. Currently DGS only puts back 2% into state buildings! That number needs to be 10% annually or more on older assets. Convicted slum lords in California hover around 5% reinvestment and are put into Receivership Court by their banks. We can do better! Currently DGS takes 98% of the rental income and spends it on unrelated programs. The buildings should get the funding they need before the landlord takes the revenue to spend on other things. The income from a building should first cover its expenses before a landlord takes profits. The practice of a property manager spending the rental income of an asset on other things is called Comingling of Funds. it is illegal and the cause of run-down slums. The plan DGS has been to run them down and then renovate at 200 times the cost of doing regular maintenance ovr time

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-22880

    A lot of funding is spent on maintaining State-owned employee housing. Employee housing is needed in many areas, but the maintenance costs are very high. A cost-benefit analysis may be worthwhile to determine if employees should pay more for rent to help cover maintenance costs. Should highly paid employees pay more for rent than lower paid employees?

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25623

    Proper strategic planning and budgeting by DGS. Hold state buildings to the same standards as the rest of the nation and what we require of private sector landlords. This is simple math, and we have chosen not to follow it. Reinvesting in our own buildings is something we should take pride in. By not doing so we put lives at risk, we waste energy, and we devalue the real estate industry we are part of.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25328

    I would like to address recruitment and retention issues from within the public sector. Lately, public sector workers have been losing important benefits that are used to attract high quality talent and leadership. Using tools like work from home flexibility and remote work will allow state units to attract top talent from sectors that are reverting back to RTO mandates.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25413

    I would address the issues of continual education for state employees. To keep workforce in shape we need encouragement and incentives to drive desire to professional education and training.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25242

    In my role I've observed decades worth of files and data being digitally stored that no one has any meaningful use for or knowledge of to make the data worth retaining. There appears to be no interest, energy, or time available to reorganize, archive, and/or eliminate this unnecessary data and it's an accepted nuisance that any file search results may provide both current and 25 year old documents. I see this as an inefficiency for two reasons: the monetary cost of securing and maintaining storage for these files that by default are considered valueless and the obscuring effect these unhelpful documents have when trying to find information. I think files should be audited more regularly to ensure content is kept highly organized and easily discoverable, with unhelpful 'junk' documents archived or eliminated to reduce what is being retained year after year.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25328

    We contract way too much to outside vendors who offer snake oil in terms of professional development and process improvement. We need to do better harvesting great ideas from within the public sector instead of relying on external vendors who do not know us. Here's an idea for sure! Offer a Project Management pathway for professional development that is state-certified! We shouldn't need to go through private vendors to certify that we know how to do project management...

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-24794

    One of the biggest challenges the state faces is people feeling they will be chastised for speaking out and the lack of communication between high level management/executives and the people actually doing the work (not managers overseeing work being done, but the actual people at department and agency level doing the work) along with the vendor community seeking to contract with the state. Politicians pushing their objectives and the people making the rules and regulations have either never done the work or haven't done it in so long they do not understand the impact of the policies and procedures, or whether it will make sense to the end user applying the rule. Which also impacts having qualified training (or lack thereof). State training is normally developed and administered by people who are far removed from having done the work. Maybe there should be more in person, role-based training with SME's to assist. Maybe if each department had 1 non-management person (kind of like a SB/DVBE advocate) from each department/agency be a procurement representative to present department issues at a bimonthly forum (like SCAN). There should procurement meetings for the department/agency representatives where new laws that have been enacted can be reviewed to ensure understanding where they can have a voice to speak about how those are impacting their jobs, etc.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-24445

    One of the most inefficient things my department sees is the push to transition to electric vehicles. We have staff all throughout the state, and charging stations (at least stations that work) are not always available to our staff. Also, it takes staff several hours to charge electric vehicles. The Ford Lightning electric truck can take up to 14 hours to charge, so while it's charging we're either down a vehicle and that workload or we have to force other staff in a gas truck to double-up on their route and we need to pay them overtime. The First Purchasing Mandate says that we can request an exemption from an EV from DGS once we meet our 50% mandate, but DGS says they're being pushed to put more state EVs on the road because of the Governor's executive order so they deny the exemption requests. What are departments supposed to do? Pay their staff overtime to do the work a person in an EV can't? Pay for a hotel and meals when field staff are stranded because they can't charge their vehicle? Rent short-term rentals to try and offset the downtime with EVs? None of this is efficient, and it costs double. Also, has anyone done an analysis on the cost of an EV vs the cost of a gas vehicle? Usually about 2-3 times as expensive for an EV when departments are also facing budget reductions. This seems like such a waste of taxpayer money, to make government more inefficient and more expensive so citizens pay more and get less. Can DGS be more helpful on exemption requests?

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25827

    Wasteful government spending - such as return to office mandates. Also, spending resources creating a platform like this that is supposed to be for state workers but that is going to prohibit state workers/Californians from speaking on return to office mandates.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-24445

    It doesn't make sense that state departments who have the same IT needs all have to separately go through the IT process with DGS and CDT. Electronic timesheets for example. All departments need them, shouldn't there be some sort of standard system? Several are using Tempo but must all go through the same business analysis, requirements identification, development, etc so that they capture the state's pay rules, tie into FI$Cal, etc. If there can be a statewide effort on projects like this so they're only done once and not done individually by every department that wants to streamline a process?

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25710

    Legacy and proprietary software systems used within the State create layers of inefficiency and encourage patching of broken systems rather than identification of better alternatives. The State becomes trapped by sunk costs as it is hard to tabulate the real value of employees and stakeholders time that is lost to using inefficient systems.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25627

    Sharing data amongst agencies is cumbersome and often requires emails and updates to multiple siloed databases. If we had interagency databases shared amongst the agencies that need access to that data we could eliminate duplicate databases and generate reports in real time automatically.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25313

    CDCR's hiring practices take a considerable amount of time from advertisement to start date. Processes need to be streamlined, from background clearance, live scan, reference checks to approval to appoint process. CDCR is under scrutiny for budget deficit however we are being tasked to do more with less. Safety and security comes at a cost and that needs to be recognized. the cost to house the incarcerated have increased significantly especially with an aging population needing costly medical care

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25312

    Employment Development Department (EDD) notices and explanatory materials are difficult for claimants to understand. Materials should be rewritten to meet standards similar to those outlined in Turner v. McMahon. If claimants better understood these materials it could avoid unnecessary appeals.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25314

    California Government is just to big and the "pieces" don't talk to each other. The California State Law Enforcement Union represents over 10 different work groups ( Special Agents, ABC Agents, CHP Dispatchers, Fish and Wildlife Wardens- to name a few) none of these groups have anything to do with each other and they are not efficient. the cost of all these work groups should be reduced and where they can eliminated.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25314

    example of better efficiency: keep, even bolster, the licensing aspect for Alcoholic Beverage Control licenses- but eliminate enforcement. Agents are expensive and the misdemeanors that they investigate are often times already investigated by local police and sheriffs- Salary alone is over 10 million a year this does not include vehicles, weapons, and other equipment. If the locals handled the ABC violations and followed up with the ABC legal dept where would still be enforcement but millions in savings.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-24319

    Because of the high cost of housing around cities where most jobs and government offices are located, there is substantial inequality of opportunity for many Californians who cannot afford to live in metro centers. By shifting to remote offerings, such as the ability to participate in hearings and government decisions by videoconference, we can ensure that people who live in more distant (and often more affordable) areas are given equal opportunities to have their voices heard and contribute to shared goals. Remote offerings further align with California’s climate goals, avoiding tons of CO₂ that would otherwise be generated as Californians waste time and money driving to formerly in-person meetings. Videoconference offerings also help us include Californians with disabilities who may struggle to join us in person due to challenges with transit and accessibility. Since the COVID-19 pandemic, many state agencies have vastly enlarged the group of individuals who can participate through the change to remote and videoconference meetings and hearings. This conversion to a more accessible meeting style should be expanded so more people can benefit.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-23564

    Problem: In many state departments, including my own, productivity is often hindered by rigid processes where documents must pass through multiple layers of review—sometimes six or more—before finalization. This not only delays critical deliverables but can render documents outdated or irrelevant by the time they are approved. Additionally, there is a noticeable gap in collaboration and communication between subject matter experts (SMEs) and executive leadership. This disconnect often leads to a reactive environment where teams are constantly putting out fires instead of proactively identifying and addressing potential issues. Solution: Introduce a tiered review system that empowers mid-level managers and SMEs to approve routine documents without requiring executive-level sign-off. This would reduce bottlenecks, increase accountability, and allow leadership to focus on strategic priorities. Implementing digital workflow tools with built-in version control and approval tracking can also enhance transparency and speed. Establish regular cross-functional strategy sessions between SMEs and executives to align on goals, anticipate challenges, and co-create solutions. This would shift the culture from reactive to proactive, encouraging innovation and long-term planning. Additionally, investing in leadership training that emphasizes trust in employee expertise and decision-making can help break down hierarchical barriers and build a more agile, responsive organization.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-22946

    As a former private sector employee, I would say that state processes are much slower to change in order to keep up with existing technology. We have fewer instances of process documentation and very little consistent messaging around project processes and procedures. This is sometimes passed off as "nuance" between projects but the procedures should be largely the same for substantively similar tasks and no task is so nuanced that it can not be documented for the staff working on a specific project. Training is often one person showing another with no supervisor oversight or written documentation, so failures in a process persist through generations of staff. Some processes are intentionally obscured to mask waste or inefficiency, where they could and should be transparent and streamlined. Much of our technology was established in the 1990s and has never been updated or improved, despite being obsolete. Tech staff retention needs to be addressed and change management for technology needs to be better handled. Rank and file staff needs to be empowered to learn and understand technology. By reducing turnover in IT, training staff and providing written manuals for processes, and improving technological literacy we can bring our processes into the 2000s.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-24974

    I think clear communication is a challenge, with the bureaucracy and so many scared to say the wrong thing so nothing is said instead. It really limits efficacy.

    August 26, 2025
  • Guest-25344

    We need digital (mobile-optimized) data intake. The state has SO MANY forms (applications, recertifications, licenses, grants, etc.). Californians should be able to do most of their interaction with the state on their phone (this will make backend data more accurate, too). Their identity should be known at the state, so programs across agencies should share their respective data on the backend so a Californian doesn’t have to enter the same data every time they interact with a new program.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-22454

    When I first joined the state employee workforce, I was told how easy it is to advance through the ranks, but in my experience it was harder to advance unless I was willing to leave my branch/division/department. I think that promoting in place should be implemented more when employees have been in a particular place long enough. This requires management to be more open to and learned on the solutions available. But it also requires more of a team mentality and that is hard when people have to keep moving in order to advance. I think providing more genuine opportunities for colleagues to work and grow together will help.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25941

    Hi. It is not a secret that in the next 5 years, technology will evolve at a much faster pace than ever before. Is our beloved State of California ready for serving our future generation? Do we know what that future may look like and what the demands are? It is certian, that If we do not plan for it now, we all lose. Thank you.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25885

    I've found it so insane that we have to rely on another department to move up and/or down positions in another department all because of "seniority". One person who has shown and put themselves out there more than another and that person don't get anything because they don't have the seniority, but that other person will get a position they don't deserve. It's not fair at all. Also, too I feel like if a person accepts a management position they should be required to takes classes and learn how to appropriately talk to and handle staff. Multiple complaints and nothing to be done because of "seniority" again. This program or "ways" need to be looked at again. Ultimately it should depend on management to determine who moves up or down.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24626

    Problem: 80/20 rule with staffing. 20% do the majority of the work, 80% do bare minimum. Solution: Radical, trailblazing idea to overhaul state service and incentivize employees at all levels. Throw out what you about state service entirely. Clean slate. Ready? Here is the concept: Departments have employment bands. Imagine layered cake - at the bottom is entry-level band, then above that is the supervisor band, the analyst band, manager and, chief band. That is it for positions. Each band incorporates all jobs for those positions. Every 8-12 weeks, staff can rotate to another job - let's call them "missions". To move up a band, you have to complete X number of missions and have led X number of missions successfully. Every employee must complete at least 2 missions annually. This kills silos, creates an innovative and growth mindset culture, creates competition, provides constant learning and gamification, etc. Each mission you complete you get a deliverable in hand to show the work you did with a score for your input. There is a list and those with the most missions and missions led, with good scores, are at the top, a constant changing leaderboard. This leaderboard is used to decide who moves up a level when there's an opening. Pay scales with tenure, not position, so you could decide not to move up a band. Folks would need to be focused, quick learners, with high accountability, excellent collaboration... tune in to my next post to see how we find these employees!

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-22339

    I would like to see more collaboration to streamline convoluted processes between Department of State Hospitals and CalHR. I would like to see staff's willingness to remove large barriers to increase results i.e. update minimum qualifications for positions. I would like to see, if there's isn't already, a time commitment to update MQ's. i.e. every 5-8 years.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24626

    Problem: Hiring excellent employees is a struggle and failing folks on probation is not common enough. let's reduce the waste in our largest spending bucket statewide - people and work to maximize the ROI on that expense by ensuring quality staff is hired. Solution: No more SOQs or interviews. Instead, we have customer experience workshops where sprints are designed to challenge and assess potential candidates. They will be scored on their creativity, problem-solving skills, curiosity, collaboration, accountability, leadership, etc. Those scoring a minimum score or above are offered positions where they may be best suited within the department. Structured rubrics with behavioral anchors and blind scoring could be used to reduce bias. Basically, show me the value you will bring to our teams. Innovative sprint outcomes may be adopted at the department and accredited to the new candidate as part of their company onboarding and give them a solid start in their careers.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25474

    California has many managers within the state government. However, there are few leaders amongst the managers from my viewpoint. And the staff of these managers have no voice. This causes both the management and teams members to stagnate and lose sight of serving the public; eventually many work environments become stressful and toxic. My idea is to one: give the voice to the team members as this forum does, but allow them to rate their managers or supervisors yearly. Two: measure the amount of turnover on a yearly basis under the tenure of a manager or supervisor. Managers, could be put on yearly training assignments where they can serve in different state organizations. Based on the metrics after 2 or 3 years use the metrics to determine if a manager is effective. If a manager doesn't prove to be effective after this tenure they should be released, re-assigned or demoted. If they are measured to be effective, can they can then be assigned to a permanent position but can be re-evaluated after another 3 years. This is the basis of my idea. Currently, when a person occupies a managerial seat, there is very little to prevent them from becoming a bottle-neck to progress and there's nothing the team can do about this. I've seen millions of tax payers dollars go to waste because of this scenario. I have seen many managers who are micro-manage out of fear and ignorance and the staff is at their mercy which is a moral killer. There is no perfect system but we need new direction

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-22339

    I would like to see an improvement in paying local vendors faster to attract more in an effort to support our local economies. I would like to see 3rd party vendors i.e. DVBE be removed from the vendor list as they are doubling supply costs and gouging the state. Directly ordering supplies from Amazon is a more fiscally responsible practice.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-22877

    I think that staff should have the ability to anonymously review management annually. I believe empowering staff to give management feedback would improve leadership and foster a culture of transparency.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-23204

    This comment is highly specific to my division. So, I am not sure how relevant this particular issue will be to others. The first thing I would do is get rid of or upgrade the two outdated Call Report forms we utilize and condense it to only one upgraded Call Report form that can be downloaded from NMLS and uploaded to our MTFD database. Before the transition to NMLS ( Nationwide Multistate Licensing System & Registry), the licensee was only required to submit one Call Report form. This form was powered by Microsoft Excel with macros built in. The form was very glitchy and required the licensee to make multiple submissions at times when the wrong button was selected or the form was saved improperly. Now, the licensee must submit two Call Reports, one in an Excel format and the other using the NMLS format. The NMLS Call Report no longer has the built in error checking or data validation of the original Call Report. So now examiners have to waste time having the licensee correct and resubmit Call Reports for minor housekeeping issues. For example, the Outstanding Transmission Liability should agree on both the Balance Sheet Page and Permissible Investment Page. In the past, the Call Report could not be saved or submitted until the corresponding amounts are in agreement. Further, the NMLS Call Report should be updated to reflect both year to date balances and quarter-to-date balances. The licensee should not have to submit a second Call Report with this information.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-23530

    The modus operandi at the state is "quick fixes" and treating the symptoms of problems rather than getting at the root cause. So, we end up with broken systems covered in Band-Aids rather than systems that work. We need to really look closely at what is happening and make changes- slow down to speed up. First fixing our support systems and then our mission systems. Usually, you would want to spend all your time and effort on the mission, but you cannot make the mission process (why the department exists) better if the process to make the mission process better is siloed and broken. For example- if it takes 2 years to fix a typo on an external form, how do you expect to do any larger improvements for the customer experience or streamline the process for internal stakeholders? This is the reality of my current job with the State.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24903

    Frivolous, unnecessary spending and paralysis by over-analysis. Too many levels of bureaucracy, and too many opinions to seek out before decisions can be made. Centralize purchasing authority within departments for furniture, IT, and office supplies. Every unit does not need to keep their own stockpile of supplies, nor control possession over certain state assets. Roll out a "How-To" process to teach people how to digitize files so we can eliminate expensive rent spending for paper file storage. Maximize the use of office space by configuring smarter. Yes, use Lean Six Sigma principles. We teach it. Let's actually use it.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25437

    Training and transparency. As new employees to the state we do not know why, what, or where. If staff were told what we are doing, why we are doing it, how it affects others jobs, where each department is and the roles that each one has, it would bring a clear understanding of our job and how best to interact with each department

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-22199

    I feel a big problem is the lack of collaboration with other state agencies about processes that need improvement. I feel like we work in silos, and it would help us all tremendously if we could share more information with other agencies to find out ways that may work better. One solution could be to have forums where people with similar responsibilities/titles could meet and help one another.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25887

    Many of the processes used at my department were not built with a focus on efficiently serving our customers. As such, there are often multiple wasteful and non-necessary steps both employees and customers are “required” to take before a customer transaction can be completed. We have failed to analyze and update many of these processes using new methodologies, ways of thinking, and tools that are already readily available to us. It has happened on several occasions that when an improvement is introduced it fails to receive the support that is needed for successful implementation because some members of management fear change and/or feel that they cannot devote employee hours to implementation and testing. I believe systematic and proven process improvement techniques (like Lean Six Sigma, PDCA, Total Quality Management, etc.) performed by state-employed process-improvement specialists that have the support and resources that can be provided by department heads and agency-level management could act to improve inefficient processes across that state.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25854

    I know we are looking for ways to be "more efficient" (spend less) but that has lead to poor service for the people of California. We always look to reducing staff costs when the state budget is tight. The way we staff our smaller departments is not resilient and the shocks are killing us. As a result, analyst work has been pushed up to supervisory/management levels, because the available staff to do analyst work is getting smaller and smaller. I'm hoping that the reclassification of the analyst series on will help, but it won't fix it. AI and technology solutions can provide some help, but they do not replace thinking people. You want to know why there is no decent onboarding, or written procedures, just learn what you can? Why so many grievances? Why it's so hard to learn state contracting or Fi$Cal? Why no updated policy or streamlined procedures? Why duty statements are over 10 years old? Why no innovation? Because the mid-level leadership and front line analysts that would be part of the team that develops those things, that can point out the real problems in the system, can only do day-to-day survival tasks, jumping from fire to fire. We have positions that are vacant for months b/c of budget, the work is spread out over fewer and fewer burnt-out staff, then we lose those positions because they were vacant. Succession planning is nice but staffing resilience and system capacity would be better.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24717

    One of the biggest issues I have seen in my service time is the fact that we are such a reactionary institution rather than a proactive institution. It has been one of my longest and deepest frustrations from my time in rank-and-file to my time in management. I would like to see us spend more time engaging with our employees from the top down and having thoughtful discussions on how some of these problems can be solved rather than allowing just the executives to make them. I was an executive and having sat in that chair, I am well aware that we do not have all the answers. It is the boots on the ground individuals that have so much to add to how we can be ahead of the game in some of these budget issues, RTW, and other critical issues we are dealing with.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24277

    There should be one statewide system for employees to have an account that contains their information. When needing to fill out any form, they should be able to log into this account, search for and select the form, then it would auto-populate with their information. Once complete, the form should be sent to the agency requesting it. Agencies should also be able to assign forms to employees to complete. Staff should be able to log in to this account and view every form submitted. A system like this should be available to all citizens in California so they don't need to type their name, address, etc., on every form to access services.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24513

    I think we should be able to work remotely. We have proven we can do it. I actually work more when I work remotely. But for those of us that have families, it gives us a great work/life balance, with less stress, more time for life and family.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25760

    The way to reduce carbon emissions and save tax payer dollars by making full time telework permanent for state employees who have worked remote succesfully.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-21665

    management can either be a conduit for change or a hindrance to change, but with training, i believe management can be champions for change.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25458

    If the employees are not doing their job, it should be a smoother process to retrain those employees. Information needs to be more fluid between agencies within and outside of each agency.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-23961

    Miscommunication (or inefficient communication) slows down and delays public work. Creating shared email group and databases can help speed up notifications of newest updates, however it is up to the individual employee to keep up with and update fellow teams.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25643

    The primary problem I like to address is homelessness in Sacramento, California especially those who have mental illness on the streets. Build/create Treatment Centers to house mentally ill patients so they can receive medications along with balanced whole food, nutritious meals until they are stable to live on their own with government aid or with family posting no dangers to others. Besides counseling, medications, is important mentally ill people have nourishing and nutritious food on a daily basis to improve their mental health and physical health. "No health, no wealth" - Ralph Emerson "Let thy food be thy medicine, let thy medicine be thy food." - Hippocrates

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25526

    Communicating to current and future owners of Mobile Home units on the proper and correct way to transfer ownership of these units. Multiple times I encounter situations where new owners bought MH units without proper documentation and without due diligence as to who the prior owners were, creating problems for them to register as the new owners.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25526

    Perhaps sending related printed material, flyers, FAQs, etc., with the annual registration or tax renewals.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25436

    Excessive bureaucracy. Outdated Technology, laws, statutes, manuals, etc. Dollar amount thresholds in many cases are not updated to reflect inflation or cost of living increases. Outdated software programs that create more manual processes needed to accomplish tasks. Allocation of funding towards deferred maintenance of equipment and buildings. Everything turns into an emergency once it's broken because no consideration is given for deferred maintenance. Government is always reactive vs proactive, and this costs time and prevents employees from getting their jobs done when equipment breaks down and the bureaucracy involved to get it repaired. Lack of resources or resources that don't fulfill their obligations or doesn't respond to emails or communications (Departments that have oversight not responding to communications in a timely manner or at all in some cases). Better HR processes to allow new staff to be trained before exiting staff leave. The state has zero regard for this, and each new employee is left to fend for themselves and try to figure out what the person they replaced was doing. This causes a ripple effect with everyone this person works with and creates more work for all involved. Departments not meeting their own requirements and posted completion promises. For example, a department quotes a 10-business day review time, but reality is 30-45+ days is the normal.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25458

    Communication, especially between different state departments. Employees that are not performing to the standard are not reprimanded and their workload falls on everyone else.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25508

    Need a weighed promotion system. This means it has to include time in service, time in grade, performance reports, interview, accommodations, and promotional test results all graded up to 100 points total.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25626

    Many rural counties have school districts that are required by law to opt into our programs, but they do this individually and are overwhelmed with the administration and compliance requirements involved. It would be beneficial if the State offered the counties some financial incentive to sponsor the program for the many smaller districts, so that they would save $ on staffing at the small district, but also the State would need fewer staff for administration and compliance to run the program. This could be a one-time incentive or an incentive built into participation in the program. So, instead of 5-10 individual small districts, there would be one. Our programs are so complex, that some would rather not take part and collect the federal dollars than deal with the red tape.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25823

    Processes for review and approval should be streamlined. An online portal system should be created for each state agency vs requiring state employees to fill out a multitude of state and department PDF forms which would reduce the review time and time spent routing items for approval, returned for editing, etc.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24909

    The absence of a flexible telework policy. Extensive empirical research links remote work to significant gains in productivity, higher job satisfaction, and reduced staff turnover. Furthermore, adopting a modern telework policy offers substantial fiscal benefits. It would enable the State to significantly reduce overhead costs by downsizing office space. The resulting savings could be reallocated to core mission priorities—such as our software and training needs—or invested in innovative downtown space utilization projects that better serve the public.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25823

    Any grants that are released from the state should require fiscal reports but just as important, progress reports. We should not be handing out state dollars without specifically knowing how and what those dollars are used for. This is inconsistent. Some grants have it in statute that an independent evaluation is done and others do not, it makes no sense. Require state departments to develop specific ways they are measuring the cost/benefit analysis of distributing funds

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25819

    Working remotely has huge benefits to accomplishing my important work. I've started my position post pandemic, and the office I've been assigned has 2 other coworkers, which makes the many meetings I have untenable. I cannot concentrate in my office nor can I provide my services with two other calls in the background. Often, I meet with tribals and it feels disrespectful not to be able to give them the privacy they need. Working in my office also drastically reduces my quality of life. My childcare is in the opposite direction from my office making pick up and drop off a burden. My quite home office allows me to concentrate on work and lowers the stress of my job because I can get my work done. I am healthier because I'm comfortable exercising at home and can make my own lunches. I joined the state during the pandemic and I have never had trouble building relationships and collaborating with my coworkers. I've successfully been mentored and mentored while working remotely. I believe that returning to the office will decrease my productivity and that of my fellow coworkers while increasing our stress.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25819

    Continuing California's groundbreaking work from home policy will increase government efficiency and help worker be more effective.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25634

    I have seen similar feedback shared. My time at my Department has highlighted just how hard it is to implement change. Workgroups, steering committees, SME participation, requests and requisitions, policies, procedures, IT procurement all making it nearly impossible for our programs to function in a modern sense. Why? It was always done ‘this’ way. Bureaucracy is good sometimes. Helps to stave off rash and quick decisions that could be costly. HOWEVER, this comes at a cost of being agile, providing better services and improvement. Working in silos, being beholden to general fund and tax payer acceptance/willingness to fund our work is frustrating. Funded or no we are just trying to do our best to provide for you!

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25438

    Efficiency and sharing innovative ideas across departments - It has been proven in many cases that working in diverse team settings and across various projects helps individuals gain a broader perspective, encounter specific scenarios, and build the experience necessary for career advancement. At the same time, teams benefit from fresh managerial ideas and a willingness to take calculated risks that often come with different leadership styles. Therefore, rotating managers and supervisors between departments can provide advantages on both sides. It fosters innovation, encourages the exchange of ideas, and allows successful practices from one department or agency to be implemented in another. This cross-pollination of knowledge and experience contributes to overall organizational growth and improvement.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25457

    I developed a spreadsheet to improve project scheduling and cost estimating. Currently, it is used in District 8, but I hope to transform it into a web-based application to enhance project delivery statewide.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25450

    I would keep duty statements current and honest and not gray the lines between SSAs, AGPAs, and lower-level support staff.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25471

    I'd like clearer communication from the state over employment/HR concerns. The recent agreement with SEIU left me confused over what actually was happening to my paycheck, and neither the state nor SEIU were helpful in determining that. I also received a promotion-related NOPA around the same time that was similarly opaque, and spent many hours back and forth with HR trying to figure out what the contradictory statements attached to it meant.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-21746

    First, a few problems affecting individuals with disabilities: 1. Currently, the ADA and other state laws do not apply to rental housing (especially single-family homes) as well as new homes. Because of this an individual with a disability cannot get a home that has a ramp, walk-in shower, light-based fire alarm, or tactile controls. Many of these adaptations are available by default when accessing public facilities and commercial organizations open to the public. Yet, in the context of housing people with disabilities have to spend tens of thousands to make these conversions. There are exceptions for affordable housing, not all people are eligible for these opportunities. The State should make an effort to make sure accessibility is improved, so that homes are available that meet these requirements and the option to make these changes in new homes by lowering barriers. 2. Many states including Washington, Minnesota, Tennessee, and Massachusetts have Medicaid working disabled programs that operate without income or asset limits. This assists individuals with disabilities would like to work but cannot because insurance does not cover services like long-term care. To ensure that these programs remain financially viable the states have imposed premiums of at least 7% of a person's income. California should consider similar programs. Second, the State should encourage remote work for employees. It improves retention, mental health and employee well-being.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25735

    One of the biggest challenges I see is the complexity and inefficiency of internal processes. Employees often spend significant time navigating redundant approvals, unclear responsibilities, and siloed systems. This slows down work and delays how quickly Californians receive the services they need. Ideas to Solve It • Streamline workflows by removing unnecessary steps and defining clear escalation paths. • Invest in modern, shared systems so departments can collaborate more effectively instead of duplicating efforts. • Empower employees with more training so decisions can be made at the right level, without excessive bottlenecks. By simplifying processes and fostering collaboration, employees can spend more time focusing on service delivery and less time on administrative hurdles ultimately improving outcomes for residents.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25229

    As a newer state employee with prior experience in the private sector, I have noticed a significant gap in leadership style and workplace culture. Too often, leadership at the state seems to operate from a place of fear rather than support. Leaders seem to focus on blame, micromanagement, and unclear expectations rather than encouragement, collaboration, and clear communication. It feels as though i'm working in my parent's and grandparent's generation. This has created an environment where employees feel disempowered, disconnected, and hesitant to share ideas or challenge the status quo. To build a more effective and modern workforce, I think California should invest heavily in leadership development and hold leaders accountable for fostering a supportive, transparent, and progressive culture. Leaders should be given the tools to lead the same virtually as they do in-person. Leaders need to be trained to authentically care about their team members, communicate virtually or in-person with kindness and respect, and engage with employees in a way that feels human and positive, rather than robotic and forced. With new generations entering the workforce, leadership needs to adapt to meet evolving workplace expectations. Shifting from a punitive to a supportive leadership model would empower employees to engage fully, collaborate openly, and drive meaningful innovation across state government.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25880

    I work in the public education arena. Not so much a problem, but a way of doing work - too much talk and not a whole lot of action.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25460

    Redundancy and outdated procedures. It's 2025 and some of the state's procedures need to be revisited and revised to reflect the modern era. I have worked for three different agencies in the last 8 years or so and I can say that all of them could be modernized in some way. We need to pivot away from using fax machines, storing paperwork, accepting the lowest bid possible (you get what you pay for), things like that. California needs to invest in it's self.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25476

    Departments work in silos, not together. There is no systemic way of sharing institutional knowledge, so innovation happens in uneven pockets and archaic processes persist. There should be experiential learning opportunities across departments for people in job-alike roles (e.g., HR, budgets, accounting, contracting, IT, EEO, regulations, etc.) so that even new State employees can develop real networks and meaningful relationships outside of conference/workshop settings. We need to say "one team" less and act like one team more.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25357

    Claimants inability to get in touch with Unemployment or Disability representatives over the phone. We are modernizing systems which allows us to better utilize technology which is great. I think continuing those efforts along with hiring more call center staff is essential for delivering timely service to californians.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25713

    You should be able to navigate to the mission, vision, values, and executive team of each department within two clicks from the home page.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25405

    Employee morale has significantly dropped over the last three years as the state tries to implement a one size fits all RTO solution, in the midst of multiple PLPs, rising benefits costs, older retirement ages, and low pay increases that do not keep up with the cost of living in California.
