State employees want to be more efficient and help California budget issues but doing it on state workers' backs is only going to drive out good, marketable employees. Consider implementing all department surveys to assess productivity, collaboration, efficiency, and department needs when making decisions about RTO. Transparency on actual costs of RTO- DGS and CalHR's responses in the budget subcommittee hearing that there was essentially no way to know the costs was disingenuous and disrespectful to state employees. Assessments for cutting costs to programs for people who do not work or unused/unmoderated social programs, before cutting pay of hard-working state employees.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25266

    I have been a state employee for 20+ years and have seen many Bills passed without the resources and input by Program(s) required to implement the Bill included which causes ineffectiveness in implementation process and results in an increase in cost of staff time and cost to the regulated communities. I had the privilege of providing input to the language in an Assembly bill in 2019 to the legislative team, which created a more robust Bill that Program was aware of and knew ahead of time how they could implement it in a more effective manner and outlined any resources that would be needed to do so. To solve this problem I think that the authors of these Bills should do a exploritory process to get a better idea of the Program(s) their Bill would be effecting and have converstions with a Staff member(s) to have language the is clear and implementable, which makes it easier when the Bill is passed for Staff to implement.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25333

    Procurement and contracting have onerous rules and are time consuming. Quality and fitness of purpose should be factored into the equation apart of other considerations. Shifting the responsibility of vendor evaluation and selection back to the authorities within the requesting entities would help reduce delays and minimize the risk of mis or inadequate procurement.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24887

    I feel the procedures are not well defined and are changed too often by control agencies, which makes it difficult for admin staff. I also feel that admin staff are not as effective due to pressures from multiple layers of management within departments. I also feel there is a growing usage of high level "specialist" jobs that are used to retain seasoned staff rather than for the actual business usage. This excess of high-level specialist jobs take responsibilities away from the supervisors and management staff, while putting more pressure on the entry level staff to do more with less (leading to more errors and more labor hours). This has also caused discourse amongst R&F staff. I feel there is too many middle management positions, which is preventing staff from receiving consistent direction and guidance. Decision making is taking longer which cause more delays. I feel the HR Manual from CalHR was a good start, but the manual need to continue to develop that resource (and CalHR needs to be given the support to do so).

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25409

    Our government websites are not built to meet the needs of the people. They are built so a hierarchy of executives in government are satisfied that *their* needs are met. This usually results in sites that are unreadable, topics the public doesn't care about, and services that are hard to navigate. Sites and services should be held to the state's Plain Language Equity Standard, meaning grade 8 reading level or below. They should be built with a human-centered design process. And they should be continually tested with real users and improved. The measure of success should be, "Are the people we serve happy with this?" We should frequently check user feedback, Google analytics, and participation rates to know if our delivery of the service is working or not. And if it's not, we should fix it.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25388

    There is no room for promotion unless we want to move to a different part of the State. I was told once you are in the State, you are set but then if you want to move departments or reach your pay cap at your current position. There is no motivation for higher ups to move you. Which as an employee, doesn't motivate me to work harder. I work in Admin and I feel that often Headquarters and the Districts seem to be working against each other. There are so many systems that don't speak to each other, rules change without warning or training. The solutions would include either extending pay caps or make promoting or moving around your own District easier. We need less systems that actually communicate and work. I feel like once I understand how to do something, it's changed in Headquarters, we at Districts don't find out until we are told we did it wrong.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25540

    I know this won't be a popular opinion, but I'd like to see LLMs integrated into workflows with the objective of reducing labor costs over time. I wouldn't want to see it displace anybody already employed, I'd like to see a workforce reduction through attrition. I'd want departments to put teams together that consist of employees of all levels who go through every workflow function to determine what can be automated or streamlined with an LLM. I wouldn't want it rolled out until it was tested by folks not on the original team. I know for my job, about 40% of what I do could easily be automated.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24688

    The primary problem I would address is the delegation process. There are too many checks and balances which address the same liability and concerns via many different documents and protocols all addressing the same issues and concerns and not adding value to the process. In order to process design exceptions there are so many software, document types, sharing platforms, and not a cohesive process that streamlines and minimizes the documentation process and signatures required. There needs to be better tracking of documents, fewer software, and a more streamlined process followed by both the consultants and the reviewers to achieve any modicum of efficiency. I would suggest singular core documents focusing on the projects and their exceptions with varied signature pages depending on the level of delegation. I would also suggest a singular software capable of handling the sharing and evolution of this document and the associated plans that would allow for real time sharing of revisions, corrections, and modification between consultants, delegated employees, and upper management with final approval authority.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-21851

    As a taxpayer and state employee the primary problem I would address is wasteful spending of tax dollars on unnecessary office space. Allowing remote work wherever possible will greatly reduce the amount of money wasted on building leases, maintenance, repairs, electricity, internet, gas, water, office supplies, soap, cleaners, paper towels, toilet paper, etc. Remote work allows for a more diverse workforce and provides opportunity to rural communities throughout the state, mothers, and people with disabilities. It improves productivity by reducing office distractions and stress caused from commuting. For neurodivergent folks working in the office environment can be very stressful and downright overwhelming. The sounds, smells, lighting, temperature, masking, etc. impact the ability to focus leading to more mistakes, work being done slower, burnout, etc. Not to mention all the physical, mental, emotional, and personal issues caused by the stress of it all. It reduces carbon emissions, vehicle miles traveled, traffic congestion, and safety risks by having fewer people on the road, thus reducing funds spent on responding to crashes. It improves employee morale by allowing time that would be spent commuting to be spent with family, friends, etc. Shifting to the electronic/virtual world wherever possible will speed up processing times across the board. Emails are faster and save more money than snail mail & provide proof of delivery. Happier workers = better customer service.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-23509

    There is no motivation for workers to work any harder than anyone else. The union ensures that all workers are treated equally if you do your job or not. Since there is no reward for working hard and there is no punishment for not doing your job, this tends to ensure that even that most dedicated employees tend to tap out and stop putting in any sort of extra effort or even leave the state for a job that actually appreciates and rewards hard work. This leads to a large disenfranchised population of workers doing the bare minimum since that is all that is required of them.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-21925

    Technology is way out of date; it's like being stuck in the 1990s. The work is very manual, redundant, duplicate, and time-consuming for menial tasks. 1) Having an updated timesheet system requiring signature only with a few modifications from the employee and contain all information within that application would save so much time! 2) Having a system where information from cross-funcational teams can be placed, assigned, emailed and/or linked to documents and information, and track progress would be so much for efficient. Like Salesforce, ASANA, or other type of communication database. 3) Approval processes are multi-layer and take WAY too long often causing work to be delayed. Having a way to make approvals easier, quicker, and in one application (as mentioned in #2) might move approval processes along more quickly.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25306

    How can we increase investment into meeting our forest health goals? We are not even meeting our current goal of 1 million acres/year (burning piles from several years ago doesn't count!), and can't expect to get to 2.5 million acres/year by 2045 if we don't do something different. This should include an all-of-government approach, but there are clearly walls preventing us from getting to real solutions (otherwise the private marketplace would have solved this!).

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25334

    Overhaul management training. Excellent analysts do not typically equate to excellent managers. The mandatory 80-hour supervisor training does not set up new managers well enough to be good leaders. They are often underprepared and have no idea what to do to help coach and train (especially new) employees. Many of them only promote for the extra compensation and don't care about their team excelling.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25299

    Communication from the top down is not clear. Often times the message construed from the Executives is not the same message/meaning which makes expectations unclear.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-23509

    As a tax collecting agency, it is way too easy for people to lie, cheat and steal to avoid paying taxes. There is nothing more frustrating than being a law-abiding taxpayer, and watching people take advantage of the system and steal more in tax revenue in one year then I will likely earn in my lifetime. People just start new corporations or other entities and ultimately have no personal responsibility after not paying any taxes for several years. The system is way too easy to take advantage of and it very demoralizing to watch it be abused decade after decade and all they would have to do is make it easier to go after known cheaters and remove some of the protections corporations or other entities have and hold the owners responsible for cheating.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25346

    When it comes to Non-Industrial Disability Insurance (NDI), why can't excluded state employees file all claim paperwork online like SDI? There is so much room for error to rely on paper mail and CalHR to manage a disability claim for a individual staff. SO much gets lost in the paper trail and having to chase down emails, attachments and snail mail with vital health information. The current workflow requires claim participants (Patient, Physician, etc.) to MAIL in claim documents, those documents are then processed by hand and an email exchange happens between CalHR and EDD. room for error includes rotating CalHR staff and EDD staff, lost documents in email, PII shared via email, having to resubmit paperwork due to error and extending the time to receive benefits, etc. The EDD claim system in digital for Californians and some State staff. Why not make it digital for ALL Californians and CA State staff? Save time, money, heartache for staff and make it digital!

    August 25, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-25290

    DGS Procurement rules that are same for different organizations. It should be different for organizations that are buying papers/pens etc vs organizations that need to buy specialized equipment or instruments. Procurement rules are not effective and efficient and hinders other departments from doing their job in a timely manner.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24858

    Problem: We have really smart employees that spend years doing their job, but their institutional knowledge often gets stuck or siloed, so when they leave, or the work increases beyond their capacity, it's hard to step in to do the work. I think some solutions could include better documentation protocols, with staff dedicated to upholding them (i.e., making sure our network and SharePoint folders get insane, which they always seem to), and staff time to document/communicate procedures and innovate ways to improve processes.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25273

    Empower leaders to take mild risks so we don't get stuck putting all our eggs in one basket. Smaller moves

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25315

    One thing I have noticed after working for CDCR for over 25 years is that they put unqualified people in administrative jobs. An example would be: instead of hiring someone with a business/accounting background to be in charge of "Business Services", CDCR hires someone that worked their way from up from an officer or secretary to run that department. This happens in the medical department also.

  • Guest-25223

    Duplication of effort due to silos. Centralized process yet streamlined.
