  • Guest-22552

    Collaboration between departments to create accurate and timely assistance for customers.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24316

    Over-regulation is the major issue making it difficult to achieve work in this position. This includes environmental regulation, contracting manuals, procurement manuals, and hiring regulations. My idea to deal with this is start with a fresh piece of paper to write clear, streamlined regulations. The current system of amending through legislation is ineffective.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-21851

    The first thing I noticed when I became a state employee was how behind the times everything is. The modern world utilizes technology wherever possible. We should aim to do the same. The state needs to rid itself of antiquated processes, procedures, technologies, equipment, etc. As the nation's leader in technology and innovation the State of California should focus on improving these things across all areas of business. We should be using electronic/virtual options wherever possible. Allow ECH/ACH/EFT payments to vendors. Use electronic communications. Strive to reduce wasteful spending on office and storage space by moving to a more modern virtual space. Revamp our websites to make them more user friendly and improve functionality. Provide more training to all state employees on the various programs and technologies that exist to improve communication and efficiency. Using modern technology would not only improve processing times but would also save the state and taxpayers money by reducing waste of paper, ink, office supplies, postage, time, space, and environmental impacts.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24539

    Using technology to improve services and remove over head with automation. State Agencies are always behind in technology solutions and have anticuated systems. By emprasing technology and find applications that can be used department wide and in several ways, would make agencies manual processes go away and be eaiser to maintain.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-21403

    A primary problem I would address is the culture within the State departments. A lot of upper management have an authoritarian complex and seem to go out of their way to punish or reprimand their employees. This is leading to less employee retention and higher stress among departments. If the state wants to begin to hire a new generation of employees, the authoritarian style of management needs to change. Younger generations will not want to be treated the way most state employees are being treated now. I currently do not have ideas to solve this problem, but I feel this issue will continue to get worse as our workforce continues to age and retire.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-21851

    While I understand that this is not where we are supposed to discuss work conditions, ignoring them is a disservice to all of California. Addressing these issues helps to ensure a happy and healthy workforce which is key to good customer service, performance, and efficiency. Workers need to have their needs met in order to perform at their best. The state should strive to provide the best work environment possible to each of it's employees. This should not be a one-size-fits-all approach as every human has different needs. Some people need more support than others. Some require more guidance. Others need less. The state should also do more to understand neurodivergence within the workforce as it plays a part in the needs of each individual. This is also true for each role, not classification. Each role has different requirements of technology, tools, communication, supplies, etc. Tailoring things to the individual and the role they fill will greatly improve efficiency, productivity, collaboration, communication, quality of work, etc.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-23503

    We tend to lose the most skilled workers becasue the state offers very low wages. When the state tries to to push for a lower quality of life for state workers, such as RTO, we lose even more good workers. This seems like a very bad strategy because we end up losing our best people and often struggle to fill the positions. The state as a whole, including the citizens, all lose when this happens. If we want the best workers putting in their best efforts, we need to make them feel apprectiated and valued. The "pizza party" to make up for low wages is not a good strategy.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-25026

    I think the CA payroll system needs to be modernized and built to support evolving workforce needs while integrating the latest advancements in technology.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-23481

    Almost everything we do on paper can and should be done online. It is 2025, we had the technology to do all these things decades ago.

    August 25, 2025
  • Guest-24557

    Every year we lose homes to wildfires and these losses are preventable. We need to mandate home hardening and defensible space including zone 0. There will be strong public outcry but often the hard thing and the right thing are the same. This will be costly and we need to provide funding for those who need it to accomplish this. This doesn't mean the $100k it may take to fully harden a home, but the low cost retrofits that are most impactful, on the order of $2-5k. Fuels reduction and prescribed fire are great and can have beneficial impacts to suppression efforts and ecosystem health, but if we need the money from somewhere it should be taken from there. Of course it's sad when the wildland burns, but if there is no loss of life or property most folks won't be too heartbroken. Work in the home ignition zone is what will have the biggest impact. We won't lose 0 homes, but the losses will be greatly reduced. This is a fixable problem, we just need the will to mandate it. There can be a timeline of multiple years to reach compliance if that will make it more palatable, but this needs to have teeth behind it.

    August 24, 2025
  • Guest-24554

    Support CA HS Dropouts California currently has the lowest high school graduation rate in the nation—an alarming fact, especially for a state with the fourth largest economy in the world. We can and must do better for our youth and for the many adults who never had a real chance to earn a diploma. In California, certain vulnerable student populations—such as those who have experienced homelessness, incarceration, or time in the foster care system—can graduate with a reduced requirement of 130 credits under AB 216. I propose that this same credit pathway be offered to adults, age 18 and older, who enroll in accredited adult school programs. Many adults who dropped out of high school faced serious obstacles: poverty, family instability, violence in their communities, or disruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. Without a diploma, these individuals are often stuck in low-wage jobs with little opportunity for advancement. A HS diploma opens doors to job training programs, community college, military service, and promotions. • The state gains from increased tax revenue as graduates earn higher wages. • Adult schools benefit from federal funding tied to improved student outcomes. • Employers gain access to a more skilled and motivated workforce. • Businesses benefit from the increased purchasing power of newly employed adults. • Communities see reduced crime rates and greater social stability. Expand the 130-credit diploma option to adults. Its a low cost, high impact solution.

    August 24, 2025
  • Guest-21108

    Staffing has been a real issue for a lot of the departments. I know when I wanted to apply to be a state working nobody wanted to tell me how the process is done. Now that I been working 4 years in the department, I noticed that across the board hiring and retaining workers is a challenge. For the interview process if people don't show up, they are unable to go lower in the tier to hire candidates that didn't score high but are willing to work. Also people go out on injury for months at a time leaving areas understaffed. They are unable to put a temporary person or hire a new person while that person is on leave. This leaves the rest of the team overworked and unhappy to be doing several people's job. Also moving up we have meetings 1 a year about moving up but they make no effort to communicate with the people interested in moving up. I had communicated several times, and it seems they are always on vacation. I think upwards mobility is a myth. Also when issue's arrive with payroll leading to Sacramento someone is always on vacation. The next person in line usually does not want to be bothered and will not cover their shift unless absolutely necessary. I feel like there needs to be oversight and accountability. Alot of California's need jobs but without proper instruction to score higher on test, or show how to apply. Or move the people up from lower positions they been working for years we cannot have a flow of employment.

    August 24, 2025
  • Guest-22693

    Lack of efficiency in our business practices. For instance, when I am purchasing supplies and services for my agency, due to the overwhelming number of rules I must abide by, the state is paying a significant amount more for the same product or service. We are bound to only using “certified” small businesses that many times are off shore companies with physical address that comes back to a private parcel pick up location. The majority of time these orders are fulfilled by the vendor through Amazon or Walmart.com. A significant amount of time and money could be saved if agencies were allowed to utilize local vendors or store fronts.

    August 24, 2025
  • Guest-22693

    Allow “regional” managers to approve purchases and services. The multi step processes we currently must go through at the field level to conduct business all end up having to route through to several channels before we get a green light from top management. Give managers at field level operation the tools and authority to ensure business can continue uninterrupted.

    August 24, 2025
  • Guest-24409

    The primary issues I'd address would the ridiculously unbalanced authoritarian power structure that the politically motivated elected officials wield over State employees without taking those same employees into consideration and only considering their own political agenda.

    August 24, 2025
  • Guest-24800

    There is a need to modernize data deidentification model and privacy compliance review to boost efficiency and unleash value of data. Current process to perform data suppression including secondary suppression is unsustainable and often involves excessive manual labor and subjective determination in excel spreadsheets. Over-suppression not only creates a bottleneck, delaying data releases for research, policy-making, and public transparency, it also renders public-use datasets less useful for researchers and community organizations. This manual, subjective model represents a significant opportunity cost for the taxpayers we serve. We need to move to more automated systems and more streamlined privacy standards and technologies. Another area of investment is to upskill analysts in robust, open-source tools in R or Python to codify privacy rules and to reduce manual hours in excel compliance review.

    August 24, 2025
  • Guest-24848

    Software standards. One example is ServiceNow. This could be used as an IT ticketing system for all state departments. However, every department selects their own IT ticketing system and then they all pay differing licensing costs. One software where it makes sense, the state could realize cost savings and also continuity across departments, saving in training and add easier coordination.

    August 24, 2025
  • Guest-22587

    We have too many state agencies solving the same technical probelm for their individual agency. This is an inefficent use of resources and public funds. If we know that all state agencies need to maintain and submit certain information (i.e., time tracking, STD 200) why don't we create a statewide solution instead of each agency developing a solution? We need to centeralize technology and systems that are used across the states. Create a central code repository that we can share across state agencies.

    August 24, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22695

    State employees fall into 2 categories.. those that work, and those that don't, it's about 50/50. It should not be an expected standard that just because you are a state employee, you don't have to work, you can expect others to do the job, and no repercussions from management because you can't be fired; it is abused in the state & there should be accountability.

    August 24, 2025
  • Guest-25109

    Telework can generate significant office cost savings for the State, provided state employees telework three or more days per week. » Under the Governor’s April 2024 directive requiring state employees to work in the office two days per week, the state auditor estimated that 19 selected departments spent almost $117 million in fiscal year 2024–25 on nearly 3.2 million square feet of office space that was often unused in the seven large state-owned office properties we reviewed. » If state employees telework three or more days per week, DGS has estimated that the State could reduce office space in DGS-managed state-owned and leased buildings by approximately 30 percent, which could generate cost savings of as much as $225 million annually. Since the Executive Order that requires employees to return to the office 4 days a week, we have lost vital IT staff that were crucial to many important projects. To hire and train for their backfills, we have been set back years. Additionally, it will cost the agency more money to train and get them up to speed. I would like to see agencies be given back the autonomy to set telework policies that work for their departments.

    August 23, 2025
  • Guest-24755

    One issue is about growing the competency of our workforce. We tend to have a lot of staff, but few people doing the vast majority of the work. This is not equitable and leads to burnout and potential loss of talent. True staff development would help this, along with formal mentoring programs, and more opportunities (with less bureaucratic hoops) to have temporary training and development opportunities, or the opportunity to partner with non-State agencies to temporarily work in the field, etc. We need staff to understand the big picture, be engaged, be empowered, and be prepared for advancement.

    August 23, 2025
  • Guest-25095

    1. Hiring Process It is my understanding that Directors and Deputy Directors may not go through HR & that reference checks are not always conducted. If that is the case, I believe reference checks should be required for all candidates. Additionally, I believe the current scoring process could be improved. In some cases, employees with many years of service in a department are not selected, which can be very discouraging. I suggest eliminating the scoring system and limiting interview panels to no more than two members to help ensure fairness and consistency. 2. Supervisor Evaluation For employees who are not in supervisory roles, I recommend implementing a survey every six months to provide feedback on their supervisor. These evaluations could be reviewed by the CEA to help identify areas of improvement. I have observed that not all individuals in supervisory positions possess the people skills necessary for effective leadership, and this type of feedback could support their professional development. 3. Open Communication and Feedback All employees, regardless of level, should feel comfortable sharing ideas or raising concerns about their department without fear of retaliation. To strengthen accountability, departments should provide feedback within five business days of receiving employee input. Too often, employees are told that leadership will “get back to them,” but no follow-up occurs. 4. When hired at a new department proper onboarding should be provided.

    August 23, 2025
  • Guest-23887

    Retention and recruitment is a major concern as departments are quite dependent on experienced staff and their institutional knowledge. Constant turnover can be disruptive to program needs and if staff are just using entry level positions as just a way to get their foot in the door, supervisors can't make any long term plans to improve processes. There needs to be more promotional opportunities and analyst positions to motivate staff to stay within their departments.

    August 23, 2025
  • Guest-22465

    Direct supervisors should have more leeway in managing the day to day of their teams. All workers work differently and have different needs that impact productivity. When decisions around the workday are made at the top as blanket mandates, it heavily affects team morale and ultimately impacts hiring and retention. These decisions tie the hands of the supervisors that know the needs of their team best. When turnover is high and hiring is slow, it impacts the entire team and service to taxpayers is slowed down heavily. As the largest economy in the country and the State with the strongest worker protections, we should be leading the way in showing the rest of the country that people should always come first and that workers that are passionate, dedicated, and productive don’t always fit into boxes built at the top, nor should they have to. The solution I propose would be to allow supervisors and teams that have proven track records to have more leeway in decisions around the workday. This would improve morale, increase talent available for hiring, increase retention, and put the focus on results for taxpayers over arbitrary outdated ideas around work.

    August 23, 2025
  • Guest-24293

    One of the primary problems as a state employee is being subjected to the whims of ever-changing political climates. It often feels like state worker's voices are ignored by those who hold our future in their hands. Example: RTO to appease landlords despite evidence that it is a waste of time, space, and money. Solution: Listen to state workers, believe the data, stand behind your employees. I believe that California is consistently at the forefront of progress for many causes. It is what makes me proud to live here. We should focus on the big picture ahead of us. What do we want our world to look like? I would like to see a world that is fighting against climate change. A world that challenges the existing 40-hour work week status quo. I would like to see a world that utilizes technology to better the lives of human beings instead of increasing profits. How can California lead the way to that world? TELEWORK.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-21547

    One primary problem at the California Highway Patrol is our use of physical mailing services like the USPS and FedEX. Our department's policies often require original signed documents to be snail-mailed or overnighted to our headquarters or division offices. Money spent on these mailing services could be avoided by switching to uploading and emailing scanned documents. The money saved could really benefit our department by using that money to increase the salaries of some of the lower paying clerical jobs such as the Office Assistant and Office Technician classifications. Our office has experienced volatile position turn-around because we don't make a livable wage working in these classifications. So, it's really frustrating to see vast amounts of money being spent on sending papers in the mail when that money could be used to better the lives of the people working in the offices throughout our department. Our policies on utilizing mailing services needs to be changed.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-22382

    I work for a department with an outright BAN on the use of generative artificial intelligence (AI). I feel that this is a huge missed opportunity to improve public service and also efficiencies in day-to-day work. CalLearns has an amazing training, showing a variety of ways that AI can directly benefit public government organizations. California State government should lean into AI, with more training, and introduction of AI tools in the workplace. The risks should be acknowledged and understood and mitigated rather than prohibiting AI altogether.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24445

    The Administration DEFINITELY needs to take a look at all the reports that DGS requires of departments. Every month it seems like there's another report that's needed, that requires tons of research and data collection from staff and facilities all over the state. What are they used for? They are very labor-intensive, and it seems like a lot of these reports just pull and compile information DGS already has in their fleet and facility databases. Some of these reports, like the sustainability roadmap, take SO much time. It probably costs each department at least a couple staff worth of people just to handle DGS' reporting requirements, and these are resources that could otherwise be providing services to taxpayers and fee payers. DGS should make a list of all the reports they require, do an analysis of what each one is used for and whether or not there's even a benefit, see where some can be consolidated or can be taken over by DGS since they already have the data, and then free those resources up so departments can better serve the public instead.

    August 22, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-25803

      Yep why do we have to complete mileage logs when the vehicles have telematics installed.

      September 05, 2025
  • Guest-24445

    The state is pushing departments to contract with and purchase from small businesses and disabled veterans, which is great. However, there should be a different set of requirements they need to comply with in order to do business with the state to make it worthwhile for them. We try to contract with SB/DVBE vendors but they don't want to do business with the state since it requires them to have a $1 million insurance policy. That's a super expensive cost for them to do business, so how are we helping them by requiring this and telling them we're giving them an advantage? If we want to help them out, DGS really needs to look at how that can be done. It's unfortunate that government gets in the way of them being successful when at the same time government is bragging about how much they're helping them.

    August 22, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-25134

    As both a Californian and a state employee, I strongly support the continuation and expansion of telework. From my perspective, telework directly addresses the two goals of this discussion: increasing efficiency within state government and improving service to the public. When state employees telework, we eliminate hours of commuting time each week. That reclaimed time translates into more energy and focus for our work, rather than starting the day already drained from traffic. Telework also reduces overhead costs for departments, with less need for office space, utilities, and parking reimbursements. Those savings could be redirected toward technology, staffing, or public programs that improve government services. Telework allows employees to be more responsive to the public. With modern tools such as secure cloud systems, videoconferencing, and shared databases, most services can be delivered as effectively, if not more so, remotely. Constituents rarely care whether the employee helping them is in a cubicle or a home office. They care about getting timely, accurate, and respectful service. By avoiding commute-related stress and delays, employees are better equipped to provide exactly that.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25134

    One of the primary problems I would address is the state’s resistance to fully embracing telework, despite clear evidence that it benefits both employees and the public. For decades, Californians have struggled with long commutes, congested freeways, and poor air quality. As a state employee, I see firsthand how commuting drains productivity, raises costs for workers, and contributes to pollution that affects every Californian. Forcing employees back into offices undermines efficiency, increases taxpayer costs for office space and reimbursements, and contradicts the state’s own climate goals. The solution is simple: expand and normalize telework as the default option whenever the duties of a position can be performed remotely. This requires modernizing policies, investing in secure technology, and measuring performance based on outcomes instead of physical presence. Employees should be trusted to deliver results, and in my experience, productivity and responsiveness actually increase when telework is supported. By keeping more workers at home, California can cut emissions, save money, and provide faster, more effective services to residents. Telework is not just a perk. It is a strategy that addresses climate change, budget efficiency, and the quality of public service all at once.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24445

    Why does DGS not consider a UC/CSU to be a state agency when it comes to interagency contracts? One department can contract with another and they can do so up to $2 million without DGS approval. Why isn't UC/CSU part of this delegation, instead of having the same delegation limits as outside contractors? The state is going to contract with them no matter what, but not giving them the same level of delegation just means the process takes longer and the contract has to sit at DGS Office of Legal Services waiting for approval. Seems like a waste of state resources that can better be used elsewhere. Why are taxpayers paying for DGS to see if the state can do work for itself?

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25168

    IT projects typically follow one of two methodologies -- waterfall or agile. Waterfall is great for smaller projects when one can reasonably estimate the scope, time, and resources needed to complete a project. Agile is better when two of those items are a little fuzzy because the project is large, but it's the only way to get the project to succeed. I have seen projects fail because state control agencies attempt to run projects one way while the department they are overseeing wants to do it the other way. First, CDT needs to have clearly defined approaches for both methodologies. Second, as part of the pre-work (e.g., FSR part), there needs to be an open discussion between CDT and the organization that is undertaking the project about which methodology should be used for the project to level-set expectations. Finally, as part of the FSR process, CDT should insist on receiving an entity-attribute diagram for the data that is passing through the system, so that they know that the IT team understands what it is that they are trying to build. All three of these points will reduce the risk of IT project failure.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24837

    one issue is the lack of communication to the general public about processes and steps individuals take to access services/programs.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-22994

    Poor communication is a huge problem. Managers sometimes have zero training on how to effectively manage and communicate with their employees. And the example and leadership really needs to start at the top. Managers shouldn't be throwing curve balls and wrenches into things when they are not really, fully involved. You should come in like a lamb and leave like a lion, not the other way around. The latter causes confusion, chaos, frustration, and the former cultivates respect, efficiency, knowledge, and experience.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23582

    I believe that the processes that are currently in place with DGS are far too cumbersome and require too much time to work through. I believe we need to streamline processes and work towards getting things done rather than figure out what road blocks can go up to slow down the process.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-22776

    The State has a penny-wise pound-foolish approach to promotions. "Promotions in place" are impossible to attain. The only way to be promoted is to find an open position req in a different department. I understand that we want to be good stewards of public funds, that we want to avoid even the appearance of nepotism or corruption, and that we don't want to promote without need. And there have been very public instances of nepotism and corruption in hiring and promotions. But the current system forces people who want a long-term career in public service to leave their departments to move up, taking all their "tribal knowledge" with them. It forces departments to cannibalize each another. The system does not consider the value of the months, or years, it takes to train the next person who eventually fills that loss. That value must be given more weight. And if a department is full of long-term people punching above their weight, there should be some sort of extenuating circumstances to make a PIP easier. It's worth it to promote people for their knowledge and ability once in a while, to encourage more people to consider long-term public sector careers.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24715

    Creating an online platform for all California state-employees to submit timesheets. All Departments throughout the state have a variety of styles of submitting timesheets. Gavin Newsom's goal to make sure California is moving forward with technology platforms. Hoping the California HR can create an online platform to submit timesheets for all Departments.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25111

    We have done additional spreadsheets to compare against entries in CalJOBS for years. Either it works or it doesn't work. Recently additional spreadsheet info has been added on. This is causing way to much angst in trying to help job seekers. Management even stated it was about process not outcomes. SOLUTION: audit CalJOBS, focus on entries there to eliminate duplicate and triplicate work. Focus on outcomes and hiring.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24899

    Staff in positions that don't require them to be in the office every week should have the option to telework full time. Telework should be encouraged for the cost savings, efficiency, productivity and work-life balance.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24605

    The line from "issues in the community colleges" to "community college legislation" seems to be missing a critical piece from the field, particularly implementation timelines and an embedded accountability or tracking mechanism. This could be mitigated with an intentional qualitative process or even "feedback from the field" as part of legislative development. This does slow things down in the outset, but can result in more efficient and sustainable efforts being put forward that don't need to be fixed later through a legislative process.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24991

    To build a more effective and future-ready State workforce, we must address outdated systems that hinder collaboration, innovation, and talent development. Leadership Culture The traditional chain of command may suit high-risk environments, but in civil service, it often stifles collaboration and trust. Leadership based solely on tenure can lead to silos, disengagement, and missed opportunities for innovation. Incorporating 360-degree peer reviews and influence-based leadership models would promote accountability, humility, and a culture of shared success. Classification System Our current classification structure, designed for factory-style work, doesn’t reflect the dynamic, cross-functional nature of today’s roles. It limits mobility, professional growth, and alignment with modern competency-based hiring practices. A shift toward a flexible, skills-based framework would better support recruitment, retention, and continuous improvement. Technology Barriers Outdated systems discourage younger, tech-savvy professionals from joining the State. Investing in modern, user-friendly tools is essential to attracting talent and signaling that innovation is a priority. These changes are critical to creating a more agile, inclusive, and responsive public sector.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24473

    As a state employee we are always told things are out of budget. I work in law enforcement and other agencies charge for release forms (an impound for unlicensed driver) they charge up to $500. My agency does not charge for release forms, but we could. Even if we charged $50 for a storage and $100 for an impound we would make a ton of money.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23674

    I've noticed problems with engagement in one's work. I've seen employees "work the system" to take lots of time off or get out of certain projects/duties. Some employees seem very bored and unmotivated, and as a result put out mediocre work. One way to improve this would be focusing on colleague relations, i.e., allow or encourage people to socialize and create a sense of community in the office (within appropriate boundaries, of course). If we all knew each other better personally and/or professionally, people may feel more motivated and part of a team that puts out good work. As someone who is newer to state service, I know this is difficult and often discouraged for a reason; however, allowing an environment where a lack of communication and respect for coworkers to flourish is detrimental.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24840

    Perception. Often times there is a negative public perception of State Employees and the work we do. The public sometimes feels like State Employees are not in touch with reality or the types of issues they are facing. I feel this could be improved by being more human in public interactions, being honest and transparent, even if it is not a positive topic, and by having more consistent training for all divisions within the enterprise, allowing for more educated and positive interactions between the public and State Employees, regardless of who they are interacting with. By having honest, educated, open interactions, even if both sides do not agree, they can understand, which can lead to acceptance. This would also apply internally between various levels of leadership and staff.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25160

    I would like to see better outreach highlighting the work State Employees do for the benefit of the public. One possibility would be a better social media presence sharing key projects and their benefits with the public.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25068

    I'm a recent hire, so I my knowledge is limited. Maybe a balance between being rigorous and efficient. I.e. follow the rules, but don't have non-sense rules that were based on one lawsuit ever, and now we must all suffer the time wasting consequences forever after.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25169

    I would want to address the career promotional process. There are very talented and eligible people who are often overlooked because of managment bias toward a friend or particular ethnic demographic to fill a position. The promotional process, though intended to be standardized, is still manipulated by hiring managers for what appears to be affirmative action reasons. I would like to see the political bias removed and the practice of hiring/promoting be solely based on merit of work performed and abilities demonstrated.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24492

    Let's address the budget shortfall and why the state worker has to suffer with loss of wage increases. My concern is that the branches of the state are not getting wage cuts just the state worker. Let's make this fair across the board.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25209

    After seven years with Caltrans, my problem is with private contractors. Since we are forced to award contracts to the lowest bidders on projects, we are constantly saddled with mediocre companies on multi-year projects. These companies often cause delays through dangerous practices, poor execution, and corner-cutting to save money. I propose the State implements a "blacklist" whereby demonstrably bad contractors are prohibited from bidding on some or all projects for a set time period, or until they can prove they are capable of greater responsibility.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25169

    I would address knowledge transfer. People are so concerned with getting promoted quickly, that they don't take the time to learn the job. Since they can't get promoted in their field, they go into supervision. Then you have people who supervise/manage and review the work, who frankly can't do the work themselves. Then they promote people who aren't qualified to do the work at that level (because they don't understand the work and what it means to get to the next level), and before you know it, you have everyone at the top of the earning ladder and no one who can adequatley do the work. Promotion is important, but they should be merit based, because you demonstrate a higher level of compentancy. And those at lower levels should accept knowlege transfer from people who have put in the time to learn and quit being focused on being at the highest level without having demonstrated that level of competency.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25207

    Guidance and access to the guidance and processes. Also, the lack of knowledge sharing among subject matter experts (SME) or not knowing who the SME is. Example: We need to obtain signatures from our chain of command for approval of an item. Sometimes the need for wet/real signatures is required and sometimes electronic signatures are acceptable.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-22338

    A mentor program for first year or new employees who come into state service. Learning how the state functions, how long it takes for certain tasks or forms to be implemented can be daunting and confusing. Having a mentor or buddy to walk new employees on what to expect would be a great way to increase turn over and have employees engage with each other in meaningful ways.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24551

    Procurement. Master agreements across departments with uniform contract policies and procedures would streamline outsourced work and allow smaller departments to leverage larger department resources.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25005

    I would like to see my department utilize more up to date ways to share information. My department still has to produce binders with documentation for auditors who have access to our data and they have a SharePoint upload documentation. It would save hours of staff's time and save on supplies and environment. There are times we make 13 4" binders filled with documentation that they have already and can view before coming for the audit.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25233

    As a state employee, I see one primary problem in the way new employees orientation is conducted. currently, NEO often requires multiple stae employees to physically present material, which uses valuable time and resources. This approach could be modernized and made more efficient. My idea is to fully diitize the onboarding process. Core orienation content could be presented throug standardized, prerecorded modules that employees can watch, ensuring consistency across the state. to keep it interactive, new employees should be able to submit questions digitally during the session. a designated proctor could collect the questions and forward them to the appropriate department for follow up by way of email. This solution would save both time and money.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23688

    The issues I see is those of every Californians are struggling with every day which are own/rent housing costs, grocery prices, gas, and taxes. Every year for the last 5 years people is moving due to these prices going higher every year. I have kids and family to take care of and when I have to go get another job when working at the state level there is a problem. There used to be a saying that if we got into the state we would be set to be able to live comfortably and provide for our loved ones and that's not true anymore. When I get a raise, something always goes up and takes it from me as if I never got it. To be able to buy anything whether house or car they are taxed to the roof and prices never go down. If they do something else goes up. California needs to be affordable to be able to thrive, more policing, and use of more natural resources that California has had for decades. You ask what ideas I have to solve these problems I address, well start thinking like the bottom person and American citizens of this state that are struggling to eat and live and put policies that create more jobs, businesses, and programs that help people. Less to those that don't deserve handouts or free assistance on the taxpayer's dime. Thank you

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25139

    Knowledge Transfer in the workforce. Ideas I have to solve the problem is provide the tools and resources to staff to know how to transfer knowledge and measure success.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25042

    Problem: Each department that has public-facing programs has its own database and systems to identify and serve people. A person must provide repetitive information to each department they interact with (e.g., FTB, DMV, EDD). Each department spends time and money verifying the identity of each person they interact with. Solution: Create a centralized portal & database for the public to access all of the services provided by the state. A person gets one access point to see what's going on with all of their different interactions with the state. The departments get a source of verified people to whom they can provide their services.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25024

    Modernization of California's government is the best way to be more effective and efficient. There's so much waste and excessive bureaucracy that keep our constituents from the programs and benefits that they need and already pay for with their tax dollars. Telework, for example, frees up hundreds of millions of dollars to be spent on social programs instead, and yet for some reason we're clinging to the past of an office mindset from 40 years ago. CalFresh eligibility and paper applications and office visits, something I've seen firsthand, are incredibly difficult for people to get through. The application and reverification process that the Housing Choice Voucher program created online is incredibly effective and simple to use. It would be great to see all customer facing departments use a simple, online system that removes barriers and hurdles between the public and their benefits.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24455

    There are too many regulations, rules, and statutory requirements that state employees have to keep track of to be in compliance within their respective areas. It is impossible to keep track of all the changes - a smart portal where an employee could submit their need, and receive a list of all the measures that need to be met would be helpful.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25040

    I would like to standardize templates and basic duties across agencies and departments. Having to relearn the job that you were already doing elsewhere because the culture is different is inefficient. Provide a standard writing style guide for Contracts and Procurements across the state. Invest in a statewide procurement solution that all departments have access to. A way to track work, control who edits, and route for approvals. Maybe even tie it into the signature processes. Something that would work for an analyst working in a small shop under 10 people as well as the large departments of thousands. Ensure consistent and appropriate levels of training. This promotes the transferability of skills and ensures a baseline for promotions. Provide information on how analysts can successfully promote through the ranks. Workforce development stops with Executive Assistants, Secretaries and Office Technicians. Analysts and other employees would like assistance and a clear, step by step process for how to promote within the state. Offering Interviewing training as a part of the career growth. Offer advanced analyst positions above AGPA or Research Analyst that does not require moving into management. Often people are subject matter experts, but that does not always transfer into leadership capabilities.

    August 22, 2025 👍 4
    • Guest-25904

      While I generally like this idea, it may not be feasible in all circumstances, for instance where state agencies are subject to different federal regulations.

      September 10, 2025
  • Guest-25097

    The California Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation (CDCR) operates multiple firehouses across the state, but currently lacks a cohesive and standardized organizational framework. To enhance operational efficiency and emergency preparedness, the department would benefit from implementing a clear chain of command. This could include appointing a Sacramento-based Fire Chief to oversee statewide operations, supported by Regional Division Chiefs and Battalion Chiefs stationed at each firehouse. Battalion Chiefs would supervise Fire Captains and ensure consistent training, coordination, and readiness. Establishing this structure would significantly improve the department’s ability to respond effectively to emergencies, particularly during statewide crises

    August 22, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-25010

    Tackle all the departmental inefficiencies. Remove all blocks, the lowest level employee should be able to point out/request that a process, no matter how small, be addressed. Just as an example: My daily job requires working on another State departments application. I need to contentiously clear a screen or remove my hands from the keyboard to move forward. It may only add minutes to my day but multiple that by everyone working on the app and 100s of hours wasted. If I want to request that application be updated, it can take hours of completing document, then it goes through no less than three of my superiors.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25097

    Current departmental structures exhibit overlapping responsibilities, resulting in duplicated efforts and suboptimal resource utilization. To address these inefficiencies, a strategic reallocation of roles and responsibilities across departments is essential. By streamlining operations and eliminating redundancies, organizations can significantly enhance productivity and operational effectiveness. For instance, rather than maintaining individual warehouses at each worksite, we could establish centralized regional warehouses supported by dedicated distribution lines to service the worksites. This approach would reduce logistical complexity, lower operational costs, and improve inventory management across locations.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25007

    Legislators write laws to benefit their constituents (which is great) but without thought to how state agencies will be affected by these laws. Some of these issues have been noted in previous comments such as additional steps to a process that adds no value to the public and in fact delays work that would be of benefit. A possible solution is to get experts that work in the field (not HQ) from different agencies as an advisory committee to the legislature.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23029

    I have been with the State for just over a year, after working in the private sector for most of my life (with a couple of temp government jobs here and there). The biggest issue that I noticed from day one is the amount of bureaucracy that envelops almost every aspect of how the State operates. I pretty much knew that going in, but when I actually experienced it I was kind of shocked. Everything is just so difficult! I'm used to having ownership of my role and various tasks and getting things handled quickly - not having to go through 15 people for something or having to wait 8 months for a PO and then supervisor approval and to only order something from one specific place, for example. Also, I get a lot of emails and phone calls from the public and it can be incredibly difficult trying to determine who to forward to. I often go to our website and sit there scrolling through trying to find the info myself, with much difficulty. Oh and the way people make payments online - the entire system needs to be overhauled. All of the phone calls I receive are either about duplicate payments on their credit card, or they couldn't log in to their account to make their payment. I then have to take down all their info and email my contacts to ask them to help. Takes a lot of time! As one of my co-workers said when I first started: "It's about accountability, not efficiency!"

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25247

    A primary problem I would like to address is the severe lack of communication. There are a number of reasons why there is a lack of communication, such as superiority complexes within management teams, lack of guidance from management teams, fear of retaliation, lack of adequate training, and so on. Because of this lack of communication, it is often hard to collaborate with one another, thus leading to frustration and complicated work processes. Also, it is greatly discouraging and prompts employees to put in minimum effort rather than their best effort. Communication is key in many aspects of life, especially work. If communication were more present in the workplace, employees would feel less stressed and more rewarded for the work that they do for the state of California.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24445

    There is definitely room for streamlining the DGS one-time acquisitions process. Departments must have DGS do their procurements if it's over their delegation. It takes DGS several months to complete this process, even if it's for an emergency; DGS staff are unfamiliar with department nuances as they relate to their operations when needing a one-time purchase; and departments spend a ridiculous amount of time going back and forth answering basic questions from the one-time acquisitions staff as they try and learn about the need for the purchase, the department's business needs, etc. Why can't DGS let the departments still complete the purchasing process if it's over their delegation and just have DGS approve/execute the PO? It would be faster for departments if they could just do it themselves, and DGS would still have oversight since they would need to execute the PO. Also, if the purchase request goes through the DGS non-competitive bid process, why does DGS need to start from scratch and re-review the entire purchase from the one-time acquisitions unit? If the NCB unit approves the purchase, isn't that good enough? If an NCB is approved, can that just count as a delegation for the department to do the purchase instead of starting over again for one-time acquisitions to do it? This makes purchases take several months when departments can do them in just a month or two. Sometimes these purchases are critical for operations and cause work delays until DGS approves.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25124

    I think a lot of times there is so much work to be done but so few staff to actually complete it, which leads to errors and issues. Hiring more staff to complete all the required work and making sure that staff is trained on how to do their job properly (and trained to look out for and share places where redundancies can be removed) would go far to fix this issue.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24538

    in our present fiscal crisis, a consolidation of governmental agencies should occur to transfer expenditures to front line positions directly impacting and/or benefiting Californians. Consolidating departments and headquarters with similar, associated, or adjacent roles and/or responsibilities will consolidate headquarters locations and all associated Headquarters personnel positions. It will streamline operations to ensure the maximum number of fiscal resources are spent where it makes the most impact on Californians or on State Employees. Supportive administrative positions should be carefully examined to see if an entry level administrative telework position could do the same work. Offering those employees to reside in areas of the state with lower costs of living and eliminating their commute time and commute related expenses. This would make the positions attractive to more prospective qualified employees and should reduce vacancies and amount of time vacancies remain unfilled. Many agencies have duplicate departments or divisions and the agencies may operate in similar or adjacent fields, consolidation of the executive and managerial leadership, elimination of duplicate faculties, transition of appropriate administrative roles to more entry level telework classifications, will improve effectiveness and efficiency of operations, improve the quantity and quality of services delivered to Californians, and reduce budgetary spending for the state overall.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25113

    Primary problem is communicating complex ideas and regulations that we work on to the general public. We need to figure out better communication channels and messaging, as well as ways to better process and organize the voluminous information that our state agencies process. Possible solutions: exploring AI tools and technology to help organize, summarize and message complex ideas and processes to the public.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-21697

    Primary problem: I would like to see more Leadership Training. I understand there are mandated hours of training (and have taken them) which covers management resposibility, performance management, understanding union representation etc. I think tangible leadership skills would also be helpful. Leading a team (of any size) can be overwhelming and staged training that covers various skills and topics would help any leader. Solution: I would have a learning path that needs to be completed in one year - since that is typical probation period. The learning path would have a series of trainings that cover soft skills as well as the content currently covered. I also think having a buddy that you could stay in contact with and bounce ideas off each other would be helpful so it is an ongoing type of training.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25193

    The recruitment process is very cumbersome. The "exams" are quite lengthy and sometimes don't fit with the achieved purpose of the job. Applicants learn how to "cheat" the exam process just to get through. That's why there's not a lot of quality. SOQ process should be revised as these could be cumbersome-- 1 question max. Also onboarding is not really onboarding. An employee doesn't learn much about their job because their unit is so short staffed, its usually baptism by fire and they're so thankful just to have somebody show up, they're just thrown into their role without the proper training period. State service should be step above and employees should be compensated to attract quality so that employees want to stay and reduce turnover in order to serve the people of CA. I would love to see value reinstated in state service.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25124

    The state spends millions of dollars a year on office space throughout the state. Allowing and promoting telework throughout state service will allow for that money to be freed up and spent on more pressing issues that affect all of California like homelessness, housing affordability, and lack of affordable healthcare.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-25201

    Ineffective leadership, antiquated resources, funding issues.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24906

    One of the challenges that needs further analysis is the replacement of staff when they move on from State Service. In most cases, departments are unable to fill vacancies until after the position becomes vacant. This leads to an exodus of knowledge and expertise from the department that is not easily replaced as it often takes years for an individual to become fully proficient in their role, establish connections with others and gain career long learning. While this practice may lead to salary savings, it creates a knowledge deficit which then leads to efficiency loss until such time as the individual staff person learns the position and becomes proficieint and beyond. If departments had the ability to hire replacement staff while the existing staff person was still present, the knowledge transfer would be more streamlined, and the institutional knowledge can be much more easily passed on to the new staff member. An in-between step might involve the creation of desk manuals for new staff to access however these will not necessarily impart the same level knowledge as the in person tutelage of the out-going staff member.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24445

    DGS acts as the building department for state-owned facilities. If a department has a repair or modification that needs to be done, it must go through DGS and they will do the work themselves, contract out, or very rarely delegate the project back to departments. DGS charges an absurd amount for their overhead and project management costs, and takes several years to complete projects. For example, our department needed a new HVAC unit that took DGS 4 years to complete and a new roof that took 5. A private contractor can do this work for pennies on the dollar and in days vs years, saving the state millions. Why can't DGS delegate some of this work to departments? They say the government code requires them to do everything, but how long do we need to keep referring to these code sections without considering amendments to streamline government? My department has had to lay off seasonal staff because DGS' costs kept going up and we couldn't afford to pay for a repair that was taking 3 years and also continue our operations. And if DGS has vacancies, the work just sits there and doesn't get done until they hire and train more staff. This is an INCREDIBLE waste of state resources. Also, take a look at Unit 9's salaries. The amount it costs to pay DGS for this work is WAY above what the state could be paying a private contractor, even at a prevailing wage. Please streamline this process and allow/require DGS to delegate more facility projects to the departments!

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-22535

    Stop hiring employees for temporary projects only to have to keep them employed after the work is complete. Either utilize more limited-term positions or hiring more contractors.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24590

    We should incorporate Airtable to facilitate communication between Headquarters and districts, keep track of large projects across the State, and have real-time data snapshots of each District. The main Headquarters base could have another base for each department, which would be able to inform other bases or be locked, and only editors or those given permission could view/use the data stored in that base. This model could be used for each District. Another good use at the District (or Headquarters) level is have a separate base for approved contractors within their district, so Maintenance, Contracts, and Purchasing could all see the same list, keep it updated, and confidently arrange services. This would allow Superintendents to review projects, adjust timelines, or give approval where needed. It would improve accountability and reduce the amount of separate apps required.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24445

    DGS makes it unreasonably difficult for departments with owned vehicles to replace those that constantly break down because of excessive mileage or such an extensive repair history that it costs more to keep them on the road than to just replace them. I understand the need for DGS to review and scrutinize additional fleet assets, but why are replacement vehicles so difficult to obtain? It takes DGS 3-4 months just to approve the purchase (and DGS recently said departments will need to submit their Fleet Acquisition Plans 60 days sooner than in the past), and then once a vehicle is identified departments find out DGS has updated the state contract and the replacement vehicle is no longer even available. From the time a department submits a Fleet Acquisition Plan (April 1), DGS approves by June 30, and then it often takes at least until December or January for the vehicle to arrive. So if a vehicle is broken down, a department knows it's going to take at least 9 months to get that vehicle replaced and they need to rent a vehicle in the meantime. DGS only allows short-term rentals for up to 30 days, anything longer tmust also be submitted to DGS for approval. The long-term rental process for DGS, even in these instances, results in tons of justification and back-and-forth between DGS and departments and in the meantime work cannot be completed or staff with a vehicle must do double the work to make up for the vehicle shortfall. Can DGS streamline the replacement process?

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23266

    The core issue I want to address isn’t just about telework policies—it’s about a deeper, more entrenched problem within government culture: the tendency to default to “business as usual.” There’s a persistent inclination among leadership to repeat the same processes simply because they’re familiar and easy to enforce. This isn’t innovation—it’s inertia. Take the push to return employees to the office four days a week. That’s a straightforward directive, but it sidesteps the harder, more meaningful challenge: making departments truly more efficient. It’s easier to mandate physical presence than to rethink workflows, modernize systems, or confront outdated bureaucratic habits. The pandemic was a once-in-a-generation disruption that forced us to reevaluate how we work. It showed us that change is possible—and sometimes necessary. Yet instead of building on that momentum, many agencies are retreating to old norms. That’s not just a missed opportunity; it’s a failure to evolve. The most urgent transformation we need isn’t logistical—it’s cultural. We must shift from a mindset of comfort and repetition to one of curiosity, accountability, and continuous improvement. Real progress begins when we stop asking, “How do we get back to normal?” and start asking, “How do we build something better?”

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24485

    Most of us can't afford to live where we work. Our wages don't keep up with the cost of living. Even when we're able to negotiate a meager raise, it can be taken away by the governor, on a whim. We put a lot of effort into fighting for our wages. That effort could be spent serving the people of California instead of fighting our employer. This conflict leads to a lot of mistrust and kills moral.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24861

    Have a system in place that allows rank and file staff to reviews their supervisor and managers. Having good leadership allows staff to be more engaged in their work. How do you know the management/supervisor are fit and competent in their roles? The beauraracy prevents meritocracy in that state, and sadly HR enables this as they will always side with management.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24445

    State employees who drive a state vehicle for their jobs, such as inspectors, investigators, etc, have an assigned WEX Fuel Card to purchase gas while on the road. With the transition to ZEVs, why can't these WEX cards be used at EV charging stations? Especially Tesla? Departments must either have a separate credit card number to provide employees on their Tesla app, which then subjects the card to misuse and unauthorized non-state purchases, or employees must use their own personal credit cards and then submit a travel expenditure claim to request reimbursement. If a WEX card could be used to charge an EV, it would be so much easier for state staff and less work for accounting staff to manage multiple cards, process travel claims, and management to review and approve so many different payment mechanisms. If the WEX card could also be expanded to be used for small repairs and oil changes, staff wouldn't need to spend so much time finding a repair shop that will accept state payment policies, and can also get repairs done much faster without downtime.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23958

    As a DTSC PM for affordable housing projects it would be nice if we could work more efficiently internally and not cause an undue burden/cost on project proponents by potentially delaying projects due to our some times slow turn-around times and multiple iterations of document review necessary prior to approval. I would propose that in general more scoping meetings should occur, and more up front requirements should be expressed by DTSC technical staff to proponents as early on in the document review process as possible. It would also be great if supervisors and staff at higher levels could provide priority lists to staff with large amounts of work requests in order to prioritize for faster-paced projects with more aggressive timelines.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23596

    Improved communication and collaboration not only between agencies, but within agencies and their divisions. One solution to break down these communication barriers is for division managers to foster collaboration beyond occasional social events.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23757

    I worked for the state 10+ years before I understood the class spec, minimum qualifications, etc. process as far as applying. I wondered, "do I qualify?", am I understanding this correctly, etc. so I can only imagine the difficulty for many from outside state service coming in and asking the same questions and getting frustrated and abandoning the process which is sad too because there are great workers out there! It feels like a little bit of "gate-keeping" if you will. I do appreciate now that they have an SOQ sort of informational document and my department has made it a priority to list that on every job bulletin that requires an SOQ. I'm only a little part of the process but I make it a point to be very clear in my explanations and answer any questions I am able to for persons, and they really appreciate that because many don't understand the process. I hope that they share, share, share and I hope all of you will too!

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24843

    Primary problem: procurement restrictions, credit card limits and restrictions. Proposal: loosen restrictions, increase credit card limit, increase fair and reasonable limit, allow for more flexible term and blanket POs.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24148

    I think that there are really 2 major problems: 1 - we've over-regulated to the point that nothing can get accomplished and we're making it unappealing for businesses to operate in California. We can't even properly rebuild after the fires and we were unable to manage water to keep our people and homes safe. We're unable to properly manage our forests to prevent fires from being as destructive. We've even prevented police from enforcing current laws to keep people safe and we're seeing a surge in crime and violence. 2 - We need a stable power grid. We retired our nuclear plants even though they're the safest and cleanest source of stable power which our grid desperately needs - especially if we want to move towards all electric cars. Solar is great on existing infrastructure, but we've been clear cutting the forests to build those instead which destroys our beautiful countryside and it only generates power when the sun is shining. We should be developing fusion and building out a grid to support our efforts to reduce carbon emissions and cleaning up our air. We need trees and plants in the cities to help clean the air there and reduce the impact of brake dust and other particulates in the air. We can't claim to be wanting green energy while simultaneously clear cutting forests and countryside for something that results in brown outs and black outs because it's not stable enough to meet the core energy demands of the people.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24485

    White supremacy is the primary problem that needs to be addressed. This is an imperfect term that I use here as a catchall for systemic misogyny, racism, and ableism that promotes "rugged individual" myth. There needs to be a major culture shift in our approach to governance and resource management that would allow all people to participate in government. An inclusive government will more effectively serve all Californian's by promoting discussion and rational thinking over our current "follow the strongman" model. This survey feels like a box checking exercise that leadership can use a proof that they asked for input that will not be acted upon. State leadership would benefit from cultural humility training, however, it will take generations to bring about the cultural shift necessary to cause meaningful change. The State of California needs to own its racist actions, past and present. We need to acknowledge that we became the fourth largest economy through genocide, exploitation, and unsustainable resource extraction that continues today, and only benefits a few. Most state employees are ignored, especially scientists who point out that our resource extraction is unsustainable, and racial equity practitioners who point out how our racist policies are hurting oppressed communities. We need to stop being defensive and own our wrongdoing. We cannot solve a problem if we continually deny it's existence.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24817

    I would love to see various departments and programs integrated. Currently we are in silos with different departments doing the same task or money being spent twice for the same service. For instance, we currently pay doctors to be on call for the incarcerated population(I/P), but since we do not have point of care testing, we send the I/P off-site for diagnostics. Looking at the data, I believe we spend an excess of $200 million that we could have avoided if we had point of care testing, telemedicine communication ability and a contract with each county hospital er for after-hours care.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24986

    As a Californian and state employee, I am most troubled by the ongoing homelessness problem in our state. In order to entice billionaires to stay in the state (and continue to fund our state government with their income taxes!) we need to do a MUCH better of making housing accessible to every Californian. Recently, I used Sacramento County 311 to report a homeless individual who was encamped near my house and abusing alcohol to the point he might have died in the summer heat. I am happy to report that someone from the County cleaned up the encampment and removed the trash. However, I have no idea who was responsible for removing the encampment, or what became of the homeless person. To improve our state, I would like it to become common practice for citizens to report encampments and homeless residents AND be able to know what organization responded to the 311 report, and the where the homeless person was taken, and what services they were offered.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23550

    Communication between departments is lacking and there is often a silo effect. Meaning each department operates in an isolated fashion and lacks cross-departmental interaction. This often leads to inefficiencies, errors, duplicate efforts, a struggle to resolve issues, and failure to learn from each other. It would be wise to implement more cross-functional teams to interact and collaborate on projects. Our leadership teams can model collaborative behavior and encourage cross-communication. Also ensuring that we are sharing information whenever possible, so that all teams are aware and included in decisions.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24856

    We should be at forefront of innovation and technology. The state government offices lag way behind on these and the corporate business and companies and using them and are helping be more efficient. The workers if allowed to telework would be a great help as it would allow them to spend more time to work and also help Climate goals for California and save millions of dollars each year by not renting/ leasing office spaces. Use of AI adoption should be fast bureaucracy takes so much time and we then lag behind. Staff if called to office are not being provided with required furniture to work which in turn makes the employee feel out of interest to work for the state. The upper management would need to listen to rank&file and get their views on things.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24911

    One of the problems that I see as a state employee is team building. I feel that team building should happen more often than it has in my department and this will lead in trusting our team together.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24946

    I would like to focus on improving the basic needs which are shelter, safety, and community. if California can just focus on providing affordable shelter and offer appropriate residency rules and quality education, everything else will far into place. We do not need to divest our attention to so many citizen or geographic specific issues.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24819

    Efficiency, cost-savings and environmental benefits. Based on the best available data (also supported by the recent State Auditor's findings) the state has the opportunity to leverage a hybrid work schedule of two days in office to create a significant cost savings by reducing unused office space, decrease transportation-related pollution, and providing significant health and financial benefits to state employees by helping increase work-life balance and reduce transportation-related cost such as fuel and parking. The data also supports a hybrid work schedule of two days per week as increasing employee efficiency by creating a work environment that is less stressful and one in which the employee is able to focus on the work without the distractions and stressors related to commuting and potential inter-office conflict.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24331

    State agency procurement is unnecessarily complex, involving too many stakeholders in processes that could be significantly streamlined through modern technology. I propose establishing a state marketplace through Amazon's platform where departments can efficiently order goods and services. Within this centralized portal, vendors could submit competitive bids while ensuring compliance with all applicable state mandates and regulations. This system would benefit vendors by allowing them to register once with Amazon and become eligible to sell to all state agencies, eliminating the current burden of navigating separate registration processes across the multitude of agencies that issue requests for bids on goods and services.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24949

    Increase community outreach programs so the public know what kind of help is available to them. Use data-driven strategies and run polls to understand what information reaches the public and what not!

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24814

    As someone who has been with the state for many years, I find Upward Mobility to be a challenge. There are trainings that are available, but how do you train for a position you have never done before? I would like to see management take an active interest in investing in those who want to promote, by providing job shadowing, mentorship, and also providing projects where the person can actually acquire real knowledge and skills to get to the next step in their career.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-21292

    New Technology - More tools for employees. Purchase one contract that can be used cross agency's so it allows for a seamless process. i.e., Civil Rights Dept. uses Tempo but DOJ uses Workday to input timesheets.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24556

    People in California feel that government isn't working for the people because government lacks representation. 40% of the population in California is Latino/Latina but most of the state agencies and department employees are non-Latino/Latina. There is clear disconnect between the community and those who are supposed to be addressing people's needs. I would solve this problem by making California government more representative of its population.

    There are too many people in the administration role. The state needs to downsize and consolidate administrative roles. A lot of their positions are redundant, ineffective, and have no meaning.
