  • Guest-22416

    We need to get the budget under control. How do we go from $100 billion surplus to a deficit. Spending is out of control. We have spent $20 billion+ on the homeless problem and the homeless issue is worse. We need to be smart on how we spend our resources!!

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24371

    There is too much tedious and meaningless project oversight on IT contracts and purchases. Project managers in the oversight agencies add little value but create roadblocks with busy work to complete so that boxes can be checked. Many IT projects could be done for less money internally with good product management.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24139

    I think job requirements for classifications can be extremely restrictive. If you didn't get a B.S. for your undergraduate degree, you are unable to apply for a majority of positions at the departments working on environmental and air quality issues, even though many of the positions never require any expertise in the "hard" sciences. There should be easily available and widely marketed programs for state employees to gain experience or get credit that allows them to transition into roles with restrictive educational requirements. This would help us retain and properly compensate great staff.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23537

    Accountability. We seem to create more unit/position to monitoring the problems instead of being creative, and hold the staff/management accountable.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24331

    Most, if not all of California's prisons were built in an era before the current focus on robust medical, mental health, and rehabilitative services. Accordingly, few of these facilities have the physical space to accommodate these missions. One solution that has yet to be considered is repurposing incarcerated person housing units into spaces that could support the programming needs of the modern correctional mission. Given the continued reduction in the prison population, there's an opportunity to convert one housing unit on each prison yard into a space that meets these needs without massive financial outlay for new buildings. There would be an expense in performing the retrofit, but this is a far cheaper alternative to constructing new facilities.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24759

    The issue is case notes it takes too much time and it is hard to write down everything that took place. Resolution: Give all QRP Counselors AI through Microsoft Teams and Zoom to listen to the meeting and draft a summary of the meeting what took place and what was the conclusion of the meeting. Counselors can proof read the summary make any changes then attached it to AWARE. It would save alot of time for the counselors and will give us time to do other important things such as meeting with more clients and make it more efficient to provide services to clients.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24356

    One of the problems I saw in a previous role, was with Fi$CAL. A lot of budget teams in various state departments have to go through invoices and purchase orders to reconcile the money to send to DGS for the DGS fees related to infrastucture and telecom services. I think Fi$CAL could be updated to allow the coding on the front end much like a tax or in addition to a tax that is already there. That way a lot of the hours and money spent doing this reconciliation would be immediate and accurate.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24744

    The Performance Management process is too cumbersome for managers. Managers need to be able to effective and efficiently discipline staff that are not meeting expectations. Due process is absolutely essential, but it should not take years to terminate an employee. This is very inefficient.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24163

    The current status of government limits the methods that can be employed to improving California. We have to be open to alternative methods and do not dismiss ideas because "we do not do things that way around here". Most of the leadership in the departments and agencies are career bureaucrats. They only know one way to address issues. When a new approach is brought up, it is almost always shot down before it can even be explored. This happens because people in leadership are afraid that if they verve from the normal process, they will lose their jobs.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-21060

    As a state employee for more than 15 years, I would like our employer to highlight the work state workers do with the focus on changing the public perception of State Workers. We work hard for the citizens of California and often dedicate unpaid time to ensure those we serve are receiving the care and attention required to prevent disruption in the services we provide. In times of extreme hardship, we work long and exhausting hours without complaint. State workers are of value to California. A campaign to lift up the heroes that work for California, highlighting the great workers that support our state is needed.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24280

    If possible, I would implement a system that allows official business with the public to be conducted safely through email, text notifications, and secure online portals. This approach would provide instant communication, ensuring that more people especially those without stable mailing addresses can be reached quickly and reliably. By transitioning to digital access, the public could view, complete, and submit forms electronically rather than relying on traditional mail. This would significantly reduce printing and postage costs, streamline processing, and enhance overall efficiency. Number of the individuals my department serves are transient due to unstable housing, making electronic communication far more effective than mail. Allowing technology to replace paper-based processes would not only save government resources but also give the public greater control and faster turnaround times when submitting information. Printing and mailing forms require multiple steps and personnel, consuming unnecessary time and resources that could be better allocated elsewhere. For some people, unnecessary delays and wait time for benefits can be quite catastrophic and we must at least explore a better process so that we can serve the public better. Perhaps a more obvious one is simply finding a way to increase staffing to reduce backlogs which is significantly impacting the public my agency serves. Staffing shortage is very concerning.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-22625

    The purchasing process costs the state tons of money. We should be able to purchase from the cheapest vendor instead of paying double or triple the cost.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24278

    The streambed alteration permitting program (EPIMS) is extremely difficult for the layperson to navigate and complete successfully. We receive requests for assistance daily, which distracts Environmental Scientist staff from other priorities. The helpdesk is understaffed, and the phone contact is no longer operational. Aside from scrapping the current program altogether for new in-house designed software (should be a top priority), a virtual in-program assistant would exponentially improve the end user experience, and scientific staff would receive far fewer assistance requests.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24051

    Seems like travel is a issue - would be more streamlined if there was a travel department to handle all travel for all staff. As it is now, there are people who don't want to attend work conferences due to the issues around knowing what form to fill out for what portion of travel. If this was centralized it would cut down on errors and really speed up the entire process. Just leave reimbursement in the hands of individuals, and have them call the "travel dept" to arrange the rest!

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24626

    As both a citizen of CA and a public sector worker, I see an issue with each state and county departments/agencies/boards being siloed with their data. This means I have to submit my information to each department every time I need services or to update my information to continue services at every stop. I would like to see all CA state depts move to a centralized data warehouse. I want to add or update my info 1x and have that info accessible by all state and county departments/agencies/boards. This reduces the burden on me to manage, increases data integrity, and allows for beneficial enhancements such as leveraging AI to review my info automatically and inform me when I qualify for a services across the state, and auto-populating my global data when I apply for these services - potentially doing all the work for me. It would also improve statical reporting statewide as the data would be pulled from one global system. CA citizens should have a CA profile, not a DMV profile, FTB profile, EDD profile, etc.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-22767

    When a construction project requires technology, that technology is out of date or obsolete by the time it is purchased for the project. A new system needs to be put in place that separates technology from the project so the latest technology can be procured closer to the time it will be installed.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23747

    Efficiency in multiple areas such as the Hiring process, grant applications for partners, contracting, procurement.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24606

    California is in a housing crisis. To make California's government more effective in responding to this crisis, I suggest that State properties be reevaluated to see if entire properties or portions of properties would be suitable for housing conversion. For example, the agency I work for has a large open parking lot adjacent to its office building, all of which is State property and near public transit. Could we maximize the use of this open parking lot by moving the parking space underground and building medium to high density housing units on top? Could there be a public-private partnership so that the State continues to own the land but leases it to a private developer so that the State ensures its needs are met? I know some transit agencies are converting transit parking lots into housing developments. Akin to some school districts, could we dedicate a portion of new housing to State employees at a discounted rate to attract and retain State employees?

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-22471

    The primary problem I would address is homelessness. I have several ideas to fix this problem, and they can be implemented in stages. First and foremost, make it easy to build affordable housing. Recognize that homelessness is a housing problem. 2nd, Prioritize the people who just slipped into homelessness and have potential and will to come out of it. Help them to come out permanently and join mainstream. Right now, I serve as board member for HART of Folsom, and we are proud to have reduced Folsom's homelessness by 10% this way. 3rd, go deeper and help people that are homeless longer and probably will never come out. Build shelters with services for them and make rules to humanely require them to take those services. Those rules might curtail some of their freedom, but they should know that it's cost to pay for not being able to come out of homelessness by themselves. Finally, to do all this work, encourage and fund the non-profits directly from the state so that the rules are uniform. One positive side effect is that more non-profit jobs are created this way. Each city and county having its own rules and managing and distributing funds will only create more red tape and confusion and dysfunction. Re-organize the current machinery and people work under one state agency with simple and uniform set of rules.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23829

    The biggest problem I've encountered is being pitted against corporate interests of the wealthy real estate developers with the return to work mandate. Lobbyists of this kind should never dictate how state agencies are run. Ignoring substantive reports of happiness, productivity, and culture thriving in work from home environments depletes the trust we have in state leadership. Ignoring the benefits of less pollution, less time wasted traveling, less money wasted traveling, decreased risk of workers' comp claims from being in the office, less money spent on parking, less money spent eating out, less money spent on childcare, etcetera ad nauseum because corporate/real estate tycoons aren't willing to evolve is punishing workers for having less lobbying power. My solution is to not ignore the data and productivity results from the executives entrusted with running our agencies. My solution is progressive and not regressive thinking. We can be a leader for change in this country by setting a progressive example starting with how we treat state workers - the proof is in the pudding, WFH works.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23847

    The rigid and structured processes to obtain and use new technology for state workers create a detrimental bottleneck. While barriers exist for a reason—such as security, cost, functional risks, and employee training—these issues can be addressed to allow the state to take advantage of improved tools and processes. Security Concerns: Instead of defaulting to denying every request, talented security teams should provide a plan on how to use the technology safely. Cost Control: Why should software or cloud services be marked up by middlemen who provide little value? Direct procurement could reduce costs significantly. Functional Risks: These can be alleviated by having capable, trained employees who have expertise in the technology being implemented. Discussions around these barriers to entry, why they exist, and how to minimize them are essential if the state of California wants to be efficient and effective in serving the people.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-23222

    Make all payments issued by the state including state employee pay, electronic.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24005

    Onboarding and employee information access should be much more streamlined and centralized. Perhaps a central onboarding portal could be setup by CDT or similar to capture all new employee info/benefit selections before moving along to agency/department-specific forms. For current state employees, perhaps combine CalConnect, myCalPERS etc into a central portal for state employees so everything from pay to leave to personal file to pension status would be in one spot with one login. It'd be like your state profile.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24692

    The reduction in telework and fully remote positions is a significant setback for the state workforce. When the State allowed fully remote positions post pandemic, I was thrilled. For me, that meant I finally had the opportunity to be in state service, which is a career path I am passionate about. Because I do not live in Sacramento, my options for state jobs have always been limited and not typically in my professional and academic field. Fully remote positions opened the door for me, and I am sure many others who want to contribute but are limited by their location in California. With the new state mandate, an essential repeal of fully remote positions, my ability to promote or grow is almost totally gone. I feel that maintaining the flexibility to offer fully remote positions strengthens the state's ability to retain a diverse and talented workforce, which will only make California stronger.

    August 22, 2025
  • Guest-24524

    I suggest that DGS look into Enterprise contracts for the state so that smaller board, dept, commissions can use those contracts. You get a better bargain as a giant contract and small organizations that can't afford contracts on their own can tag in to make their work for efficient. For example, one news clip service, one social and media monitoring service, an outreach eBlast function, one that checks writing so that our communications are professional, etc. I think that would save the state money and improve work functions for employees.

    Red tape needs to be minimized. I think using common technology platforms would help.

  • Guest-24284

    I have been working dealing with Contractors and we required from them to submit a breakdown of their bidding amounts. If they were to submit to me a breakdown of any project that I am doing at home, I paid close attention to the cost and figured out cost of materials and Labor. I can go online or go to the hardware stores and see the items prices. I have experienced many times in the State do not pay attention to the cost given by contractors and as long as we, State, have the money we approve the Contractor to start work. I was asked in a survey about improvements that we can recommend and I mentioned about that way we put a price on a project (estimate). The following year it was a requirement for us to show how we came up with the Estimated Cost to do the project- Breaking down all the work and cost. Someone created a database of cost and we started using that database without much thought and my opinion it became worse in coming out with estimating the cost of a project. The project cost is inflated 60 percent more to make sure we have enough money for the project- meaning still do not know what we are doing. If someone comes to my house and give me an estimated cost to place a lighting pole, trench 100 feet, dig a foundation, add a breaker. Do you think that I can get a quick estimate by going to lows, Home Depot? Yes! State estimate Cost $65,000 My estimated Cost $17,000

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23672

    The State of California fails to provide alignment between California State University certified degrees in Technology to the evolving needs of the state. Specifically, Computer Science Engineering where data optimization and web application development is learned fails to be supported by the state's second most influential union, the Professional Engineers in California Government (PECG). As a Civil Engineer myself, I have had to learn these skills on the job, with no vision ahead supporting the recruiting of the necessary talent needed. I served in PECG as a union rep and I learned it's up to Department of Technology to change their ways, not PECG. Overall, this is the biggest misalignment statewide affecting our ability to remain cutting edge and adapt to the future with AI.

    Not accepting "it's always been that way" as a reply.

  • Guest-24365

    Outdated technology has been a big obstacle to improving efficiency. Employees are often working on multiple systems and performing duplicative work.

  • Guest-24110

    I feel the organization I work is so "top" heavy, which creates siloed departments within the organization and constant miscommunication and confusion.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24468

    I am concerned about latent return-to-office mandates. I joined the state in November 2020, and have held excluded and represented positions at three different departments within the same field since then. I am now considered a statewide leader in that field. My work is best accomplished remotely; the same is true of my colleagues. I cannot speak for others, and many roles are critically accomplished through in-person attendance. However, a blanket return to office mandate is out of touch with the ground reality of the public employees who keep the state running. A blanket RTO mandate also puts undue pressure on departments and individuals to justify exceptions, particularly when it comes to Reasonable Accommodations; especially those RA's addressing invisible disability. Although I am aware (as an EEO investigator) that bad actors try to manipulate these civil protections, folks who have engaged in good faith interactions with their employers for many years are extremely anxious. Although their limitations have not changed, the expectations surrounding their employment will if RTO moves forward in 2026 (which, despite protective statute, is common via employer-friendly interpretations of RA law and policy). To me, the solution is obvious: reject blanket RTO so departments can TRULY treat each person & job uniquely. In addition to cost saving, the state will retain employees enthusiastic about their jobs and committed to careers that don't pay like the private sector.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24416

    One of the primary issues that I have observed during my 11 years of state service with the Department of Fish and Wildlife is the overall comprehensive need to establish new funding sources for fish and wildlife conservation, management, and research. Funding sources that were utilized decades ago will likely not be enough to fund the department in future decades. Hunting and fishing are still pertinent recreational activities for several sportsmen and women; however, the percentage of the California public that hunts and fish has decreased dramatically since the 1980's. The lack of funds coming from hunting and fishing tags has a direct impact on fish and wildlife conservation and management. At both a regional and statewide level, it would be pertinent for California state government to invest time and research into developing new funding sources for the department to support conservation activities.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24103

    The primary problem is that we cannot combat the public perception of being a bureaucracy when we only pay lip service to transparency. It's one thing to have information posted to a web site, but it's a data dump unless it's descriptive and explanatory to everyone accessing it. For one example, if there is a process that may take 60 to 90 days, such as obtaining a license or certification, have it clearly explained why there is a range of expected time. If people understand the process better, they become less frustrated with it. They may not like it, or agree with the decision, but at least they know that it was a deliberate decision made by actual people and not arbitrary or rubber-stamped.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24303

    - Bureaucracy & Slow processes: /Red tape makes things take forever /Innovation can stall because of approval processes taking too long/risks *Solution=introduce "Sandbox" spaces for innovation to test new processes before scaling (like Engaged California) - Pay vs Cost of Ling: /Salaries are steady but often don't keep pace w/housing costs. /Younger employees can't see the long-term pension payoff benefits & often leave for higher private-sector salary jobs *=Offer housing stipends for higher cost regions; introduce student loan repayment assistance as a benefit to attract younger employees; provide flexible bonus structures tied to performance - Generational Divide: /Older generations value stability, pensions & hierarchy /Younger generations crave flexibility, purpose-driven work & growth opportunities, often clashing with the older generation's work style and ethics *=create mentorship circles btwn younger and older staff (ie: sharing tech/digital savvy & institutional knowledge) -Technology Lag /Outdated software, clunky intranet systems, lack of modern tools that could make day-to-day operations smoother *=invest in cloud-based platforms for workflow automation -Rigid Culture: /Leadership sometimes struggles to communicate vision, changes, or policy shifts clearly /Employees feel out of the loop, which breeds mistrust and low morale. *=encourage intrapreneurship, celebrate wins publicly; use human centered design approach for solutions

    August 21, 2025

    Doing more work with less money The "padding" of HQ and Regional office staff, as an employee at an institution, we have hiring freezes, but these offices are so "top" heavy and certain positions seem unnecessary in the scheme of things.

    August 21, 2025

    Solve the compaction issue so people with experience will move up. It is not conducive to have a rank and file employee to have a higher salary than a manager. This is bad for morale and encourages people with less experience to apply for management positions they are not ready for.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-22598

    There is a lack of clear "Succession Planning" of current roles and potential vacancies within the departments, which jeopardizes the continuity of essential services. There is significant importance in knowledge transfer because people need to know what to do and how to perform and answer the questions correctly. Ideas to solve the problem: Mentorship programs - pair a seasoned professional with emerging talent. Skill development for all levels (supervisor/managers) should be more involved with development Workshops aimed at raising awareness Use scenarios to anticipate potential changes Develop a plan with regular evaluations Ensure the team is diverse enough to get different opinions Collaboration work groups Engaged California is a fantastic opportunity and platform that you have going, and I am glad to be a part.       September 30, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-24198

    Ongoing training that would assist state workers to do their jobs. Not all state agencies are open to continuous skill training for non-supervisory and non-managerial staff. However, support staff have heavy loads, and training helps staff work more efficiently and helps create a better use of staff as resources. For example, supervisors and managers are required to take certain trainings and they are provided great tools to become leaders. Not all state workers are going to be managers and supervisors; however, they will be more likely than not serving the state for more time as rank-and-file non-supervisory staff. Some agencies look more to trying to save on overtime, instead of focusing on ensuring that staff have necessary training. This would include technology and on the job training as well. Training helps staff want to stay in their jobs and helps them do a better job. This is good for the state and for morale of an employee.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23092

    I see a lot of disparaging comments about the state workforce in the press and social media, which depresses morale for existing state workers, and probably reduces the applicant pool for open positions. It seems like the general public's perception is that state workers don't work hard, when the opposite is true. I would like to see the State do more outreach supporting and uplifting its workforce while messaging the amazing array of jobs our staff perform and the incredible number of environments those jobs take place in. My department has done some of that internally, and I was really impressed.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24364

    Outdated minimum qualifications and lack of ability to promote in place which leads to a lack of expertise in state positions. For lack of expertise in state positions, low paying and low qualification positions see high turnover. In administrative units we lose someone with years of knowledge and experience so that they can promote. That person is worth more in their current position with the knowledge they have amassed. If they are eligible to apply to a higher administrative position, they should be eligible to promote in place. The high turnover makes state processes extremely slow as we wait for someone to be trained or wait for the position to be filled. For outdated minimum qualifications, an example is the Water Resource Control Engineer position. It requires a limited type of degree from a limited set of schools, yet engineers at other agencies (such as CalTrans, air resources, CalFire, etc.) allow for engineers that have an EIT and are on the licensing path (which is allowed by the Board of Licensing for engineers). This limits our pool of qualified candidates, when there are licensed civil engineers who cannot be hired because of outdated qualifications. Circling back, the process to even get this looked at is slow because of admin unit turnover and lack of expertise.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24479

    Lack of uniform training or lack of central units in doing the same assignment. For example, a person works in contract, well the person leaves the agency to go into another contract unit. If there was a central place where all procurement and contracts can be done then there shouldn't be people transferring out to go to another unit. It's all the same work and should be all the same instructions out of the SCM manual. This would save hiring time, recruitment process, re training the new employee. In summary, all the contracts should be handled by the Agency called the Department of Contracts.

    August 21, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-24412

    One primary problem I would address is the idea that supervision/management is just the next step in making more money. A supervisory position should be one that helps the team, not one that makes it worse by 1) removing an expert and 2) giving someone a position they're either not ready for or don't really want. Supervisors and managers need better soft, people skills. We need people in those positions who want to lead, connect, and improve their teams. We don't need someone who just didn't have any other way to increase their salary. So, start by fixing the salary system. We need good people in every classification and series, and we need to be able to reward them and their expertise.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24358

    There are far too many hurdles in the required purchasing requirements and approval processes for equipment, supplies, and contracts. More streamlined processes and granted authorities are needed. Additionally, utilizing large retailers would save the State abundant funds.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24129

    One problem I have seen many times is silo-ing where programs do not talk to each other even when their actions will affect one another. This could apply to many situations but one I've seen a lot is during regulatory development, the regulatory team does not always reach out to other programs like admin/finance, enforcement, community outreach, etc. They also do not always provide resources for those groups to help implement the regulation via positions or money. I would suggest improving collaboration across divisions/agencies, and doing so as early in the process as possible. This will also prevent needing to make as many changes to the reg or process in the future.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24171

    I know state agencies aren't supposed to have contractors long-term, but almost every agency has contractors in their IT department because it's hard to offer a competitive salary for good talent and the infrastructure for getting good IT contractors is robust. It would be great if there was support for integrating contractors smoothly when they come on board. My team acquired two IT contractors in the last year or so, and I wish some kind of training had been available to help set my expectations for what contractor teammates are like. They were highly skilled, but needed much more specific, hands-on direction than I expected. They also had different expectations for interactions and I think there was some confusion as to who their directions were supposed to come from.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24145

    Modernize our payroll system, PLEASE!!!

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24232

    I find that the processes become more important than the outcomes and we may want to focus more on principals/outcomes and guidelines to achieve that versus tasks and processes.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23967

    >>Problem: The Six-Month Probationary Period for AGPAs is Inadequate to Properly Train and Evaluate >>Solution: Extend AGPA Probationary Period to One Year ------------ I recommend we align the probationary period for Associate Governmental Program Analysts (AGPA) with that of Staff Services Analysts (SSA), Staff Services Manager I (SSM1), and most other state classifications. The current 6-month probationary period for AGPA's is insufficient for thorough training and evaluation, particularly for new hires without prior program knowledge and experience. This short period forces managers to make premature decisions about an employee's capabilities, potentially leading to the rejection of individuals who could excel with more time. By increasing the probationary period to one-year, new AGPAs will have adequate time to become proficient in their roles, ensuring fair evaluation and comprehensive training. This change will also expand the hiring pool, allowing managers to confidently consider AGPA candidates, knowing they will have sufficient time to develop necessary skills. The 6-month probationary period should be retained for AGPAs promoted in place, as they already possess relevant experience from their SSA roles in the same program. Extending the AGPA probationary period to one year for new hires would be a win-win for staff and managers.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23778

    Organizations are too top heavy. This creates resistance to innovation and progress. Get rid of middle management or consolidate and disperse.

    August 21, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21488

    As a unit wildlife biologist working in the Bay Delta Region's Wildlife and Lands Management Program, one of the biggest problem areas I see comes down to a lack of staff, training, and resources to be able to effectively address the public's needs surrounding human-wildlife conflict education and response. For example, we typically have one biologist assigned to cover 2-3 counties in the Bay Area. That single biologist is responsible for managing thousands of acres of CDFW lands, responding to human-wildlife conflict incidents in the counties they cover, AND monitoring/studying various taxa of wildlife, whether it be threatened and endangered species on our lands or game species. The vast majority of the time, we receive calls from the public about injured wildlife or smaller wildlife that are stuck in netting or fences, etc., and we do not have the level of staff or resources to address such calls. Which leaves the public asking: "If animal services won't address this and you guys can't address it, then who will?" To which the answer is, sadly: nobody. We used to have temporarily funded conflict wildlife staff who could help address such calls - and we do not have them anymore - and our wildlife and public affected by such wildlife suffer the consequences - and it doesn't make the Department look good when we aren't able to address such calls on a more regular basis. We also need to have a much better presence on social media and spend more time educating the public

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23880

    I would stop dividing people and bring them together. Our politicial system is not geared towards that goal. the hate expressed by those of both parties is just sickening. The salution is simple but not easy. Do unto others as you would have them do unto you. People need to stop blaming others for their failures and take responsibility. Averyone is trying to change others when the fact is the only one you can truly change is yourself, start there. Stop looking for the speck in your nighbors eye and remove the plank from your eye first.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-2749

    I wish that all departments were on the same instance of Microsoft so that we could more easily share files and collaborate. Maybe we need some more SharePoint architecture training to make sure our systems are set up in a way that makes then functional and not a barrier to working collaboratively.

    August 21, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-2749

    I think the CDT PAL process should be made simple enough that departments do not need to hire an outside consultant to complete the paperwork. If we have created a cottage industry just to navigate our processes then our process isnt working.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24145

    One of the biggest problems I've seen is centered around adoption of technology. When FISCAL was rolled out statewide by the Department of Technology, the Department of Technology was among the last to adopt the program themselves. The should have been among the first departments along with finance, to adopt the program, fix its flaws and learn it themselves before rolling it out every other agency.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24137

    Administrative processes, such as time accounting, expense reporting, and field vehicle maintenance, take way too much time to complete. This includes the software used to input information, management approval process, and revision of forms that need to be resubmitted. Expense reporting, including receipt submission should be done all electronically (rather than having a requirement to hard mail in forms that can get lost). The process for expense report is not intuitive and expense reports often get rejected, resulting in additional staff to correct these forms and correspond with reviewers. An updated program, even if one created by a vendor, should be used for this. The systems used for time accounting is very outdated and clunky. I have used much better time accounting systems in previous jobs, which require less time to complete and are easier to use. State vehicle maintenance is a nightmare - there is no fleet maintenance program for our state vehicle - we are required to track maintenance ourselves. Getting repairs done is difficult, even if the repair is urgent, requiring us get procurement approval for the repair and to use a very limited number of maintenance shops in our area. In addition, we have to use multiple maintenance shops (one for tires, another for battery change out), which are typically about 20-40 miles away from our office and require staff to lose at least half a day to wait for these repairs.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23857

    In my field, the lack of the implementation of current technology in almost every aspect of my duties becomes more and more evident. Much of our personnel are used NOT to accomplish our primary mission but to collect and track stats, prepare memos and reports, and process literal paperwork. Modern digital databases and internet-based solutions has the ability to eliminate much of this time-consuming work. We need an investment in the right people and talent to find technology based solutions to eliminate the need for manual data entry, stat collection, and paper forms.

    August 21, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22363

    Remove the layers of hierarchy. I think it is possible to work in more of a self-managed team (shared leadership, distributed responsibilities, etc.), but in our current environment the various levels of hierarchy discourage taking on "more" responsibilities when the pay levels are so different. I think that if the levels of hierarchy were removed, teams would be open to working as self-managed team by starting to think and operate as a collective (vs. as individuals) - establish how decisions within the team are made, how conflict or tensions are resolved, how strengths are leveraged, how feedback is given, etc.

    August 21, 2025 👍 6
  • Guest-23857

    A small example of a common problem: We need three padlocks for a new storage container. These could be had for a combined cost of $35 and a 30 minute trip to the local Home Depot. Instead, there is a ridiculous purchasing process which will require at least a couple hours and result in a cost closer to $200. Although in the end it may be saving money as more often then not would rather do without then go through the process, or someone will just buy what they need with their own money.

    August 21, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22593

    Some continuity between different agencies

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23864

    Problem: Inefficiency and Redundancy California state departments often operate in silos, with structures built on highly centralized approaches. This creates disconnection across agencies, leading to duplicated efforts, wasted time, resource, and limited collaboration. As a result, departments struggle to share knowledge, coordinate resources, and work toward common goals, reducing overall efficiency and effectiveness. Solution: Proposed Solution: Foster Collaboration and Shared Resources Shift from siloed, centralized structures toward a more connected, collaborative model. This can be done by creating cross-departmental working groups, shared digital platforms, and standardized processes that encourage transparency and knowledge sharing. By streamlining workflows and pooling resources, departments can reduce redundancy, improve efficiency, and deliver more consistent and effective services to Californians.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24112

    While public service offers meaningful work and job stability, California's large footprint means state employees may work more than 30-50 miles or half hour to an hour from a state office, with California's high cost of living - often times outpace state employees' salaries. This creates challenges such as: Struggling to afford gas going into offices more than 2xs a week. Commuting long distances (adding stress and reducing work -life balance), difficulty retaining skilled employees in state service, overall increased pressure on mental and financial well-being. Solution would be to retain the 2 day a week in office work schedule for state employees without negotiating a reduction in pay in exchange (2026). This effort will demonstrate the value that our current government feels about out state employees and their overall well-being.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-22588

    I think universal access to productivity tools would allow agencies to streamline so much. But some agencies decide not to procure certain things because they have different priorities. If I transfer between agencies, I want to be able to use similar tools across the board for project management/tracking, and database creation. The entire Microsoft suite should be bare minimum for those of us who live to create efficiencies no matter where we are.

    August 21, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21215

    We seem to be dismissing the importance of educating the public AHEAD of a problem. We are very reactionary. After they have a problem, we have call-centers staffed with huge numbers of State workers trying to solve their problems one-on-one...individually. How much of that could be addressed more efficiently by educating groups of business owners and individuals on how to do required tasks before they've made a mistake? One State worker could educate hundreds rather than less efficient one-on-one help. Once the public has made a mistake, we have huge numbers of State workers to try and collect a penalty or interest. Wouldn't it be more efficient to just try and educate them so they do things correctly in the first place? It makes one wonder if the State has become RELIANT upon penalties and interest and can't afford to give those up?

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24075

    Quality of work performance. Hold employees accountable for quality of work and quantity of work.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-21943

    In private sector, folks expect that gen AI and other LLMs will make work more efficient therefore contracts are baking in 1-5% reduction year over year in allocated costs. We often bake in inflation but don't expect efficiencies from new tech etc when we reprocure. I would recommend that our contract template includes an expectation for efficiency year over year from our vendors.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23812

    From my short experience here, it seems the reps are left out of a lot of the planning, problem solving, and tasks to get projects through the system. In our team, meetings are often cancelled when we are going through the heaviest of tasks like getting ready for ILC, I believe this is a time when management needs to be transparent about what is happening behind the scenes and getting reps involved to help speed up the process. I will go days without having a substantial amount of work to do while specialists and managers are working overtime. I have a feeling many of my peers are doing little to no work most days of the week and I believe there is a lot of waste here. I believe our managers should empower and TRAIN the reps to be able to confidently take on more tasks. It is taking far too long for projects to get approved and to closing. I am not sure what the process looks like to fix it, but it should not take this long and there is often no sense of urgency from anyone on the team. I believe that many reps feel that they have the ability and the desire to play a bigger role, and they have the knowledge and experience to be able to do so but are often overlooked and undervalued

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23464

    No idea how to answer this. I have tried 5 times now. Let's start with one initiative that affects us all, and FIX IT. Water? Immigration? Cost of housing? Rising Energy Prices? The list goes on. Whatever it is, even though we live in a Republic....multiple elections and voting by the people would be nice...as well as within the areas that are affected most. Such as water...does Northern California near Shasta really have a water issue...NO, so then maybe the Northern Californians do not make decisions based on where the water comes from, because they have a plethora...and etc... Such as Homelessness...what is the impact per census on the area...the area with the greatest impact has the larger say so?

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23403

    Using technologies that were burgeoning 10-20 years, while not keeping up with modern stacks. Promote courses that allow staff to stay up-to-date with innovative technologies and provide them access to them.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-21551

    The primary problem is the state trying to take away telework. Telework is proven to work, productivity is up and morale from the staff is high.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-21567

    The State, in general, is quite inefficient. Having spoken with many relatives and friends who are employed with the State in various different departments and agencies, there is simply not enough work assigned to each employee. For example, employees are assigned a weekly workload that are able to be accomplished within a day or less and as a result, employees have nothing to do for the remainder of the week. This may be caused by the fact that there are excessive number of employees assigned to a specific project/assignment/role. Employees who speak up to seek more work are often frowned upon because their peers do not want to be assigned more work. Employees who are comfortable with their low workload would not speak up (and sometimes even exaggerate how "busy" they are) in order to preserve their low workload. As a hard-working State employee and a California taxpayer, I believe the State needs to do a thorough time study to evaluate the amount of work being performed by State employees and determine if there is sufficient workload assigned to employees before considering to hire more people. Highly recommend that the time study is conducted by auditors or a third-party and not conducted internally or "self-assessed" workload analysis because there will be inherent biases.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24088

    It would be amazing if lower ranking HR employees had more opportunity to learn niche topics from CalHR. I have struggled finding where I belong in the HR world as a State of CA employee, and I believe that is due to not being exposed or officially trained in most areas of HR.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24058

    I think longer tenured middle management positions are unwilling to adopt new tools or technology that would assist in efficiency. While the adoption phase of new technologies may be inefficient at first, once people get used to them, it may make completing tasks much easier. I think this would require more top-down enforcement and accountability standards from the executive directors and branch chiefs.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-21922

    In my 17 years experience, the CalHR rules have become so rigidly enforced that our organization has many problems because CalHR doesn't allow flexibility for us to optimize operations. We end up keeping many positions that could have been consolidated, as certain work gets deprioritized. We used to be able to move positions around to meet division needs, but now we have to request a new position. We are constantly losing high quality staff because strictness of HR rules enforced means high quality candidates often fall outside the narrowness of the HR rules. We would be so much more effective and efficient if CalHR could allow the flexibility they used to give to allow us to determine what are needs are and move around resources to meet them. Everything has become so top down in recent years, with no opportunity for discussion to figure out how to get the department's needs met. So this feedback tool is appreciated.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23422

    Hiring is a primary problem, first in the length of time it takes to hire and in the merit hiring process. I would propose using an AI system with CalCareers to connect key words in the application packages (resume, SOQ, app) to the desirable qualifications. They system could then screen applications and redact information like names and places of employment before apps are released to managers. This has been discussed as a manual process but most HR shops do not have the manpower to implement, so AI would provide a more effective and affordable process.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24149

    I believe that the state should be taking more advantage of the buying power they have by having more standardization of product in use. This would also allow departments to "borrow" another department's expertise if they lose a SME for a product.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-24199

    Cut the Red Tape, just because some training is applicable for one agency, doesn't means it will work for all. Make policy and guidelines easier to find instead of having to bounce all around the internet.

  • Guest-21051

    Problem: There is no uniform policy among the Dept of State Hospitals for their library programs. Ex: There is no policy on what type of books to buy for the patients nor is there anyone to report patron statistics to. What legal resources do we need to offer and what forms are patient's entitled to. There should be uniform and consistency among the library hospitals as well as accountability in the form of a Library department for the Dept of State Hospitals.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-22937

    I believe that we (state) can be more fiscally sound when purchasing items/or making contracts required for operations- day to day, emergencies, etc. I feel that the contracts that we put out into the communities are being price gouged by the bidders. Also if contracts are not adhered to, we still pay the amount the bidders originally stated. (IE, Construction issues, delays, costing more than originally bid for) I think that the end product after a project is completed is also not up to par more often than not and then the State (and its entities) Has to pay more money to have it fixed either by the original contractor or by a new contractor. What about charging for delays? If a contract for construction takes longer than originally planned (by the bidder) then they are charged per day/week/month extra. If a contract cost more than originally planned for (by the bidder) than the bidder eats the cost, not the state and the tax payers? I know some of these options are not viable but there are ways to help save the taxpayer and the state overhead on projects and contracts.

    August 21, 2025

    management literacy and consistency, evaluations of management classifications by rank and file staff to gain effective leadership skills and feedback

    more training and accountability for all classifications and levels of staff

    A more innovative hiring process. Update classification specifications, ensure salaries are competitive, provide incentives, expedite but allow for a quality way to screen applicants (i.e., applicants use AI and its difficult to assess their capabilities on a AI-developed SOQ).
