  • Guest-23693

    My department spend countless thousands of staff hours and dollars on preparing for the 4-day RTO order. Ultimately, it was deferred. While we were working on this half-baked directive, we did so at the expense of our core work, namely serving our fellow Californians. Attrition due to this anti-worker mandate has also led to a measurable loss in productivity and has again diverted hours to recruiting and retraining instead of serving the State.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-21771

    The system of CEAs, or Career Executive Appointments, and appointments to heads of government agencies is perhaps the single largest problem in California government. The appointment of people with little to no direct experience in the work of an agency results in these CEAs hiring teams of consultants to advise them on what actions to take, which bill at rates of $400-$600/hour ($800,000 to $1.2mm FTE salary). Then these political appointees hire people that they know who also are not experts in government or the agency's business and use consultants to teach them how to run the business. Then, anyone with actual knowledge of the agency's business leaves because they're not valued and they see the waste all around them. They know that they would make more money as a consultant instead or have less frustration working for an ignorant boss and being a punching bag for the general public who don't value the work of government either. Many state employees are just contract managers and have little or no private sector experience. The state needs to be serious about paying people what they are worth so they can attract real experts to run departments and to eliminate the system of appointments that destroys expertise. Politicians are unwilling to pay for the true worth of a worker that knows what they're doing and instead they pay multiples of a true, market-rate salary to consultants and appointees that produce no actual work. Furloughs also kill retention efforts.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23824

    Agencies have shared contractual needs. Would like to see a repository of common services statements of work templates in DGS library as an appendix or via a link in the SCM that can be utilized at different agencies understanding there still may be a need for customization to include specific logistical instructions for various facilities, frequency of service and other items. E.g. for pest control, trash/compost, fire extinguisher testing/certification, IT server room cleaning, etc. Would like an organization chart with email/phone information at various agencies available for state employees to access for networking and collaborative problem solving. An activity or program may be new to an agency but is not new to another agency, example a new grant program authorized for an agency to implement that has not managed grant program funds for years or previously at all. Having the ability to ask how a different agency has set up a program recently and leverage existing operating procedures would be more efficient than starting from scratch.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-21051

    Having an overreaching library policy for the Dept of State Hospitals - right now we have a limited policy at my particular hospital and no consistency among other state hospitals. The policy does not address the types of books to buy nor is there a lead librarian among the state hospitals to reports patron statistics to.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-21346

    State of California should act as a consolidated Entity and Department for purposes of time reporting, procurement and Administration. Every department having these functions segregated, implementing their own tools and procuring software and services is wasting significant tax money. If everyone gets paid through SCO Payroll, time reporting governed by CalHR and SCO should also be a central function and statewide portal for State of CA employees.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23340

    We need more effective systems for capturing institutional knowledge. So many folks are retiring/nearing retirement and their replacements--if the position isn't swept or reclassified--have to reinvent the wheel. Obviously we don't want to keep doing things the way they've always been done, but we could save a lot of time and energy by having comprehensive job aides and reference materials kept as living documents to support new hires.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23792

    The State would benefit from better succession planning and diversifying staff roles. There is a lot of "siloing" in the State where one person does only one primary task or project. This means that if this staff person leaves, the majority of the knowledge regarding that task/subject is gone. If we diversified staff roles and allowed staff to branch out and learn new skill sets, then we wouldn't be scrambling every time someone retires or leaves State service.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23825

    Management buy-in and disconnect from rank-and-file workers and workload. Proposing more leadership audience focused webinars encouraging change/growth mindset. Especially regarding updates to new practices for Gc requirements like plain language.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23619

    I think the largest problem is keeping up with work orders and maintaining the highway. We have more requests than we are able to handle.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23839

    As a newer state employee, I believe we waste too much time updating content on our internal and external websites. Every hour wasted has to be funded from the taxes of hard-working Californians, and so we should try to reduce that waste. I believe the latest State Web Template is a great solution to get all of our sites using a similar codebase. However, our department is having to update HTML code by-hand, using nearly obsolete software. I propose using a open-soure framework like Laravel, which runs on PHP. It would allow us to easily port the State Web Template components into reusable componenents that could be placed on web pages without having to painstakingly copy code. We also could probably provide much of the content in Markdown format, which could be converted to accessible HTML on the fly. Laravel also has some database-driven content management system options such as Statamic. The end result is that we could save time and money for Californian, while also providing more consistent and timely content.

    August 21, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-23065

    Problems & Solutions: P1) People with state experience have too much of an advantage over potential candidates with no state experience, limiting the expansion and diversity of employment opportunities S1) Provide open training and learning opportunities to all potential hires to familiarize people with state operations prior to applying. Example: An overview video discussing FI$Cal which is a system that is heavily used by many departments but would be unknown to anyone outside of state work. P2) Management do not seek the opinions of rank-and-file staff when proposing changes, limiting the potential for innovative ideas S2) Implement systems like Engaged California on a department level. P3) State exams that test experience, education, and training do not accurately test whether a candidate is fit for a position S3) Phase out this type of exam, as it only provides unnecessary hurdles to qualified candidates, and give more discretion to departments to hire candidates. Hiring process would still check Minimum Qualifications for the position.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23854

    The primary problem with state services is a lack of expense consciousness. Because government is structured to focus on a need being met, there is little interest in ensuring that the need being met is done in an efficient and cost effective manner. Agencies are punished for not spending all the money allocated to them because the next year, their budget gets reduced. This creates a use it or lose it mentality, and less concern is given on whether we are being good stewards of the public's money. To solve this problem, policies must be set that reward agencies that save the government money rather than penalizing them by reducing their future budget. Another problem is that state agencies do not talk to each other. We are all under the State umbrella, however, no collaboration or connection opportunities exist to be able to compare notes. Most agencies face similar problems and the ability to discuss possible solutions or even understanding what other agencies have done to address their problems is a missed opportunity. There is no where that allows us to see the organization structure, duty statements, or reports completed by other agencies. There is no directory to reach out to other agencies and collaborate. I would create a state agency directory and a policy would have require state agencies to comply by sharing their organization charts as well as contact information to other areas. I would restrict access of executive leadership to only executive leadership.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23872

    The State has too many levels of managers/management that have no primary responsibility other than managing people. This creates a bottleneck of work that needs to be reviewed by multiple people who aren't directly involved in the work and often do not provide value-added feedback. Additionally, having managerial positions takes away a position that could be filled by an analyst or specialist. With the rise of technology like Teams and DocuSign that make it easier to work collaboratively from different locations, we no longer need the number of managers that many departments have.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23850

    One of the challenges in California is that residents often don’t feel government is transparent or responsive to their needs, especially when they face delays or can’t find clear information.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23578

    The biggest problem I have encountered as a state employee has been the lack of accountability or actions taken for toxic management. I have now been in two units where this was a problem. In one there was a mass exit of employees who had been there for a long time (including myself), and in the other, they had extremely high turnover, most employees using return rights and not staying more than six months. Exit interviews should be conducted for every employee, no exceptions, and taken seriously, with actions being put in place to retain future employees. In the first scenario, losing the long-term staff put a massive workload on those of us who stayed, causing a decrease in moral and a loss of historical knowledge, which lead to more staff leaving. The management's poor behavior continued and because no changes were made, the work environment was extremely toxic. In the second scenario, the manager was an extreme micromanager who would single out and belittle staff, frequently teaming up with another manager to gang up on a single staff member. I know these scenarios are not unique, which is why I believe exit interviews for each and every exiting employee are essential, but only if they are taken seriously, good or bad.

    August 21, 2025 👍 3
  • Guest-23903

    I personally think AWARE is the main cause of almost all the Problems at the DOR. The website we use is beyond slow and takes up so much time. I timed how long it take to open a Attachments or a plan and it takes 32 seconds, and I open almost 50 documents a day in AWARE. I did the math and that equals 26 minutes of waiting at the end of the day. That is precious time that I could have met with a participant, but instead I'm waiting for an attachment to open. Also, each time they update aware it messes up almost everything, every time. In the last update it make it so I couldn't see SSN or any valuable information and it took weeks to figure it out. secondly, I think "Activity Dues" (AD) are an awful way to delegate tasks. When changes are made or notes were left on the AD we don't get told at all. We have to remember to go back and check, and with a caseload of 200, that is almost impossible. Lastly, The state need to live in reality and realize that people don't want to work/stay at the DOR is because our pay absolutely absurd!! For the amount of work we do, and the amount of situations this job put us in, we are treated and paid like our work doesn't matter. People at the DOR feel like the state of California doesn't care about their civil servants who dedicate their life's to helping the lives of people with disabilities. If you care about this organization and want it to be better, act like it.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23900

    All of California's departments should be sharing training resources for efficiency, consistency, and to save money. Rather than each department purchasing their own training resources contracts, there is one contract that provides them for every department. The more licenses there are being purchased, the higher the discounts are.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23963

    As a state employee, I am embarrassed by the tone of the Twitter posts coming from the Governor's office. I strive to maintain professionalism and civility when I conduct business on behalf of the state. It is demoralizing to see the tone that is being set from the top of state government, regardless of whether or not I agree with the underlying principles.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23809

    One primary problem that often arises is the lack of streamlined processes and the underutilization of advanced methodologies like Six Sigma, especially in the context of AI advancements. Here are a few ideas to address this problem: -Recognition and Integration of Certifications: Advocate for the recognition of Six Sigma and other relevant certifications within your department. This could involve presenting case studies or data that demonstrate the tangible benefits of these methodologies in similar organizations. -Training and Development Programs: Implement comprehensive training programs that focus on process improvement techniques, including Six Sigma. This could be part of a broader professional development initiative aimed at enhancing the skills of state employees. -AI-Driven Process Optimization: Leverage AI to analyze current processes and identify inefficiencies. AI can provide insights into areas where time and resources are being wasted and suggest more efficient workflows. -Pilot Projects: Start with small-scale pilot projects to demonstrate the effectiveness of new processes and methodologies. Successful pilots can build momentum and support for broader implementation. -Cross-Department Collaboration: Encourage collaboration between different departments to share best practices and successful strategies. This can help create a culture of continuous improvement and innovation. -Feedback Mechanisms: Establish robust feedback mechanisms to continuously gather inpu

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23693

    DHCS pursuing non-competitive bids to award contracts worth tens of millions of dollars. Urgency in procuring a vendor is manufactured due to poor planning by leadership and the only solution proposed is to circumvent the competitive, transparent procurement process. Current vendors who are not meeting their obligations (and who were similarly retained through a non-competitive process) are not held accountable. Leaders are awarding contracts which pay state and federal funds based on personal relationships with vendors and not as the result of a comprehensive, objective assessment of costs and capabilities.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23161

    Employee motivation - As managers, you don't have a lot of incentive to offer, as well as you don't have a lot of authority for discipline.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23159

    Communication between supervisors and employees is a huge issue with every department I have worked in. There just does not seem to be ways to build as a team within when supervisor's don't take an active role in being personable and approachable to their employees. Also, "suits & ties" at headquarters need to stop making decisions and changing policies when they haven't taken the time to get to know the issues and successes of the employees and supervisors. Administration should be focused on true employee wellness, not just making it look good for the public's eye. Part of that means being personable and truly transparent with staff members. Find out what they are really dealing with in their departments/fields.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23465

    In my career with the state, I've always been surprised as to how different departments use different tools to do the same thing. Similar to the FisCAL effort, which shows how hard this can be, all departments should be using the same software and tools for things like personnel and HR information, web site content, and project management (SharePoint).

    August 21, 2025 👍 2
  • Guest-22930

    PUBLIC POLICY AND BENEFICIARIES OF THE LAW SOUROUNDING IT. IT SEEM LIKE PEOPLE IN GENERAL THINK FOR INSTANCE THAT PUBLIC BENEFITS ARE A HANDOUT BUT IT IS NOT BECAUSE THOSE BENEFIT FROM IT HAVE TO QUALIFY FOR IT.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-22803

    Duplicated work within state government agencies remains a significant concern, leading to inefficiencies, increased costs, and delays in service delivery to the public. Overlapping responsibilities, siloed operations, and lack of coordination between departments often result in multiple teams performing the same or similar tasks independently. Addressing this issue requires a focused effort to streamline processes, improve inter-agency communication, and implementing integrated systems that promote transparency and accountability. Reducing duplication not only enhances operational efficiency but also ensures that taxpayer resources are used more effectively to serve the public interest.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-22411

    Learning about the state budget. There could be much more time invested in training and onboarding staff to support fiscal stewardship.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23703

    Sometimes I feel like we are working in silos and competing rather than working as one cohesive group. I think there could be better communication from the top down, and the burden shouldn't be solely on management who are already overwhelmed.

    August 21, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-23528

    I believe the Prospective Payment System (PPS) methodology for Federally Qualified Health Centers and Rural Health Clinics should be reformed. The PPS for Medi-Cal is highly overinflated and requires capping like the Medicare program. This type of reform would require legislation to change statute.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23172

    I think that we have to fix the contracting process. It simply takes too long to be efficient. A large part of the problem is the multiple hurdles that have been imposed on us from the Legislature, which passes these changes to the law, but never seems to revisit them to see if it's working. I had been working on a contract for months when I was told at the last minute that I had to use a different contracting process because of a legal change. Madness. My recommendation would be for the Governor to call a special session of the Legislature to focus on how the State conducts business, specifically contracting. Maybe include some State employees who actually do the work to testify, rather than just the upper management.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23662

    As a state worker, we have access to guides (for ex, DRE uses elicensing and we have instruction guides to help callers through a non user friendly portal). I have suggested that these guides be shared to the public to help lower elevated calls on the call center. The elicensing guides are supposed to be added to the public website in the next update. I would suggest we add similar instruction guides in regards to assisting filing out apps or guides to online portals to help the public decrease the processing wait times and with the call centers.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23647

    I have been with the state for 9 years and 3 months. As a state employee I feel that the new hires are not getting the proper training to succeed in their job, that is why we have a lot of turn around and a lot of mistakes. I believe that our state should train these new hires properly, with in classroom training and not just virtually on the computer. The state would save more money by just investing in sending these new hires to get the proper training instead of having them retake the virtual classes again and again it’s just a waste of money and time.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23612

    The primary problem I'd like to address is modernizing our technology and streamlining processes. Coming from private sector, I was shocked to find the very manual processes due to budget constraints and inability to invest in system to streamline processes (e.g. HRIS)

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-22142

    I very much appreciate the need for all State Employees to have a shared structure so we all have a similar experience with our State Employment through CalHR. Every department however has their own functionality and needs. Creating a structure that allows for more flex and takes into account the needs of each state department. This would be parameters outside of the Union contracts. Creating a framework that has less structure but provides broader guidance instead would allow different departments to create more tailored pay scales and position classifications enabling a more competitive recruiting position. This could include types of bonus plans, performance incentives, singing bonuses, efficiency creation, etc.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-22956

    I'd like to easily be able to see the most recent comments on this page, instead of having to click the comment button a bunch of times. Just to start!

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23533

    I think a great way to make California's government more efficient is to allow those that can to work from home as much as possible. This saves the state money, it often allows for a better quality of living and saved money for the worker and doesn't seem to impinge on productivity as has been shown since working remotely since the pandemic. Those in management would do well to understand a happy workforce is a productive one, and forcing people into working conditions where they have to spend more money to work will not help. You can entice better workers and keep those that are doing well by offering this kind of incentive that so many are doing away with. The primary job of the government should be to serve its people. Working from home does this, it helps everyone in this State.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23646

    California needs to stop throwing money into it's useless GenAI platform. The data it was trained on is hilariously outdated and a huge waste of money. The use of GenAi makes State workers lazier and its impacts on the environment are vast.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23631

    hiring staff- it takes 3-6 months there are so many layers that are redundant being able to let go of staff is also a challenge- poor performing staff leave one divsion for another and the hiriging staff are not made aware the employee was a challenge

    August 21, 2025 👍 2
  • Guest-23600

    Forms, processes, systems, and software are extremely outdated. Suggesting the State maybe have one large contract with Microsoft or a vendor for supplies that way we all have access to the same quality of resources.

    August 21, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-23689

    Housing! Housing! Housing! As a civil servant, I do not earn enough money to even afford a 1 bedroom apartment where I live. It would be amazing if the state could build more housing for low-income people who work full time, like me! Allowing people to telecommute is good for the environment, reduces traffic, and increases a department's ability to fill positions. I don't telecommute myself, but I think telecommuting should remain an option. If more people could telecommute, that means they can live anywhere. Spreading out the demand for housing outside of the Silicon Valley, Sacramento, and Los Angeles may help decrease the overall prices in those already congested areas. The state already owns a lot of land. Build work centers (hoteling?) for state employees in different areas where housing is cheaper. Build employee housing at places like Napa State Hospital to attract good candidates and keep them. We have a high turnover and our employees commute from all over. Our campus is beautiful and we already have our own police and fire department. Build little cottages that employees can rent for a decent price! Or, provide housing subsidies for state employees who do not earn enough to afford rent in high cost areas.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23324

    Talent retention is a problem as many private sector jobs have higher compensation. Here are a list of solutions: 1. Market rate compensation 2. Flexible work hours 3. Get rid of the RTO mandate 4. Put work from home into law 5. State worker compensation should not be a political issue

    August 21, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-23710

    Remove communication barriers between departments and strengthen partnerships with California businesses to reduce fraud. Better coordination ensures benefit applications are verified efficiently, eliminating redundant checks and reducing fraud risk. Streamlined communication unites internal teams and creates more effective collaboration with businesses. This makes the government faster, simpler, and more trustworthy.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-21924

    I believe we could do better when it comes to employee training, from the onboarding process all the way through the employee's career. Providing more classes that are free would help during these financially challenging times for the departments. I also think CalHR should have oversight on this matter to ensure employees are able to take advantage of the training that is available while also providing career guidance tools. Setting a minimum threshold for training hours per employee, per year, could help with this goal, along with creating a Career Support website full of tools and guidance. More education will result in better retention, a more educated workforce, and a reduction in sick hours because employees will feel supported and enriched. This will all lead to more production within the tasks that are required to run the state, which will save the state money over time. You see this in the private sector, from which many of us came from.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23147

    The primary problem I would address is inconsistent/unfair in the hiring practice. For example, mid/senior management with no prior state service experience, skills or knowledge matching the job description getting the job whereas internal candidates are overlooked. I understand there are some positions that are elected positions in the state. Majority of the positions are filled with who you know basis rather than merit based. The idea to resolve the problem is to have regular audits on hiring processes for all the state departments. There should an oversight by the California State Auditor's Office.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23221

    As a new employee of my Department and in this State, I had a large learning curve. I didn't totally understand my role and responsibilities until a person with my same job in a different District came to our office to train me and shadow me while I worked. Before, it was very hard to understand due to the amount of unique acronyms used by the state even within guidance and or training.

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-23063

    The problem is repetitive work (ie. data entry) that can be minimized or streamlined using readily available software and/or AI.

  • Guest-23748

    I believe that state employees should have a higher degree of data literacy. Most (if not all) of my colleagues work with data in some way - whether it be simply reading/reviewing reports, to complex data analysis. Yet, so few of my colleagues possess modern skill sets to analyze/process data, and limited understandings of statistics (which is often the most key aspect of regulatory science!). I have witnessed piecemeal efforts to increase data literacy and train employees to use modern coding tools (e.g., R, Python, GitHub, etc.) across CalEPA BDO's, however such efforts often require significant internal willpower to overcome inertia (which sometimes is due to deliberate resistance from management, but often is simply a product of good ol' fashioned government inefficiency).

    August 21, 2025
  • Guest-22960

    I believe if we invested in the development of a user-friendly CDTFA cellphone app for business owners, CDTFA could 1. Gain more transparency with the business owners and 2. Become more efficient the collect sales taxes for the state. For example, my collectors and I meet with the business owners with collection accounts at the field office and during our field calls at the business locations. Almost all the business owners we meet do not use a computer or own one and therefore, rely on their cellphones conduct day-to-day business activities like accepting a payment through Zelle for the sale of a t-shirt or food, business banking, creating a sales invoice to give to a customer, using Square to swipe a credit card payment, etc. Specifically, I would like to see that a user-friendly cellphone app is developed for CDTFA to make it easy for a business owner to register for a seller’s permit, make changes to the account (like an address changes or adding/closing business locations), pay sales taxes/state revenues, eFile tax returns for up to two locations, show past due taxes owed, setting up payment plans, and making a request for tax relief from penalty, interest, etc. I specifically would like this app to cross-register the business owner for other licenses and fee accounts under the authority of CDTFA. The interface of this app should be easily uploaded to CDTFA's database in real time or within 24-hours. Add a chat function using AI to answer basic questions.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22960

    At a broader statewide level, I believe that a universal statewide department cellphone app should be developed that cross-registers all state-required permits and licenses applicable for a business owner to operating in the state of California. The purpose of creating a universal statewide department app is because each department has different information from the business owner. For example, one state department is notified timely that a business has closed or moved to a new location, yet another state department was never notified. the unversal app solves this problem by streamlining the changes for all departments at once. Also, the purpose of developing a universal statewide department cellphone app is to ensure 1. Efficiencies in collecting state revenues yet simplifying the process for a small business owner to register for state permits and licenses specifically related to their business. 2. Creating a universal database for state departments to track data. Then, I would like to see that a QR code is then created for the business owner to use to easily identify themselves and could also be used for all the state permits and licenses held by the business owner. Account changes to any state permit and licenses like an address change could be handled through this app and that the changes are in real time or within 24-hours.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23128

    Homeless. Convert malls to shelters and help people get back on their feet.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23170

    One of the issues that I have noticed is a lack of cohesion in local services versus state services. In understanding both entities are connected with the claimant, we should work better together and have systems that support a unified effort in providing services.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21286

    The primary problem I see is a complicated state government apparatus and body of state laws that do not reflect the true interests of Californians as well as they could. To address this, I would recommend a dual effort to map out / simplify the functions of existing governmment agencies, and to educate Californians about the structure of state government and these functions so that the government and the people will be more in-sync with each other.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23000

    One of the primary challenges I see as a Californian and a state employee is the culture of leadership that often relies on the mindset of ‘I endured it, so others must as well.’ This approach can create stagnation, where old practices remain simply because that is how things have always been done. While this may maintain stability, it can also limit innovation and discourage growth among both workers and leaders. I believe that true leadership development means breaking this cycle and fostering a culture where leaders are empowered to challenge the process, question outdated methods, and seek improvements that benefit both employees and the public we serve. By emphasizing open communication and a willingness to innovate, we can build leaders who not only draw from their experience but also strive to leave systems better than they found them. For me, ‘challenging the process’ is not about disregarding tradition, but about ensuring we continually evolve to meet the needs of Californians with greater efficiency, safety, and accountability.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23418

    A primary issue with the State is that we have geographically locked the vast majority of our jobs to a city that doesn't even rank in the top five of our State's population. Sacramento is less than 2% of the state's population; being able to recruit and retain talented individuals from across the the state would dramatically increase the quality of our workforce. To achieve this, we would need to implement more robust teleworking policies.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22264

    I don't believe it's fair to be charged for parking at a state-owned parking garage. I feel like I'm being charged unnecessarily, just for going to work. Can state garage maintenance be included in the CA budget? That way workers don't have to pay for parking directly. At least the financial burden gets spread amongst all taxpayers.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23127

    I work in one of the many Legal departments. I receive in service of process. When someone sues the department and its employees, it is codified in law that each person/department gets a copy of the document. Just last week, I had an individual name/sue 30 different people. So per law, each one needed a copy of the over 500 page document. We need to change the law so that we can take electronic service. That way instead of piles and piles of paper (where I scan in only one copy) and distribution happens electronically. What a waste of paper and human effort. I have heard the law needs to be changed. Okay, let's change it! This effects every legal department in the state and also anyone else who serves papers on anyone in any law suit.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23491

    Simplify TAX for everyone! Ideally, all taxes should be administered by ONE agency and only ONE phone number for help! Please get rid of all these fancy tax agencies in this state! Many people leave California because they are taxed on things like how much oxygen they consume on a daily basis! Create a NEW agency called California Department of Tax! ONE phone number for taxpayers to call and get answers to anything related to TAXES and FEES. Make it EASIER for businesses and individuals to pay their taxes. ONE PAGE tax returns! This will create efficiency and effectiveness and make the 45 million Californians happy!

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23460

    One of the primary problems I see in California government is that out technical infrastructure is outdated and creates unnecessary barriers for both employees and the public. For example, many departments are still relying on PDF forms for timekeeping management, which slows down the processes, increases errors, and limits accessibility. To solve this I would prioritize modernization of internal systems by moving toward cloud -based, user friendly platforms that allow for real-time updates, digital signatures, and mobile access and also provide adequate training especially for LEADERSHIP! Another major issue is how state departments communicate to the public. Too often, forms, notices, websites, and presentations are written in dense bureaucratic language that can be confusing. I would recommend shifting toward PLAIN LANGUAGE communication across all state agencies, making information clear, simple and accessible to all Californians. Alongside this, we should invest in practical resources like website tutorials, FAQs, or short instructional YouTube videos, that guide people step by step filling out forms and accessing services or joining public online meetings.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23148

    1. Load balancing throughout the state by reevaluating what has changed post-pandemic. Many departments/agencies found more efficient ways to do things using technology. We should share any significant changes that might help others and redirect the extra bandwidth to help folks who saw an increase in workload, even if they work in a different department or agency. 2. Create a surcharge for vehicle registration based on mileage to ensure that people using the roads more than others help maintain them consistent with the wear and tear they put on them.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23460

    One of the primary problems I see in California government is that our technical infrastructure is outdated and creates unnecessary barriers for both employees and the public. For example, many departments are still relying on PDF forms for timecards and approvals, which slows down processes, increases errors, and limits accessibility. To solve this, I would prioritize modernization of internal systems by moving toward cloud-based, user-friendly platforms that allow for real-time updates, digital signatures, and mobile access. This would save staff time, reduce administrative burden, and increase efficiency across departments. Another major issue is how state departments communicate with the public. Too often, forms, notices, and websites are written in dense, bureaucratic language that can be confusing. I would recommend shifting toward plain language communication across all state agencies, making information clear, simple, and accessible to all Californians. Alongside this, we should invest in practical resources like website tutorials, FAQs, or short instructional videos (e.g., YouTube walk-throughs) that guide people step-by-step in accessing services or filling out forms. Additionally, California has many departments that offer overlapping or complementary services—such as the Department of Rehabilitation (DOR) and the Department of Developmental Services (DDS), which often work with the same populations but in different ways. Stronger cross-department collaboration.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-23348

    More support for lean 6 sigma program and implementation of the project solutions. The state is full steps/activities that do not add value.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23262

    Most departments operate in a silo and frequently are doing work that is duplicative and/ or serving the same populations. The CA Climate Initiative came closer to changing this. CA Jobs First is getting at that problem, but may not make it to the finish line when the administration changes. There is no CA tracking mechanism or over arching council that holds that type of knowledge. There is No system in place to make sure that anyone trying to do workforce development knows what each department is doing in that area or if multiple departments are trying to attack affordable housing all departments have a mechanism to get information. All these programs exist but I have found so many state employee have no idea. Then employees approach their departments with a "novel" idea to address these types of examples above and a whole new program is started including money being put toward it, when it exists somewhere else.

    August 20, 2025 👍 3

    I am a collector/investigator, and I often see people who sells at swapmeet thinks they are not required to collect taxes. I think work with local swapmeet organizations maybe beneficial to the vendors and CDTFA can benefit from having so many account that are delinquent.

  • Guest-22956

    Honestly, we really need an updated, super user friendly, accessible Calcareers site. We need an app for it as well. I know we hire a ton of really capable IT folks and developers and I bet experts in that field would have a ton of great ideas on how to make this happen.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23352

    One big problem is the great brain drain. i.e. older employees that have been working the area for years are retiring and from what I have been seeing is that most of the time their supervisory positions are filled after they have retired. And taking the wealth of knowledge with them. There is also a bit of a disconnect between the age groups of the older staff soon to retire and the younger staff coming it. If we could get new hires in place to shadow some of these great wealth of knowledge employees before retiring, that would help. And then some basic training on connecting different generations. I know with is happening in other states already, as I've personally been to those trainings as an employee of another state. And seen them help.

    August 20, 2025
    • Guest-22261

      Many agencies also lack reasonable promotional pathways that allow employees to stay in one location rather than having to "move to move up". So there is no opportunity for these experienced employees to train up a replacement, because there is no entry or journey level position who can learn the ropes. Instead, the person with the wealth of knowledge retires and then their replacement is hired months after they leave and has years of catching up to do with practically no guidance.

      August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23267

    1. Improve the Budget Process to support an efficient and timely implementation of State IT projects. Establish a funding mechanism that allows for project implementation start dates outside the budget year, with the possibility of aligning funding for the next FY.

  • Guest-23352

    Smart spending. Communicating with the boots on the ground about their needs and wants instead of blind fully purchasing.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23443

    I think policies and technologies need to keep up with the times. We are quick to adopt hot political topics and policies, but when it comes to implementing change that can benefit everyone, and likely even save money in the long run, we're not allowed to do it. There's a growing double standard on the needs of the state.

  • Guest-23331

    Caltrans Struggles to hire on new surveyors, especially ones with the specialized experience needed to do things to the Caltrans standard. This problem could be solved in two ways; 1. Short term, allowing people to telework from distances normally not practical to commute from will allow recruitment for office positions that are both hard to fill and do not require an in person role. This also incentivizes private sector surveyors looking to earn their License to join Caltrans for the hard to get office hours. 2. Long term, emphasizing recruitment in high schools and colleges through our JET and TET program will let us get people that would normally go into the private sector apprenticeships and expand the number of people considering surveys at all.

  • Guest-23352

    Smart purchasing. Communicate with the boots on the ground with their needs and wants instead of blind fully purchasing.

  • Guest-23343

    I would eliminate unnecessary boards, commissions, departments, and agencies. California is a big state, yes, but our bureaucracy has grown without boundaries, and we add new organizations every year. Gov. Schwarzenegger tried to streamline government, that effort should be resurrected.

    Changing the ideas from archaic times to current changes in the environment and equipment.
