    Experience, & dedication is the key. Passing the experience, motivating & open mind can continue to transform & upgrade us. I would share my knowledge & experience with my colleagues. And stay open to suggestions.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22760

    Having things centralized online onto the cloud would help tremendously when locating items that need to be found. The current system where items are located in file rooms creates delay in work. A solution would be scanning all documents in and having them in centralized online location.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22733

    There are too many agencies doing the same thing/same type of work. Unfortunately, many of the agencies are led by politicians who needed to be placed somewhere by the governor. We should consolidate agencies to streamline funding and combine expertise.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23076

    Working with limited federal and state funds and maximizing the services we can provide. I suggest making all backend/support/non-customer-facing roles telework first, to reduce expenditures on office rent and utilities.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23233

    Problem: Upward mobility if you are NOT located in a major metropolitan city such as Sacramento and Los Angeles. Those of us in smaller or rural areas have a lack of opportunities available to us to move upwards in positions. Solution: Make certain positions available across the state that are 100% telework and are still able to do the work assigned remotely or provide some sort of incentive to move to Sacramento.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23188

    I am still fairly new, and I came from the private sector, specifically I most recently came from a tech start up environment. The biggest problem I see is that people up and down the ladder aren't sure about questions asked and don't know a lot of institutional knowledge, and from what others have said it's likely to be because of high turnover. My main suggestion would be better benefits, specifically dental options (I think this is usually determined through the unions but figured I would throw it out there). The other thing I would suggest is performance-based raises on top of the structured ones. This gives the harder workers more incentive to stay and work hard and more money which usually improves moral.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23001

    Data suggests that there is a lot of taxpayer dollars that could be saved by fully embracing as much telework as possible, while simultaneously consolidating the amount of physical space and building leases the state takes up. I would love to one day see the state consolidate multiple departments into something like the FTB campus, freeing up space by making telework the default work type for any position that does not require work to be done in office. Embracing telework in this way opens up state jobs to many more Californians all across the state rather than just people that live in a couple metro areas, it would reduce carbon emissions and traffic, as well as lower the cost of road maintenance in those areas, as well as be a huge benefit to the all employees. The FTB campus also has lightrail access, as well as a free parking lot, which would be a cost savings to employees who currently work downtown and have to pay for parking. the money saved from things like this could be used to help departments balance their budget and we would be able to better fund things that actually benefit residents of the state. I know this is an incredibly lofty idea, and would require a lot of planning, but I think it could provide a great deal of savings, and would make jobs with the state more appealing to most people. if we as a state are committed at being the best, we need to embrace forward thinking tactics, not stick to "what we always have done"

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22046

    I come from the private sector, and I believe the problem of efficiency, accountability, and integrity is a primary problem (Culture) needing to be addressed. I'm not a supervisor but it appears that the culture needs to be addressed so that work can be a more enjoyable safe place for all.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22973

    The state hiring process needs more efficiencies

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22792

    Everyone wants state government agencies to work together towards the same ends. Currently, staff within different agencies cannot see each other's calendars, work on shared documents, or use other technology that promotes fast and easy collaboration. A simple fix would be to allow cross agency calendar viewing and shared documents.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22957

    Internally, the State of California and how it operates does not reflect that we are the 4th largest economy in the world. The fact we would ever have a budget deficit defies all logic and basic arithmetic. Since, in the United States, we are considered a "donor state" in reference to our relationship with the federal government, anytime California projects a deficit, we should just reduce our "donation" to the federal government to keep it balanced at the very least, and create a buffer of revenue as a means to be fiscally disciplined. Secondly, we need to take large corporations to task for boasting California as their home, but slighting the State when it comes to taxes and being disciplined environmental stewards. As an example, the oil and utility companies are running rough shod over the State and poisoning our lands and waters. Ultimately, this recouped revenue would go along way to make State worker pay, not only competitive, but superior to the private sector. Sweetheart deals between the State and big business need to be thoroughly scrutinized and contracts rescinded for bad actors.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21785

    I believe the state of California takes as good of care as we should for our Police, Corrections, Fire services, Dispatchers, and EMS personnel. I believe more could be down to provide essential services as it pertains to substance use disorders and PTSD. We demand so much of these individuals and we treat them as a replenishable resource. They are not. If we do not properly care for them and maintain their mental and emotional health, it could destroy recruitment and retention, causing devastating results for the residents of California that rely on these responders for essential emergency services.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21798

    A primary problem I would like to see addressed is vendors who do business w/ the finance side of the state understand the concepts of public fund accounting and how departments operate.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23150

    California’s government could become more effective and efficient by embracing full time telework for positions that can be done effectively remotely. The recently completed audit showed that this would not only increase job satisfaction, and can even result in increased productivity but it would also save the state millions of dollars.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23150

    Contractors, many things that can be done efficiently and effectively by state workers are contracted out, whether this is program development, private law firms, etc. The work being done by these contractors is expensive and often subpar. By developing and utilizing internal talent the State could save money and provide better service for the people of California.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-2749

    I think reducing wet signature requirements, allowing electronic submission of documents (looking at you EDD), and having a statewide intranet would be helpful in streamlining work.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22333

    I'm all about helping our team grow and unlocking everyone's potential. Right now, our department is seriously missing the mark when it comes to developing talent. My boss hasn't really been stepping up to guide and mentor people who could be future leaders. It feels like we're stuck in this old-school management mode that's more about checking boxes than actually developing people. I'm pushing hard for a different approach – one that's all about building real leadership skills. We need to focus on helping people connect, understand emotions, and level up their professional game. Imagine managers who can actually spot what each team member needs to grow and thrive. This isn't just about training; it's about creating a workplace where people feel supported, challenged, and excited to bring their best selves to work. By investing in our people like this, we're not just checking HR boxes – we're building a team that's flexible, motivated, and ready to crush whatever challenges come our way. It's time we stop managing and start truly leading.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-2749

    I think CEAs should have to take leadership/mentorship classes to make sure they know how to lead and manage respectfully.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22730

    the RTO is just all about politics and not the quality of life for state employees. State employees are productive workers remotely. Remote work allows for flexibility, and quality of life. No, we are not spending all day doing household chores but we are spending the time that we would otherwise be commuting doing our family duties. Commuting is a waste of our time. Packing breakfast and lunch every day is a waste of our time. Picking clothes to wear to work is a waste of time. When the work day ends, we want to be home with the family not on the road. Commuting is congestion, pollution and stressful to our health. The building developers can figure out how to fill their vacancies on their own. The restaurant owners can figure out how to serve more customers in the private industry. These cannot be reasons end remote work for state workers. As a remote worker, I have time to wake up and exercise, drink my tea, coffee or smoothie in peace and not in the car driving and make a healthy breakfast to get my day started. It allows personal time in the morning that cannot be enjoyed while commuting. For the state employees who take advantage of teleworking, they need to be held accountable for their work by their supervisors. Don't take away telework for everyone. For those who meet with the public, they don't get to telework. It's that simple. My building has been vandalized multiple time s and we had to go home because of no water. No reimbursement for missed

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23219

    I am often troubled by the lack of public support for government employees- ie- when budget concerns are made, it seems like the public immediately blames too high salaries or pensions or benefits for employees as the reason. I would love to see something- maybe a series on social media? where the importance of government employees and what they accomplish is made clear- especially why it is fair to pay government employees a fair and living wage.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22931

    I think people's perception of the prison system and what actually goes on inside the walls is dramatically different. As a department we communicate the different ways we offer rehabilitative opportunities as well as the incidents that occur, but how do we communicate this to the public? I don't know the solution but believe it's crucial for the public to be aware of all of it.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23074

    Medicaid provides health insurance to low-income populations in the U.S. Because private health insurance premiums have become so expensive, many people on Medicaid would be uninsured if not covered by Medicaid, while others could pay for private insurance only by cutting back on other necessities that indirectly affect health. Extensive research has shown that cutting Medicaid coverage leads to worse health and increased risk of premature mortality. As a possible solution, the State of California could implement corresponding state-level tax increases to partially backfill federal safety net cuts. Since some of these cuts won’t take effect for a couple of years, there is the possibility that the control of the House and Senate could change in 2026, and then legislation could be passed to undue some of the federal cuts to Medicaid. Time will tell whether these cuts will have the devastating impact they are intended to have on people’s health.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23028

    There is bias for external stakeholders. It seems certain entities/external stakeholders are given preference and better treatment. We need to level the playing field. We have oversight entities that constantly call out bad actors and yet, our departments continue to fund them because leadership chooses to. This is inappropriate and undermines the work of state employees who do oversight and hold those accountable.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23079

    when staff is expected to complete a job or task. be sure they have the equipment or tools necessary to get the work done effectively. if they don't have time to do it properly the first time, when will they be able to do it again? we need to be sure that they are ABLE to get their work done correctly the first time, by giving them the correct tools and training to get their jobs done right!

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23028

    The telework audit showed that $225 million could be saved at the current two day in office schedule. If we were full telework, we could probably save even more. That money isn't chump change and could be used to better fund our infrastructure. Isn't the purpose of being efficient also being fiscally responsible to avoid deficits?

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21964

    For my current department I think that a primary problem is some people are promoted without meeting MQ's while others are told they can't promote due to not meeting MQ's despite experience and relevant education. I would suggest making the field fair, either follow the MQ's or don't but it should not be unfair. Also why do we retain and promote non-productive people, a supervisor in my office has been the worst at every position she has held however due to favoritism/ nepotism she keeps promoting and keeps being a really terrible employee/supervisor. She is also friends or relative to at least 3 current employees and one who recently separated. How is this nepotism still happening?

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23171

    I find that as a 10 year employee, why is it so complicated to get something repaired and something ordered. All the forms needed are ridiculous. Also, a lot more goes into our jobs and we are definitely underpaid. I see what vendors are charging the State to repair stuff and if the State can pay those extremes amounts out than we ALL deserve a higher pay for what we do and put up with.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23210

    We waste a lot of money on mental health with 1:1 observation status. Currently when an i/p cites that he/she is suicidal we send them to an alternative housing unit temporarily for up to 24 hours before placing them or transferring them to a MHCB or back to the yard. This requires multiple nursing staff to watch and round for this task. INSTEAD: pull them out, strip down their cell and place a CO on them for the observation. 1. a CO is a cheaper overtime than nursing. 2. this would cut down on the secondary gain from the observation and the more behavioral i/p will think more about what they are doing before eliciting a CIT. This way we can pay more attention to the truly SI/SA i/p.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23157

    I feel there should be less "red tape" to cut to solve issues. Permits take time to be ratified because of lawyers and special interest groups

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23157

    I feel the largest hurdle we have to address is excess bureaucracy.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22987

    We lack uniformity in knowledge and training. We need more of the philosophy of "when you learn, teach". Open communication, current and updated training, updates to critical work equipment are essential.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23026

    There is a disconnect between those in authority (i.e. Sacramento) and those who have the responsibility to enact the policy "on the ground." this has been my experience both as a public school teacher and as a psychologist with Department of State Hospital. Sacramento is top heavy with staff (at least in DSH) and they have little exposure or spend limited time in the hospitals with the patients. Therefore, they do not appreciate what the staff limitations are. We are short-staffed on the floor and struggle with budget restrictions, while many in SAC work from home and create policy that are not practical in their implementation.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23165

    Full time telework for all state workers. If any agency or department wishes to deny telework, it should be supported by verifiable data. If there is a legitimate need to report in office, the reasons should be specific to the state worker and their department, division, branch, unit, etc.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23067

    Accounting/Trust: Gobs of inefficiencies with a chain of command that doesn't know the details when I bring up better ideas. HQ does NOT understand what we do in office at institutions and policies contradict each other. We waste thousands of sheets of paper per month on statements when they could go out on the tablets. We spin our wheels answering GA22s for balance inquiries for hours a day when again, make it available on the tablets. We print monthly court checks for mandatory payments from IP accounts, however each court seems to be entered several times with a slightly different title (ie: Fresno US District vs. Fresno USD) and so multiple checks print out to the same exact place. This is wasteful and time consuming. Correct the duplicate entries so that one check prints per court. Also, SOMS pay sheets mostly print ONE IP per page. This is so wasteful and makes for lots more signing and takes up more room in storage. When asked about at HQ, no one knows how it works so it cannot be fixed. Someone has to know.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23026

    Shorted the distance between those in "Authority" and those with the "responsibility" of enacting the policies created. Create programs as needed by those with responsibility rather than programs that meet the needs of those in authority. There is an inevitable Us v. Them- but work to acknowledge and address that, rather than pretend that it doesn't exist.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23026

    A glaring example is the fact that the DSH does not have an electronic health record. In this century, it is inexcusable that a system this size does not have a modern way to document and share medical information. Yet, this process has been in the hands of SAC for over 10 years, with no progress.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21766

    For improved state government efficiency, I’d suggest waiting until the AI hallucination rate decreases before implementing AI into state work. As an example, Microsoft Copilot has a hallucination rate of 27% and some specialized technical models have up to 60-80%. While policy requires double checking AI output, it is incredibly inefficient and embarrassing to have to retroactively correct published AI-generated hallucinations. I have used Copilot for non-work purposes, but these inaccuracies make me extremely hesitant to use it for my work, which involves data analysis and coding. Why should we as state workers trust AI output when there’s a proven record of hallucinations?

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-23190

    It sounds basic, but staffing is vital to do the job. Personnel is policy. Without adequate clerical staff, our unit is paying Engineers or Scientists to do data entry.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-23223

    I am a problem solver. I would focus on what is working and what needs to be grown positively.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22496

    The primary problem that I see are executives overloading lower management and analysts with projects and unrealistic goals. I also feel like we as State Workers are not appreciated by our own government and we are used as the patsies when things go wrong. We end up being the ones to suffer when the government fails to meet their goals. Billions are being diverted for other programs yet State Workers get their pay threatened constantly by going over budget. We need to be appreciated by our executives and our own governmental leaders. These are the issues I hear amongst coworkers.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22792

    The contracting process is broken. Californians want government to have easy to use online tools, one-stop-shop permits, data access portals, and to use the latest technology to make people's lives better. Both IT and non-IT contracts are cumbersome and require navigating gate keepers who do not understand agency needs. If a project is deemed IT by contracting personnel, my agency will simply not move forward. If a contract gets flagged for the PAL process with CDT, the contract is dead, and we will never resubmit. Money lasts 3 years, and the PAL process can take that long, leaving no time for the money to be used on the intended project. Further, getting non-IT contracts through DGS is time-consuming and cumbersome with little added value, decreasing the time for the money to actually do work. My suggestion would be to loosen up contract oversight on the frontend, actually provide training to staff, and provide more resources for auditing and enforcement of contract rules and ethics on the backend. In this way, SME staff will be trained on contract creation and execution (which is currently not the case) and can quickly get money out in flexible ways, taking advantage of as much of the 3 year money lifetime as possible, while knowing that they must strictly adhere to contract rules or their money will get pulled back and contracts will get cancelled. If you want staff to move fast, and use technology, this has to change.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22959

    I think there is problem in understanding the relationships between taxes and the public services they fund. I feel the state should work on a marketing campaign to help Californians better understand this relationship and the value we all receive from the public programs in the state.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22780

    Better management evaluations and hiring. I would make a way for staff to evaluate them, if too many negative evaluations, they are not put into place.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22994

    Low morale is the biggest killer of efficiency. Low morale is caused by at least two things: 1) not listening or respecting state workers; 2) bad management/unfair division of labor. When policies are implemented that do not effectively consider the potential impacts on the lives of civil servants, such as RTO--state workers will lose all drive to do good work. Why work hard for a system that does not care about you and won't listen to you? Poor leadership has the same effect. Managers can get stuck in their ways and refuse to rethink processes or challenge other policies/processes that are clearly inefficient--even when their employees are expressing the need for change. When employees are able to live and work comfortably (by being paid a fair wage, have a good work-life balance, be able to work in safe environments, like their homes, free from distracting co-workers and racist/sexist/inappropriate conduct), and when they see their management step up and be good leaders (by listening, pivoting when needed), that is what will improve efficiency!

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22999

    I believe there should be a universal intranet site with agency-specific and subject-specific subsites. The main site could include spaces for disseminating information (e.g. statewide policy), posting training and job opportunities, hosting webinars, sharing news on projects and subjects of statewide interest, and collaborating/idea sharing. This site is a good start but access to a statewide community should be automatic, not opt-in. I also believe the state would benefit greatly from further standardization of web/software design and a collaborative community of engineers. (e.g. having templates for commonly needed software, like procurement, inventory, appointment scheduling, marketing/newsletter, learning management, certification tracking, mobile apps, etc.) Agencies could then customize templates to fit their unique needs. This could make software more accessible and affordable, encourage development of the state IT workforce, reduce reliance on private sector contracts, and create a more universal user experience, reducing the need for training when someone changes agencies. Lastly, I believe data required/used by multiple agencies should centralized to reduce errors/discrepancies and time spent on data entry. A first step would be auditing existing databases to identify what data is being collected by multiple agencies currently.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22912

    Onboarding and succession planning. When joining a new team or department, there have been instances where I was thrown into the job with little training or onboarding materials. I've also noticed that when some folks leave, there is not much planning or knowledge sharing happening. This results in taking many steps backwards on progress previously made. I would also address flexibility in the workplace. Many polices are created for an entire department, but they do not fit all the working conditions of the individual branches and units. I would allow for more flexibility within each branch/unit.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21933

    The unnecessary spending. For example, in my line of work, we send people down south for training when the following month we do the same training her in Northern CA. I am all for training but when we are spending $20,000 to send four people down south, I feel like it is unnecessary money, especially when at times these are for new hires. Also, in my department, we have sent the same people year after year to the same training when the employee has come back and said that they didn't learn much more than the year before. Another example is the amount of time wasted on doing the same procedure multiple times, just different ways.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22934

    Use modern and efficient software. All of our software is outdated and convoluted and causes a profound waste of time, both for employees and the public. Refine the way we contract software development or purchase software.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22824

    Teleworking, or lack thereof. Look, I understand not all jobs can function properly through telework but for those that are more than capable to telework, it needs to be a full 5 days a week thing. If you want to go into the office, by all means, but it should be decided by the employee and the quality of work they provide. Full telework is the closest we can get to what I think is the perfect work/life balance - 8 hrs sleep, 8 hrs work, 8 hrs your time. Instead, my current schedule is train station at 615, in Sac at 705, at work at 735, back to my car at 530. That's over 11 hrs dedicated for my job. In what world is that balanced?

    August 20, 2025 👍 6
  • Guest-22994

    The primary problem I have seen is low morale. This has been caused by at least 2 different things: 1) RTO policies and refusing to listen to state employees; 2) poor management and bad leadership that results in inefficient or unfair division of labor. Leadership, starting at the very top, needs to respect and listen to state workers. When policies, like RTO, are implemented without any real consideration of how it will upend the lives of civil servants--that is disrespectful and will always lead to low morale. I have also seen morale be impacted by poor leadership and bad supervisors (i.e. ones that do not listen to their employees and refuse to pivot when needed, or just flat out assign work unfairly). Once morale is low, people will begin doing the "bare minimum." They will take very little pride in their work. They will not care about outcomes. They will be so stressed and they will lose their focus at work. This is what leads to inefficiency. When you have workers who are able to pay their bills comfortably (because they are receiving a fair wage), when they see management take on the hard tasks (because management is making more money and should be taking on the more challenging projects), when they are allowed to work in places of safety and comfort (such as their own homes where they are mostly shielded from inappropriate conduct such as workplace violence, racist or sexist comments, physical-micro-management, etc.), that is what will improve efficiency for all!!

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22170

    If the goals for the Engaged California project include to find challenges that undermine our ability to work efficiently and effectively, and to discover ways to better serve the people in our state, it doesn't make sense to take virtual work (or RTO) off the table for discussion. Additionally, this is supposed to be a deliberative democracy forum, as such reasoned positions are valid and should not be limited. Overall, as a state employee that is in a hybrid position working both in person and virtually at times, it is important to validate the values that virtual work affords state employees, state agencies, people in our state, and the state budget. It is also important to see how virtual work solves for problems in inefficiency and ineffectiveness, and can help to solve for ways to better serve the people in our state. A primary problem right now is the position that virtual work shouldn't be part of the Engaged California discussion to find solutions to problems (because it is supposedly doesn't "stay on topic" - see Engaged California's "Sharing Guidelines" ). It makes no sense to talk about workplace efficiency and effectiveness, and to talk about better ways to serve the people in our state without considering talent management, workplaces, work mediums/channels, and work spaces. A set proposed solutions include opening this forum to the topics of virtual work, talent and workplace management, and work conditions.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21964

    As a state employee with the LCO I have seen a lot of people promote to higher positions when they were not doing their previous position well. While others are not being promoted despite working hard and caring about what we do. Because of this I have seen us lose a lot of great workers and will continue to, as I am working on leaving the LCO as well. I have been here over 10 years with only a promotion from OT to MST. If we retained and promoted quality workers and not terrible workers, we would be far better off. I personally feel that the entire wage training team has been a waste of time and money because they do not appear to be effective or productive.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22826

    The most prevalent issue that I have witnessed since becoming employed by the state is how poorly employees with disabilities are treated during the reasonable accommodation process. From my experience as an employee and union steward, it seems that disabled employees are treated like a burden during the reasonable accommodation process, especially if they are requesting telework. In most cases when the employee's doctor is recommending telework as a reasonable accommodation, departments will do anything to delay or deny telework as a reasonable accommodation. Departments have continually been allowed to ignore and violate the ADA, EEOC, and FEHA policies and regulations and oppose doctor's recommendations for appropriate reasonable accommodations. As a whole, the state needs to hold departments accountable for following appropriate policies and regulations pertaining to reasonable accommodations. More timely processes and procedures should be implemented. It is unreasonable to allow each department's EEO unit/section 90 days to investigate themselves, while not providing an employee with a disability their recommended reasonable accommodations. A more consistent and streamlined approach needs to be developed to help support California's employees with disabilities to better provide them more equitable and inclusive work environment when working for the state.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22878

    My major issues are recruitment and retention. I came up with a well thought out plan to allow teleworking for specific classifications and after hiring I have had zero turnover rate in that specific classification.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22865

    A modern HR System that allows employees and managers to document and interact with their progress at the state. A lot of departments are still filling out Excel sheets or pdfs via Docusign. My performance reviews and other personal documents are either in a filling cabinet or on a shared drive somewhere within the State.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22692

    We're still using some antiquated systems and paper processes (for example, a paper timesheet required after month end). I would like to see a statewide initiative to modernize technology

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22951

    Orgnaizational Structure. There seems to be overlapping of leadership in layers, with multiple director level positions in addition to the main Director of an organization. In some cases, program directors oversee division chiefs while also maintaining separate subordinate staff. This may lead to duplication of responsibilities, unclear lines of accountability, and inefficiencies. That increases admin cots, slow/hold up decision making, and create confusion about roles and responsibilities as well as drawing resources away from direct program support. Recommend consolidating director level positions. Redefine the scope of program directors and focus on strategic guidance rather than duplicating operational oversight. Basically, simplifying the leadership structure to operate more efficiently, reduce overhead, and strengthen the ability to focus on its mission.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22252

    Business process digitization, transformation, and automation. Top-down and bottom-up open communication, support, and willingness to change and adopt to using technology.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21569

    Outside of pay. I would say the biggest problem is policies being made by people who have not done the position or did it when it was first introduced so they do not understand why certain policies do not work. Those making our policy and procedures should be do on-the-job training in the positions that they are making rules for every 2-3 years. This will help them with having actual hands-on and relevant experience to know why certain procedures cause unnecessary and additional work, that is to no benefit of our consumers and participants that need assistance. I would also say the lack of training when coming into a role.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22843

    I never understood why every agency buys the same product(s) at a different contract rate when DGS handles the Statewide contracts that do not have dollar limits for their use and can purchase at scale for the whole state? I realize there is always a cost concern. From the numerous Department I have worked at throughout my career, I found many agencies were able to save money by not renewing their own Agency contracts and leveraging statewide contracts at scale . I think there is something to be said about the state as a whole consolidating services especially when most are moving to cloud/SaaS . It blows me away that each agency has their own contracts with the same vendors for the same service and one might pay 1 million a year and the other 200K? Microsoft? Nessus? Cisco? Apple? HP? DELL? RSA? Splunk? Just a couple of examples….

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22527

    In my career as public employee I have worked for different state departments. I have noticed there is a huge difference in the type of software and hardware that is being provided. In some departments I have had to purchase my own ergonomic equipment due to budget issues. As part of title 8 make changes to the ergonomic program where departments are required to have a separate fund for office, and ergonomic equipment. Including software programs that can assist employees improve completing their essential duties. Increase awareness in workplace bulling and harassment. Make it a mandatory yearly training and provide more resources to employees to encourage reporting.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22936

    This is not an issue for me, but State Workers should not have to pay for parking. State Workers should have a pay dedicated to paying these fees. It makes it very difficult to for them to get to their job's sites. There should be dedicated and safe parking for them.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22783

    State Parks should use Cash machines at park entrances, which should accept $20 bills and provide change (i.e. $5 for $15 parking fee)

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22472

    I think technology and our internal workflows need a major overhaul. I started in January 2025 and was utterly shocked about how far behind we were compared to the rest of the country with business operations. I think our offices could greatly benefit from project management software and/or real customization of our software to makes us more efficient. Our office can't take any other payment other than checks, which sounds crazy to most of the public who walk into the office. I see a lot of filing and literal paperwork happening in our office and we waste so much time "looking for files," which I feel is a waste of taxpayer dollars when we should be able to find what we need and do our jobs. And while it is great that we have a lot of long-time tenured employees in our ranks, we also need some major training overhauls once these updates are implemented. I also think training when I started was haphazard at best. No one explained and has not since explained how to properly navigate our manuals, or how to navigate the legal language used. This also deeply surprised me as someone new coming into the state. My expectations was that their training would be top notch. Myself and it sounds like many before me had to teach themselves many topics. Our offices could benefit from streamlined training programs when folks start their positions. Not just about how to be a supervisor, the only training I got, but how to do the actual job and workflows, manuals, concepts, etc.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22301

    For HQ departments that process paperwork for all the districts, there should be more training offered to the districts on how the process/procedures works. The policies keep changing too quickly, so the districts who had people trained a long time ago are working off old information and passing that down to newer employees. It could be online sessions or onsite training, but somehow the districts need a better way to be updated.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22268

    Purchasing. We are required to purchase certain items from CAL PIA. Many items are much more cost effective to purchase from a local retailer even after the potential savings from purchasing from another state agency. Also we are restricted on whats available. Allowing a credit card purchase at a local retailer with supervisory oversite could potentially save a large amount of money, allow for better products and faster aquisitions.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
    • Guest-26498

      And to add insult to injury, CalPIA seems to be constantly "expanding" their footprint, not by developing new internal competencies, but by leasing out inmates to other companies.

      September 30, 2025
  • Guest-22268

    Eliminate purchasing requirements that cost the state more money. Many vendors know the thresholds and come in on a low bit and then quickly up the cost to the max before crossing the threshold. This increases the overall cost and ultimately costs much more than necessary. Eliminate small business requirements to allow a competitive rate.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22320

    I have found the State's technology is severely lacking in many areas. Simple things like completing timecards, requesting leave and determining how many hours of leave you have accrued require the employee to access three different databases, and even these databases do not reflect real-time, useful information. I have worked as a County employee in CA, and even the County had real time leave data that could be accessed easily by the employee. In my current employment, I have to track my time, by five minute increments, in one database, I have to add this information to my Outlook calendar, and I have to note it on my timecard. I am hoping there is way all of these databases can be integrated so that we are not spending so much time across multiple platforms to track our time working for the State. I also feel that during the mapping process with the Organizational Improvement Office, OIO, best practices should be shared with all department employees as they are working through their mapping and not at the very end once they have mapped all of the processes for the entire division. This would allow for immediate changes and streamlining to occur, instead of waiting for two years or more for the mapping to be completed before the results are released to the department. Change and innovate as we go, share the knowledge and best practices as soon as it is identified so employees can save valuable time and State money.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22598

    The S90 or Rejection of Probation code needs to be modified so that it reflects two possibilities: an actual rejection and an employee exercising their mandatory right to return to their former agency. During the time of probation the supervisor/manager is evaluating the employee, but the employee should be doing the same thing and determining whether the agency is a good fit or not for them or not. The employee should be able to switch without any negative impact if the agency is not a good fit. At present, the code S90 is seen as negative, but there needs to be a way to distinguish what actually happened. It should be noted that the order and decision are not always in the Official Personnel File (OPF) and are based on the agreement or settlement between the parties involved.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22355

    Hiring. Why is it that I can look at a list of interview candidates, and guess with good accuracy who will be offered the position- before the interviews occur? Why is the new contractor for equipment maintenance coincidentally the husband of the supervisor?

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21335

    I am a teacher and the process of attendance takes a long time, involves myself, as well as education officers and housing officers. It seems to me that it would be so much more streamlined if we could somehow use the barcode on each IPs ID. As IPs came through a building or left a building, they would scan their ID and their whereabouts would be uploaded directly to SOMS. This would eliminate the need for so many phone calls and such. Seems like a no brainer to me. Of course, their IDs would probably need to be changed to a bracelet or anklet of some kind so as not to be lost or used by a different IP.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21335

    I've been a teacher with CDCR since 2016. I never in my lift have seen so many "hands" being involved in the easiest of processes. For instance, to get student prior transcripts sent to us, I have student fill out transcript request, hand that form over to our OT, the OT then emails/mails the form to the said school, the school sends transcripts back. If student is a graduate, I fill out another form to request their removal from education, DocuSign it to my supervisor, and the supervisor then requests the drop from the institution's assignments officer. Attendance works similarly. Within 15 minutes of class starting, I fill out a form showing absent students and email it to our education officer. Education officer spends his time calling each IPs housing officer, if they can be reached, to verify if IP is coming to school or not, then the education officer notes "A" time, "S" time, etc ... and returns to me to fill out SOMS attendance. I feel there is so much wasted energy and time that could be used elsewhere to make our programs run more smoothly and with less time to process things.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21904

    I recall that our Department gets people asking for help that we can't really provide. We once gathered lists of resources (food, homeless shelters, advocates, etc.) that all of us could send to those requests. Maybe other departments could try that? I have a more specific suggestion, but it is very small-scale. I remember that some libraries have games or puzzles to play with; maybe it would boost public morale if some free TTRPGs were added to those collections?

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22449

    The websites are hard to navigate for the public. Try to make a user friendly website or have a calling center so that the public can contact someone that can direct them to the appropriate department.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22712

    As a seasoned CA employee, I see waste/fraud in the use of licensed BU 9 classifications. I would recommend an audit of all BU 9 duty statements to determine if a license is actually required to do the job. I say this because I have seen the state increase the use of "licensed" BU 9 staff when a license is not necessary to complete the work. DGS' Division of the State Architect, the Building Standards Commission, and the Office of Sustainability, as well as CalTrans previously hired non-licensed employees to complete the work that does not require a license. However, in the past decade these Departments/Divisions/Commissions have stopped using classifications that do require a license. The problem is that the work required by these entities can be completed by a combination of licensed and unlicensed staff, but by hiring only licensed staff, the state is wasting hundreds-of millions of dollars. A licensed architect/engineer gets paid (after benefits) $250k/year or more. An unlicensed person doing the work that doesn't require a license gets paid $150k/year. Multiply that $100k/year savings by the thousands of staff at DGS and CalTrans that are paid for their license but don't perform license-level work. By hiring unlicensed staff to do the unlicensed work, there is also an opportunity for the state to recruit and maintain staff. But STOP paying licensed BU 9 staff to do work that non-licensed staff can do. It will save the state at least 1/2 a billion dollars.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21947

    Procurement process. Sometimes needed items for efficient work can take 5-8 weeks if you are lucky to get even if you are out or plan ahead. What about the items that you cannot plan ahead for? There is no way to get things quickly, even paper, pens, cleaning supplies, and other needs. This often results in people paying out of pocket for supplies. There should be a way to get needed supplies when they are needed. The procurement process is redundant, too many unnecessary steps to aquire easy things.

    August 20, 2025 👍 2
  • Guest-22506

    Across two state agencies, poor communication has consistently hindered productivity and morale. In one agency, management prohibited cross-unit dialogue, slowing progress and isolating teams. In another, annual surveys repeatedly flagged communication as the top issue, yet leadership insisted on injecting themselves in every conversation or discouraging communication with other units and divisions. Instead of seeking answers from the sources, managerial direction has focused on the interpretation of resources that often prove incorrect. These decisions negatively impact morale, cause frustrations in a job that would otherwise feel ideal, and reminds of the axom that people don't quit jobs, they quit managers. Managers often lack subject matter expertise, causing delays and confusion. Many openly promote a “fake it till you make it” mindset, prioritizing their own job security over the work required to excel when serving the public. The solution is simple: empower staff to communicate directly and collaborate freely, hire a strong human resources team to handle all of our regular issues, and reduce the layers of management. We can build a more agile, informed, and customer-focused workforce.

    August 20, 2025 👍 2
  • Guest-22613

    The amount of taxpayer money spent on state office space is staggering, especially considering most work can now be completed remotely. Even without considering working conditions or what's best for the state employee (although I think telework supports those, too), utilizing telework dramatically reduces overhead costs with no negative impact to work product. Many departments reported improved efficiency during the pandemic with 100% (or near 100%) telework. Telework also improves recruiting, as departments aren't limited to candidates local to Sacramento and can effectively and efficiently hire from across the state. With finances growing tighter and tighter every year, telework is an easy way to address budget shortfalls in a way that unions and employees will also support (as opposed to layoffs and furloughs). I could talk about management inefficiencies, obtuse procedures, and other issues all day long, but this inefficient use of taxpayer dollars is the problem which has the most obvious and straightforward solution that will have the most significant immediate impact.

    August 20, 2025 👍 3
  • Guest-22328

    I have seen many issues such as management not having good leadership skills, or knowledge of the duties their employees should be doing, along with bad organization and communication. All managers should be trained and efficient and regularly evaluated on this matters as it can really have a negative affect on staff.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
    • Guest-22261

      Lack of clear communication regarding roles and expectations is a huge problem in my workplace, too, and the problem comes from the top for sure.

      August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22353

    Primary problem that I'd like to address is how to work around the forte of employees rather than their weaknesses. Employees need internal trainings to sharpen the skills they already have to make it more effective and strong. Roles should be given on the basis of their expertise and the bandwidth depending on their job duties. Mark their personal and professional achievements using different platforms.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21988

    Rebuilding our local and statewide economies. - Strengthening our education system - Creation of new products, markets, businesses - Creation of newer good-paying jobs and careers - Lowering taxation across the board - Creation of educational financial aid programs to help people who financially struggle to make it possible for them to achieve a good-paying career.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22272

    As a state employee, that is focused on cyber security related issues, the primary problem I would like to address is the state not properly addressing Data Sovereignty. As it stands a majority of the data collected, generated and used by departments is physically hosted and stored with third-party companies. Companies who have issues of their own with keeping themselves out of their customers data. Although we have contracts governing how they handle this data contracts alone are not enough to protect citizens from the fallout should these companies' data centers become compromised. The sole responsibility for governing and protecting California citizens data rests with the state and its civil service. My solution to this problem would be for the California Department of Technology restart it's program to create a state cloud. The technology to do so has come a long way since it's last attempt and there is opportunity to recruit recently laid off tech workers into the civil service to support such a project as well as opportunities for more smaller local technology companies to engage in contract work to support the open-source software that will underpin it all. This would ensure that California data is protected and guarded by Californian's as well as California leading the way in Data and Technology governance.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21575

    Too much and sudden involvement of AI/Gen-AI in state; and this is taking away our old school lifestyle. Nothing seems real or genuine. Soln: put a pose on too many incoming AI/Gen-AI requests. These requests are overcrowding our tradition workspace and workload.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21575

    PACe should be make free to all State Staff, to support and enhance our career growth within state ecosystem.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22272

    Another problem I would like to address is the lack of knowledge sharing between technology teams. There is a bill AB869 in the Senate right now that looks to make each state agency implement a Zero Trust Architecture by 2030. I think this is a worthwhile endeavor and as part of this mandate funding should be made to have technology teams be trained in Infrastructure as Code and DevSecOps. Once technologists within the state are trained in these tools technology teams will not only have a more reliable and secure environment but also the ability to share how they built those systems via Git. Technology teams in different departments can fork/download the code that was used to build other department's systems and use it themselves, make/commit improvements, iterate to create other systems, etc. This can foster a more collaborative environment for IT professionals within the state service.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22516

    OK, I don't want to throw shade on other departments, but why does it take the Department of Toxic Substance Control 6 months to 2 YEARS to review and respond to reports that other environmental agencies respond to in a month? They have generally larger and more complicated sites, but many of their cases would have been closed years ago if assigned to other agencies. Sometimes it feels analogous to assigning a Homicide Detective to investigate a traffic ticket. Perhaps reducing the number of reviews and "draft Final Final" report reviews is in order? They do great work, but quite a bit of it seems like overkill.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22516

    PROBLEM: I work at the North Coast Regional Water Quality Control Board in the Cleanups Unit. We transitioned to a paperless file system back in 2006 when the State began requiring reports/data to be submitted and uploaded to the Geotracker database. There are still 100s of existing folders, reports, etc. (one estimate was that our unit's file room contains 1/3 of a mile of paper). Environmental pollution cases are unique: we're required to keep records of all sites, closed or not, in case land use changes in the future (also, it's the law). We routinely have PRA/FOIA requests to pull files for consultant review, and more than 75% of the time they'll ask us to scan and email them. Our region is also vast- some of these consultants and landowners live 6 hours away from out office, and some have physical handicaps that limit their mobility, so we try to oblige. But actually taking apart a file, scanning it, categorizing it, and uploading it is extremely labor intensive. A small file will take a few hours, but even a medium sized one requires one or two days (the scanning is rapid, uploading and naming is laborious). SOLUTIONS: 1) hire dedicated admin staff to do this work, 2) subcontract it out to a bonded document scanning service. BENEFITS: 1) Reduced labor costs for professional and admin staff 2) improved public access for these records 3) potential to upload to AI for detailed summation and analysis. Thank you,

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21615

    We need to get Californians to trust us again. We need to invest in more opportunities to listen to them and their ideas. We have to stop favoring one side and really create solutions that meet all people in the middle.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22502

    As a new state employee, one thing I have noticed is the lack of trust between leadership and analyst level staff. There are so many steps to complete the most minute tasks, and those tasks seem to be complicated because leadership makes it complicated. The strict hierarchical approach - at lease from my experience - has just created fear and insecurity about performing daily work. There isn't an open forum to freely share ideas or talk about new ways of doing things. We are relegated to a desk manual that is incomplete and doesn't actually allow anyone to think creatively. I have been assured that every department is not like this, but I have also been told that I work in one of the best departments - so does that mean it's worse out there?? I would LOVE to see more flexibility, mentoring from leaders, allowing for mistakes, and basic access to leaders so we can ask how they got to where they are in the organization. We look to our leaders for support and guidance. When they only effort they make is to remind us about our timecards, that's not actually leadership.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22656

    As someone who has been with the state for a few years, it appears that there is a culture where people feel its acceptable to neglect their obligations and duties without any real accountability. Most times the work is shifted to individuals who are actually doing the work and there is no real disciplinary action for those who don't. This attitude not only undermines productivity but also fosters a frustrating environment for individuals who are committed to their work. This disconnect can result in lack of motivation among employees who strive to uphold high standards. I believe we can do better in promoting accountability and recognizing the contributions of those who genuinely contribute to the team's success in order to have a more positive work environment. Employees should, in my opinion, have more ways to voice their opinions without fear of retalition, and they should be fairly offered opportunites for advancement based on their performance. Creating an environment that encourages open communication and rewards good work can make employees feel more motivated and involved.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22526

    Our agency had a robust telework policy before the pandemic for professional staff. We invested in more technology to make it even more efficient and expanded telework to support staff duirng covid. We formerly spent significant taxpayer money traveling to conduct events in person, despite a regulation authorizing virtual events. Since we've invested in the technology and nearly all events are virtual, we can reduce office space thus saving significant funds. Converting the positions to telework permanently can allow for strategic planning. For example, we can invest in training and productivity tools to ensure taxpayers receive the best quality service while offsetting the unnecessary expense of partially unused offices and excessive travel.

    August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22747

    It's not a simple problem to fix, but I think it cuts across many Departmental efforts which would save money and lives. I think we need better Public Transportation. The desired outcome: less traffic and congestion, fewer auto accidents, and reduced emissions. I know for example it would save quite a bit money and Staff hours in DHCS, a seemingly unrelated Department, if we can reduce the number of auto accidents occurring in CA. It's also known that the majority of DHCS funding goes to paying for medical services, so any opportunity to reduce serious injury will also provide state savings. Beyond that, better public transit can reduce costs of living for Californians, promote better public health outcomes, aid CalEPA's mission, and hopefully in the long term also make Caltrans' job easier, with hopefully less travel infrastructure needing maintenance.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22614

    The primary problem is excessive costs the State is paying for work from vendors and contractors that could be performed by full time state employees. Additional, state buildings should be state owned properties that are regularly maintained and updated instead of using third party real estate owners. The state can save tax payer dollars, be a good a steward of public funds, conserve and preserve our environment, and decrease traffic congestion, by implementing policies that are modern and utilize technology such as remote and hybrid work. The State used to maintain a website showing all the cost saving effects of telework.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-22123

    Why California has to always be the FIRST, BEST or Waymaker. So many other States are ahead of us in efficiencies, but we can't seem to accept that and leverage their efforts to build our own. That wastes taxpayer dollars and state employees time and motivation.

    August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21794

    Fire the people responsible for this wasteful site. All that will be accomplished by this site is more waste. Employees will be typing pointless things in here instead of doing the job they were hired to do. Over 25 years of state service tells me this site will accomplish nothing, it is a feel good idea that is doomed to fail. People will just get in here and rant (just like this), they will propose ideas that won't be scalable or usable, and some that are counter productive. Wanna make the government more efficient, make it easier to fire the dead weight.

  • Guest-22599

    Bureaucracy. Also the amount of time it takes to hire someone.

    Health expense account reimbursement is inefficient and should be moved away from a third-party administrator (ASI Flex) and just have the account transactions be directed by the employee themselves, such as with an FSA.     Fragmented implementation, outdated solutions, and missed opportunities for system-wide transformation. Existing systems are no longer effective.
