  • Guest-22618

    There are a lot of duplication within the State. Too many people are serving in the same capacity to do the same job. I also feel that it takes too long to onboard someone into State service. There are many people who want to serve the State but cannot wait 6 months to a year in order to get a job. I feel that once people are onboarded, there is a lack of training because there is no one who can train that person on the job they are expected to do. There needs to be a transition period when one person is leaving and another is coming in to do that job for the new employee to be properly trained in all aspects of their job duties. There also needs to be consistency throughout the State in the expectations of a job.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21466

    The problem of adding more plastics in the landfill. Caltrans is doing a great job picking up litter from or highways and freeways, we are filling up thousands of plastic bags every month statewide and all the plastic bags end up in our local landfills. I started recycling the plastic bags and reusing them. My idea is to ask Caltrans maintenance personnel, volunteers and contractor to empty all plastic bags and recycle them and send to the land fill just the litter they collected.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22392

    A primary issue in state offices is the lack of open dialogue and willingness to learn from employees at all levels. Current leadership lacks mentorship opportunities to provide management with the legal, communication and general human interaction skillsets necessary for optimal performance.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22288

    Could we find a way to encourage employees to do a better job since it seems the same paid among the people who doing a good work and the people who do not doesn't work. Salary increase can be based on performance review and customer service survey including units support each other internally and clear responsibility/goal.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21808

    Let the frontline / lowest level managers decide whether their staff needs to be in office to do their jobs or whether they perform at the same level or better remotely Managers should know their staff well enough to know what works for their team. What works for one team is not necessarily what works for another Stop this one size fits all telework mandates and let the managers at the lowest level decide. Morale would increase, work productivity and dedication would increase and staff would be happier, saving money on cost of commuting Happy and satisfied employees mean productive and dedicated employees

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22329

    There are several departments under the Health and Human Services Agency that serve the same individual. It would be helpful and cut redundancy if all the departments could share one data system. The idea is to have one database with one record for each individual served. This would allow the departments to better coordinate care and services. This could potentially cut the intake process and waiting times as data may already have been collected. It would also help cut down the number of times an individual has to share their information. I understand that California has the Open Data Portal, but not all departments participate and that is to report deidentified data. A solution to solve this problem could be to require all departments to participate in a shared data base, sharing information and working together to have the most up to date information. Departments could also potentially share the cost of creating such a platform. This could potentially eliminate some of the separate database changes taking place at other departments and modernize older systems. This could also help HHS comply with some of the new CMS Final Rules by allowing cross reporting and by making sure each individual is only counted one time. I would love to hear other solutions.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21950

    We need to reduce hurdles to efficiency. I have staff that work overnight and the lighting in the building is broken. I can either go through facilities and get bids and have multiple companies deny working with me because they didn't get paid the last few times by the state, or I can work through the DGS process (getting two levels of approval as well) to buy a new lamp through small vendors that meet criteria and spend a fortune on a lamp. Both of these options take a ton of time to process, which reduces my efficiency as a supervisor when I could run to home depot and buy a new bulb or even a new lamp in 15 minutes and save tax dollars money both in my salary and the hardware's cost.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22292

    I would address the issue of need vs. waste. I would suggest a questionnaire aimed at what resources were utilized, what resources were not utilized, and what resources were needed. I would allow employees to verbalize if a particular resource is not needed and is thus wasting state funds.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22475

    By far the largest issue I see in the state is the problem of turnover and employee retention. Our knowledge bases can take years to develop. Processes like contracting have extremely specific protocols and therefore very specific training. Whenever we lose an employee, that gap can take over a year to fill and the next person might take over a months to over a year to get up to speed. This issue also dovetails into the issue of severe under staffing. Some units are operating at well under 50% of the necessary staff, or even more. When appropriately staffed units need to interface with those who have poor staffing, that causes bottlenecks which lead to months of delay. There just are not enough people to do the jobs necessary to keep the fundamental processes of our bureaucracy moving smoothly. If we can improve retention and staffing, it will go a long way to accelerating timelines and improving efficiency.

    August 19, 2025 👍 1
    • Guest-26498

      There need to be more opportunities to promote-in-place, allowing people who have gained expertise in their Agency/Department to stay, rather than forcing them to look for another job in State Government in order to be able to promote.

      September 30, 2025
  • Guest-22207

    The requirement of the State for agencies to use prison industries for purchases is a huge waste of tax payers' money. $800 dollars for folding tables, $300 desk lamps. It's insane.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22468

    A primary problem is the number of antiquated and inefficient statewide processes still in place across departments. Many workflows remain manual or paper-based, which slows productivity and increases operational costs. The focus would be on leaning out these processes through digital transformation—replacing outdated systems with streamlined, automated, and user-centered technology solutions that improve both internal operations and public service delivery.

    August 19, 2025 👍 5
  • Guest-21779

    The most problematic part of my job is the archaic computer systems which cause redundancy in performing my primary tasks. Upgrading SCO would be my primary focus.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22304

    One issue I see in my department is duplicative efforts throughout our headquarters operations. I think we could save a lot of money if we looked at our processes, leaned them out, and really focused on streamlining everything. I see a lot of duplicity happening in areas that could be centralized to work more efficiently.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22429

    Our Governor has made important strides, but he is missing the mark on one key issue. If California truly wants to reduce its carbon footprint, the solution isn’t to force everyone into electric cars, it’s to reduce the need for cars and commuting altogether. REMOTE WORK is the future. It cuts emissions, lowers costs, and allows employees to be more productive while leaving face-to-face gatherings for when they matter most, social and collaborative purposes. The Governor’s RTO order ignores both technology and the cost-saving benefits that remote work provides. It especially harms employees with disabilities who cannot safely or reasonably commute, even though they have already proven they can perform their jobs effectively from home. California should lead by example and show that sustainability starts with the state workforce, by embracing remote work, not resisting it.

    August 19, 2025 👍 20
    • Guest-22170

      Additionally, requiring everyone back into an office increases the burden on the outdated, insensitive, inhumane, and abhorrent accommodations procedures. State agencies become overburdened with accommodations procedures that also overburden our already under-resourced health system. The current accommodations process comes from a suspicious perspective that puts people with disabilities and those needing accommodations into a position where they are not trusted and respected. It overall devalues and dehumanizes our workforce. And, most people in their life will need an accommodation - so this process should be timely, respectful, flexible, and accommodating. People know what is best for them and if they are empowered to make decisions about their needs they are more likely to be successful in their work increasing worker satisfaction, productivity, and efficiency.

      August 20, 2025 👍 7
  • Guest-21873

    The biggest problem is a forced, in-person in-office presence wherein physical infrastructure (equipment, cubicles/offices, conference rooms, etc.,) is lacking and employees are often still required to participate in virtual meetings. It is a waste of time, money, and other resources. Morale remains low which contributes to lower productivity.

    August 19, 2025 👍 2
  • Guest-21942

    Favoritism and Nepotism in Hiring Unqualified candidates getting hired or promoted due to personal connections, not merit. Workplace morale and trust suffer when people perceive that opportunities aren't fairly earned. Erosion of public trust (especially in public or regulatory roles) when the public suspects favoritism in staffing. We should advocate for more transparent recruitment and selection processes. Push for independent hiring panels, blind application reviews, or standardized interviews. Encourage whistleblower protections for those who report unfair practices. Circumvention of Minimum Qualifications (MQs) Some candidates may be circumventing qualifications by enrolling in quick, sometimes low-rigor or unaccredited online graduate programs, just to “check the box.” This undermines the intent of MQs, which are supposed to ensure a candidate has a certain level of baseline expertise. It can devalue the profession and create inequities against those who pursued more rigorous paths. We should push for HR and classification teams to clarify standards: not just “enrollment” but substantial progress or completion from accredited institutions. Consider evaluating equivalencies in a more meaningful way — e.g., experience + coursework, or requiring specific coursework from recognized programs. Advocate for quality assurance checks on accepted degrees — ensuring they come from regionally accredited institutions, not diploma mills or for-profit degree factories.

    August 19, 2025 👍 4
    • Guest-25904

      I hear research suggests that interview success is not predictive of work effectiveness. We all know the eligibility exams are a joke. Why don't we have actual tests to measure fitness for the jobs we need filled instead of encouraging applicants to lie about how great their experience is? If we hired on the basis of exams that were predictive of role success, we could dispense with interviews, which in many cases present a non-job-related barrier.

      September 10, 2025 👍 2
  • Guest-22118

    Bureaucracy. In many spaces we’ve created workflows that are impossible to navigate and often create unnecessary hoops for people to jump through. Streamlining some of the duties that are cross-cutting would aide in getting projects over the finish line. Departments that are responsible for policies that are implemented statewide(DGS, CalHR) could benefit from months long job shadowing to see the policies they’ve created in action. They would be able to bring best practices back and hopefully use that information to process improve.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21437

    One problem I have is my supply budget. The price of cleaning supplies have gone up significantly but my budget has gone down as in I have to add items that are not cleaning supplies like toilet paper and paper towels, thus lowering the amount of supplies I can order. I also believe that that PIA should have less say in what I can order. Some of their products do not work and yet I'm not able to get the supplies I know work.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22457

    Some state systems are really outdated and have workarounds built in instead of replacement. If we are looking at efficiency, there are many systems out there that need to be updated as these are the tools that workers use daily.

    August 19, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-25803

      Yes, it's always a bandaid. Things need to be built from the ground up, led by those who do the work! Exes make these decisions, leading to garbage software that does not work for those using it day in and day out!

      September 05, 2025
  • Guest-22256

    I think homelessness is a huge issue. There are many different organizations that work to provide support but no one actually making significant change. We need an overarching solution. People don't become homeless overnight, and they won't get out of it overnight. We need to have a 2-pronged approach. 1st - before someone becomes homeless, we need to have a program that would allow them to access grant funding to catch them up and keep them from becoming homeless. It's far less expensive to catch someone up than to pay for all of the services they will need once they are homeless. 2nd - once someone becomes homeless, we need the ability to get them into mid - term (1-2 year) housing with wrap around services to ensure they can have their physical/mental health needs addressed, get or increase their employment, find childcare if necessary, and save money. During this time, they should also have access to financial literacy education, job training, and if needed, drug treatment programs. Waiting until someone has exhausted all options and is sleeping on the street before "helping them" by providing them 30 days in a motel, is only perpetuating the problem and keeping people in poverty.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22442

    A community-based program utilizing goats or sheep to manage vegetation on CDCR grounds could offer significant benefits, including reduced operating costs, environmental sustainability, & community engagement. This approach could also pave the way for a vocational rehabilitation program for inmates, teaching ranching skills. A viable skill to make money and raise meat for your family. Some of the benefits: 1. Reduced Operating Costs: Using animals to manage vegetation eliminates need for fuel, equip maintenance, & groundskeeper's salary. 2. Environmental Sustainability: Goats & sheep provide a natural, eco-friendly alternative to mowing, reducing reliance on fossil fuels & machinery. 3. Community Engagement: Partnering with 4-H or FFA youth groups provides a valuable community outreach opportunity & educational experience. 4. Inmate Rehabilitation: A successful pilot program could lead to development of a ranching rehabilitation program, offering incarcerated individuals marketable skills & a potential path to employment upon release. 5. Fire Hazard Reduction: Regular grazing by livestock can help reduce build-up of dry vegetation, a fire hazard, especially in California's climate. 6. Security: A thorough security assessment is crucial to ensure that program does not compromise safety & security of facility. 7. Long-term Sustainability: Develop a plan for ongoing maintenance, animal care, & program evaluation.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22265

    I think the State of California’s agencies should stop overcharging each other for work/services that are supposed to benefit our citizens. Our agency leases buildings from the Department of General Services (DGS). Anytime anything needs to be updated or fixed the quotes for a solution are usually 3x-5x the cost and 2x-3x the timeline for completion of work versus hiring a private company to do it. Example: A few years ago a loading dock door’s heavy duty motor reached its end of life, and our landlord (DGS) quoted us upwards of $25K to replace it with the same exact model. I researched the exact motor, and the part alone was $2.5K. We’ve got to figure out a better system as a state.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21985

    Many Caltrans processes still rely on PDF forms that require downloading, filling out, routing for signatures, and re-entering data into spreadsheets. This creates delays, duplicate work, and access issues — especially when PDFs are hard to use on mobile devices or when handwritten forms are difficult to read. Instead of routing documents for signatures, we can use interactive online forms to collect structured data and automate approvals. Online forms are faster to set up, reusable, and mobile-friendly. They also ensure accuracy by requiring fields, validating entries, and feeding submissions directly into tabular formats like Excel or SharePoint Lists. This eliminates manual transcription, reduces errors, and cuts down on repetitive administrative work. The benefits are clear: - Saves time by reducing steps (no printing, scanning, or configuring signatures) - Improves efficiency with real-time submission tracking and notifications - Avoids waste by reducing paper use and redundant tasks - Supports accessibility by providing legible, mobile-friendly entry options for both office and field staff This small change could save staff and the general public hours each week and provide cleaner, more usable data for decision-making — aligning with Executive Order N-30-25’s call for operational efficiencies across state government.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22262

    There are a few, there are multiple examples of administrative layers that have created massive expenses which are intended to provide savings but do not. Office of State Publishing. The mandate to pay more for a lower quality product is counter productive. The savings does not equate to the personnel and infrastructure expense. We can produce superior products for less in private industry. Prison Industries, the requirement to purchase furniture from them at often prices exceed 300% more than the same higher quality item for elsewhere. We are forced to pay $700 for a $60 office chair. If it’s more than 10% over a competitor price for like item, we should have an automatic exception from the requirement to purchase from them. Small Businesses. State mandated purchasing for high price items also causes appalling markups to costs. Though it may support small business it costs the state an exorbitant amount of money. Because of procurement limitations this essentially forces the state to pay 15-80% more for a product if it’s above $9,999. Our procurement limitations/rules create a massive expense to the state. DGS auto inspectors. Again this one is staff and infrastructure overruns. In all of my years they have never assisted in saving any money, never disproved of any service. It is my opinion that they do not provide commensurate savings to come close to their cost to operate.

    August 19, 2025 👍 5
    • Guest-25904

      Maybe CalPIA will be more competitive since they won't be subject to tariffs.

      September 10, 2025
    • Guest-26498

      CalPIA and OSP both regularly cause my projects to run over-time, over-budget, and under-value. Neither has any incentive to provide competitive service to the state. Setting aside cost and quality, their noncompetitive customer service experience costs taxpayers intense amounts of wasted staff hours. Any time I email OSP or CalPIA, even with a basic inquiry or status update request, I now budget a 2 week turn around before I can expect to hear anything in response. On a recent project, a private consultant had a California based publishing company produce a run of sample items for us in less than 2 weeks. To get OSP to produce the exact same item I have been waiting for 4 months with no end in sight, and I expect we will pay 2-3 times the cost.

    There is a big wage gap between Private sector and public service. There should be an effort to equalize the wages. I believe that will improve the productivity of state employees.

  • Guest-22261

    We are desperately under-staffed and it is difficult to keep people with institutional knowledge. Often the next promotional opportunity is in another district or another agency, which leads to loss of specialists. It takes a really long time to learn everything about a region, or just about how a particular agency works and which laws our particular work is governed by, and it slows everything down so much to lose that.

    August 19, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-26498

      Because the state hires positions, rather than people, there are often almost no opportunities to promote in place. Someone will take an SSA position, become competant enough at their work to do the work of an AGPA, and the only way for them to do that is to go to some other job, often in a different Agency, to become an AGPA. WHen they do so, the original Agency loses all of their investment in that person. It should be MUCH easier and common place to convert a high-performing employee into the next level classification while staying (and expanding) in their current position.

      September 30, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22371

    Virtual training gave a great resolve for COVID, I feel like the classrooms need to come back now. People learn differently, but as a manager, I noticed that more of my new hires come out of their virtual training confused and unclear about the topics that were covered. Some really don't appreciate the electronic platform because it's impersonal and doesn't allow them to feel engaged in the lesson. I also find that the electronic platforms do not offer the opportunity for networking, which is so valuable as you move on in your career. I also find a lot of these trainings are being cut too short. My employees don't feel like they are being given the opportunity to really retain the information, which only sets them up for failure. I am a "hands-on" learner myself, and so when my new hires tell me that they would have liked to physically handle some of the example processes, instead of virtually working through them, I can completely empathize with that. New hires are going out into their offices and causing even more exhaustion and congestion amongst those that already know the job and are trying to slow down enough to help the new hires catch up. I find that there is much more "on-the-job" training needed, but we are already so stressed for workers that this causes a major inconvenience for the office to have to pick up the slack where the training is lacking. Conclusion: Bring back classrooms for "beginner" classes and elect dedicated mentors for new hires.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21698

    Improve the training process by increasing tuition reimbursement for licensing and certification.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21432

    Make more transactions/tasks accessible via online forms & scanning documents to a state website. This will cut down on parking & space requirements to handle crowds of public customers and can free up staffing to either work from home or use remote workers far away from the actual worksite and/or work 24 hours varying shifts reviewing documents online instead of just 8-5 in physical office. For example, aside from the actual driving test the DMV could accept scanned documentation for all other transactions. The federal government for passports allows private businesses like UPS to take photo for applicants, could just take step further & allow approved businesses to scan photo as well instead of crowds standing in line at DMV.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21919

    One of the largest problems I see with the State of California is mismanagement of funds. It seems that certain programs are not thoroughly thought out before implementing, and rather than being proactive in thinking, Department's then have to be reactive and search for cost cutting measures to make up for shortfalls. This is a disservice to state employees and citizens alike. My suggestion is to allow for more open forums on what programs the public actually wants and needs to best serve them, and then make sure those programs are more well thought out before implementing.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22410

    One challenge in California government is that well-intentioned processes often pile up until they become bottlenecks. Ezra Klein’s Abundance talks about “bottleneck deserts,” and that idea really resonates with me. We spend so much time on checks, reporting, and auditing that sometimes the work of actually serving people drifts further and further away. One solution I’d like to see is the idea of “bottleneck detectives.” These would be small cross-functional teams—made up of staff who know the programs inside and out—tasked with finding the points where work slows down unnecessarily. Twice a year, they would sit down with their teams, map out processes, and identify steps that don’t add value. If a new check or reporting requirement is added, an old one has to be removed. The detectives would track and report back on what’s been eliminated, making sure accountability is preserved without building endless new layers. This kind of practice could help restore balance: keep oversight strong, but clear the path so the real work—serving Californians—stays front and center.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21808

    Since the RTO order went out, the morale and the dedication of the employees have hit rock bottom. My idea is to NOT have a mandate but rather make it based on the needs of the job. If a job can be performed fully remotely, then let those employees work remotely. Those that are allowed to work remotely are more engaged, happier, more dedicated as their life / work is better balanced and this is also good for the environment as there are less cars on the roads. Also maximum remote work helps hire from a larger pool of candidates which allows us to have more educated and qualified candidates and can hire the top talent, which is a major win and an asset for our state. Especially when it comes to professionals

    August 19, 2025 👍 23

      This! It is a proven fact that telework works well for the State and for the employee. I cannot for the life of me understand how anyone can't see that. It defies logic...

  • Guest-22421

    Reduce bureaucracy and forms whose only goal is to save miniscule amounts of state funds. For example, requiring employees to fill out TECs (with receipts) in order to receive the transit subsidy is very time consuming. It takes the staff 30 minutes to 1 hour to prepare the TEC for each month, and then there are 3 approvers who spend their time reviewing the TEC and associated receipts. The cost of these 4 people's time is significant and likely much greater than the amount of money saved (not paid to staff) by requiring TECs and receipts.

  • Guest-21194

    I would say that a lack of functional knowledge is one of the largest issues I see in government work. I would like to see more comprehensive training regarding writing and research skills. I would like to see more comprehensive training regarding professional behavior and correspondence. Most of our trainings are self paced, online trainings, where if there is a quiz at all, it is easily defeated with a google search. I would like to see trainings structured more like college courses where there are not just multiple choice knowledge checks but reviewed responses to open ended questions at several stages in the learning process, and work completed for review, feedback, and revision. I'm guilty of sometimes phoning in a training myself but I also take time to continue my education and I take pride in my work. Not everyone does and then I have to explain to them why or how what they've submitted is incorrect or incomplete. That is not always appreciated and I shouldn't have to train folks that are my superior or at my same level. Also, I would like to see us not use AI very much as it will enable these folks to continue not knowing what they are doing. Plus, the horrific impact to the environment in a state where we should be extra mindful of our environmental impact.

    August 19, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21909

    Strengthen leadership to increase efficiency, reduce turnover and reduce costs of hiring and training new employees by reconsidering classification levels; the only way to advance should not be through promoting into a management role. That leads to having the wrong people in management roles which can create toxic workplaces. (Think Michael Scott of The Office.) Instead, the state should establish advance levels of classifications with earning potential on par with managers, which will result in the retention of seasoned employees, decrease hiring and training costs of having to hire new employees when these seasoned employees leave the workforce for higher pay, and would be more efficient because the state would not lose those seasoned employees with advance knowledge and skill and historical knowledge and continue to be a valuable resource to newer employees.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22206

    I am fresh to the State but have decades of experience in the private sector. I have always been a self-leaner and the way I learn is by reading as much (requirement, design, technical, source code, etc) as possible. The challenge I found as a new state employee is that there is nothing available and there is no clear path as to where and how to make requests, even longer tenure colleagues need non-trivial effort to help me out.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21881

    I have seen a number of large IT projects fail or limp along for years costing the taxpayers a lot of money. Rather than go out and have contractors propose solutions without fully understanding the ever-evolving business needs, the state should develop a "proof of concept" in house prior to going to RFP. This means developing enough of the tool to see system limitations and get an idea on its overall functionality. This would help develop requirements of the tool as we see shortcomings of what is developed but could also be used as a way to help contractors understand what is being sought out. I would suggest involving program (the internal state teams that would use the tool) early on to ensure the business rules are represented. At the discretion of the State, then we could move to a formal RFP and demo the proof of concept or we could continue development in house. I will avoid referencing specific projects, but I can confirm that this approach would have ruled out proposals that relied heavily on tools that did not meet the need while saving a considerable amount of money.

    August 19, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21844

    The biggest issue during my 5 years with the state as someone in IT is seeing computer illiteracy amongst employees. I often hear people say "I'm not a computer person" whenever they come across something they don't understand or have an issue with. I don't think this is an acceptable mentality when over 90% of their job is on the computer! I'm not asking them to troubleshoot everything but I would like to see some effort in at least Googling how to fix minor issues like adjusting a view in Outlook or their display settings. Here's a few other common things I've encountered. -Not knowing how to shut down a computer or the difference between shutting down, restarting, sleep, and closing the lid. -What file explorer is and how to navigate network folders. -How to use the search (Windows key) to find stuff. -How to connect to the WIFI and VPN. That is why I think we need some sort of basic computer literacy exam before people can apply for a job. Maybe add it as a component in CalCareers. Another big problem I see is paper waste. We spend so much money on printers/faxes, and supplies when most things could just be a PDF. In my dept, I think we spent over a million on a filing system that can be moved electronically.

    August 19, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21857

    We in State service focus on implementing programs that distribute funds to solve a problem. Distributing those funds are often under a regulatory time constraint. The internal culture leaves little room to perform the Project Controls used in the private sector to hold grantees accountable. Project Controls track the money and schedule progress to hold stakeholders accountable. The current culture is to assume everyone is honest and playing by the rules, so we can hand out the funds and solve problems "efficiently" by skipping accountability. But leaving standard Project Controls out of the process isn't an efficiency; skipping a step in basic project management is a problem. It is an invitation to some to commit fraud. This isn't a "poor performance on the part of some people" issue. It is a cultural habit in our institutionalized processes, maybe because we don't have to show profit and loss. Incorporating the principles of Project Controls in our contract agreements with grantees - mostly invoice format requirements - is a part of the solution.

    August 19, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21432

    Permanent/long term tasks being done in small temporary structures. For example: The Vacaville prison itself where inmates are is made of concrete, but the HR & clerical tasks buildings nearby are a couple of wooden portables. A prison is around so long it really merits a permanent office building for its administrative aspects.

    August 19, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22044

    Economies that are structured with a primary goal of concentration of wealth have unintended negative results such as poverty, isolation and scarcity and ideologically based conflicts. One of the ideas to diversify economic security is a fairer flat tax for all (removing exemptions). This flat taxation idea also helps secures housing and jobs for all thru subsidies offset by the flat tax and creates a liquid cash flow (capital) to help maintain the economy as we shift to more sustainable systems not dependent so much on non-renewable resources with are finite. By broadening the definitions of success we can address phenomenon like corruption, toxicity, hoarding and emphasize values like sharing and improve empathy and caring generally. As we share intelligence and apply it to these common goals people experience and learn from the experience of others so problems that threaten societies are minimized as the cooperation crosses typical socio-economic and other common isolating conditions that threaten an optimal healthy form of democracy and threaten environmental sustainability. The results are predicted to offer a more sustainable way of life where greater forms of equilibrium and harmony may thrive and the diversity of our intelligence more positively be expressed and celebrated.

    August 19, 2025

    Bureaucracy. Red tape. Lack of ways of measuring and awarding productivity.     Stop spending so much money on outside consultants, work with actual employees. Set up SME's (Subject Matter Experts) and make them actually accessible to employees.

  • Guest-21874

    I have seen a lot of wasted taxpayer money due to the lack of modern technology and practices, as well as the state's refusal to create a space to save millions of dollars by allowing their employees to have a right to work-life balance. Many city and county agencies are far more advanced, provide healthy work-life balances, and provide proper training to management. The little bit of training provided is toxic and our managers are taught poor leadership behaviors. We as adults, are treated like children and are not allowed to have a work-life balance because management uses unfounded information to justify saying no to its employees. We have been told by our management that we are all replaceable and are not valued as employees and this is very disheartening. For our commissioner to feel that way about his staff is demoralizing. This message from the commissioner and management is not going to motivate us or drive performance. As a state employee, I previously valued my work, my team, and the contributions I make every day. Mutual respect would create a healthy work culture, but unfortunately, this attitude from management is creating the opposite effect. No state agency is exempt from the law and exempt from transparency.

    August 19, 2025 👍 6
    • Guest-25904

      Right? Even though the consultants all say to improve employee engagement, morale, and productivity we should allow flexibility, even salaried staff are expected to be available during core business hours, which are set by statute as MF 8-5. This even though the huge majority of business is conducted asynchronously and I get maybe one unscheduled call per month. Limiting staff availability to core business hours if anything makes it difficult for blue collar Californians to access state service if they do need to talk to someone in real time; many would need to clock out and take a pay hit.

      September 10, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22238

    Make telework flexible and based on operational needs as determined by first line managers and staff. A one size fits all telework model (RTO 2 or 4 days per week regardless of operational need) is not appropriate for most technical positions that can be done effectively from home, is wasteful of taxpayer dollars, and decreases employee morale, all of which impacts public service. Having a one size fits all telework model is also bad for retaining talent. We need technically proficient staff. If talented staff can telework full time with better work life balance and greater physical and mental health, and be treated with respect in managing their workload in the private sector, why would they opt to work for the state where we are treated like we can't be trusted to manage ourselves or allowed to telework based on operational need? Who wants to work for an entity that frames the need to be in office as "because teachers and janitors have to report to a job site everyday so should all other state workers". This approach disrespects the life choices that folks have made regarding their career path. A teacher and a janitor are aware that their work involves reporting to an offsite office daily, whereas technical careers or those that do not require direct face to face with the public should be allowed to telework based on the operational needs of their position. Flexible telework fosters trust, is just as effective for collaboration, and more cost effective.

    August 19, 2025 👍 2
  • Guest-21776

    Compliance requirements are not evaluated by those performing the compliance check to determine if they are providing the intended value. There is no feedback mechanism to provide this information back to lawmakers to undo ineffective regulations.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21781

    Encourage collaboration and cooperation using TEAMS, (perhaps), across ARU's to ensure uniformity. It takes too long to get an answer while on VCC from management. There is a closed-mindedness that says everything must remain under the control of the ARU instead of Department-focused responses.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-22124

    Outdated systems. Easier access to new technologies and platforms to streamline. Get outside of the paperwork, into automation. Yearly mandated training for employees for Microsoft Excel, Word, SharePoint, Adobe.

    August 19, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-22116

    We can make California government more effective by implementing long term telework. With digital systems, agencies can process requests, respond to the public and complete requests faster. In emergencies, public health crisis, or severe weather, employees can safely and efficiently report to their home offices without additional risk. Cost savings for California is clear when telework is implemented. Agencies save money on buildings, equipment, utilities, office supplies, etc. Telework will eliminate daily commutes, thus improving air quality in California, reducing risks of accidents, and giving employees time back to improve their mental health. California can attract top talent by offering this perk.

    August 19, 2025 👍 3
  • Guest-21854

    Telework, when possible, is the biggest issue. Telework works. Consolidating office space (combine multiple departments/agencies into spaces) will save money, as per the state auditor's report. State Workers working remotely will also save money by reduced commutes and the associated climate change savings, which used to be important to our state government, and by putting money back in their own communities, versus being expected to prop up the communities around the office buildings their departments are located in. It also increases employee happiness, and as a long-term state worker who has been in offices with both great morale and awful morale (both prior to the availability of telework, and not related to telework at all) happy employees make for more productive employees. Telework works, the state has shown that. Reversing course will only hurt the state going forward.

    August 19, 2025 👍 144
    • Guest-22170

      Virtual work is also more accessible to a range of persons, including persons with disabilities. In-office centric positions are inherently inequitable and create additional burdens and obstacles reducing productivity, efficiency, and effectiveness.

      August 20, 2025 👍 32
      • Guest-24547

        Absolutely! And housing costs can keep highly qualified employees from applying to positions in areas where they can not afford to live.

        August 21, 2025 👍 10
        • Guest-27000

          There should be "affordable" housing downtown that average working people such as state workers can afford. There are many state employees who would like to live downtown, whether working hybrid, in office or remote. Businesses could be supported 7 days a week, rather than relying on state worker schedules (Mon-Friday 8am-5pm).

          September 29, 2025 👍 1
    • Guest-22994

      Telework also allows us to work in safe environments, i.e. in our homes. In my home I do not need to worry about witnessing racist things written on walls or on notepads (which I have experienced in my office downtown and reported to HR). In my own home I also will not likely hear inappropriate sexual comments made to women in my office (also something I have experienced in my office downtown and reported to HR). Working in my own home I also will not have to worry about cockroaches (something I have also experienced in my office downtown). Working in my own home I also will not have to worry about workplace violence (something I luckily have never had to experience but many others have). For those people who are not worried about the above (or do not care), they can go work in the office. I am much more efficient, and less stressed, on my telework days. Telework works!!

      August 21, 2025 👍 31
      • Guest-24485

        This is so true! I really struggle with all of the racism in the office and hate that I have to either bite my tongue and accept it, or face retaliation for reporting it. I filed a bullying complaint for some racist comments and the solution was to sweep me under the rug and rearrange my schedule so the person who made the comments would not have to see me. Another insidious aspect of being in the office is that the dominant culture crushes any thoughts or perspective that it doesn't agree with. I see great ideas and suggestions made by women, BIPOC, and LGBTQIA+ folks get swept aside which makes us less likely to contribute to the discussion. Telework helps me maintain my sanity.

        August 22, 2025 👍 15
        • Guest-21174

          This is so awful. I'm sorry you all have experienced this. :(

          August 29, 2025 👍 3
        • Guest-25537

          This reminds me of something I read from women in Congress, when facing this same issue they realized if they banded together they were able to reinforce ownership for example in a mtg when a minority person makes a good suggestion, another minority person makes sure to back it up for the record like, "What a great idea Anika, let's make sure and submit that to management right away, I'll cc the group" and then when it doesn't go anywhere until later when Justin pretends it's his idea, whoever hears it should respond with, "Oh right that's what Anika came up with at the last mtg, what a great reminder" etc etc - this is the supportive behaviour we should all normalize.

          As an EEO employee, I find this very troubling, but not surprising. Sadly we live in a time in which many people feel comfortable openly discriminating and harassing people.       • Guest-23524

        Wow. I'm sorry to hear that happening to you and your co-workers. I thought that there were policies for things like this statewide regardless of where one works in state service. I hope you don't take away that everyone feels that way toward you. I hope you do have some folks that aren't like that and that you focus on them. I am a strong believer that we "hang with people like ourselves", so if you are experiencing very negative things in your workplace, seek out and "hang" with folks that aren't like that in order to keep positive. My experience is different, but I don't like the lies folks are allowed to spread about me or others I know, and I would like to see more hands-on training about this. It usually goes that when you report it, you have a mark on your back and now you are a dart board for anything anyone dislikes. I have a co-worker who works in another dept. and their take on it is that people who are angry with and unhappy in their own lives generally are the people that spread this sort of stuff. It is still up to administration to take it seriously and do something about it.

    • Guest-27188

      Agreed. There is absolutely nothing that I can't do from home that I do from my work office. It's a control/trust issue.       October 03, 2025 👍 4
    • Guest-25846

      I'm not sure I entirely agree. I've found that the loss of the human element has been devastating. The lack of "doorway" conversations, and "watercooler" chats has really impacted our ability to quickly problem solve and come up with new solutions that these natural and organic conversations and relationships bring about. As someone that was new to an organization during telework, I felt that I was never really a "part" of the organization. There are people that I've only seen on Teams. The relationships that they have with others that they knew "pre-pandemic" had a place to start. So they have no issue with IMs/etc to communicate. But when there is no opportunity to actually get to know people in the workplace, the team dynamic suffers. I'm happy with a mixed model telework program of 3 days in office.

      October 03, 2025 👍 5
      • Guest-27240

        For those that desire the comraderie of in-office interaction, there has always been an open door policy to come into the office to work. Everyone performs better in an environment that they are comfortable in, not one in which they are forced to be in.

        October 06, 2025 👍 4
    • Guest-26383

      I disagree. What frightens me most about Telecommuting is that people are only thinking about how it benefits them now. It is the one thing that is harming our environment and community the fastest, and nobody seems to be paying attention. Think about it. People that telecommute can live anywhere else in the world and buy up property that at one time was very cheap. The remote places and small, quiet, serene towns that some of us love to drive through and visit, will soon not exist at all. During Covid, I lost half my friends and family who have moved across the country to buy up property that was once inexpensive due to Telecommuting. Now I don’t see them. Property is now being bought up in droves, which has brought up prices of homes at an astronomical rate. At this rate, perhaps in as little as 20 years, all those remote places will be gone and the big cities will be unlived, hazardous and unkept. You think it helps the environment because people don't drive, you are wrong. People have time to shop online get things delivered to their homes, they no longer support their community like they once did, and that means no more stores or restaurants, and all of those boxes and packing material, where do you think it goes? And I know for a fact that Telecommuting means you are not as productive. When I visit a friend who telecommutes, he watches TV with me and takes care of his kids and dogs, goes shopping, etc. and I wonder what work he really gets done. Its harmful.

      October 05, 2025 👍 4
      • Guest-26695

        I agree completely. There are jobs that for sure can be done remotely and yes large amounts of money can be saved. The trouble the many of these posts is that they are only thinking of how to make their day better. Remember, the job we applied for did not list in the duties, taking care of kids or shopping. After 36 years of state service and raising 3 kids, there are sacrifices that are always made. After covid we became a society that has been taught that my life comes first. You're not paid for that. sorry. State service is like all other industries that relate to service. You give to make others' lives better through helping or doing for someone. Lastly, someone mentioned that they work and those who don't should be dealt with. you are completely right whether in or out of the office.     There is a lack of available technical asynchronous training, including in programs used daily by employees, such as Microsoft programs.   • Guest-21989

    Working in a human resources division, the largest complaint I have been given from the public is the lack of communication. I find that many NEW to State service candidates apply to multiple vacancies and do not receive any feedback or notice if a job was closed. I believe communication from hiring manager(s) needs to improve; whether a candidate interviewed or not, they should be receiving an auto-email explaining why they were not chosen. There are options for this in ECOS (from CalHR), so I am not sure why it is not being used (at least in my Dept). I feel this may alleviate some of the frustration from those wanting to join State service.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21187

    I have been a state employee for 5 years, i have worked for two separate boards in the same department. A problem I have noticed is the lack of camaraderie and unity within departments and boards. A solution I may suggest would be quarterly employee enrichment activities. I believe such activities have a tremendous impact on morale. I think that happier state workers can produce more positive interactions with the public.

    August 19, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21343

    Two primary challenges I’ve noticed in my workplace are the lack of standardization and the amount of repetitive, manual work. Like others have mentioned, inadequate training resources, limited access to information, and redundant forms often slow down our ability to work efficiently. To address these issues, I’ve taken initiative by using Microsoft Office tools (SharePoint, Power Automate, Power Apps, etc.) that are already available to us at no additional cost to the state. Here are some of the steps I’ve implemented: 1. SharePoint Site Development -Centralized and standardized all forms required for my role. -Used Power Automate to auto-fill redundant fields, reducing repetitive data entry. -Migrated resources from local drives to the cloud in compliance with IT policies, improving accessibility and collaboration. 2. Interactive Training Resources -Built a Wiki page to consolidate and standardize the training I’ve received from management. -Created step-by-step work instructions with embedded GIFs (instead of static screenshots) so employees can visually follow the process in real time. By streamlining processes and digitizing resources, I’ve helped improve efficiency, reduce errors, and make information easier to access—all while keeping costs neutral for the state.

    August 19, 2025 👍 8
  • Guest-21588

    Having worked for the state for over ten years across two departments and three locations, I have consistently noticed a lack of standardization and collaboration that affects overall efficiency. Even within the same department, different offices often perform the same tasks in completely different ways. This creates confusion, inconsistent training, and inefficiencies. There is also a widespread lack of clear and accessible documentation for processes and procedures. Without it, onboarding is more difficult, and new employees spend unnecessary time trying to figure out how to complete routine tasks. There is also very little collaboration or sharing of knowledge between departments. When I have reached out for guidance or documentation, I rarely receive a response, and when I do, it is often not useful. It can feel like departments are operating in silos, keeping information to themselves instead of working together. I think focusing on standardizing processes and building a more open, collaborative culture would significantly improve how effectively we function as a state.

    August 19, 2025 👍 8
  • Guest-21295

    The primary problem is no accountability. We have management that treats employees horribly, and micromanages them to the point of trying to control individuals personal property. We also have employees that come up with every excuse possible to not do something, abuse state time, and purposefully make uncomfortable or problematic situations in the office. Nothing seems to happen, its have a conversation, send a recap email, etc. but to get to the actual accountability part takes so long and so much effort that managers often give up. Or if they don't give up and see it through, nothing changes, everyone continues to act the same, and all that happens is good employees, and managers leave because they don't want to deal with the drama any longer.

    August 19, 2025 👍 4
  • Guest-21706

    One primary challenge I would address is ensuring that state resources are used as efficiently and effectively as possible, particularly in programs that directly impact communities. Too often, valuable funding is underutilized, delayed due to administrative hurdles, or misused. To address this, I would focus on streamlining processes, improving interagency communication, and increasing transparency for both staff and the public. By simplifying procedures and making information more accessible, we can ensure that funds are deployed on time, maximize their impact, and maintain public trust.

    August 19, 2025
  • Guest-21728

    Recruitment and retention of state peace officers is harder than ever over the last 10 years. Many police agencies have adjusted to the times and now allow officers to wear well groomed beards and display tattoos that are of good taste and not objectively offensive. However, the California Highway Patrol has not allowed tattoos to be displayed nor male officers to have beards, all for the sake of tradition. Our modern culture has grown to accept both as acceptable in the workplace, and in fact, makes officers more relatable. Many communities may be able to relate to an officer who has tattoos, especially younger people. As the melting pot of the United States, many foreign cultures co-exist in California and universally respect male officers with beards as being elders with serious life, and job experience, particularly when they see gray in the beard. This makes them more willing to listen and being compliant with an officer. It's already permitted for religious exemptions, i.e. for Sikh officers. It should be permitted across the board for all officers. This may seem like a non-issue to someone not in law enforcement, but other agencies have noticed higher levels of recruiting and retention by allowing tattoos and beards. It is also a huge morale booster and allows officers to express themselves as individuals and humans, rather than as de-humanized, monotonous, faceless agents of the government. I would like to see State Personnel Board force this issue with CHP

    August 18, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21671

    I find that the primary problem is that too many large corporations take way too much from the CA taxpayers. Many were not eligible for PPP loans and there's not enough law enforcement so it encourages criminal and/or misrepresentation in obtaining free funds from the State of California. Some corporations illegally or inappropriately received PPP loans based on the honor system and took PPP loan deductions in their returns. If the FTB shared this misrepresentation found during routine audits with other agencies such as SBA to enforce those (i.e. large and publicly traded corporations) that inappropriately received funds, the State of CA would be able revive the income. When CA funds are inappropriately used to fund profitable corporations during the panddemic such as PPP loans and online criminals continuously stealing money from the State of CA's IT systems, more law enforcement is needed to avoid the illegal loss of funds . Rather than cutting state salaries, budget cuts, and other reductions that harm the State of CA and our taxpayers, we should pursue the large corporations and criminals that illegally received the funds in the first place. Placing more security on our IT systems is crucial for protecting our taxpayer funds from continual hacking and misappropriation of funds. Instead of hitting the CA taxpayers with more costs and illegal use of taxapyer funds, the politicians should invest more in our IT crime prevention to protect the State of CA & its people.

    August 18, 2025 👍 2
  • Guest-21430

    CalEPA's Boards, Departments, and Offices (BDOs) routinely require regulatory data to be uploaded to public databases annually, or within days of certain events (e.g., a rain event). For example, the State Water Board operates the SMARTS database wherein industrial permittees upload data throughout the year to demonstrate compliance with the General Industrial Storm Water Permit. Other BDOs operate similar types of databases. The state should strongly consider a system to compare the applicable numeric values in the self-reports against the regulatory thresholds. Automatic notices could be issued to the permittee or a report could be generated and provided to the enforcement unit of each BDO. This would improve compliance and accountability, while also benefitting human health and the environment. The opportunity for facilities to "hide and wait" for enforcement would be diminished.

    August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21270

    Many of government processes are riddled with inefficiency and redundancy, which causes things to take an unnecessarily long amount of time. Streamlining policies to remove redundancy and create one master set of requirements to approve projects would be beneficial. I believe it's possible to to reduce bureaucracy while still keeping protections for people and the environment in place.

    August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21459

    The most challenging meetings and problems I have been in are those that are trying to solve the problems of the 5th or 6th standard deviation from the norm. By doing so, we create so many exceptions and (my favorite word) nuances that a simple policy to would clearly affect 98% of the state is now confusing for 99% of the state for the sake of the 1% who we tried to carve an exception for. We are a large and diverse state. One size does not fit all. However, nuanced policies are extremely difficult to put on paper, create black and white rules, and thus subject to abuse. We must accept that there will be some who will be left out by policies we write, but also appreciate that a well drafted policy will help far more than will be left out. A poorly drafted policy, while perhaps well intentioned, often creates more waste and confusion than solutions.

    August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21614

    With so many forms needed from any company to purchase products, or repair products we often go to the level of replace over repair simply due to the excess amount of forms required. If a repair of an item is $150, but a new one is $1400, many business will not spend the time required to fill out forms that don't really apply to the repair or problem. So the state ends up buying new and from a business that is charging a premium for simply drop shipping from major retailers only because they are willing to fill out the paperwork. We should be spending money on quality equipment at a reasonable price. But all the red tape to purchase cost 2x as much for the same item that can be purchased with a credit card. Today's day and age with online shopping and shipping, three bids for items we can look up and know what they should cost is just wasting the retailer or employees time. Trim the paperwork red tape, and allow online purchases with known items removing the bid process. Hold the person responsible for buying outside of approved items with Credit card.

    August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21126

    The best way to increase efficiency, morale, and positive impact is to start with the facts (in this case, data). If data shows that one work method is more favorable/optimal than another, then that information could become an actionable item. If data is not immediately available, then look to your labor force for feedback. If your labor force is non-committal, then maybe it is time for a trust building exercise...these ideas are regularly explored in books regarding leadership. Read "The Five Dysfunctions of a Team" or Carly Fiorina's "Tough Choices". In this moment, millennials and younger generations have a lot to teach. They were born into the world of data. They know exactly how to improve their jobs. And having a platform to do so will not only increase productivity but save the state millions upon millions on labor force hours, lawsuits, and resource expenditures.

    August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21520

    Antiquated procedures like time keeping/payroll which still use various systems across Departments, ranging from Excell spreadsheets to external systems.

    August 18, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21107

    A problem I'd like to raise is the importance of providing timely and meaningful feedback. Strong managers use the STD 638 to offer the truly earned feedback, where mediocre managers often rank everyone as "M" and provide little, if any, constructive feedback there or in day-to-day interactions. For those who have been over-achievers all of our lives, to have a mediocre manager provide this is frustrating and off-putting. Encourage managers to work hard on these as this engagement will motivate staff, support professional growth and ensure that high performers feel valued. Less effective managers tend to provide only average or generic reviews, even for excellent employees, without offering guidance on how they can advance. This lack of feedback discourages staff and can hinder development. Additionally, promotions should be based on demonstrated leadership qualities rather than circumstance. A stronger vetting process is needed to ensure promotions go to those who consistently model dedication and performance. HR should also allow and encourage higher rankings for employees who truly give 110%. When staff exceed expectations but their reviews do not accurately reflect their effort, it creates deep frustration and drives talented employees to consider leaving for agencies where their contributions will be recognized. By strengthening both the feedback process and evaluation system, the organization can better retain top talent and truly promote a culture of excellence.

    August 18, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-25904

      When I started with the state, my supervisor apologized for giving me Meets Expectations, saying that the Execs would not allow Exceeds Expectations on probationary reports.

      September 10, 2025
  • Guest-21576

    Allowing individual departments and work teams to make their own decisions about telework would significantly boost recruitment, retention, morale, and productivity. Per the Aug 2025 report released by the California State Auditor, it would also save taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars and reduce the state's carbon footprint. Enabling staff at all levels to work from home allows staff to avoid expensive commutes and devote more energy to their work and families. As a manager, my observations have been that in-office work has done nothing to boost productivity among my staff and only hurts morale. If the state wants to operate at its highest capacity, it needs to recruit the best staff from across the state. The best way to do this is to empower individual work teams to make their own decisions about telework.

    August 18, 2025 👍 7
  • Guest-21491

    Provide maximum teleworking to all state employees that are able to perform teleworking as a part of their job function as a codified benefit. This will result in the ability for the state to consolidate its building footprint into a few major buildings throughout the state which can be used for hotelling. Take the remaining buildings no longer in use and convert them into section 8 or subsidized housing for homelessness which will reduce the need to spend billions on homeless shelters/programs. Take the savings from those programs and rebuild the rainy-day funds. This will also reduce the impact of vehicle emissions with at least 100k state workers being taken off the daily commute throughout the state. Everyone wins. I understand this would require a lot of logistics, but if the state wants to play hard ball with state workers, then they could trade the 3% GSI (general salary increase) for permanent teleworking enshrined in major MOUs between the unions and the state of California. Please execute this ASAP. Thank you.

    August 18, 2025 👍 7

    more advancement

    August 18, 2025 👍 1

    (Specific to Downtown Los Angeles Offices) There is a lack of internally automated work flow processes. Lax or undocumented standardized operating procedures. Over-hiring of staff ahead of department optimization.

  • Guest-21423

  • Guest-21161

    I would address the primary problem of departmental technology silos hindering effective digital government service delivery. Currently, departments solve similar problems other departments are solving or have already solved in isolation, resulting in inefficient and redundant workstreams. The digital service delivery is fragmented and inefficient, also contributing to a confusing and frustrating experience for Californians. To solve this, I would propose the following: 1. Mandate Inter-departmental Collaboration: The state should create a framework that incentivizes and, where necessary, requires departments and agencies to collaborate on key initiatives. This could involve creating a statewide playbook and process that all digital service projects align and follow with a central oversight. 2. Standardize Technology and Data: We need to break down the technical barriers that prevent departments from working together. A statewide mandate should be put in place to standardize technology platforms and data-sharing protocols. For instance, all digital services should use a common API frameworks and be inter-operable. This would allow different agencies to easily share information, integrate services, and build a more seamless, user-friendly experience for the public. By implementing these changes, we can move from a system of isolated, inefficient solutions to a collaborative, and cohesive government that is truly focused on better serving the people of California.

    August 18, 2025 👍 20
    • Guest-22170

      Silos within and across organizations are contributing to inefficiencies and decreased productivity. Also, silos create openings for legal risk.

      August 20, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-22261

      On a related note, there really should be at least one IT specialist at least in every county, if not in every office building. Having to rely on headquarters for all IT issues when some districts are 5+ hours away is ridiculous.

      August 20, 2025 👍 3
    • Guest-21174

      Yes!! Siloes are so inefficient.

      What would help this is if everyone adopted abstracting their environments into code. That way we could all work under a common language and share ideas and solutions.

  • Guest-21432

    The work can't stop when the 1 person who does the task calls out sick. There are tasks for example several people can request office supplies but processing office supply refills that just 1 person handles & when that individual are out of office a backlog of requests not getting processed just waits. Management ideally should have a team for each task already designated so that no 1 person becomes a chokepoint and potential bottleneck stopping work from proceeding just for taking time off.

    August 18, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-22261

      When I worked private sector we had a three-tiered SME spreadsheet. It was nothing fancy, it was just an Excel document that everyone had access to that showed who knew how to do what. So if Hannah was out of office and you needed to ask her a question about a specific thing, you could pull up the spreadsheet and see that Chris was the next best person to ask. It also meant that Hannah was only responsible for training Chris (and Chris was responsible for training the third tier) so the expectations were extremely clear and no one felt crippling anxiety about the deluge of emails that would await them after vacation.

      August 20, 2025

    as a state employee and working for CDCR, I fell we don't let the inmate population grow as if they were outside in today's environment, for example not letting them make their own medical appointments within the institution so we can cut down on the appointments within the institution. CDCR Officers are also expected to become Office technician when Officers sole responsibility is security and to make sure the inmate is accounted for, not check in and out after their appointment with medical. I believe if we as a department ran the medical areas as they do outside of the prison setting, we would be moving in the right direction. CDCR also does not provide inmates vocational classes (i.e. HVAC, plumbing, etc.), so they can go out into today's society and excel. expand to job ready programs that way inmates can get a job once getting out and then we can offer more job fairs for inmates as well.

    Create more skilled managers. Turnover is often the result of poor management, a lack of leadership skills, and/or a lack of experience supervising people. An 80-hour online training is not sufficient.

      Each team under a Sup or Manager or Leader should have a dashboard chart that shows employee turnover rate under their organization. These leaders should be measured based on turnover rate. If management and leaders are doing a good job, coach, grooming, motivating and addressing issues, turnover should be low.

      September 03, 2025 👍 4
      • Guest-25870

        Also, leaders/sup/managers should manage employees by results and workload. This will help leaders to stay in touch while employees work remotely.     • Guest-22068

      How about something as simple as managers should talk less and listen more? In my brief time with the State, I have had (and currently have) managers who drone on and on and on at the expense of staff's time. Also, shorten the meetings. A meeting that can be an email should be an email. And if you've sent the email, it doesn't need to be restated and explained in a meeting. There is SO MUCH overexplaining. The amount of having to be a captive audience to managers who like the sound of their own voices is exhausting.

      September 11, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21300

    Issue 1 - Lack of software and interface training for new and existing employees. Solution 1 - Make software training and tool updates easier to find and advertised. This includes Microsoft products and new tools. There are tools out there we don't know about which can be useful as changes come, or new tools are available. Issue 2 - Too much internal Spam. Solution 2 - Set standard subject lines for Department/Statewide emails to better filter them to email folders. E.g. use "Job Posting" at the beginning of the subject for any kind of new or backfill positions being advertised. Issue 3 - Reasonable Accommodation (RA) process. Solution 3 - Allow 1st or 2nd level supervisors/managers to determine RA approval, and not have it go to headquarters. IF denied, then the employee should have the option to push it up the chain. HQ is a bottleneck and some folks have to wait 6 months or more for approval. Issue 4 - Inflexible Telework Solution 4 - Allow more flexibility of telework schedule by having 1st or 2nd level supervisor/manager to determine viability of telework for the position and the schedule (required in office vs. remote time), instead of one size fits all coming from headquarters or statewide. Issue 5 - Most forms not e-sign ready Solution 5 - Convert electronic forms to be e-sign ready instead of requiring convoluted procedures to "flatten" them.

    August 18, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21692

    State workers who commute to and from the office using public transportation who used to receive monthly vouchers for that expense are more recently required to submit reimbursement claims to the State Controller's Office (to CalAters Global) for review for each individual month and each request currently has to be reviewed forensically and approved as a unique non-travel expense for each individual commuter. This forensic review process and account reconciliation would seem to be waste of State resources if the commuter is going to continue to use the same public transportation each month. The suggestion is to bring the voucher system back. If the cost of a monthly transportation pass increases (which is infrequent) then the voucher amount would increase accordingly. Surely that would be less costly than CalAters reviewing every individual commuter's request for reimbursement every month by the accounting team.

    August 18, 2025 👍 4
    • Guest-23029

      Using CalATERS is such a hassle. I was delayed getting my reimbursement one month because 1.) My supervisor missed it and only approved it after I followed up with her 3-4 weeks later, and 2.) they are so backlogged and often working OT or weekends and it takes time to enter into the system. It's beyond antiquated!

      August 23, 2025 👍 1

    • Guest-26468

      I think that is a great idea. I avoid using CalATERS because of how difficult it is. The feedback on what needs to be changed when a claim gets sent back is ambiguous. ASIFlex has a great reimbursement site, which tracks the weeks you have filed pervious claims for.

  • Guest-21691

    I would like to address the issue of recidivism at CDCR. I have introduced an anti-recidivism initiative to CDCR, called 'Trade Ready' where we train the incarcerated people to be Tradesmen through on the job training maintaining the facility at Plant Operations. They are not only taught a trade, but more importantly how to be tradesmen, how to work with other Tradesmen to get the job done, under stressful time sensitive conditions. They learn the skills to be successful workers out on the streets. This is achieved by modifying the way, we utilize and train the Incarcerated. We want to release Tradesmen, not criminals into society. And all this is done at no additional cost to the State.

    August 18, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21452

    The State's hiring process is confusing, cumbersome, and inefficient for applicants AND hiring managers. From the internal perspective, hiring managers are inconsistently trained, some not at all, on the process. When will we hear back from CalHR with an offer? Do I have to/can I write to people who didn't get the job? Am I required to contact references? Can I give a verbal offer before I get the go-ahead from CalHR? Once when I was hiring, HR screened the applications, I interviewed people, chose a candidate, and then HR told me that person wasn't qualified -- that shouldn't happen! The whole process takes a very long time, and we lose the most qualified applicants because they start new jobs before we even finish interviewing people, or get the $ offer from CalHR. The confusion and lack of communication around the process causes extreme inefficiency and time wasted. From the perspective of an applicant, the process lacks transparency: When will I hear back?; How high do I have to score on an exam to be considered?; I got a 95% on the exam, was highly qualified for the job, but I didn't ever hear a word from the agency or CalCareers after I submitted my application. Applicants shouldn't need to go to Reddit for answers to these questions. Staff need consistent-across-the-board training and guidance materials. We need to give applicants better, transparent guidance and communication. We need to make the hiring process more efficient and less cumbersome all around.

    August 18, 2025 👍 20
    • Guest-22261

      Additionally, the people at CalHR are often not educated on the job being hired for. I heard a story once of a person with a masters degree in anthropology being told they didn't meet the minimum qualifications for the archaeologist exams because the person at CalHR didn't realize archaeology and anthropology are essentially the same degree. I have sat at event tables to recruit for my department and it's always awful hearing so many people who sound incredibly qualified saying they didn't get a position because an exam wasn't offered on time, or they didn't understand list eligibly, or (one time) that they passed the exam but their score got posted to a different CalCareers account. There is no reason for it to be this hard.

      August 20, 2025 👍 1

      Additionally, anyone involved in hiring needs to be trained to avoid discrimination practices and to ensure inclusive hiring practices. Without such measures in place agencies open themselves up to legal and reputational risk which decrease productivity and efficiency.

        I went to a training by Dept of Rehabilitation for inclusive hiring practices and learned so much. When I was interviewing, I was shocked that no one knows them or implements them.
