    • Guest-23029

      I applied for my job early last year and when I was contacted about setting up an interview several months later, I had forgotten I had ever applied for it! I also find it extremely hard just to find other positions within CalHR that I might want to apply to or get updates on.

      August 22, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-26671

      Reimagining Recruitment for Analyst Roles The current recruitment process for analyst positions across state agencies is inefficient and outdated. Most new applicants just want to work for the State—they’re not necessarily tied to a specific department. Yet, they’re forced to apply separately to dozens of similar positions across different agencies, which wastes time and creates unnecessary competition between departments for the same talent. Here’s a better approach: consolidate analyst hiring into a general pool. The State could recruit analyst generalists centrally, then place them at agencies based on need, fit, and interest. This would streamline hiring, reduce duplication, and help agencies fill roles faster with qualified candidates. It’s time to rethink recruitment as a statewide strategy, not a department-by-department scramble.

      September 26, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21688

    I feel a challenge which our office experiences are employer engagement and commitment for student/apprentice placement. We are actively forming partnerships and entering into agreements to address this challenge, but would like the culture to shift so employers are more actively involved in fulfilling roles through apprenticeship opportunities.

    August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21639

    Problem: Managers are overextended and lack the capacity to coach and guide their teams effectively. Why this happens: -High performers are often promoted to management based on individual success, not leadership ability. -Many managers have never received ongoing coaching (distinct from one-time training), so they may be unaware of their gaps or the impact on their teams. -People don’t know what they don’t know, so they’re unlikely to seek out coaching on their own. Solution: Provide ongoing coaching for managers, including peer-to-peer formats. This isn’t about formal training programs, but rather open, topic-driven dialogue. For example, live forums could focus on a key topic, with breakout groups (2–4 people) every 10–15 minutes to discuss real-world scenarios and share solutions. CalSTRS does something similar once per quarter for their managers, so that might be a template to start from in other agencies. It's not required, but I think that's a misstep. Why it matters: When managers receive consistent coaching, they’re better equipped to guide their teams, set clear expectations, and connect individual work to broader goals, ultimately improving team morale and efficiency over time.

    August 18, 2025 👍 7
    • Guest-24371

      There is NOT a good management or leadership development program in state government.

      September 26, 2025
  • Guest-21529

    One of the most pressing challenges I see is the restriction on using AI tools in our daily work. This limitation not only slows down innovation but also prevents us from serving our communities with the efficiency, creativity, and responsiveness they deserve. AI, when used ethically and intentionally, is not a threat—it’s a partner. It can help us streamline repetitive tasks, generate accessible content, analyze data for equity gaps, and even support inclusive language in public communications. These are not luxuries; they are necessities in a modern public service environment. To solve this, I would propose a pilot program that allows the use of AI tools within a secure, policy-guided framework. We could start with low-risk applications—like summarizing meeting notes, drafting outreach materials, or organizing project timelines—and build trust through transparency and training. My vision is not just to adopt technology, but to steward it wisely. With the right guardrails, AI can become a tool for justice, clarity, and care—reflecting the very heart of public service.

    August 18, 2025 👍 3

    first problem I encounter was the run over dollars to save pennies, Standards are way too high for this date and time, .     I feel the biggest problem I have experience is the higher ups in management/executive staff have no idea how their decisions affect staff. It seems they don't really care either.     I feel that there are way too many people at the top and not enough of us who are "boots on the ground" making the state run. I also think we deserve more appreciation than we receive. That alone might make the state work more efficiently.

    • Guest-22261

      At the very least, things would go more smoothly with less staff turnover. I have never worked HR but I have heard it is a micromanaged nightmare, and as a result no one ever knows how things are supposed to work because no one can stand to stay there long enough to learn. This leads to everything having to be resubmitted multiple times with multiple edits and approvals which is a waste of time and money, as well as a blow to morale.

      August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21150

    The primary problem with working for the State of California is the way staff are treated. Most supervisors receive thorough training on how to issue disciplinary actions, but very little, if any, on how to actually lead, motivate, and support their teams. This results in a culture of enforcement over empowerment, where employees often feel undervalued and micromanaged rather than inspired to perform at their best. Suggested solutions: Implement hiring and promotion practices that prioritize: People skills, Proven ability to develop others, and Desire to lead not just manage. Select supervisors based on leadership potential. The work can be learned on the job but emotional intelligence needs to be there before they become a supervisor and have power over peoples careers. Build accountability for “how” supervisors lead. Tie supervisor performance evaluations and promotions to: Team morale and retention metrics (if there is a high turn-over then something is wrong that shouldn't be ignored), 360-degree feedback from staff (this is one I have implemented upon myself), and Demonstrated leadership behaviors — not just output numbers. Shift the culture from punitive to supportive. Encourage a workplace philosophy that emphasizes: Employee engagement over enforcement Problem-solving conversations instead of automatic discipline, Psychological safety, where staff feel comfortable speaking up. Invest in real leadership development not just a book club.

    August 18, 2025 👍 37
    • Guest-22261

      I worked for CALFIRE years ago and they really invested in leadership over management. It was the best work environment I have ever been in!

      August 20, 2025 👍 1
      • Guest-24885

        Still is! Best leadership I have ever worked for!

        September 18, 2025
    • Guest-22170

      Additionally, punitive management styles are not flexible or inclusive opening up legal and reputational risks. Both risks reduce efficiency and productivity.

      August 20, 2025 👍 1
    • Guest-23825

      Yes!!! I’ve even heard complaints from managers saying the mandated trainings don’t provide any practical leadership applications. And the person who mentioned this to me severely needs help but I also just think some people might not be a good fit/don’t understand what managing people is. I usually experience managers will only engage if you’re a problem. Yes men who over extend SOMETIMES get rewarded w a promo for more $ but are treated like a personal assistant. That being said, I know other agencies and sometimes just units/people within can really make a difference. But don’t be breadcrumbed into a miserable environment bc one manager is good at their job (note to self). I wish management had more required feedback sessions. Like read the Reddit thread, reflect, and make real changes.

      August 21, 2025 👍 3
    • Guest-27872

      This! This is what I came here to say. As a relatively new employee to the State, I’ve observed some concerning disparities in management oversight across departments. While I understand that some areas utilize tools like 360-degree reviews and engagement surveys to support accountability and continuous improvement, this is not the case in my department. It is disheartening to witness talented and dedicated employees become disengaged or leave entirely due to unaddressed management issues. There appears to be no formal mechanism for providing feedback on managerial performance—no 360 reviews, no engagement or pulse surveys, and no exit interviews. As a result, problematic management behaviors often go unchecked, with no accountability or opportunity for constructive feedback. The high turnover in the department should serve as a clear signal that something is amiss. Without systems in place to evaluate and improve leadership practices, managers are able to operate without oversight, while employees are left vulnerable. Since managers control performance evaluations and career progression, employees are placed in a difficult position where they may feel unable to speak up without fear of retaliation. Introducing even basic feedback systems would be a meaningful step toward a healthier and more effective workplace culture. Doing so would not only improve morale and retention, but also support the professional growth of both staff and leadership.

      October 15, 2025 👍 1
    • Guest-22068

      I second all of this and particularly the 360-feedback for managers. ALL managers, including executive level (chiefs, deputy chiefs, etc.).

      September 12, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-26597

      This is a great idea, to have supervisors evaluated based on the quality of work and the work environment of employees beneath them. Who is keeping management accountable for perpetuating this culture of fear and apathy?

      September 27, 2025 👍 2

    I agree with all of those comments talking about the need to develop a resource hub and sharing information about the different programs and resources are available both intra and inter-departmentally.     August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21486

    Right now, ASL Interpreters in California state service are often managed by people who don’t understand the profession or the needs of the Deaf/Hard of Hearing community. This creates mismanagement, inconsistent access, and unnecessary strain on both interpreters and the people they serve. A practical, immediate step would be to require each state department that employs ASL Interpreters to have an ASL Interpreter Coordinator—a position held by a qualified interpreter. This model already exists in the State Schools, where interpreters are managed effectively by someone with lived professional expertise. Expanding that model across departments would ensure proper support, accountability, and better access for Deaf/Hard of Hearing employees and clients. Longer term, California could take a bold step by creating a Department of Language to house and organize all interpreters statewide—including ASL, Spanish, and other languages. This would centralize resources, guarantee professional management by people who understand the work, and ensure that language access is treated as an essential public service. Importantly, this would also allow interpreters to be shared across departments, so that if a DMV office (for example) suddenly needed an ASL Interpreter, the Department of Language could quickly dispatch one. This approach strengthens equity, efficiency, and accessibility across the state.

    August 18, 2025 👍 4
    • Guest-22956

      I completely agree - I don't even want to get in to how hard it was to get an interpreter for my Upward Mobility meetings with a deaf client simply because the client was not in my same division. And then the appalling push back I got from accounting asking "how could this cost be approved" - things like that. First of all, it should be as easy as picking up a phone to access a virtual interpreter and similarly as easy if I needed to book interpreters for in person occasions, and second of all I should never ever be asked how cost for an interpreter could have been approved. period. I asked for an interpreter and we pay for it. end of story.

      August 20, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21369

    A fresh look at procurement practices is needed, for cost-saving measures. When ordering hundreds or thousands of items like we were having to do, the wasted tax dollars add up quickly.

    August 18, 2025 👍 21
    • Guest-24145

      Agreed, PIA is not the cheapest or the best quality. This is a disservice to the tax payers. And the fact that it takes an hour or more worth of accounting to buy one item is ridiculous.

      August 21, 2025 👍 4
      • Guest-23029

        my co worker ordered me a standing desk about 8 months ago, and just received her third update on the delay in it arriving due to "manufacturing issues"....now it's expected in MID NOVEMBER! I could have just ordered an inexpensive one from Amazon or Costco! UGH

        August 22, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-21174

      I will never understand why we are required to purchase from middlemen, just so we can say we support disabled, veteran, and small businesses. All this does is encourage new small businesses to just resell things. That doesn't seem like its adding any value to either the business owner or the state.

      August 29, 2025 👍 2
  • Guest-21380

    One of our biggest problems within state service that affects (likely) every service and product we offer is - Leadership. The way our duty statements are written and who we prioritize within the hiring process often promotes individuals into leadership roles with very specific (often niche) knowledge, but frequently lacking leadership skills (like compassionate communication, relational intelligence, teambuilding, conflict resolution, creative intelligence, etc). I would suggest two things: Add new AGPA roles (e.g., AGPA 1, AGPA 2, etc) to allow for growth within analyst positions and reduce the need to transition into leadership for monetary purposes. The same is likely true for other position types. And I would also institute better leadership development programs. What we offer now is below minimum standard and/or often suggestive in nature - you "could" take X Course, but why do it when you're so busy. I would like to see distinct and explicit development programs, mentorship opportunities, and a clear progression path that tracks which leaders are not only learning these skills but applying them, and then make job opportunities for them to succeed and evolve within their career. I think with better leadership a lot of the bureaucracy, bottlenecking, communication, and problem-solving issues we face would likely "get better" on their own.

    August 18, 2025 👍 11
    • Guest-22261

      Strongly agree with everything you said here!

      August 20, 2025
    • Guest-22170

      Absolutely! We need across the board upskilling in a variety skill areas, including but not limited to leadership, conflict management, communication, AI, critical thinking, inclusiveness, and more.

      August 20, 2025 👍 1
    • Guest-25904

      The new Generalist Consolidation SPB just approved creates Analyst III and IV classifications above Analyst II (formerly AGPA).

      September 10, 2025
  • Guest-21492

    I think a lack of uniformity across state agencies and even within the organization creates a challenge. It seems each organization interprets the rules/regulations differently and as a result, the public is not necessarily receiving accurate information, and the red tape that our analysts end up dealing with causes major delays in our internal processes. Especially within the hiring process.

    August 18, 2025 👍 5
    • Guest-25904

      One factor contributing to this is that a small agency will be funded at the level it needs to carry out its core mission, but then there are dozens of mandatory auxiliary roles, so they've got one person wearing 20 hats and not really understanding many of them very well. Certainly there are some like the Ergonomics Officer where someone couldn't really do it remotely, but why in the world does each department need its own HR shop to do data entry when employees can submit address changes and withholding requests through CalConnect?

      September 10, 2025
  • Guest-21029

    It would be important to teach employees how to speak truth to power, as it were. We are often in positions where we are not heard, not consulted, and not valued. Yet, we are the front line, in the trenches and know what is going on with the work of serving Californians. Teach us how to speak to closed ears, to gain attention, and be heard will go a long way in state service becoming more effective. How can we navigate the politics and still keep our shirts (and jobs) would be very useful. Seems I can hear leadership laughing. However, if the purpose of service is truly important, then we need to be taken seriously. Leadership gets what leadership allows. It may be time to shift what leadership allows. Ty. Peace,

    August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21330

    As a new employee, I have struggled with receiving proper training. I think there should be a monthly onboarding call/orientation for all new state employees. Since we are all under one employer, there should be a standard for what information is shared to new staff. Each Department's HR has different capacities and people shouldn't have to wait 3 weeks to complete onboarding with HR.

    August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21350

    From my observations and experience, the misuse of consultants versus upskilling staff and providing a pathway for advancement. Some CEA leadership not being qualified for their role and no way to address via HR or other pathway since they may be appointed. Poor leadership allows for poor moral. Have noted leadership in some agencies unwilling to work with leadership from other. While not the 'norm', it does happen and creates barriers. Would like to know that GO leadership encourages leaders from within agencies to work together and track for accountability. I think that having a feedback loop that includes a 360 review tool for reviewing 'up' is really important for culture development as well as building trust within an agency. Even if some of the feedback sounds trite or personal, it is still feedback and encourages transparent conversations. Encourage lunch and learns that focus on emotional intelligence and communication versus just via onboarding training. While not required as part of annual trainings that I'm aware of, could be promoted as 'optional and encouraged' - it is empowering for self-reflection and relationship building.

    August 18, 2025 👍 22
    • Guest-22261

      I strongly agree with your first point. I took an entry-level job in my specialty expecting it to be an opportunity to build my skills but instead I am mostly doing contract management and deliverable review. I could have done that as an AGPA and made more money.

      August 20, 2025 👍 1
    • Guest-23418

      Overuse of consultants/contractors is a big issue, especially in "emergency" focused departments. I am surprised that Labor Relations and the unions do not push back harder on those contracts.

      August 25, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-22068

      Agree that Exec Leaders should be encouraged to work with other leaders. This is a problem in my office. Our Exec Leaders (previous and current) seem incapable of creating partnerships or relationships with leaders above them, let alone other offices and agencies that are relevant to our work. They cannot get information or help that we need. It has absolutely impeded some of our work.

      September 12, 2025 👍 1
    • Guest-26498

      I regularly see our Department turn to consultants to fill holes that should be part of our core competencies. This leads to cost increases, but even more troublingly it leads to a gradual loss of institutional capacity.

      September 30, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21318

    Bring in honest feedback from the ground up. Let it be safe and constructive. This platform may be a good first step for that.

    August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21274

    I think we need to (where appropriate, legal, and feasible) leverage AI and or machine learning to reduce the time spent on routine compliance and monitoring functions. For example, machine learning can be used to identify fraudulent or suspicious transactions for high volume systems (i.e., CalFresh). AI could be used to speed up user access reviews for critical systems by identifying users who haven't logged on for a certain period of time, moved to different position within the department, or are no longer on paroles. The biggest hurdle to this would be getting ever department access to ML/AI specialists who could appropriately implement these technologies as well as training existing compliance subject matter experts on how to use these AI tools. However, the potential cost savings arising from a reduction in manual compliance and monitoring tasks could be well worth the investment in AI technicians and new trainings for compliance staff.

    August 18, 2025 👍 10
    • Guest-22613

      If backed up by human operators who can review the results, this feels like a good and appropriate use of AI and machine learning. Instead I'm in a department where we're starting to explore the use of ChatGPT to speed up writing and editing original documents, despite LLMs being notoriously bad at these things. I would love to see effort put into exploration of how to use AI where it has documented benefits and usefulness with low margin of error, instead of charging ahead with the shiny Star Trek fantasy applications that the technology isn't actually ready for yet.

      August 20, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-22329

      I agree that there is a need for review users accessing critical systems. Too frequently, when a person changes positions, their prior access to systems is not changed accordingly allowing the potential for fraud. AI could definitely help monitor this.

      August 20, 2025
  • Guest-21386

    making forms and questions accessible for Californians

    August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21364

    The extremely outdated technology we work with on a day to day basis. With modernized software we could be more efficient with less errors and time wasted. Invest in looking into the most outdated software in the state and make a prioritization list to start modernizing with experienced professional in the IT world, not your average office worker without the experience who might put another system in place that doesn't meet the needs.

    August 18, 2025 👍 10
    • Guest-25904

      CDT implemented the PAL process under pressure from the legislature to reduce costly failed projects, which means that extensive business requirement documentation is expected to take place before programming begins. Even assuming business users are able to fully articulate their needs up-front (big if), the speed with which technology and business needs can change means that by the time a big project is approved, there is a good chance the plan is already outdated. Such slow-to-launch projects also encourage shadow-IT, with analysts developing their own internal business tracking practices while waiting on IT. This leads to having x number of various conflicting half-solutions in place that make change management and clean implementation of a centralized solution more challenging.

      September 10, 2025 👍 1
  • Guest-21409

    I agree with the excess bureaucracy, and I feel like there is a disconnect between the higher management like the chief of staff and the lower management like supervisors. I don't see why we have set days in person or reorganizing when there is no feedback for us to give.

    August 18, 2025
  • Guest-21099

    I'm reading the comments and recognize some of the suggestions mentioned have been used at one time or another based upon the direction of the exec staff at the time. I was sharing my vision of California State government equating it to a large corporation with consistent processes connecting each department within it with a friend who then corrected me in responding with each department is a company and they operate independent of each other.........which is what we have. I think we can do better. A member of the church I attend complained that they sell a publication to several state departments and they have to complete several forms from each department who is interested in purchasing their publication. Lean Six Sigma is a good continuous improvement methodology tool as well as, total quality management and there are others that might be worth looking into. The State had an external directory and call center. I like the idea of a Resource Hub for all employees. We had an Innovation lab at one time. Be great to have a Statewide lab and Statewide Resource Hub for all State employees. Mentorship programs come and go. Cross Training that includes T/D (an opportunity to work in a position for training and development for a period of time) should be required of all departments.

    August 17, 2025 👍 3
    • Guest-22261

      I especially agree with your last point. Shadowing or T/D opportunities would be extremely valuable. The National Parks Service does this very successfully, so there is a model we could base the State version on.

    Upper management, executives being more objective (not subjective) to technological advancements for public agency operations. Wherever there is proven efficiency to invest in should be supported, not being tied down to redundancy and beholden to lobbyists.

    That there is an open door policy for regular front line workers to share ideas.

    Less "social engineering" and more direct problem solving.

  • Guest-21243

    Leadership: The executive leadership team does not have the best interests of the department in mind. Nothing kills a team faster than a leader who doesn’t trust them. Leaders—your people aren’t there to make you look good. You’re there to make them successful. Without them, you don’t have impact. You have a title. Lead with trust. The results will take care of themselves. There is misalignment, miscommunication, trust, and a continued need to break down the silos. Also, for women in leadership: women should be lifting up and celebrating the successes of others. There is a lot to be gained from learning from others. Lastly, leaders, managers, supervisors: do not elevate yourself on the backs of others.

    August 15, 2025 👍 39
    • Guest-22994

      I agree. I see a lot of executive officers worrying about their own jobs rather than worrying about what is best for their employees and the State as a whole. Middle management just follows whatever higher level management wants without any questions or voicing concerns. Many supervisors are worried about the wrong things.

      August 20, 2025 👍 6
      • Guest-23825

        Middle management huge pushover with no backbone and execs just want the fluff work. It’s insane to me the amount of work assigned that is useless. Nothing useful, just another tool for them to use to reprimand staff instead of solving the issue at the root of the problem. To me improvements don’t automatically mean no job security.     • Guest-25846

      It would be good if the GO considered the Leadership skills of appointees prior to appointing them. Or having a training program for them to complete on leadership.     August 15, 2025 👍 2
  • Guest-21039

    As a newer state employee, one of the biggest problems I’ve noticed is the lack of proper training and clear guidance for staff, especially when it comes to understanding their roles, responsibilities, and how to grow within their careers. A lot of employees are expected to learn on the fly, but they don’t always know who to go to for help or even what other units or departments are responsible for. This creates confusion, delays, and sometimes discouragement, especially for new hires. If I could address one thing, it would be to improve communication and access to information. I’d love to see a more organized, user-friendly internal directory or onboarding resource hub that clearly outlines what each division does, who to contact, and what training or career development opportunities are available for your classification. I think mentorship programs or cross-division shadowing could also be super helpful to give employees real insight into how other parts of state government work. At the end of the day, I believe that better training and communication leads to more confident employees, better service to the public, and stronger teams overall.

    August 15, 2025 👍 239
    • Guest-21270

      This is very true! Even during the job application process, it is incredibly unclear what exactly the roles I was applying for were. The duty statements were often vague and incomprehensible, so I had no little understanding of what the day-to-day responsibilities of the roles were.

      August 18, 2025 👍 8
    • Guest-21432

      Agreed, training feels like swiss cheese full of gaps. For example Dept of Veterans Affairs part of training for newly onboarded staff clerical staff mgmt administered exams to new hires about where various departments can be found within the facility months before giving orientee a tour of the facility (for example relias exam question: where is radiology?, answer only known to current staff: radiology closed years ago we just can't update the online relias exam), nurses are being left alone at ward before being shown how to request medication refill, etc.

      August 19, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-21857

      Our little unit of 6 people was just as you describe, until we developed an "Onboarding Training" consisting of modules to orient, train, and simulate the work we do so new hires get direction, skill set training, and experience/rehearsal before being unleashed into the fray. Our onboarding program takes each new hire about 7 to 9 months to complete during their probationary period.

      Agree. Onboarding is not complete and unclear. Often left with questions about what to do when you first start.

    • Guest-21933

      I am not sure what department you are in but mine has a mentor program. I didn't know about it when I first started until I asked upper management. It has been a great program, and I put all my starting OAs in this program. I too wish that CalHR had the classification information. I have learned to network with others to get that information. Working in hiring, I do understand that just because someone is an OA and their next step would typically be OT doesn't mean that they don't qualify for an SSA or AGPA, skipping steps or maybe even a whole other possible route of executive secretary or way more. With this being said, I think this is why there is not what we are seeking. I think it would be too broad of a spectrum, especially since there are so many different qualifications for each position. I have been asking for years and have gotten the run around by multiple agencies. I would keep asking questions, network with others, speak to other people in and out of your department. Maybe speak to your department manager about your concerns and see what can be done about it. I always encourage my employees to come to me with their concerns, with a solution and be the change. We can always develop and be a better agency but without voicing it, we will never get better. I wish you the best in your career as a new state employee.

      August 20, 2025 👍 5

        Especially since we have so many positions that have similar duties.         August 21, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-24712

      We were having the same issue. We established a committee that coordinated the effort of all sections within our Division to create an informational document about their section. It included and org chart of their section, roles and responsibilities, and a contact list. The committee reviewed and revised each sections information and created a one page document for each section and stored it in a centralized location. They also created an onboarding document that gave an overview of the Agency and detailed information about our Division, Mission, Vision, Goals, Values, and Leadership Team. As part of another effort each section created a Knowledge Exchange Session (KES) video of what their role is within the Division. We still have pain points for new hires but this is a step in the right direction.

      August 22, 2025 👍 3
    • Guest-24860

      The ISO 9000 Process Standardization and Quality Management System would be a great place to start, with clear flow charts and process maps for the things that happen to every new employee like benefits enrollment, I9 forms, everything. And process maps would be available to the HR folks and the employee so that everyone can track the process. I think New Mexico State Government used ISO 9000 standards in some fashion. California is too big to 'wing it' which is what it feels like.

      I agree 100%! Communication is key and we should strive to move forward as a team with no 'gate-keeping' of information.

        It does take some time to network here. it can take a few years to get your head wrapped around who does what and you feel more impowered once you get a good network of likeminded working in the same direction folks.         August 26, 2025
    • Guest-22422

      I have to agree, when I was hired for my position, I asked what my job responsibilities would be and was told "we don't know". I have been fortunate to be able to define and add services to my role, but I hope this will not be an issue in the future.

      August 26, 2025 👍 1
    • Guest-25846

      I think one of the challenges that many departments face is a lack of staffing resources to provide this kind of support. Throughout the past 25 years of my state career, I have experienced several furloughs, and more hiring freezes and position sweeps than I'd care to remember. Over the years, staffing resources have eroded away to the point that there is just enough support to keep the lights on. With each of these cuts, we lose capacity for the true in-depth training and development that is called for commensurate with the positions. This, coupled with being constantly asked to do more with less, means that many of our staff are hired and "thrown into the deep end". It is extremely difficult to get more resources to replace those lost to position sweeps/etc. In fact, many departments have even lost sight of some of the things that they 'used to do' because they have not been staffed for so long. This becomes a vicious cycle, as people get frustrated with the amount of workload and lack of training. There has also been a noticeable gap in management training. Yes, it is required. However often times new managers start and are immediately thrown into fighting fires. The trainings turn into an inconvenience resulting in feeling even more overworked trying to make up for the time lost, rather than a respite to focus on growth and development for this exciting new career step. And although this thought is unpopular, I do not think that Telework has helped this situation.

      October 03, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-27253

      As a six-year employee, I've noticed that after training by a peer, information given and supported by "procedure" didn't match with provided *procedure documents* and sometimes did not match to *procedural sources provided by legal*. There is frequently a disconnect between the legal definition of words and the perceived meanings of them by managers and workers as information is handed down.

      October 08, 2025 👍 2
      • Guest-27253

        There are even remade documents that are interspersed with original procedures to "update" to something that appears inaccurate but based on authority structure it isn't possible to get a straight answer. Gatekeeping possibly preventing review of suspicious procedure.

        October 08, 2025

      Creating a desk manual for all the state employees including the supervisor role will help when there's a change in personnel. I like the idea of creating a back-up and a secondary back-up person who can mentor a new employee.

      October 15, 2025 👍 2

        I agree that a desk manual is needed. A way to simplify the access and update to such a manual would be to provide an electronic version via Wiki or SharePoint site which can be modified/updated easily and made available to targeted audiences.

        October 16, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-27805

      There are also outdated administrative practices that waste time. Why do timesheets still require manual signatures? Surely there’s an app that can verify absence requests and sync with timesheet data. And why must managers handle IDPs alone? Could HR assist with this process? Not every employee seeks career advancement—some are content as subject matter experts. Forcing development plans on everyone doesn’t reflect individual career goals. Process for training, roles for managers and procedures for tasks should be maintained and updated so staff can be successful rather than set up for failure.

      October 16, 2025 👍 2
      • Guest-26891

        Our department is enrolled in the SCO (State Controllers Office) system CEC (Cal Employee Connect) which includes a timesheet system that syncs to your position/timebase/tenure/ leave balances and an e-signature format. It has helped so much!

        October 16, 2025
      • Guest-27253

        We now do a daily time sheet - sign in/sign out. A daily work log, and must send a Teams message when we go on breaks - that we also put on the sign in/out sheet. It's horrendous. And we have the ability to do better.

        October 20, 2025

      I totally agree with this! There isn't a lot of guidance for newer staff on how to succeed, and we're left to either figure it out or find a coworker willing to help us figure it out.     • Guest-25325

      I have worked for the same Agency for many years, and I can tell you that training has never being available. Learn as you go (swim or sink). It seems that management get the training and stays with them because it's not disseminated to the staff.

      September 11, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-21907

      To do my small part, I implemented and easily searchable index of procedures for the work done by my unit. I make it clear to staff that they are responsible for updating the procedures for tasks they are responsible for. In addition, I identified a backup person for all tasks. Sometimes even a secondary backup. This has worked well for many years as team members come and go. Also recently, our department has been creating eLearning videos for new staff to introduce them to the different sections/programs and what they do. For example, an Accounting 101 class, Budgeting 101, Introduction to the Training Section, etc...

    • Guest-26675

      The agency I work for trains the heck out of our new hires until you get to management then it's similiar to what you have experienced.

      • Guest-26695

        I agree. In my 36 yrs of service, I've noticed one thing. Good Leadership is lacking. if you happen to get someone who is a great leader, you got lucky.     • Guest-26125

      As someone who recently joined the state from private industry I completely agree that more training and guidance is needed. While there is some HR specific training it is very basic. Even just navigating the building and finding what was available to us as employees was difficult. I was able to get some information from my supervisor (but they were also somewhat newer) and some coworkers once I had met enough people but it was a bit daunting initially. Another useful thing would be to have someone gather information about the surrounding location and businesses. I know many provide State discounts but there's no resource to see that and you have to ask each individual business.

      September 26, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-26597

      I completely agree. When I was hired, there was no onboarding conversation, no career path outlined, not even direction towards a reputable supplier for uniforms.       September 27, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-27487

      There are artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots including Microsoft Copilot Pages, which many state departments have access to. An administrator can upload all of their written policies to this AI and the chatbot can answer questions. For example, all HR documents can be uploaded and a chatbot can answer all (or many) general questions that do not require a human staff member to pull information from different systems or database. Of course better training is preferred, but having access to an AI-chat bot that can search and give new employees answers about written policies would possibly be a big improvement.

      September 29, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-22904

      I wholeheartedly support mentoring programs. In fact, I think that providing an assigned mentor to new hires should be a routine practice. This could be a coworker, someone doing the same or similar work in the employee's department (if not a coworker), or someone who is doing the same or similar work for another department who wants to serve as a mentor.

      September 29, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-26537

      I completely agree. That has been my experience as well. I was trained by a peer who didn’t explain the steps I was doing to complete the tasks. It really doesn’t make sense.

      September 29, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-26219

      This is 100% accurate and my biggest frustration. I have worked for various governmental agencies my entire life, and I have never experienced the lack of communication, guidance and training. Or there are areas that were never discussed, and each employee is expected to forge their own way. While I encourage building a better mousetrap, we do need standards, communication, and support.

      September 30, 2025 👍 2
  • Guest-21236

    Working in an institution, I would say one of the primary issues is valuing the inmate population over the staff. I know it may not be the actual case, but it definitely feels like that a lot of the time. I think staff needs to feel like they can do their jobs without the fear of getting in trouble all of the time because an inmate is going to file on them and they're going to be reprimanded. The entitlement by the inmate population has grown significantly.

    August 15, 2025 👍 4
    • Guest-24089

      Yes! Its like they know they will be able to have a 1-on-1 with the higher ups that will be confidential. Even though we all know they are manipulative, I think everyone, higher ups and all staff actually, are constantly trying to undermine each other and I have noticed that it happens even more when inmates are involved in the conversation. Like it makes you feel like a hero or something? I dont understand either... Definitely a huge problem though and it is part of why the turnover is so high and then subsequently staff are getting hurt more often as there is more inmates than correctional staff, and its just a domino effect of safety and security problems. Yet complaints about this put you on the radar so everybody just does their best to avoid being in that spotlight. I definitely agree there is a lack of trust from exec staff to their ground level staff and they demonstrate more trust in inmates. It makes lower level staff feel undervalued when we carry alot of the tasks nobody else will do but are necessary for the institution and carry a lot of legal weight.

      September 10, 2025
  • Guest-21136

    The Problem California’s current model for investigating and prosecuting insurance fraud is fragmented and inefficient. The Department of Insurance (DOI) houses the Fraud Division, a law enforcement unit operating within a regulatory agency. Simultaneously, $80.9 million in annual grant funding is distributed to county District Attorney’s (DA) Offices to prosecute fraud cases. This dual structure presents several challenges: Conflicting Missions: Regulatory agencies prioritize industry oversight and collaboration, which can conflict with the enforcement and prosecutorial roles of law enforcement. Operational Inefficiencies: DOI lacks the infrastructure, procurement agility, and legal support systems typical of dedicated law enforcement agencies. Jurisdictional Gaps: Only counties receiving grants can pursue fraud cases, leaving many areas underserved and creating geographic inequities. Short-Term Incentives: Grant-based funding encourages quick wins over long-term, complex investigations. Ethical Concerns: Financial incentives tied to prosecutions raise questions about the impartiality of justice. Recruitment and Retention Issues: DOI struggles to retain investigators, many of whom leave for better opportunities at the Department of Justice (DOJ). The Proposed Solution The proposal recommends transferring the DOI’s Fraud Division (163 peace officers and 75 support staff) and the $80.9 million in grant funding to the California Attorney General’s Office.

    August 15, 2025 👍 3
    • Guest-25803

      Yes, law enforcement units operating within regulatory agencies present many problems for law enforcement employees. This sounds very similar to Investigators at the Department of Health Care Services, who I learned are required to refer healthcare provider fraud cases exclusively to the CA DOJ for prosecution, harming County DA relationships. I heard Investigators in regulatory agencies (and probably others) work in programs that do not want them to exist, and the program's actions/lack thereof, have not led them to believe they are desired or valued. They also stifle their speech and do not solicit honest feedback. Regarding efficiency, these referral workflows are incredibly inefficient and take months just to pass the case over. Some cases get stale by the time they are delivered then a DOJ agent has to read the case and perform duplicate work to resume investigation.

      September 05, 2025
      • Guest-25904

        Similar problems arise from having EEO Officers report to departmental directors. Creating a centralized authority assigning investigators to departmental complaints would reduce this issue.

        September 10, 2025
  • Guest-21160

    I feel the largest hurdle we have to address is excess bureaucracy. In operating such a large government, it is easy to have too many fingers in the mix. It is our responsibility to analyze and identify areas we can cut down on bureaucratic surplus. For example, I find it absurd to be required to submit an 18-page document just to repair an ice maker. We must cut down on the number of forms and unnecessary hoops needed to complete the smallest of tasks.

    August 15, 2025 👍 179
    • Guest-21432

      Agreed, similarly job advertisements are far too wordy and some do not allow applying online, so it discourages would be applicants that can apply to multiple other jobs in the time it'd take to apply to 1 state job.

      August 18, 2025 👍 3
      • Guest-22442

        Also from job posting, interviewing to hiring is too long and convoluted - some positions take over 6 months to secure - for an office technician - that is ridiculous. It doesn't need to be that complicated and convoluted.

        August 19, 2025 👍 4
        • Guest-23792

          Agreed. I have lost some good candidates because they accepted other job offers during the months-long hiring process.

          August 21, 2025
          • Guest-24367

            I agree our department is the same way, candidates app goes to sacramento, they approve who we may interview, we interview then send back to sacramento who would like to hire, then they decide out of that whom we will hire. It takes 1-2 months to get it all said and done by then we call the applicant and their response is often thank you but I have already found a job.

            August 29, 2025
    • Guest-22410

      I agree with you on excess bureaucracy being a major hurdle. What really resonates with me is Ezra Klein’s idea of bottlenecks. I think we should go further and intentionally assign “bottleneck detectives.” Their role would be to find the unnecessary hurdles that don’t add enough value for the work they produce. Each team could have a benchmark for eliminating a certain number of these barriers every year. That way, we’re not just adding new checks and requirements—we’re also systematically cutting the ones that no longer serve a purpose. It would keep oversight in place while making sure the real work doesn’t get buried under endless forms and hoops.

      August 19, 2025 👍 3
    • Guest-22415

      I agree. The amount of documentation can be cut down and the processes can be simplified.

      August 19, 2025 👍 4
    • Guest-22442

      Yes! Bureaucracy is a big hurdle - I come from private industry and am just amazed it has become very difficult to do "busy" on a daily basis. How can we cut the bureaucracy? How can we become more efficient and effective? How do we encourage and reward our employees for their efficiency and effectiveness?

      August 19, 2025 👍 4
      • Guest-24510

        Ther needs to be emergency spending exemptions that remove the bureaucracy and allow the agencies that are responsible for emergency spending to get it done immediately. Emergency is the key word. When California is having natural or manmade disasters, emergency operations are bogged down by the control agencies that make the rules for spending. In my 37 years of experience in emergency response, the biggest hurdle in mitigating an emergency is the handcuffs placed on the response by the control agencies. When the control agencies are in the room with a seat at the table, they can't even figure out how to navigate through their own bureaucracy. I have personally had to remind them that they made the rule, they can change it. The control agencies need to sit with SMEs from response agencies and ask them how to make the processes more effective and efficient. We need people that want to collaborate and make positive changes, not just because it's the way it's been done for ever. California is the leader in emergency response and in many cases in the world, but we are handicapped by our archaic processes mandated by the control agencies.

        August 28, 2025
      • Guest-25669

        Hiring - anyone who has already passed an exam and proven themselves eligible should not have to retake the exam/have their eligibility expire. Anyone who is already a state employee and qualifies based on years of experience for the next classification should also not have to take the exam to be put on the eligibility list, they should automatically be added. For example someone with 1 year as AGPA should automatically be on the eligible list for SSM1, someone with 1 year as SSM1 should automatically be on the eligible list for SSM2, and so on. It doesn't make sense to have to retake exams year after year for classifications you already qualify for.

        August 29, 2025 👍 3

    • Guest-23675

      You bring up a great point. Excess bureaucracy can really slow down progress and drain resources. I completely agree that it's our responsibility to identify and eliminate unnecessary steps.     • Guest-24477

      Yes, I have been part of "committees" when I felt like one or two people could have made all of the decisions. Too many people involved with small decisions.

      October 01, 2025 👍 3
    • Guest-25846

      I do not disagree. But part of the reason we have an 18 page document is because there are layers upon layers of accountability and legislation that we are trying to adhere to. It's easy to say we need to cut down, but how to do so while still ensuring that: We meet recycled content certifications, bids, check if the vendor is delinquent on CDTFA or FTP taxes, whether SB/DVBE has a CUF evaluation, ensure there is backup for the awarded bid and solicitation, ensuring general provisions (GSPD 401) are incorporated if necessary, ensuring they are verified to do business in the State of CA (registered with Secretary of State), that they have a sellers/resellers permit through CDTFA, the Std 16 DFEH (complianceprograms@calcivilrights.ca.gov), Bargaining Unit Notices have been sent to unions for any contracts to ensure that state staff can't do the work, Bidders Declarations (FOR ALL COMPETITIVE SOLICITATIONS OR RFOS GSPD-05-105/GSPD-05-106), Bidders instructions, CalRIM approval, Certificate of compliance with State IT Policy (SIMM 71B-IT PURCHASES $5,000 AND OVER), etc.... there are literally two more pages of listed items that procurement staff need to ensure are present. How do we maintain accountability to the public, and litigators, if we do not have these documents? I'm not a fan of all this either... I think we've just made things complicated because, government....

      October 10, 2025 👍 3
      • Guest-27423

        There is an old saying that bureaucracy provides stability--it certainly can be bloated but I think it's a good thing and typically keeps things rolling when new administrations come on board and start making broad, sweeping changes....oh, wait?

    • Guest-25904

      To some extent, bureaucracy is "baked in" to our current legal system: the governor is elected and is answerable to the electorate, so while he delegates many things, the largest ones have to go through him for approval to implement. Traditionally, if the analyst has a big idea, it needs to be approved by the I, the II, the III, the Division Chief, the Deputy Director, the Chief Deputy Director, the Department Director, the Deputy Secretary, the Undersecretary, and the Agency Secretary before it goes to the Governor's Office. That's why this platform is such a vital innovation! Thanks for setting it up, ODI! I'm expecting great things.

      September 10, 2025 👍 3
      • Guest-22814

        Exactly--this bottleneck cripples so many good ideas. There have been ideas that I have had in the past that I considered sending just as a citizen, to my legislator because I knew it would be impossible to send up my chain of command--it would never get anywhere.

    Communication. Internal operational support units do not proactively communicate their procedures so business users cannot determine what is needed and in what order. Updates are made but not documented/communicated in a uniform, prompt, orderly process. Managers and executives do not communicate uniformly nor clearly with staff to set expecations and follow up with accountability. The level of safety for staff to communicate ideas and have them be heard objectively and considered operationally is often low.

    • Guest-21029

      September 10, 2025
  • Guest-20117

    Problem: Some of our regular processes have extra steps that don’t add much value but slow everything down. Solution: Pick one process—like reimbursements or onboarding—and walk through it as a team to see what steps we can cut. If three people have to sign something, maybe one signature is enough. Audit a single workflow in a small team, cut redundant approvals, and share the updated process with others to replicate.

    August 15, 2025 👍 96
    • Guest-21029

      This is suggesting using Lean Six Sigma Process Improvement for examining the processes within organizations. Great idea! CalHR offers courses.

      August 15, 2025 👍 14
    • Guest-21029

      Hello, it seems that a RACI Matrix chart may be of use in outlying the responsibilities for the phases. This is from project management. RACI stand for Responsible, Accountable, Consulted and Informed. It allows individuals to be identified in terms of what level of inclusion is needed in a process.

      August 18, 2025 👍 13
      • Guest-21529

        I am working on a project right now and came across RACI - there are different versions of it, but it is a useful tool. People perform better when they know what is expected of them.

        August 18, 2025 👍 2
    • Guest-21639

      This is a great point. Often, in the spirit of innovating, people think about what MORE we can do to improve. Sometimes, the best way to improve things is by removing, not adding. Remove steps. Remove unnecessary approvals. Remove old guidelines and job aids. Remove processes that haven't been updated in at least five years and start from scratch. No, don't simply update. Start over as if this process were being created today. It's extremely effective.

      August 18, 2025 👍 14
      • Guest-22780

        Yes, stop throwing money at new initiatives and improve processes that aren't working. Slow down and re evaluate what we already have in place, take feedback from the staff who are working the process.

        August 20, 2025 👍 5

      Definately streamline - Reimbursements go to one person for signature in my department before we send it to the accounting division for processing.
